rationale: Ruff has no rule that detects semantically redundant comments (comments that merely restate what the code does). ERA001 detects commented-out code blocks, not explanatory prose that duplicates code logic — that distinction requires semantic understanding Ruff does not perform.

Ruff performs no test-coverage analysis. Its PT (flake8-pytest-style) rules enforce pytest idioms and syntax, not whether changed code has corresponding tests. Coverage enforcement requires a separate tool (e.g. coverage.py/pytest-cov).

C901 (mccabe) measures cyclomatic complexity and flags functions above a configurable threshold — a syntactic proxy for 'too many responsibilities' — but it is not selected by default and does not capture true SRP violations (e.g. a low-complexity function that still mixes concerns). No rule performs genuine semantic SRP analysis.

Ruff has no rule that detects file-level SRP violations (a module doing too many unrelated things). All its complexity/refactoring rules (C901, PLR09xx) operate at the function or class level, not the file/module level.

Ruff's flake8-pytest-style rules (PT001–PT031) cover fixture style, parametrize formatting, assertion patterns, and exception handling in tests, but require opt-in and do not cover test file/class/function naming conventions or structural organisation beyond pytest-specific idioms.

F401 (unused-import) and F841 (unused-variable) are enabled by default and reliably catch those cases, but unreachable code after return/raise and dead branches are not covered — Ruff has no general dead-code flow analysis rule.
Ruff is a Python static analyser; it has no rules that check whether a CHANGELOG file has been updated. This is a process/repo-level check outside its scope.
Ruff is a linter, not a coverage tool. It has no rules that measure or enforce test coverage percentages or untested code paths; coverage.py/pytest-cov are needed for this.

SLF001 (private-member-access) flags access to private (_name/__name) members and, when applied to test files, discourages testing internal implementation details — but it requires opt-in, only catches the underscore-naming proxy, and misses many other forms of implementation coupling.

PLR2004 (magic-value-comparison) flags unnamed numeric literals in comparisons (opt-in), and PYI062 flags duplicate members in Literal[] annotations (default-on), but Ruff has no rule for repeated arbitrary string literals across a file — the general 'duplicated constant' case is not covered.

SIM rules (SIM201, SIM202, SIM300, etc.) normalise comparison expressions (double-negation, yoda conditions) and flake8-bugbear catches useless comparisons, but these require opt-in and cover only syntactic comparison patterns — broader semantic ambiguity (data normalisation, type consistency) is not detected.

Ruff has no rules that detect heuristic or probabilistic logic and suggest deterministic replacements. FIX/TD rules flag TODO/HACK markers but are incidental, not a semantic detector for heuristic code patterns.

Ruff has rules for specific brittle sub-patterns (B020, B023, B031, PT018) but 'brittle patterns' as a class requires semantic/contextual judgment about fragility, coupling, and change-resistance that no Ruff rule addresses generally.

No Ruff rule detects the design smell of over-specificity or failure to generalize. SIM116 suggests a dict over if-elif chains but that is a narrow mechanical refactor, not detection of the broader generalization vs. specificity design judgment.

PLR0904 (too-many-public-methods) counts public methods per class with a configurable threshold, catching one dimension of Large Class. However it is preview/opt-in only, does not measure lines of code or field count, and misses classes that are large due to long methods rather than many methods.
Primitive Obsession (using raw primitives instead of domain value objects) requires semantic understanding of domain concepts. No Ruff rule detects this pattern.

Data Clumps (groups of parameters/fields that always appear together and should be encapsulated) requires cross-function/cross-call-site analysis of argument co-occurrence. No Ruff rule addresses this.

SIM116 flags consecutive if-elif chains replaceable with a dict lookup, catching a mechanical subset of the Switch Statements smell. However it only suggests a dict refactor (not OOP polymorphism), misses long chains that don't fit the dict pattern, and is narrower than the general design smell.

PLW0201 (attribute-defined-outside-init) detects attributes assigned on self outside __init__, which is the closest static proxy for Temporary Field. However it misses fields set conditionally inside __init__ or used only transiently, so it covers only a subset of the smell.
Refused Bequest (subclass ignores inherited methods/state) requires semantic analysis of inheritance hierarchies and usage patterns. No Ruff rule detects this.

Ruff has no rule that detects two classes implementing similar functionality with incompatible interfaces. This requires semantic comparison across class boundaries, which is beyond static pattern matching.

Divergent Change requires analysing why a class changes across commits — a change-history smell that static analysis cannot detect. PLR0904 (too-many-public-methods) is a tangential size metric, not a detector for this pattern.
Shotgun Surgery requires correlating changes across many files over time; no Ruff rule approximates this cross-file, cross-commit coupling analysis.
Detecting Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies requires tracking paired subclassing relationships across the codebase over time; Ruff has no rule that targets this structural pattern.

PLR6301 (no-self-use) flags methods where `self` is unused, which is a symptom that a class may not need to be a class at all, but it does not directly detect an undersized/thin class and misses many Lazy Class instances.

The Data Class smell (a class with only fields and no real behaviour) has no Ruff rule. Ruff understands Python's @dataclass decorator as a feature, not a smell, and does not flag classes that lack meaningful methods.

Speculative Generality (dead or never-triggered abstractions added for hypothetical future use) requires understanding developer intent and cannot be distinguished from legitimate abstraction by any Ruff rule.

Ruff's FIX001/FIX002 detect TODO/FIXME tags, and pydocstyle D-rules enforce docstring presence, but none target the smell of over-reliance on comments to explain poorly named or complex code. Quality and density of comments is not assessed.
No Ruff rule detects Feature Envy. Determining that a method uses another class's data/methods more than its own requires inter-class data-flow analysis unavailable in Ruff.

SLF001 (private-member-access) flags access to _private / __private members of other objects, catching one symptom of inappropriate intimacy (reaching into internals), but misses over-coupling through public APIs and does not detect bidirectional intimacy.
No Ruff rule detects message chains (a.b().c().d() style traversals). Ruff has no chained-call depth or Law-of-Demeter rule.
No Ruff rule detects Middle Man. Identifying that a class delegates most work to another requires semantic analysis of delegation ratios across methods, which Ruff does not perform.

PLR0904 (too-many-public-methods) flags classes exceeding a configurable method count (default 20), catching one dimension of God Object, but it is preview/opt-in only and does not detect excessive responsibilities, size in lines, or cross-cutting concerns.

B903 (class-as-data-structure, preview) flags the narrow case of a class with only __init__ and no other methods, suggesting a dataclass or namedtuple. It misses domain classes that have some methods yet still lack meaningful domain behavior (the core ADM problem).
No Ruff rule detects Poltergeist objects (short-lived objects whose only role is to invoke methods on another object). This requires lifetime and purpose analysis beyond static pattern matching.
No Ruff rule measures inheritance hierarchy depth or detects the Yo-Yo Problem. Ruff has no MRO-depth or inheritance-chain-length check.
Sequential coupling requires tracking state and enforcing call-order invariants across methods — no Ruff rule performs this kind of inter-procedural control-flow analysis.

No Ruff rule detects a class or abstract base class whose sole purpose is to hold constants (the Constant Interface anti-pattern). B024 flags ABCs with no abstract methods but does not target constant-only classes specifically.

PLR0904 (too-many-public-methods) detects classes exceeding a configurable public-method count, which is a proxy for interface bloat. However it is a preview rule requiring the --preview flag, so it is not active by default.
Ruff has no rule that detects use or overuse of the Singleton pattern; this requires semantic/architectural judgement beyond static linting.

PLR0913 (too-many-arguments) and PLR0912 (too-many-branches) catch proxies — excessive parameter counts and branching — that often accompany combinatorial explosion, but they do not detect the semantic concept itself. C901 (McCabe complexity) similarly measures complexity symptoms only.

Tramp data (parameters forwarded unused through call chains) requires inter-procedural data-flow analysis. ARG rules flag locally unused arguments but cannot detect data that is only passed through to another layer.

C901 (McCabe complexity), PLR0912 (too-many-branches), and PLR0915 (too-many-statements) measure structural complexity proxies that correlate with spaghetti code, but none detect unstructured flow directly. C901 also requires explicit opt-in via lint.mccabe.max-complexity configuration.

PLR0801 (duplicate-code) is not implemented in Ruff — its documentation URL returns 404. PIE794 only flags duplicate field definitions within a single class, not copy-pasted code blocks across functions or files.

Golden Hammer is a high-level architectural smell (over-applying a familiar pattern to all problems) with no reliable syntactic footprint. No Ruff rule targets it; Ruff operates at the AST/token level and cannot detect design-level pattern misuse.

Ruff catches unused imports (F401, default) and unused local variable assignments (F841, default), plus commented-out code (ERA001, opt-in) and unused arguments (ARG001-005, opt-in). Critical gaps: no stable rule for unreachable code, no dead-function detector (vulture-style), and ERA001 is heuristic-prone and opt-in.

PLR2004 flags magic-value comparisons (opt-in, exempts 0/1), and S105/S106/S107 flag hardcoded passwords (opt-in, heuristic variable-name matching). Coverage is limited to numeric magic values in comparisons and credential-like strings; hardcoded paths, URLs, and general config values are not detected.

PLW0603 flags the `global` keyword (opt-in), which is a syntactic proxy for action-at-a-distance via global state mutation. B003 flags wholesale `os.environ = ...` reassignment (opt-in) but misses the more common `os.environ['K'] = v` and `os.environ.update(...)`. Module-level side effects and mutating shared singletons are not detected.

FBT001 (boolean-typed positional parameter), FBT002 (boolean default positional argument), and FBT003 (boolean literal at call site) directly target the boolean flag argument pattern (all opt-in). Coverage is limited to boolean type hints and literals; enum-style flags that partition function behavior are not detected.

Hidden Dependency (a function secretly relying on global state, environment variables, or mutable module-level singletons instead of explicit parameters) is a semantic design smell. PLW0603 only flags `global` keyword writes, not reads of global state; no Ruff rule detects `os.environ.get()` inside functions or access to module-level mutable singletons as hidden dependencies.

No Ruff rule specifically detects premature optimization. C901 (mccabe complexity, opt-in) flags overly complex functions but cannot distinguish complexity added for performance from any other kind. The PERF family actually flags code that is insufficiently optimized — the opposite direction.

The SIM family (flake8-simplify, opt-in) covers many reimplementation patterns: SIM110 (reimplementing any()/all() with loops), SIM105 (try/except/pass instead of contextlib.suppress), SIM118 (key in d.keys() instead of key in d), SIM401 (manual dict.get pattern), and others. C4 (flake8-comprehensions) and PTH (use-pathlib) also catch stdlib reinventions. All require opt-in; FURB118 (reimplemented-operator) is preview-only. Coverage is good but not exhaustive.
Big Ball of Mud is a system-level architectural anti-pattern requiring whole-codebase coupling and cohesion analysis; Ruff has no rule that detects unstructured monolithic design.
Stovepipe System requires cross-module communication and integration analysis; Ruff operates at the file/AST level and has no rule for siloed subsystem detection.
Database-as-IPC requires semantic understanding of inter-process communication patterns via a shared database; Ruff has no rule that identifies DB calls used as a signaling/messaging mechanism.
Vendor lock-in requires dependency and API-usage pattern analysis across the codebase; Ruff has no rule for detecting over-reliance on proprietary or framework-specific APIs.
SRP is a semantic design principle; PLR0913/PLR0915/C901 measure size/complexity proxies but cannot determine whether a class or function mixes unrelated responsibilities.
OCP requires reasoning about extension vs. modification intent across a class hierarchy; no Ruff rule detects modification of existing code where extension should be used instead.

LSP violations require behavioral/contract reasoning about subtype substitutability; B024 only checks that ABCs declare abstract methods structurally, not whether implementations honor the supertype contract.
ISP requires understanding which clients use which interface methods; ARG002 flags unused method parameters but does not detect fat interfaces or clients forced to depend on methods they don't use.

Ruff has no rule that detects structural dependency inversion violations (high-level modules depending on low-level ones). TID251 can ban specific APIs but requires manual per-project configuration and is not an automatic DIP detector.
Ruff has no rule that checks whether code depends on concrete implementations rather than abstractions/interfaces. This requires semantic type-relationship analysis that Ruff does not perform.

Ruff has no rule that detects when inheritance is used where composition would be preferable. B024 flags ABCs without abstract methods (an inheritance correctness smell), but that is not a composition-over-inheritance detector.

SLF001 enforces access to already-declared private members but does not detect design-level failures to encapsulate varying behaviour. Identifying what should be encapsulated requires semantic/design judgement beyond Ruff's syntactic reach.
Ruff has no coupling-analysis rule. Import-management rules (F401, TC001-TC003, TID251) reduce incidental coupling mechanically but do not detect or measure design-level coupling between modules.
Ruff is a static linter and cannot execute tests. Whether code passes a test suite is a runtime concern entirely outside its scope.

Ruff's N-series pep8-naming rules (N801-N816) enforce PEP 8 naming conventions, a mechanical proxy for intention-revealing names. SIM rules simplify convoluted expressions for clarity. These catch a real subset but cannot judge whether a name is semantically meaningful or logic truly reveals intent.

SIM101 (duplicate isinstance calls), SIM114 (combinable if-branches), SIM117 (nested with), and related SIM rules catch specific syntactic duplication patterns. Ruff has no structural clone-detection rule; broader DRY violations across functions/modules are invisible to it.

source_label: F401 unused-import (representative; also F841, ARG001-005, ERA001)
rationale: Ruff detects several categories of unnecessary/unused code (F401 unused imports, F841 unused variables, ARG001-005 unused arguments, ERA001 commented-out code), which collectively cover mechanical YAGNI signals. However, YAGNI in its full sense — 'don't add abstractions for hypothetical future needs' — is a design-level judgement Ruff cannot make. Coverage is partial: dead-code proxies only.

Ruff has no rule that validates dependency direction between architectural layers. Its `ruff analyze` subcommand can graph imports but does not assert that inner layers must not reference outer ones. Enforcing the Dependency Rule requires a dedicated tool such as import-linter.

Ruff has no concept of 'layers' or 'concerns'; it cannot detect that business logic, persistence, and presentation code are mixed in the same module. Separation of concerns is a semantic/design judgement beyond any static pattern Ruff checks.

Ruff cannot validate import directionality between named architectural layers. TID252 (ban certain relative imports) is a style rule, not an architecture rule. No Ruff rule detects that an inner-layer module imports from an outer-layer module.

The Boy Scout Rule ('leave code better than you found it') is a process/discipline principle, not a static property of a snapshot. Ruff's simplification rules (SIM*) encourage cleaner code but do not and cannot detect whether a given change degraded or improved surrounding code quality compared to its prior state.

Ruff has no rule requiring that ports be declared as ABCs or typing.Protocol. B024 detects ABCs that lack abstract methods, but does not enforce that port contracts must be expressed as interfaces at all.

Ruff cannot verify that a class labelled as an adapter actually inherits from or satisfies a port interface. Interface-implementation compliance requires type-level analysis (mypy/pyright), not pattern-based linting.

Ruff cannot determine whether a module belongs to the 'core' layer, nor whether its imports cross into infrastructure. Purity of the domain core from infrastructure dependencies requires import-boundary tooling (import-linter) or semantic naming conventions that Ruff does not enforce.
Hexagonal architecture driving-side (input ports/adapters) is a semantic design pattern. Ruff has no rules for detecting port/adapter boundary violations or driving-side architectural structure.
Hexagonal architecture driven-side (output ports/adapters) is a semantic design pattern. Ruff has no rules for detecting port/adapter boundary violations or driven-side architectural structure.
Testability-by-design (dependency injection, interface abstraction) requires semantic understanding of class design intent. Ruff has no rules for this pattern.

Dependency direction toward the domain core requires dependency graph analysis across module boundaries. Ruff's import rules (isort, TID) handle style and naming but not directional dependency enforcement.
Reuse/Release Equivalence Principle requires release-unit and reuse-unit analysis that Ruff does not perform.
Common Closure Principle requires co-change history analysis. Ruff is a static linter with no such capability.
Acyclic Dependencies Principle requires a full import-cycle graph analysis. Ruff has no circular-import or dependency-cycle detection rule; that is left to tools like import-linter or deptry.
Stable Dependencies Principle requires measuring component stability (fan-in/fan-out) and enforcing dependency direction. Ruff performs no such analysis.
Stable Abstractions is an architectural metric (abstractness vs. instability across modules); Ruff has no rule that measures or enforces this principle.
DDD Bounded Context is a design-time architectural boundary; Ruff has no rule that detects cross-context coupling or missing context definitions.

Ubiquitous Language requires semantic/domain knowledge to evaluate naming consistency across a bounded context; Ruff's N-series naming rules enforce PEP 8 style but cannot detect domain-language inconsistency.
DDD Domain Model correctness (anemic model, leaking infrastructure concerns) requires contextual semantic understanding; Ruff has no rule for this.
DDD Repository pattern violations (e.g. queries scattered outside repository classes) require architectural-level analysis; Ruff has no rule for this.
DDD Service Layer violations require understanding of layered architecture; Ruff has no rule that enforces or detects service-layer boundary breaches.

Encapsulate Variable/Record/Collection requires judging whether an internal data structure should be wrapped — a design decision Ruff cannot make. SLF001 only flags access to already-underscore-prefixed members, not the absence of encapsulation.

Indecent Exposure (over-wide public API surface) requires semantic judgment about what should be private; Ruff's SLF001 flags external access to private members but cannot detect that a member is missing its encapsulation in the first place.
Tell Don't Ask is a semantic OOP principle (commands vs queries at object boundaries); Ruff has no rule that detects external code querying object state then acting on it.
Replacing subclass with delegation is an architectural refactoring judgment; Ruff has no rule detecting inheritance that should be composition.

Collapsing a hierarchy requires judging whether an intermediate class is semantically necessary; Ruff has no rule for this (PLR0901 flags excessive depth but not unnecessary intermediaries, and requires opt-in pylint rules).
Refused Bequest (subclass ignoring inherited members) requires cross-class semantic analysis; Ruff has no rule tracking unused inherited methods or fields.
E741 flags the three visually ambiguous single-letter names (l, O, I), a narrow mechanical proxy for intention-revealing naming; the general case of unclear or generic names is not covered.
N801 enforces PascalCase for class names and N802 enforces lowercase for functions — purely syntactic case conventions, not semantic enforcement that names be nouns (classes) or verbs (methods).
One-word-per-concept consistency requires cross-codebase semantic reasoning about terminology uniformity; no Ruff rule addresses this.

Avoiding disinformation (semantically misleading names) requires understanding intent vs implementation; Ruff's E741 covers only visual ambiguity from specific single characters, not semantic misleadingness.

Ruff has no rule that detects abstraction-level mixing within a function. Complexity proxies (C901, PLR0915, PLR1702) measure size/nesting/complexity but cannot determine whether high- and low-level concerns are co-located — that requires semantic judgment.
No Ruff rule enforces Command-Query Separation. Detecting whether a function both mutates state and returns a meaningful value requires semantic analysis beyond static pattern matching.

Ruff cannot detect unexpected side effects in general. B006 (mutable-argument-default) and B909 (loop-iterator-mutation) catch specific narrow mutation patterns but not the broad class of unintended side effects, which requires semantic/dataflow analysis.

Ruff has no rule targeting boolean flag arguments specifically. PLR0913 (too-many-arguments) flags excess parameter counts in general but does not identify boolean parameters that bifurcate function behavior.

PLR0913 (too-many-arguments, default threshold 5) detects the symptom — too many parameters — which is the direct trigger for 'Introduce Parameter Object', but it does not distinguish cases where grouping is appropriate from cases where signature reduction is the fix.

RET505 (superfluous-else-return) directly flags the else branch after a return, enforcing the guard-clause style for that specific pattern. SIM102 (collapsible-if) helps with simple nested ifs. Together they cover common cases but miss complex nested conditional structures that guard clauses would fully flatten.

Ruff has no rule suggesting replacement of isinstance/type-dispatch chains with polymorphism. SIM101 and SIM116 simplify repeated isinstance calls or suggest dict lookups but do not flag the architectural smell of type-switching that polymorphism would eliminate.

PERF401 (manual-list-comprehension) and PERF403 (manual-dict-comprehension) detect for-loops that should be list/dict comprehensions; C417 (unnecessary-map) flags redundant map() calls. These cover the most common loop-to-pipeline transformations but not chained functional pipelines (map+filter combos, generator chains) or all set-comprehension cases.

Ruff has no rule that detects slow or performance-heavy tests. ASYNC250/251 flag blocking calls in async functions but that is not the same as detecting tests that violate the Fast principle generally.
Ruff has no rule that detects tests sharing state or depending on execution order. B006/B019 touch adjacent mutable-state issues but do not detect inter-test dependency.
Ruff has no rule for detecting non-deterministic or environment-dependent tests (e.g. unseeded random, uncontrolled time, filesystem side-effects). This requires runtime or semantic analysis.

PT015/PT017/PT009 enforce correct assertion style in pytest tests, but no Ruff rule detects a test function that contains zero assertions — the core self-validating violation — so coverage is partial.
Timely is a TDD workflow practice (write tests before production code); it requires git-history or temporal ordering analysis, not syntactic inspection. No static-analysis rule can detect it.
Red-Green-Refactor is a TDD cycle discipline tracked across test-run states and commits; it cannot be detected from a static snapshot of source code.

Fake It Till You Make It describes an intentional early-TDD technique (hardcoded return values); distinguishing it from accidental hardcoding requires intent-level understanding beyond static analysis.

Triangulation is a TDD generalisation practice (adding test cases to force a general implementation); determining whether a test suite triangulates sufficiently requires semantic and design-level judgement, not syntactic rules.
Ruff has no rule for 'Assert First' test ordering — PT rules cover assertion style (PT009, PT015, PT017, PT018) but not the pattern of ensuring tests make assertions before other logic.
Ruff has no rule for detecting untested or legacy code. ERA001 flags commented-out code, but there is no concept of 'code without tests' in any Ruff rule.
Characterization tests are a testing strategy concept; Ruff has no rule to detect, enforce, or flag their presence or absence.
Sprout Method/Class is a refactoring technique; Ruff has no rule to detect or enforce this pattern.
Wrap Method/Class is a refactoring technique; Ruff has no rule to detect or enforce this pattern.
Extract Interface is a refactoring technique. Ruff has ABC-related rules (B024, B027, FURB180) but none detect the absence of or need to extract an interface/protocol.
Parameterize Constructor/Method is a refactoring technique; Ruff has no rule to detect hardcoded values in constructors or the absence of parameterization.

ANN001-ANN206 flag missing type annotations and FA100/FA102 flag missing 'from __future__ import annotations', covering mechanical annotation gaps — but all ANN rules are opt-in (not enabled by default) and gradual typing strategy (where to start, what to prioritize) is beyond static detection.

Ruff has several rules touching isinstance/type-comparison patterns (E721, FURB168, SIM101, UP038) but these address specific anti-patterns like using == instead of isinstance or redundant isinstance calls — they do not enforce correct type narrowing logic or flag missing/incorrect type guards in the semantic sense.
Prototype pollution is a JavaScript-specific vulnerability (modifying Object.prototype). Python has no prototype chain, so this construct does not exist in Python and Ruff cannot apply.

Global scope pollution via window/global assignment is a JavaScript concept. Python has no equivalent browser/Node global object; the closest Python concern (shadowing builtins) is a different pattern handled by A001-A006 but that is not the same construct.

Ruff has no rule that detects implicit request context being passed through models (e.g., Flask g, Django thread-locals). This is a design/architecture concern requiring semantic understanding of the call graph, not a syntactic pattern Ruff can flag.
Ruff's S307 flags eval() and S102 flags exec() — together these cover the primary dynamic execution functions. Both are in the bandit (S) rule family and detect usage deterministically.

Ruff's A001-A003 rules flag shadowing of builtin names but do not detect runtime monkey patching (e.g., replacing methods on existing objects or classes). No rule detects arbitrary attribute replacement on third-party or stdlib types.

Ruff is a linter, not a type checker, and has no rules enforcing Protocol usage or structural typing discipline. Enforcing duck typing conventions (e.g., requiring Protocol annotations) requires mypy or pyright.

Lint as build failure is a CI/CD pipeline configuration concern (exit codes, gate configuration), not a code pattern. No linter rule can detect or enforce this; it is outside the scope of what Ruff rules cover.
Ruff has no rules about test existence or coverage thresholds; pytest-style rules (PT prefix) govern test structure only, not coverage requirements.
Ruff has no rules for pinning dependency versions or validating external API contracts; TID251 (banned-api) is a general import ban tool, not a contract-validation rule.
Ruff's G-series rules (G001-G004) prevent pre-formatted strings in log calls, which is a prerequisite for structured logging, but no rule enforces JSON/key-value structured log formats directly.
Ruff has no rules about including correlation IDs or trace context in log calls; this requires semantic/contextual judgment.
Ruff has no rules detecting whether a service exposes health-check or readiness endpoints; this is an architectural/operational concern outside static analysis scope.
Alerting strategy (symptom-based vs. cause-based) is a monitoring/operational concern with no corresponding Ruff rule.
Postel's Law is an API design principle; Ruff has no rule that enforces or detects violations of liberal input / strict output behavior.
Ruff has no rules for validating semantic version strings or version bump conventions; this requires external tooling.

Ruff has no rule that detects backward-incompatible API changes (removed/renamed public symbols, changed signatures). UP035 flags deprecated stdlib imports from typing, which is a narrow stdlib concern unrelated to general backwards-compatibility enforcement.

DOC501 (preview) flags raised exceptions not documented in docstrings, directly addressing explicit error contracts — but it requires opt-in preview mode and only covers the docstring-documentation proxy, not runtime error contract design more broadly.
Ruff is a Python source linter; it does not parse requirements.txt, pyproject.toml dependency pins, or lock files and has no rules about transitive dependency pinning.

F401 detects unused imports in source files — the closest mechanical proxy for unnecessary dependencies — but does not analyse requirements files, detect redundant packages, or address package-level dependency footprint beyond unused import symbols.

Ruff has no rules for detecting outdated or vulnerable dependency versions. The S-series (bandit) rules flag insecure code patterns, not known-CVE dependency auditing — that requires separate tools (pip-audit, safety).

Package-level single-responsibility violations are an architectural/semantic concern. Ruff's PLR0912/PLR0913/PLR0915 measure function/class complexity, not module or package cohesion; no rule addresses package-level SRP.

B006 (mutable-argument-default), RUF012 (mutable-class-default), RUF008 (mutable-dataclass-default), and RUF024 (mutable-fromkeys-value) cover common mutable-default patterns, but Ruff cannot detect broader immutability violations such as in-place mutation of passed-in containers or unnecessary variable reassignment.

PLW0603 (global-statement) flags explicit use of the `global` keyword — a syntactic proxy for shared mutable state — but misses class-level shared variables, module-level singletons, closure captures, and inter-thread shared state.

Idempotency is a semantic property — Ruff's related rules (B006 mutable defaults, RUF012 mutable class defaults) catch mutable shared state, which is a related smell, but they do not detect non-idempotent operations generally. No Ruff rule checks whether a function or operation is idempotent.

Ruff's FBT001/FBT002/FBT003 (boolean trap rules) discourage boolean flags and nudge toward enums, which is a weak proxy, but they do not detect implicit state machines or enforce that state transitions be explicit. No Ruff rule analyzes state machine patterns.

PLW0603 flags `global` statements (mutation of module-level shared state) and B006/RUF012 flag mutable shared defaults; these catch the most common Python patterns of incorrectly assuming ownership of shared/global resources. However, thread-safety issues, shared container mutation, and object ownership assumptions are not covered.

Ruff has no rule that distinguishes 'why' (decision) comments from 'what' (mechanical) comments. Pydocstyle (D) rules only check docstring presence and formatting, not whether documentation captures decisions vs. mechanics.

DOC102 (docstring-extraneous-parameter) and DOC201/DOC202 (missing/extra returns) catch drift between a function's signature/behavior and its docstring, partially enforcing docs-close-to-code for signatures. Requires --preview flag; does not catch semantic drift or stale prose.

Ruff has no rule that enforces the presence of doctests or runnable examples. Its formatter can reformat code blocks inside docstrings but does not verify executability or enforce that examples exist.

S103 (bad-file-permissions) detects overly permissive chmod modes, and SLF001 (private-member-access) discourages accessing private internals. These cover narrow, mechanical privilege cases but not broad least-privilege design (unnecessary public APIs, capability over-granting).

Ruff's S-prefix (bandit) rules detect individual attack vectors (hardcoded passwords, weak RNG, unsafe subprocess, etc.) but do not check for layered validation or defence-in-depth patterns. That requires architectural/semantic reasoning beyond rule-based analysis.

Ruff's S110/S112 (try-except-pass/continue) and BLE001 (blind-except) flag broad exception swallowing, but none can determine whether a suppressed exception is in a security-sensitive context or whether the fallback value is permissive rather than restrictive — the fail-secure semantic requires domain-aware reasoning that Ruff does not perform.

Pylint flags empty docstrings (C0112) and pointless standalone string literals (W0105), but has no rule that detects semantically redundant comments — i.e. comments that merely restate what the code already says.

Pylint is a static analyser and has no awareness of which functions are tested or whether new code has corresponding tests; test-coverage enforcement requires a dedicated tool such as coverage.py / pytest-cov.

Pylint offers size/complexity proxies (too-many-branches R0912, too-many-statements R0915, too-many-arguments R0913) that can surface overly large functions, but these are heuristic thresholds, not semantic SRP detection — a function can violate SRP without tripping any threshold.

Pylint's too-many-lines (C0302) flags overly long modules, which is a rough proxy for a file doing too much, but it is a pure line-count heuristic with no understanding of whether a module has multiple unrelated responsibilities.

Pylint has no rules for test file organisation, layout conventions, or naming patterns specific to test files; test structure is enforced by test runners (pytest) and linters like flake8-pytest-style, not Pylint.

Pylint provides comprehensive dead-code detection: unused imports (W0611), unused variables (W0612), unused arguments (W0613), unused private members (W0238), and unreachable code after return/raise (W0101), covering the main categories deterministically.
Detecting whether a CHANGELOG file was updated is a repository-process check, not a static-analysis concern; Pylint has no rule for this and it is out of scope for the tool entirely.
Pylint performs static analysis only and does not measure or enforce code-coverage percentages; coverage is a runtime metric handled by coverage.py / pytest-cov, not by Pylint.
Pylint has no rules about test methodology or whether tests verify requirements vs. implementation details; this requires semantic/design judgment beyond static pattern matching.

R0801 (duplicate-code) detects similar code blocks across files but not repeated literals specifically; W1308 (duplicate-string-formatting-argument) only covers .format() call arguments. No built-in rule targets repeated use of the same string or numeric literal across a file.
Pylint has no rule targeting normalization of ambiguous data or code patterns; convention rules like C0103 (invalid-name) cover naming but not the broader remove-ambiguity class.
Detecting heuristic approximations that should be replaced with deterministic logic requires semantic understanding; Pylint has no rule in this space.
Pylint has no rules for brittle patterns such as fragile string comparisons, version sniffing, or platform-specific hacks; these require contextual judgment.

R2004 (magic-value-comparison) is the closest match but requires opt-in via the pylint.extensions.magic_value plugin, not part of the standard/default ruleset. No built-in rule detects over-specificity or hardcoded values in general.

R0902 (too-many-instance-attributes) and R0904 (too-many-public-methods) are built-in, enabled by default, and directly flag classes exceeding configurable size thresholds, covering the Large Class smell.

R6101 (consider-using-namedtuple-or-dataclass) addresses the dict-to-dataclass case of primitive obsession but requires opt-in via pylint.extensions.code_style plugin; no rule covers using raw strings/ints where domain types should be used.

Pylint has no rule that detects recurring groups of parameters/fields that always travel together. The closest rules (R0914 too-many-locals, R0902 too-many-instance-attributes) flag quantity thresholds, not the semantic pattern of data clumps.

R0912 (too-many-branches) flags long if-elif chains by branch count, and C0123 (unidiomatic-typecheck) / R1701 (consider-merging-isinstance) catch some type-dispatch patterns, but no rule specifically identifies if-elif chains used as a polymorphism substitute.

W0201 (attribute-defined-outside-init) catches attributes assigned outside __init__, covering the common Python form. It misses the case where the attribute is set in __init__ to a sentinel but only meaningfully populated in certain code paths, which is the core of the Temporary Field smell.

Pylint has no rule detecting a subclass that inherits but refuses to use parent behavior. W0223 (abstract-method) only flags unimplemented abstract methods; it does not detect the broader pattern of silently discarding or stubbing inherited non-abstract functionality.
Detecting classes that perform equivalent roles but expose different method names requires cross-class semantic comparison. No documented Pylint rule addresses this pattern.
Divergent Change requires understanding why a class changes across commits (SRP violation by change history). Static analysis cannot detect it; Pylint has no rule for it.
Shotgun Surgery is detectable only by observing that one logical change forces edits across many unrelated classes — a change-history pattern, not a static structure. Pylint has no rule for it.
Detecting parallel inheritance hierarchies requires recognizing structural correspondence between two separate class trees. Pylint has no rule for this cross-hierarchy semantic pattern.

R0903 (too-few-public-methods) flags classes with fewer than the configured minimum public methods, which is a syntactic proxy for Lazy Class, but it is a count threshold — it does not assess whether the class does meaningful work, and it can false-positive on intentional single-responsibility classes (e.g. exceptions, mixins).

R6101 (consider-using-namedtuple-or-dataclass) detects uniform-key dict patterns and suggests converting to a dataclass, but does not flag manually written classes that are pure data bags (fields + trivial getters/setters). R0903 may also hint at it indirectly. The canonical Data Class smell — a class with no real behaviour — is not reliably detected.

Pylint has no rule for Speculative Generality. Detecting unused abstraction layers, dead hook parameters, or over-engineered extension points requires semantic/contextual judgment that Pylint does not perform.

R2044 (empty-comment) only flags bare '#' lines with no text — it does not detect over-reliance on comments as a design smell (i.e. code that needs heavy comments because it is unclear). No Pylint rule measures comment density or flags comments substituting for readable code.
Pylint has no rule that detects Feature Envy (a method that accesses another class's data/methods more than its own). This requires cross-object usage analysis that Pylint does not perform.

Pylint has no rule for Inappropriate Intimacy (two classes that are excessively coupled to each other's internals). Detecting bidirectional private-member access across class boundaries is not in Pylint's standard rule set.

Pylint has no rule that detects Message Chains (train wrecks — long chains of a.b().c().d()). The chained-comparison rule (R1716) is unrelated; it addresses boolean operator chains in comparisons, not attribute/method call chains.

W0246 (useless-parent-delegation) detects the narrow case where a method override does nothing but call the identical super() method, which is one instance of Middle Man. It does not detect the broader pattern of a class that delegates all meaningful work to a contained/associated object.

R0904 (too-many-public-methods) and R0902 (too-many-instance-attributes) together serve as size-based proxies for God Object, but they catch quantitative bloat only — a class can have many responsibilities without exceeding thresholds, and a large class is not necessarily a God Object. No semantic SRP-violation rule exists.

R0903 (too-few-public-methods) flags classes with very few public methods, which can surface data-only classes lacking domain behaviour — a weak proxy for Anemic Domain Model. However, it fires on any class with few methods regardless of intent, generates many false positives (e.g. dataclasses), and completely misses anemic classes that happen to have a few utility methods.

Poltergeist is a class that exists only to delegate to another class and adds no state or logic of its own. Pylint has no rule that detects short-lived, stateless delegator classes — this requires semantic/contextual analysis beyond static metric checks.

R0901 (too-many-ancestors) counts the number of direct parent classes (horizontal breadth), not the depth of the inheritance chain. Yo-Yo Problem requires detecting deep vertical inheritance hierarchies; Pylint has no rule for inheritance depth.

Sequential Coupling requires that methods be called in a specific order and is a runtime/semantic constraint. Pylint has no rule for detecting enforced method-call ordering or missing setup/teardown sequencing — this requires dataflow or semantic analysis.

Python has no formal interface construct, so the classic Constant Interface anti-pattern (implementing an interface purely to inherit its constants) does not directly apply. While a class used solely as a namespace for constants exists in Python, Pylint has no rule detecting this pattern. 'na' is inappropriate because the underlying construct (a class holding only constants, inherited for namespace access) can exist in Python — but there is no Pylint detector for it.

R0904 (too-many-public-methods) directly flags classes whose public API surface exceeds a threshold (default 7), which is the canonical symptom of Interface Bloat. However, the threshold is arbitrary and configurable, it only catches extreme cases, and it does not distinguish intentional wide APIs from bloat.

Pylint's singleton-comparison (C0121) detects improper comparisons to None/True/False, not the Singleton design pattern. There is no Pylint rule that detects over-reliance on the Singleton creational pattern — detecting this requires understanding global state usage and design intent.

No rule targets combinatorial explosion semantically. Pylint flags structural proxies: too-many-branches (R0912), too-many-boolean-expressions (R0916), and optional McCabe complexity (R1260 via pylint.extensions.mccabe). These detect overly branchy code but not the specific pattern of parameter-combination explosion.

Tramp data (passing data through multiple intermediate layers unused) is a semantic/architectural pattern. Pylint has no rule that traces data flow across call boundaries to detect unnecessary threading of parameters.

No rule targets spaghetti code directly. Pylint flags structural proxies: too-many-branches (R0912), too-many-statements (R0915), too-many-nested-blocks (R1702), and optional McCabe complexity (R1260). These catch symptoms of tangled control flow but cannot assess overall structural coherence.

R0801 duplicate-code (similarities checker) targets copy-paste directly and is enabled by default. However, it uses token-level similarity with a minimum line threshold (default 4 lines), so small duplications, renamed-variable clones, and structurally equivalent but lexically different blocks are missed.
Golden Hammer (reflexive over-application of a familiar tool/pattern) is a purely semantic/architectural anti-pattern requiring contextual judgement about design intent. Pylint has no rule for this.

Pylint catches some dead code: unused-import (W0611), unused-variable (W0612), unused-argument (W0613), useless-return (R1711), pointless-statement (W0104). However, it cannot detect dead functions/classes that are syntactically referenced but never called at runtime, or legacy modules kept for historical reasons.

R2004 magic-value-comparison exists but requires opt-in via pylint.extensions.magic_value plugin (not enabled by default), and only covers magic values in comparison expressions — not hard-coded strings/numbers used in assignments, function calls, or other contexts.

W0603 global-statement flags explicit use of the 'global' keyword, which is the most common Python mechanism for action-at-a-distance via global mutation. However, it misses mutable class attributes, module-level mutation without 'global', and indirect state changes through mutable default arguments or shared objects.
Pylint's R0913 (too-many-arguments) counts parameters but never inspects argument types or semantics to flag boolean control-flow flag arguments as a design smell. No rule targets this pattern.

No Pylint rule detects hidden dependencies (hardcoded singletons, implicit global state, undeclared module-level side-effect coupling). W0603 (global-statement) flags explicit 'global' keyword use but does not cover the broader design smell of hidden coupling.

Premature optimization is a semantic/judgement issue (choosing the wrong abstraction for speculative performance). Pylint has no rule that detects this pattern; it would require contextual understanding beyond static syntax analysis.

Inner-Platform Effect is a macro architectural smell (reimplementing a framework/language within the application). Pylint has no rule targeting this pattern; detection requires holistic design understanding.

Big Ball of Mud is a system-level architectural anti-pattern requiring whole-codebase structural assessment. Pylint has no rule for this; R0801 (duplicate-code) and complexity metrics touch related symptoms but do not diagnose the pattern.

Stovepipe System (siloed subsystems with no shared infrastructure) is a cross-module architectural smell. Pylint has no rule that detects this pattern; it would require understanding intended vs. actual module coupling at architectural scale.

Database as IPC requires understanding the intent behind database calls (coordination between processes vs. persistence). Pylint has no rule targeting this anti-pattern; it cannot distinguish legitimate DB use from IPC misuse.

Vendor lock-in is a strategic architectural smell. Pylint has deprecation rules (W4901 deprecated-module) but no rule that flags tight coupling to vendor-specific APIs as a design anti-pattern. Import analysis is not sufficient to diagnose lock-in intent.

Pylint has heuristic size/complexity proxies (R0902 too-many-instance-attributes, R0904 too-many-public-methods, R0915 too-many-statements) that flag bloated classes, but none of them test actual cohesion or single-responsibility semantics; a small but multi-responsibility class is invisible to these checks.
Open/Closed Principle requires reasoning about whether a class is designed for extension without modification — a semantic design property that no Pylint rule addresses.

W0221 arguments-differ and W0222 signature-differs catch method-signature violations of LSP (the docs explicitly link W0221 to LSP), but behavioural LSP violations (wrong return types, strengthened preconditions, weakened postconditions) are not detected.

W0223 abstract-method flags concrete subclasses that fail to implement inherited abstract methods (an ISP symptom — fat interface forcing unwanted implementation), but ISP violations where the interface is simply too broad without raising NotImplementedError are not caught.
Dependency Inversion Principle (depend on abstractions, not concretions) requires reasoning about whether injected types are abstract or concrete — a semantic judgement no Pylint rule makes.
'Program to an interface' means coupling to abstract types rather than concrete classes; Pylint has no rule that checks whether code depends on abstract base classes vs. concrete implementations.

R0901 too-many-ancestors flags excessively deep inheritance chains, nudging toward composition (the docs suggest composition as the fix), but the rule only triggers on depth, not on cases where inheritance is used inappropriately for code reuse when composition would be better.
'Encapsulate what varies' is a design-time principle about isolating change points; Pylint has no rule that detects the presence or absence of such isolation.

source_label: R0401 cyclic-import (only partial coupling signal, insufficient for this item)
rationale: Pylint has R0401 (cyclic-import) which catches circular dependencies, and various too-many-* metrics, but no rule evaluates whether a design is 'loosely coupled' in the GoF sense — that requires semantic/architectural judgment about abstractions and dependency direction that no Pylint rule addresses.
Pylint is a static analyzer and cannot determine whether tests pass or that test coverage is sufficient; test execution and coverage are outside Pylint's scope entirely.

Pylint has naming convention rules (C0103 invalid-name) that enforce regex-based naming patterns, but 'reveals intention' is a semantic quality — whether a name communicates purpose — which requires human or LLM judgment; Pylint cannot assess semantic clarity of names.

Pylint's R0801 (duplicate-code) detects textual/syntactic similarity across files via its similarities checker, covering a subset of DRY violations. It misses semantic duplication (same logic expressed differently), logic duplicated within a single file, and requires a minimum similarity threshold to trigger.

YAGNI (fewest elements / no speculative code) requires understanding intent and future necessity — whether code, classes, or abstractions exist beyond current need. Pylint has no rule that detects over-engineering or unused abstractions in the YAGNI sense; this is a design-judgment concern.

Clean Architecture's Dependency Rule (inner layers must not depend on outer layers) requires knowledge of layer assignments and enforcing directional import constraints. Pylint has no mechanism to define or enforce layer boundaries; R0401 only catches cycles, not directional violations.

Separating concerns into architectural layers (presentation, domain, infrastructure) is a structural design property that requires semantic understanding of layer roles. Pylint has no rule that identifies or enforces concern separation across layers.

Whether inner layers 'know nothing of outer' (Clean Architecture's inward-only dependency flow) requires layer-aware import analysis. R0401 catches cycles but not unidirectional violations where an inner module imports from an outer one; Pylint cannot enforce this without user-defined layer configuration, which it does not support.
Boy Scout Rule (leave code cleaner than you found it) is a process/discipline norm, not a detectable code pattern; Pylint has no rule for it.
Whether ports are defined as interfaces (ABCs/Protocols) is a design convention; Pylint has no rule enforcing that boundary components must be abstract types.
Whether concrete adapter classes implement the correct port interface requires knowledge of which classes are adapters — a semantic/architectural distinction Pylint cannot make.

Purity of the core (no I/O, no framework imports) depends on knowing which modules belong to the core layer; Pylint's import rules (cyclic-import, import-outside-toplevel) are unaware of layer membership.
Identifying driving-side actors and verifying they interact only via primary ports is an architectural intent check; Pylint has no rule for it.
Identifying driven-side adapters and verifying they are reached only through secondary ports requires layer labelling that Pylint does not support.
Testability by design (swappable adapters, injectable ports) is a structural property assessed via design review; Pylint has no rule for it.
Dependency direction (outer layers depending on inner, never the reverse) requires layer annotations; Pylint's cyclic-import (R0401) only detects circular imports, not directional layer violations.

Reuse/Release Equivalence is an architectural design principle (units reused together should be released together) requiring human/semantic judgement about packaging intent; Pylint has no rule for it.
Common Closure Principle (classes that change together belong together) is a cohesion heuristic requiring semantic understanding of change reasons; Pylint has no rule for it.

Pylint's cyclic-import (R0401) detects circular import chains between modules, which is the Python-level proxy for acyclic dependency violations; however it only catches import-time cycles and cannot enforce component/package-level DAG boundaries or detect looser coupling violations (e.g. runtime dynamic imports), so coverage is partial.
Stable Dependencies Principle (depend only on modules more stable than yourself) requires computing and comparing stability metrics across the codebase; Pylint has no such metric or rule.
Stable Abstractions Principle (stable components should be abstract; instability correlates with concreteness) requires cross-module abstractness/stability ratio analysis; Pylint has no rule for it.
DDD Bounded Context is an architectural partitioning concept requiring domain knowledge to identify context boundaries; no static analysis rule can enforce it.
Ubiquitous Language requires matching code identifiers to domain terminology, which demands domain knowledge; Pylint has no rule for naming conventions tied to domain models.
Domain Model correctness (entities, value objects, aggregates, domain events structured properly) requires semantic understanding of DDD patterns; Pylint has no rule for DDD structural conventions.
Mypy is a type checker; it has no rule that detects redundant, outdated, or low-value comments — those require semantic/contextual judgment.
Mypy performs static type analysis only; it has no concept of test coverage measurement for new or changed code.
SRP violations at the function level require semantic judgment about responsibilities; mypy has no such structural design rule.
SRP violations at the file/module level require semantic judgment; mypy has no module-cohesion or responsibility analysis rule.
Test file structure conventions (naming, organization, fixture usage) are not within mypy's scope; it checks types, not test layout.

Mypy's optional `unreachable` error code (--warn-unreachable) detects statements/expressions that can never execute, covering a subset of dead code; it does not detect unused functions, unused imports, or logically dead but reachable code.
Changelog maintenance is a process/documentation concern entirely outside mypy's static analysis scope.
Mypy has no coverage measurement capability; it does not track which lines/branches are exercised by tests.
Mypy is a type checker and has no rules for evaluating whether tests verify requirements vs implementation details; this requires semantic/design-level judgement.
Mypy has no rule for detecting duplicated literals or magic constants; it performs type inference and checking, not value-duplication analysis.
Mypy has no concept of data/code normalization or ambiguity reduction; no documented error code addresses this class of issue.
Mypy does not analyze algorithmic intent (heuristic vs deterministic); it checks types only, with no rule for this pattern.
Mypy has no rules for detecting brittle patterns (e.g. fragile string matching, tight coupling to implementation details); this is a semantic/design concern outside type checking.
Mypy has no rules for over-specificity or insufficient generalization; abstraction-level analysis is not within the scope of a type checker.
Mypy has no class-size or complexity metrics; Large Class detection requires structural analysis not present in any mypy error code.
Mypy does not flag Primitive Obsession (overuse of primitive types where value objects/types would be appropriate); while it enforces type annotations, it has no rule for this design smell.
Mypy is a type checker; it has no rule to detect groups of parameters/fields that repeatedly appear together (data clumps) — this requires semantic/structural analysis beyond type inference.
Mypy does not flag switch/if-elif chains as a polymorphism smell; it only checks that match/if expressions are type-correct, not that they should be replaced with dispatch.

Temporary Field (an attribute set only in certain states/paths) is a semantic object-design smell. Mypy checks attribute existence and type, not whether an attribute is conditionally unused, so it cannot detect this pattern.

Refused Bequest (subclass ignores inherited interface/data) is an OO-design smell. Mypy enforces LSP-compatible signatures via [override] but does not flag a subclass that simply never calls or uses inherited methods.

Alternative Classes with Different Interfaces is a cross-class design smell requiring knowledge that two classes serve the same role. Mypy has no rule for this; it only checks types within each class independently.
Divergent Change (one class changes for multiple unrelated reasons) is a cohesion/SRP smell requiring reasoning about change history and responsibility — entirely outside mypy's type-checking scope.
Shotgun Surgery (one change requires edits to many classes) is a coupling smell requiring cross-module change-impact analysis. Mypy has no such rule.

Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies (adding a subclass in one hierarchy forces adding one in another) is a structural design smell. Mypy checks type compatibility within hierarchies but cannot detect cross-hierarchy parallelism.

Mypy is a static type checker; it has no rules for detecting classes with too little responsibility (Lazy Class). All mypy error codes are type-system checks with no coverage of class-size or design-quality smells.
Mypy performs type checking only; it has no rule detecting classes that are mere data bags with no behaviour (Data Class smell). It does not analyse cohesion or encapsulation.
Speculative Generality requires reasoning about unused abstractions and premature extensibility — a semantic design judgement. Mypy's error codes are all type-system checks and cover no such pattern.
Over-reliance on comments is a documentation/clarity smell. Mypy never inspects comment content or density; it has no such error code.

Feature Envy (a method that mostly accesses another object's data) is a cross-class coupling smell. Mypy type-checks method signatures and call sites but has no rule about where data accesses are concentrated.
Inappropriate Intimacy (classes knowing too much about each other's internals) is a design-coupling smell beyond mypy's type-checking scope; mypy has no encapsulation enforcement rule.
Message Chains (a.b().c().d()) are a structural coupling smell. Mypy resolves attribute and return types along a chain but emits no warning about chain length or intermediate-coupling patterns.
Middle Man (a class that only delegates to another) is a design-quality smell. Mypy has no rule for detecting delegation-only classes; it checks types, not method purpose or delegation ratio.

God Object / Blob is a design smell about a class accumulating too many responsibilities. Mypy is a type checker and has no rule that counts methods, attributes, or lines-of-code per class to flag over-large classes.

Anemic Domain Model means domain classes contain only data (no behaviour). Mypy checks types, not whether methods are present or meaningful; it cannot distinguish a data-only class from one with rich behaviour.

Poltergeist (a class that briefly delegates to another and adds no value) is a structural/behavioural smell. Mypy has no rule that detects pass-through delegation classes or classes with minimal independent logic.

Yo-Yo Problem (deep or zigzagging inheritance hierarchies that are hard to follow) requires reasoning about hierarchy depth and readability. Mypy enforces type safety across hierarchies but has no rule limiting or flagging inheritance depth.

Sequential Coupling (methods must be called in a specific order) is a runtime-behavioural constraint. Mypy cannot model or enforce call-order invariants; it only checks static types of arguments and return values.

Constant Interface (using an interface/Protocol solely to share constants) is a design smell. Python doesn't have a formal interface keyword, and mypy has no rule that detects interfaces or classes whose only members are constants.

Interface Bloat (a Protocol or ABC with too many methods) requires judgement about cohesion and method count. Mypy enforces that implementors satisfy the interface but has no rule flagging interfaces as too large or incoherent.
Singleton Overuse is an architectural smell about over-reliance on global singletons. Mypy has no concept of singleton pattern detection or rules limiting global-state usage patterns.

Combinatorial Explosion is a design-level smell about exponential growth of state/case combinations; mypy has no rule targeting this pattern — it is purely a type checker with no complexity or design-smell analysis.

Tramp Data (passing data through intermediaries that don't use it) requires data-flow / coupling analysis across call chains; mypy performs type-checking only and has no rule for detecting unnecessary parameter threading.
Spaghetti Code is an architectural/structural smell (tangled control flow, high coupling); mypy has no cyclomatic complexity, coupling, or control-flow-structure rules — it is a type checker only.
Copy-Paste Programming (duplicated logic blocks) requires clone-detection analysis; mypy has no duplicate-code detection rule whatsoever.
Golden Hammer (over-applying a familiar solution/abstraction) is a design judgement smell; mypy type-checks signatures and types but has no rule for detecting inappropriate or over-used abstractions.

Lava Flow (dead/legacy code left in place) requires semantic dead-code detection beyond what mypy offers; mypy can flag some unreachable branches via type narrowing but does not detect abandoned dead APIs, obsolete modules, or legacy scaffolding as a class of smell.

Hard Coding (magic literals, hardcoded paths/credentials/config) is a value-content smell; mypy is a type checker and has no rule for detecting literal values used where constants or config should be.

Action at a Distance (non-local side effects through shared/global state) requires data-flow and side-effect analysis; mypy has no rule for detecting implicit global-state mutations or spooky coupling through shared mutable state.

Mypy is a type checker; it has no rule detecting boolean/enum parameters used as behavioral flags. Detecting flag arguments requires semantic analysis of control-flow branching on parameters, which mypy does not perform.

Mypy can flag unresolved imports (import-not-found) but cannot detect hidden runtime dependencies — e.g. implicit globals, singletons, or ambient state accessed without explicit injection. No mypy rule covers this design pattern.
Premature optimization is a design/intent judgement requiring knowledge of performance impact vs. readability trade-offs. Mypy performs no performance or complexity analysis whatsoever.
Detecting reimplementation of framework/language features requires understanding of what the framework provides and what the code duplicates — far beyond type checking. Mypy has no such rule.
Big Ball of Mud is an architectural anti-pattern (lack of structure, tangled dependencies). Mypy checks types, not module structure or coupling metrics. No applicable rule exists.
Stovepipe systems involve duplicated, siloed subsystems — an architectural concern. Mypy has no duplication-detection or cross-module coupling analysis. No applicable rule exists.
Using a database as an inter-process communication channel is an architectural misuse pattern. Mypy cannot distinguish intended from misused database access; no rule exists for this.

Vendor lock-in is a design concern (tight coupling to vendor-specific APIs vs. abstractions). Mypy checks type compatibility but has no concept of 'vendor API' vs. 'abstraction layer'. No applicable rule exists.
SRP is a semantic design principle — mypy has no rule that detects classes with too many responsibilities. Type checking cannot determine cohesion or responsibility boundaries.
OCP (open for extension, closed for modification) is an architectural intent that requires reasoning about change patterns over time. Mypy has no rule for this.

Mypy's [override] code checks that overriding methods have compatible signatures (contravariant params, covariant return types), catching type-level LSP violations in method signatures, but it cannot detect behavioral LSP violations (e.g. strengthened preconditions, weakened postconditions, side-effect changes).

ISP (interfaces should be small and client-specific) requires judging whether a Protocol/ABC exposes more methods than a client needs. Mypy checks structural conformance but has no rule that flags fat interfaces or unused-method dependencies.

Mypy's Protocol system enables programming to abstractions, and can flag when a concrete type is passed where an abstract Protocol is expected. However, mypy does not detect DIP violations where high-level modules directly depend on concrete classes instead of abstractions — it only enforces declared type annotations, not architectural dependency direction.

If code declares Protocol types in annotations, mypy enforces that only protocol-compatible values are passed — a partial mechanical proxy for 'program to an interface'. But mypy cannot detect when a developer uses a concrete class in an annotation where a protocol should be used instead.
Mypy has no rule that detects when inheritance is used where composition would be preferred. This is a design-level judgment beyond type checking.
Encapsulating what varies is a design pattern principle about isolating volatile behavior. Mypy performs type-level checks only and has no rule for detecting failure to encapsulate variation.
Mypy is a type checker with no rules for coupling or dependency injection patterns; loose coupling is a design-level concern beyond static type analysis.
Whether code passes tests is determined by running tests, not by type checking; mypy has no rule for test correctness or coverage.
Intention-revealing names are a semantic/stylistic concern; mypy only checks type correctness, not naming quality.
DRY/duplication is a structural design concern; mypy has no duplicate-code detection rules.
YAGNI (avoiding unnecessary elements) requires judgement about requirements vs. implementation; mypy has no such rule.
Clean Architecture's Dependency Rule (inner layers must not import outer layers) requires import-graph analysis with layer metadata; mypy does not model architectural layers.
Separating concerns into layers is an architectural design principle; mypy checks types, not layer membership or cohesion.
Inner-layer ignorance of outer layers is an architectural constraint; mypy has no concept of layer boundaries or directional dependency rules.
Boy Scout Rule is a code-quality discipline (leave code cleaner than you found it) with no mechanical pattern; mypy is a type checker and has no rule for this.

Whether ports are defined as interfaces (Abstract Base Classes / Protocols) is an architectural constraint; mypy enforces type compatibility but does not enforce that a given module boundary must be an abstract type.

Mypy can verify that a class satisfies a Protocol structurally, but it cannot enforce that adapters must implement ports — it only checks types declared in annotations, not architectural role assignments.
Whether the domain core is 'pure' (free of I/O or infrastructure imports) is an import-graph constraint, not a type error; mypy has no rule for this.
Driving-side (primary adapter) structure is an architectural designation; mypy has no concept of hexagonal sides or adapter roles.
Driven-side (secondary adapter) structure is an architectural designation; mypy has no concept of hexagonal sides or adapter roles.
Testability-by-design (injecting dependencies through ports so they can be swapped in tests) is a design property; mypy has no rule detecting its absence.
Dependency direction (inner layers must not import outer layers) is an import-graph / module-boundary rule; mypy performs type checking only and has no dependency-direction enforcement.
Mypy is a type checker with no rules for component cohesion principles like Reuse/Release Equivalence; architectural grouping decisions require human/LLM judgement.
Mypy has no rule for Common Closure Principle (classes that change together belong together); this is a design-level cohesion judgement beyond type checking.

source_label: mypy command-line docs (import cycle = impl detail, not a reported error)
rationale: Mypy is aware of import cycles internally for performance but does not report them as errors or provide any flag for acyclic dependency enforcement; dedicated tools (pydeps, importchecker) are needed.
Stable Dependencies Principle (depend in the direction of stability) is a coupling metric; mypy has no rule or metric for module instability/stability.
Stable Abstractions Principle (stable packages should be abstract) requires measuring abstractness vs. instability ratios; mypy performs no such architectural analysis.
Bounded Context is a DDD strategic design concept about semantic boundaries; mypy has no concept of domain contexts and cannot detect violations.
Ubiquitous Language (consistent domain vocabulary in code) is a semantic/naming concern; mypy checks types, not naming conventions or domain terminology alignment.
Domain Model integrity (entities, value objects, aggregates modelled correctly) is a design pattern concern; mypy enforces type correctness but has no DDD-aware rules.
Mypy has no rules for DDD Repository pattern compliance; it only checks types, not architectural layer responsibilities or domain pattern adherence.
Mypy has no rules for DDD Service Layer compliance; it cannot detect whether business logic is correctly separated into service layer objects.
Mypy has no rule for Encapsulate Variable/Record/Collection; it does not flag exposed mutable collections or bare fields that should be wrapped behind accessors.
Mypy has no 'Indecent Exposure' or visibility rule; Python lacks access-modifier enforcement at the language level, and mypy does not check for over-exposed internals.
Tell Don't Ask is a design principle about pulling data vs. issuing commands; mypy performs type checking and has no rule detecting this structural anti-pattern.
Replace Subclass with Delegate is a refactoring pattern; mypy's override and abstract codes relate to correctness of inheritance, not whether delegation should replace it.
Collapse Hierarchy is a refactoring smell about unnecessary inheritance depth; mypy does not flag shallow or degenerate class hierarchies.

Refused Bequest (subclass ignores inherited interface) is a semantic OOP smell; mypy's override error checks type compatibility of overrides, not whether inherited behavior is meaningfully used or intentionally suppressed.
Mypy is a type checker and has no rule for evaluating whether names are intention-revealing; naming quality is a semantic/human judgement concern.
Mypy has no rule enforcing that class names be nouns or method names be verbs; this is a naming convention concern outside the scope of type checking.
Mypy has no rule detecting inconsistent vocabulary (multiple words/synonyms for the same concept); this requires semantic understanding of naming intent.
Mypy has no rule for detecting disinformation in names (e.g. a name implying a type or behavior it does not have); this is beyond type checking scope.
Mypy has no rule for detecting mixed abstraction levels within a function; this is a structural design concern requiring semantic judgement.
Mypy has no rule for Command-Query Separation; it does not analyze whether a function both mutates state and returns a value as a design violation.
Mypy has no rule for detecting side effects; it does not track or enforce purity of functions.
Mypy has no rule for detecting flag arguments (boolean parameters that switch function behavior); this is a design smell beyond type checking scope.
mypy is a static type checker; it has no rules for detecting long parameter lists or the absence of a Parameter Object refactoring pattern.
mypy has no rules for detecting deeply-nested conditional logic or the absence of guard-clause patterns; that is a structural/complexity concern outside type checking.
mypy enforces type correctness of polymorphic dispatch but does not flag type-tag / isinstance chains that could be replaced with polymorphism; it cannot evaluate design intent.
mypy has no rules for identifying imperative loops that could be replaced with functional pipelines (map/filter/comprehensions); it only checks types.
mypy cannot detect whether a test is slow (e.g. performs I/O or sleeps); it performs no runtime or temporal analysis.
mypy cannot detect shared mutable state or ordering dependencies between tests; independence is a runtime/semantic property invisible to a type checker.
mypy cannot detect non-deterministic behaviour (random seeds, timestamps, network calls) that would make a test non-repeatable; it only checks type contracts.
mypy cannot detect whether a test has assertions or produces a clear pass/fail signal; it only verifies type correctness of the code.
clang-tidy has no rule that detects semantically redundant or obsolete comments; google-readability-todo only enforces TODO comment formatting, not comment relevance.
clang-tidy is a static linter and has no mechanism to measure or enforce unit test coverage of new or changed code; that requires a coverage instrumentation tool (e.g. gcov/llvm-cov).

readability-function-size and readability-function-cognitive-complexity flag overly large or complex functions as a size/complexity proxy for SRP violations, but neither detects true single-responsibility violations semantically.
clang-tidy operates at the AST/translation-unit level and has no rule that evaluates whether a file violates the single responsibility principle at the module or file scope.

clang-tidy has no rule that enforces test file structure conventions (e.g. naming, layout, describe/it nesting); the closest checks (google-upgrade-googletest-case) only address googletest API migration, not structural conventions.

clang-analyzer-deadcode.DeadStores catches dead store assignments, readability-redundant-control-flow catches some unreachable control-flow paths, and misc-unused-parameters catches unused parameters — but there is no general detector for unreachable blocks or unused functions/variables at link scope.
Changelog maintenance is a process/documentation concern; clang-tidy performs AST-based static analysis of source code and has no rule that checks whether a changelog file has been updated.
Test coverage measurement requires runtime instrumentation (gcov/llvm-cov); clang-tidy is a compile-time static linter with no rule for enforcing or reporting coverage thresholds.
Whether a test validates requirements versus implementation details is a semantic/intent judgment that requires understanding the design contract; no clang-tidy rule addresses this.

readability-magic-numbers flags unnamed numeric literals (a syntactic proxy for constants that should be named/de-duplicated), but it does not detect the same literal value or named constant duplicated across the codebase — cross-file duplication is outside its scope.
Normalizing data/code to remove ambiguity is a design-level refactoring judgment (e.g., collapsing equivalent representations); no clang-tidy rule targets this class of issue.
Identifying intentional heuristics and proposing deterministic replacements requires domain-level semantic understanding; clang-tidy has no rule for this.

bugprone-branch-clone catches structurally brittle copy-paste patterns (identical branches), and misc-redundant-expression catches redundant logic — but 'brittle patterns' as a broad class (e.g., fragile coupling, magic-value dependency) is a design judgment not fully covered.
Judging whether code is over-specific or should be generalized is a design-level decision requiring contextual reasoning; no clang-tidy rule addresses this.
C has no classes; the Large Class code smell (a class with too many responsibilities/fields) does not exist as a construct in C, making the check inapplicable.
Primitive Obsession is an OOP smell about over-using primitives instead of small domain value-objects/classes; C has no class mechanism for such wrappers, so the construct does not apply.

Data Clumps require detecting correlated parameter/field groups that co-occur across multiple sites — a cross-file semantic pattern. No clang-tidy rule addresses this; it requires structural/semantic analysis beyond static pattern matching.

source_label: bugprone-switch-missing-default-case (flags missing default, not type-dispatch smell)
rationale: The Switch Statements smell (Fowler) is about using switch/type-dispatch as a polymorphism substitute. clang-tidy's bugprone-switch-missing-default-case and hicpp-multiway-paths-covered flag incomplete coverage, not the design smell of dispatching on type. No rule targets this pattern.

Temporary Field (a field meaningful only in certain code paths) requires semantic data-flow analysis across method calls to detect when a field is conditionally set. No clang-tidy rule targets this smell.
Refused Bequest is an inheritance smell (subclass ignores inherited interface/data). C has no classes or inheritance, so the construct does not exist in the language.
