Alternative Classes with Different Interfaces requires comparing class interfaces across the codebase. C has no classes, so this object-oriented smell does not apply.

Divergent Change is a class-level SRP violation (one class changed for many reasons). C has no classes; the concept of a single-responsibility module boundary is architectural and has no clang-tidy detector even if mapped to translation units.

Shotgun Surgery requires detecting that a single logical change forces edits across many classes. C has no classes, and cross-file change-coupling analysis is not within clang-tidy's scope regardless.
Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies requires detecting mirrored class hierarchies. C has no inheritance or class hierarchies, so the construct does not exist in the language.
Lazy Class requires judging whether a struct/module justifies its existence — a semantic design judgment that no clang-tidy rule addresses.
Data Class describes an OO class with only getters/setters and no behavior; C has no classes or methods, only structs and free functions, so the construct does not exist.
Speculative Generality is about intent (unused abstractions added for hypothetical futures); no static tool can detect developer intent, and clang-tidy has no such rule.
Over-reliance on comments to compensate for unclear code requires qualitative judgment; clang-tidy has no rule evaluating comment necessity or quality.
Feature Envy is an OO smell where a method uses another class's features more than its own; C has no methods or classes, so the construct does not exist.
Inappropriate Intimacy describes two classes overly coupled to each other's internals; C has no classes, so this OO construct does not exist.
Message Chains (a.getB().getC()) are an OO method-chaining pattern; C has no object methods or chained member calls, so the construct does not exist.
Middle Man is an OO smell where a class delegates most work to another; C has no classes or delegation pattern, so the construct does not exist.

C has structs but no classes; a 'God Object' (one struct/module doing everything) is an architectural smell requiring cross-file semantic judgement. clang-tidy has no check for class/module size or responsibility count. readability-function-size only measures individual functions.
Anemic Domain Model is an OOP pattern where domain objects lack behaviour (only data + getters/setters). C has no classes or methods — the construct does not exist in C.
Poltergeist (a short-lived object that just delegates to another) requires dataflow/lifetime analysis across call sites to detect. clang-tidy has no architectural delegation-smell check.
Yo-Yo Problem arises from deep inheritance hierarchies causing constant class-hierarchy navigation. C has no inheritance, so the construct does not exist in C.

Sequential Coupling (API must be called in a specific order) requires understanding of API preconditions and call ordering across code paths. clang-tidy has no check for enforced initialization/call-sequence contracts.
Constant Interface (an interface used purely to expose constants) is a Java/C++ interface idiom. C has no interfaces; the construct does not exist in C.

Interface Bloat (too many methods on one interface) requires counting responsibilities across an interface/struct and judging cohesion. C has no interfaces; while large structs-of-function-pointers exist, clang-tidy has no check for their size or cohesion.

Singleton Overuse is a design judgement (too many singletons, not that a singleton exists). clang-tidy has no check detecting singleton pattern instances (global state via static local or file-static variable) let alone overuse of the pattern.

Combinatorial explosion is a design-level anti-pattern (too many interacting state combinations from cross-product of inputs/branches); clang-tidy has no rule identifying this structural problem — complexity metrics like readability-function-cognitive-complexity flag high per-function complexity but cannot detect that the root cause is combinatorial interaction of parameters across the design.

Tramp data (data passed through intermediaries that don't use it) requires whole-program data-flow analysis across call chains; clang-tidy has no rule tracking whether a parameter is forwarded unused through multiple layers — misc-unused-parameters only flags parameters never referenced within a single function, not the tramp-data pattern.

No single spaghetti-code detector exists, but readability-function-cognitive-complexity flags functions exceeding a cognitive-complexity threshold and readability-function-size flags oversized/deeply-nested functions — these catch structural symptoms but miss poor separation of concerns, entangled globals, or design-level coupling.

bugprone-branch-clone detects identical consecutive branches in if/else chains and switch statements — a syntactic proxy for copy-paste programming — but misses duplicated code blocks that are not adjacent conditional branches (e.g. copy-pasted loops or helper functions), so coverage is narrow.

Golden Hammer (over-applying a familiar solution to all problems) is a design/architectural anti-pattern requiring contextual judgment about whether the chosen approach is appropriate; no clang-tidy rule addresses this.

Lava Flow is partly detectable: misc-unused-parameters, the Clang Static Analyzer's deadcode.DeadStores, and misc-unused-using-decls flag some dead-code symptoms, but the broader anti-pattern — retained legacy code nobody dares delete — requires historical/contextual knowledge no static rule captures.

readability-magic-numbers (aliased as cppcoreguidelines-avoid-magic-numbers) detects unexplained numeric literals — the most common hard-coding pattern — but does not cover hard-coded string literals, URLs, or file paths, and its default exemption list (0,1,2,3,4,-1) lets many literals pass unchecked.

Action at a Distance is partly addressed by cppcoreguidelines-avoid-non-const-global-variables, which flags mutable global state — the primary mechanism enabling this anti-pattern in C — but does not detect non-global shared mutable state (e.g. static locals) or the broader semantic issue of unexpected side-effects at a distance.

No clang-tidy rule flags the Flag Argument anti-pattern (boolean parameters that control function behavior). bugprone-easily-swappable-parameters explicitly excludes bool-typed parameters by default and targets accidental swapping, not the design smell of passing control-flow flags.

No clang-tidy rule detects hidden dependencies — implicit coupling through global state, singletons, or ambient context accessed inside a function without being expressed in its signature. This requires semantic/architectural judgment beyond static pattern matching.

clang-tidy's performance-* checks flag actual inefficiencies (e.g., unnecessary copies), not the meta-level anti-pattern of optimizing prematurely before profiling. Detecting premature optimization requires contextual and design-level judgment.

No clang-tidy rule detects the Inner-Platform Effect (reimplementing language/OS/library abstractions that already exist). Identifying this anti-pattern requires understanding intent and available alternatives, which is beyond static syntactic analysis.

Big Ball of Mud is a system-wide architectural anti-pattern (lack of structure, pervasive coupling). While readability-function-size or readability-function-cognitive-complexity can surface complexity symptoms, no clang-tidy rule addresses the pattern holistically; it requires architectural judgment.

Stovepipe System is a cross-component architectural anti-pattern involving isolated, non-interoperable subsystems. This is entirely outside the scope of a file- or translation-unit-level static analyzer like clang-tidy.

No clang-tidy rule detects the use of a database as an IPC mechanism. Recognizing this anti-pattern requires understanding system architecture and communication intent, which cannot be inferred from C source code alone.

No clang-tidy rule detects vendor lock-in at a design level. portability-* checks flag narrow syntactic portability issues (e.g., pragma-once, SIMD intrinsics) but do not address strategic dependency on proprietary APIs or platforms as an architectural anti-pattern.
C has no classes or modules with encapsulated responsibilities; Single Responsibility is an OOP design concept that does not apply.
C has no classes or inheritance; Open/Closed Principle requires OOP constructs absent from C.
C has no classes or inheritance hierarchy; Liskov Substitution Principle is an OOP concept inapplicable to C.
C has no interfaces or abstract types; Interface Segregation Principle does not apply to C.
C has no dependency injection or interface abstraction in the OOP sense; Dependency Inversion Principle does not apply.
C has no interfaces or abstract types to program against; the GoF 'program to an interface' principle does not apply.
C has no inheritance mechanism; 'composition over inheritance' is an OOP trade-off that does not exist in C.
C has no classes with encapsulated variability in the OOP sense; 'encapsulate what varies' as a design principle does not apply.

Loose coupling is a high-level architectural property (e.g. low fan-in/fan-out, dependency inversion) that requires semantic understanding of design intent; clang-tidy has no rule that evaluates coupling between modules or components.
'Passes the tests' is a runtime correctness criterion evaluated by executing a test suite, not a property a static analyzer can determine; clang-tidy has no such check.

Intention-revealing naming is a holistic readability judgment; while clang-tidy's readability-identifier-naming can enforce naming conventions/patterns, it cannot assess whether a name actually reveals intent, which requires semantic understanding.
DRY / code duplication detection requires semantic equivalence analysis across code blocks; clang-tidy has no clone-detection or duplication rule in its standard check set.
YAGNI (avoiding speculative generality) requires understanding design intent and future requirements; no clang-tidy rule addresses unnecessary abstractions or over-engineering.

Clean Architecture's Dependency Rule (outer layers may not be imported by inner layers) is an architectural constraint that requires knowledge of layer assignments; clang-tidy has no layer-aware dependency enforcement rule.
Separating concerns into architectural layers is a structural design principle requiring understanding of module responsibilities; clang-tidy performs no layer-level concern analysis.

'Inner knows nothing of outer' is the directional isolation aspect of Clean Architecture's Dependency Rule; same reasoning as 17.2.1 — no clang-tidy check models layer membership or enforces inward-only knowledge.

Boy Scout Rule (leave code cleaner than you found it) is a team discipline/process concern, not a syntactic pattern; clang-tidy has no rule for detecting whether a change improved surrounding code quality.
Hexagonal Architecture ports-are-interfaces is a design-level structural constraint; clang-tidy has no rule that enforces or detects port/interface boundary definitions in the hexagonal sense.
Whether an adapter correctly implements a port is an architectural role assignment; clang-tidy has no rule for detecting adapter/port conformance in hexagonal architecture.
Hexagonal core purity (no I/O or infrastructure calls) requires semantic knowledge of which calls are infrastructure; clang-tidy has no rule modelling this boundary.
Driving-side correctness (callers go through ports) is an architectural role concern; clang-tidy has no rule that identifies driving-side actors or enforces they interact only via ports.
Driven-side correctness (secondary adapters called through ports, not directly) is an architectural role concern; clang-tidy has no rule for this.
Testability-by-design (core injectable/swappable via ports) is a design intent that cannot be inferred from syntax alone; clang-tidy has no rule for this.

Dependency-points-inward (dependency rule of hexagonal/clean arch) requires whole-module dependency-graph analysis with layer labelling; clang-tidy has no rule for enforcing inward-only dependency direction.
Reuse/Release Equivalence Principle is an OO component-packaging principle; C has no module/package system and clang-tidy has no architectural packaging analysis.

Common Closure Principle is an OO component-cohesion principle (classes that change together belong together); C has no class/component abstraction and clang-tidy performs no architectural cohesion analysis.

source_label: clang-tidy checks list (no ADP check present)
rationale: Acyclic Dependencies Principle applies to C (header-include cycles exist), but clang-tidy has no check for inter-component dependency cycles; misc-header-include-cycle only detects circular #include chains, not component-level DAG violations.
Stable Dependencies Principle requires measuring component instability metrics across a codebase; C has no package/component system and clang-tidy has no dependency-stability analysis.
Stable Abstractions Principle relates abstract classes to component stability; C has no abstract classes and clang-tidy has no architectural abstraction-ratio analysis.
Bounded Context is a DDD strategic pattern about domain boundary design; it is a socio-technical concept with no language construct in C and no representation in clang-tidy.

Ubiquitous Language is a DDD practice about consistent naming aligned with domain vocabulary; assessing UL alignment requires domain knowledge — clang-tidy has no such semantic naming check at the domain model level.

Domain Model integrity is a DDD concept requiring understanding of business invariants and aggregate boundaries; C has no OO constructs to express a domain model and clang-tidy has no domain-model analysis.
DDD Repository is an architectural pattern (data-access abstraction behind a collection-like interface); clang-tidy has no rule that understands domain layer boundaries or repository semantics.
DDD Service Layer is an architectural concept requiring understanding of domain logic placement; no clang-tidy rule addresses service-layer design or domain responsibility assignment.

misc-non-private-member-variables-in-classes flags non-private data members (a syntactic proxy for the 'encapsulate field' case), but cannot detect the broader pattern of encapsulating collections or records behind proper accessors/mutators.

misc-non-private-member-variables-in-classes catches non-private data members, covering one dimension of indecent exposure, but does not detect unnecessarily public methods, over-broad APIs, or exposed internal types.
Tell Don't Ask is a behavioral design principle (avoid querying object state then acting on it externally); no clang-tidy rule models inter-method control-flow semantics at this level.
Replace Subclass with Delegate is a structural refactoring pattern (swap inheritance for composition); clang-tidy has no rule that identifies subclasses suitable for delegation replacement.
Collapse Hierarchy (merging a superclass and subclass when the distinction is no longer justified) requires understanding class purpose and hierarchy intent; no clang-tidy rule addresses this.

Refused Bequest (subclass ignores or misuses inherited members) requires semantic understanding of which inherited interface members are used/overridden/suppressed; no clang-tidy rule detects this pattern.
readability-identifier-naming enforces only structural style (case, prefix/suffix); it cannot assess whether a name reveals intent — that requires semantic/human judgement.

source_label: clang-tidy check list (no class/method checks for C)
rationale: C has no classes or methods; the noun/verb naming convention for OOP constructs is inapplicable to the language.
No clang-tidy rule detects inconsistent concept vocabulary across identifiers; readability-identifier-naming only checks structural casing/prefix patterns, not semantic consistency of terminology.

misc-confusable-identifiers detects look-alike Unicode characters, not semantically misleading names (e.g. a list named accountList that is actually a set); no clang-tidy rule covers naming disinformation.

readability-function-cognitive-complexity and readability-function-size measure raw size/complexity metrics, not abstraction level mixing within a function; detecting mixed abstraction levels requires semantic judgement.

No clang-tidy rule enforces Command-Query Separation; detecting whether a function both modifies state and returns a value in a semantically problematic way requires design-level reasoning beyond static AST checks.
bugprone-assert-side-effect only flags side effects inside assert() conditions; no clang-tidy rule enforces a general no-side-effects constraint on arbitrary functions.
No clang-tidy rule detects boolean flag arguments used to select behaviour within a function; identifying this pattern requires understanding caller intent and function branching semantics.
Clang-tidy has no rule that detects long parameter lists and suggests introducing a Parameter Object; this is a design-level refactoring beyond static pattern matching.

readability-function-cognitive-complexity flags overly nested functions but does not prescribe guard clauses as a fix; no clang-tidy rule specifically detects the 'nested conditionals → guard clause' refactoring opportunity.

C has no polymorphism (virtual dispatch), but the underlying pattern of type-tag switch/if-chains that could be replaced with dispatch tables or function pointers is not flagged by any clang-tidy rule. No rule targets this refactoring.
modernize-loop-convert converts index-based for-loops to range-based for-loops (C++11), not to functional pipeline idioms; no clang-tidy rule suggests replacing loops with pipeline constructs in C.
FIRST 'Fast' is a test-suite quality property (execution time); clang-tidy performs static analysis of source code and has no rules that evaluate test speed or identify slow test patterns.
FIRST 'Independent' (tests don't depend on each other or shared mutable state) requires semantic understanding of test execution order and inter-test state; clang-tidy has no such rule.

FIRST 'Repeatable' (tests produce the same result in any environment) involves detecting environment-dependent side effects (time, network, random); no clang-tidy rule targets this test-quality property.

FIRST 'Self-validating' (tests emit a clear pass/fail without manual inspection) is a design principle about assertion usage; clang-tidy has no rule auditing whether test functions contain assertions.
Timely (write tests at the right time) is a TDD process discipline; clang-tidy has no rules for test authoring timing or workflow.
Red-Green-Refactor is a TDD development cycle; no static-analysis tool can observe or enforce a developer's iteration order.
Fake It Till You Make It is a TDD strategy for incrementally generalising implementations; clang-tidy has no rule for this pattern.
Triangulation (driving generalisation from multiple test cases) is a TDD reasoning technique undetectable by static analysis.
Assert First (write the assertion before the setup) is a TDD test-authoring practice; clang-tidy has no rule for assertion ordering intent.
Identifying legacy code by absence of tests requires test-coverage data; clang-tidy performs AST-level static analysis and has no coverage-gap detection.
Characterization Tests (golden-master tests documenting existing behaviour) is a testing design concept; clang-tidy has no rule for test intent or classification.
Sprout Method/Class (isolating new code in a new method/class to enable testing) is a refactoring strategy; clang-tidy has no rule to detect or enforce this pattern.
Wrap Method/Class is an OOP refactoring pattern requiring classes and methods — constructs that do not exist in C. clang-tidy has no such check and the concept is inapplicable to C.
Extract Interface is an OOP refactoring pattern requiring interfaces/abstract classes — constructs that do not exist in C. The concept is inapplicable to C.
Parameterize Constructor/Method is a refactoring pattern targeting constructors and methods — neither of which exists in C. The concept is inapplicable to C.
Gradual typing is a type-system concept for dynamic/hybrid languages (e.g., TypeScript, Python). C is statically and fully typed at compile time; gradual typing has no meaning in C.

bugprone-narrowing-conversions detects implicit narrowing conversions (integer-to-narrower-integer, float-to-int, etc.), but the check is documented for C++ and its alias cppcoreguidelines-narrowing-conversions is explicitly C++ Core Guidelines scoped. Coverage in plain C translation units is incidental and not the documented target; semantic type-narrowing issues in generic code are not fully covered.
Prototype pollution is a JavaScript-specific vulnerability exploiting JS prototype chains. C has no prototype-based object model; the construct does not exist in C.

Global scope pollution (JS) refers to JavaScript-specific mechanisms such as implicit globals via undeclared var and window object leakage. C has no equivalent runtime global-object model; the JS-specific construct does not exist in C.

Passing request context explicitly vs. implicitly through models is an architectural design pattern requiring semantic understanding of data-flow boundaries. No clang-tidy rule addresses this class of issue; it requires human or LLM judgement.

bugprone-command-processor flags system(), popen(), and _popen() — the primary C mechanisms for delegating to a command processor — but does not cover dlopen-based dynamic dispatch or function-pointer indirection that could also constitute dynamic execution.
Monkey patching (runtime replacement of methods/classes) relies on dynamic object systems; C has no such runtime class or method infrastructure, so the construct does not exist in C.
Duck typing is a runtime polymorphism discipline rooted in dynamically-typed languages; C is statically typed at compile time and has no duck-typing mechanism to enforce or violate.
Lint-as-build-failure is a CI/build pipeline policy, not a detectable code pattern; clang-tidy has no rule that checks whether lint is enforced in the project's build system.
Test coverage enforcement is a CI/toolchain policy concern; clang-tidy analyses source code patterns and has no rule that detects absence of tests or enforces a coverage threshold.
Pinning and validating external API contracts (dependency versioning, schema checks) is an architectural/package-management concern with no corresponding clang-tidy rule.
Structured logging (use of key-value log formats vs. free-form printf) is a semantic logging practice; clang-tidy has no rule that enforces or detects structured vs. unstructured log calls.
Correlation ID propagation through logging is an architectural observability pattern; clang-tidy has no rule that checks for the presence or absence of correlation IDs in log call sites.
Health checks and readiness probes are infrastructure/runtime concerns; clang-tidy analyzes C source ASTs and has no concept of service lifecycle or health endpoints.
Alerting on symptoms vs. causes is an operational observability design principle; clang-tidy has no rules for alerting strategy or monitoring semantics.
Postel's Law is an API design philosophy requiring semantic understanding of input validation breadth; no clang-tidy rule addresses this.
Semantic versioning is a release/packaging discipline; clang-tidy operates on source code ASTs and has no awareness of version numbering conventions or release metadata.
Backwards compatibility requires cross-version semantic reasoning; clang-tidy has no rule that compares current code against prior interface contracts.

Explicit error contracts require semantic understanding of intent; clang-tidy has no rule enforcing documented error contracts, only narrowly-scoped return-value checks unrelated to contract specification.
Pinning transitive dependencies is a build/package management concern; clang-tidy analyzes C source and has no access to dependency resolution graphs.
Minimizing dependency footprint is an architectural decision; clang-tidy has no rule evaluating whether a dependency is warranted vs. excess.
Dependency auditing (checking for outdated/vulnerable packages) is a process/toolchain concern that clang-tidy has no rules for; it operates on source AST, not dependency manifests.
Single-responsibility per package is an architectural/cohesion judgment that cannot be mechanically detected by clang-tidy; misc-include-cleaner only catches unused includes, not SRP violations.
misc-const-correctness explicitly does not analyze C code (only C++), so this check is unavailable for the c language target; the construct exists in C but the rule is excluded.
Share-nothing architecture is an infrastructure/deployment design pattern; clang-tidy has no rule that detects or enforces process isolation or shared-state boundaries at the architectural level.
Idempotency is a semantic property (same result on repeated execution) requiring knowledge of external state and side-effects; no clang-tidy rule addresses this.
Making state machines explicit is a design pattern choice; clang-tidy has no rule that detects implicit state machine implementations or enforces explicit state machine patterns.

cppcoreguidelines-avoid-non-const-global-variables and cppcoreguidelines-owning-memory address related concerns but are C++-only checks; no equivalent clang-tidy rule applies to C code for detecting improper assumptions about shared/global resource ownership.
Distinguishing comments that explain 'why' (decisions) from those explaining 'what' (mechanics) requires qualitative semantic judgment; clang-tidy has no rule for comment quality or content.
clang-tidy has no rule that enforces or checks proximity of documentation/comments to the code they describe; this is a style/organisation concern beyond static pattern matching.
Executable documentation (doctests, literate tests, etc.) is a toolchain/documentation-framework concern; clang-tidy has no rule addressing it.

No clang-tidy rule enforces the principle of least privilege in general; the narrow android-cloexec-* family catches FD privilege leakage across exec but does not address privilege minimisation broadly.
Defence in depth is an architectural design principle; clang-tidy has no rule that can mechanically verify layered defences.
Fail-secure semantics (defaulting to deny on error) require semantic reasoning about control flow and policy intent; clang-tidy has no rule that detects insecure failure modes.
Cppcheck has no rule for evaluating comment quality, redundancy, or whether comments add value — this requires natural-language understanding beyond static analysis.
Cppcheck performs no test coverage analysis; it cannot determine whether new or changed code is covered by unit tests.
Cppcheck has no rule for Single Responsibility Principle at the function level; SRP requires architectural/semantic judgment about cohesion that static pattern matching cannot provide.
Cppcheck has no rule for SRP at the file level; assessing whether a file has a single responsibility requires design-level judgment far beyond cppcheck's scope.
Cppcheck has no rules about test file structure, naming conventions, or test organisation patterns.

Cppcheck detects several dead-code forms: unreachable statements (checkUnreachableCode), unused functions (unusedFunction, requires --enable=unusedFunction and whole-program scan), unused/unread variables (unusedVariable, unreadVariable), and redundant assignments (redundantAssignment). However it misses dead code gated by runtime-determined conditions, dead code inside macros, and unused static functions in multi-TU builds without whole-program mode — so coverage is partial.
Cppcheck analyses C source code only and has no awareness of changelog files or project documentation; changelog compliance is entirely outside its scope.
Cppcheck does not measure or report test coverage (line, branch, or otherwise); it performs no coverage instrumentation or analysis.
Cppcheck has no rule that distinguishes tests validating requirements vs. implementation details; this is a semantic/design-level judgement requiring human or LLM review.

Cppcheck's documented rule set covers bugs and undefined behaviour; it has no rule for detecting duplicated literals or magic-number constants. clang-tidy's readability-magic-numbers covers this for C/C++ but that is a different tool.
Normalising code to remove ambiguity is a design/refactoring concern; cppcheck has no such rule category.
Identifying heuristic logic that should be replaced with deterministic logic requires semantic understanding of intent; cppcheck has no rule for this class of issue.
Brittle patterns are design-level concerns. Cppcheck focuses on undefined behaviour and concrete bug patterns, not brittleness as an abstract quality.
Over-specificity vs. proper generalisation is a design/architecture concern requiring contextual judgement; cppcheck has no rule covering this.
Cppcheck has no code-metric or size-threshold rule for detecting large classes; its documented class checks cover constructor/destructor correctness, not structural bloat.
Primitive Obsession (overuse of primitives where value-objects are more appropriate) is a design smell requiring semantic judgement; cppcheck has no rule for it.

Data Clumps is an architectural smell requiring cross-location semantic analysis (identifying repeated groups of parameters/fields). Cppcheck has no rule for this pattern; its checks are bug-oriented (null dereference, buffer overflow, etc.) and do not reason about parameter grouping conventions.

Cppcheck has no dedicated missing-default or general fallthrough rule, but it does flag switch-adjacent issues that serve as proxies: 'redundantAssignmentInSwitch' (variable reassigned before use, implying missing break) and 'checkSuspiciousCaseInSwitch' (logical operators in case labels). These catch narrow sub-cases only; the general Switch Statements smell (replacing type-code dispatch) is not detected.
Temporary Field is an OO smell: a class field that is only valid during certain algorithm invocations. C has no classes or objects, so this construct does not exist in the language.
Refused Bequest requires class inheritance (a subclass ignoring parent members). C has no inheritance or class hierarchy, so the construct is absent from the language.
Alternative Classes with Different Interfaces is an OO smell about classes performing similar roles under incompatible method signatures. C has no classes or interfaces.

Divergent Change (a module that must be modified for many unrelated reasons) is a high-level architectural smell requiring understanding of change history and responsibility boundaries. Cppcheck performs purely syntactic/semantic bug detection and has no metric or rule for this.

Shotgun Surgery (a single logical change forces edits across many files/modules) requires cross-file coupling analysis and change-impact reasoning. Cppcheck has no such rule; it does not model module cohesion or coupling at the architectural level.
Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies requires two mirrored class hierarchies that must grow in lockstep. C has no class inheritance, so the construct does not exist in the language.

Lazy Class (a class that does too little to justify its existence) requires semantic judgment about design intent and class responsibility scope. Cppcheck has no rule for this; it only flags unused code at the symbol level (unused variables, unreachable code), not under-responsibility at the class design level.

Data Class (a struct/class with only public fields and no meaningful behavior) is a design smell requiring architectural judgment. In C, plain structs with no associated functions are the norm, not a smell, and cppcheck has no rule detecting this pattern as problematic.

Speculative Generality (unused abstractions added for hypothetical future use) requires intent-level analysis. Cppcheck can flag unused functions/variables as style warnings, but cannot distinguish legitimate abstraction from speculative generality — it has no rule for this design smell.

Over-reliance on comments as a code smell (comments compensating for unclear code) is a subjective, semantic judgment. Cppcheck performs no analysis of comment quality, density, or necessity — it has no comment-related checks whatsoever.

Feature Envy (a function/method that uses data from another module more than its own) requires cross-module coupling analysis and design intent. Cppcheck has no rule for this; it analyzes individual constructs for correctness, not inter-module access patterns.

Inappropriate Intimacy (excessive coupling between modules, accessing internals that should be private) in C manifests as direct struct-member access across translation units. Cppcheck has no rule measuring or flagging excessive inter-module coupling of this kind.

Message Chains (a->b()->c()->d() call chains) is an OO design smell. In C, chained function calls or pointer dereferences exist syntactically but cppcheck has no rule flagging deep call/dereference chains as a design problem.

Middle Man (a class/module that does nothing but delegate to another) requires understanding delegation intent across the design. Cppcheck has no rule for this; pass-through wrapper functions are syntactically valid and cppcheck does not analyze delegation depth or purpose.
God Object is an OOP anti-pattern requiring classes/objects; C has no class construct, so the pattern cannot arise in idiomatic C.
Anemic Domain Model presupposes domain objects (classes with methods); C has no class/method construct, making this anti-pattern inapplicable.
Poltergeist is an OOP anti-pattern about short-lived objects that exist only to invoke other objects; C has no class instantiation or object lifecycle in this sense.
Yo-Yo Problem describes deep inheritance hierarchies causing navigation difficulty; C has no inheritance, so the construct does not exist.

Sequential Coupling (mandatory call ordering between functions) can occur in C, but cppcheck has no rule to detect required initialization sequences or enforced call order; this requires interprocedural semantic analysis beyond cppcheck's documented checks.
Constant Interface is an OOP anti-pattern where a class implements an interface solely to inherit its constants; C has no interfaces or class inheritance.
Interface Bloat refers to overly large interfaces (too many methods on a type); C has no interface or class construct, so this anti-pattern does not apply.

Singleton Overuse can manifest in C as global variables/static state, but cppcheck has no rule targeting singleton overuse or global-state design smells; it is an architectural judgement call beyond cppcheck's documented checks.

Combinatorial Explosion is an architectural design anti-pattern (excessive parameter combinations / state space) that requires semantic understanding of system design; cppcheck has no rule for this class of issue and focuses on low-level memory/UB checks.

Tramp Data (data passed through many intermediate layers without being used there) is a design-level coupling smell that requires inter-procedural semantic analysis of data flow intent; cppcheck has no rule for this pattern.

Spaghetti Code (unstructured control flow, deeply nested logic, tangled responsibilities) is a holistic design quality judgment; cppcheck does not measure cyclomatic complexity or structural coherence and has no rule targeting this anti-pattern.

Cppcheck detects duplicate expressions and identical code in both branches of if/else or ternary operators (duplicateExpression, duplicateBranch), which catches a narrow syntactic subset of copy-paste programming, but it has no clone-detection across arbitrary code blocks or functions.
Golden Hammer (over-applying a familiar solution/pattern regardless of fit) is a design-level judgment about technology or paradigm choices; it is entirely outside the scope of any cppcheck rule.

Lava Flow (dead/legacy code that cannot be removed) overlaps with cppcheck's unusedFunction (--enable=unusedFunction) and unusedVariable/unreadVariable checks, which flag some unreachable or never-used code; however these rules only catch locally verifiable dead code, not the broader socio-technical 'nobody knows if it's safe to delete' pattern.

Hard Coding (embedding magic literals, config values, paths in source) requires detecting magic numbers / non-symbolic constants; cppcheck explicitly lacks a magic-number check and does not flag hardcoded literals as an issue class.

Action at a Distance (a change in one part of the system causes unexpected effects elsewhere via shared mutable state or hidden coupling) requires whole-program semantic reasoning about implicit dependencies; cppcheck has no rule targeting this design-level pattern.
Flag arguments are a design smell (boolean params that switch behavior); cppcheck has no rule detecting this pattern — it focuses on low-level correctness, not API design.
Hidden dependency (implicit coupling between modules) requires semantic/architectural understanding; cppcheck has no rule for this class of issue.
Premature optimization is a design judgment call; cppcheck offers no rule that identifies over-engineered code for speculative performance gains.
Inner-platform effect (reimplementing a platform/language inside the app) is an architectural anti-pattern requiring contextual judgement; cppcheck has no detector for it.
Big Ball of Mud is a macro-architectural pattern (lack of structure across an entire codebase); cppcheck operates at the statement/expression level and has no such check.
Stovepipe System (siloed subsystems with no shared infrastructure) is a system-design anti-pattern; cppcheck has no rule for inter-module coupling at this level.
Database as IPC (using a DB as a message bus between processes) is an architectural misuse pattern; cppcheck has no semantic awareness of inter-process communication intent.
Vendor lock-in is a strategic/architectural concern; cppcheck has no rule for detecting proprietary API coupling or portability at that level of abstraction.
Single Responsibility Principle is an OO class-design concept. C has no classes; cppcheck's class checks apply only to C++, and no cppcheck rule addresses SRP in any language.
Open/Closed Principle concerns class extension without modification. C has no classes or inheritance; the construct the principle governs does not exist in C, and cppcheck has no OCP rule regardless.
Liskov Substitution Principle requires subtype polymorphism via inheritance/virtual dispatch. C has neither; the construct does not exist in C, and cppcheck has no LSP rule.
Interface Segregation Principle presupposes interfaces as a language construct. C has no interfaces; the principle's underlying construct does not exist in C, and cppcheck has no ISP rule.
Dependency Inversion Principle (depend on abstractions/interfaces) requires interface constructs absent in C. C has no interfaces; cppcheck has no DIP rule in any language.

'Program to an interface' is a GoF OO principle requiring interface/abstract-type constructs. C has no such constructs; the principle's applicability to C is architectural at best, and cppcheck has no such rule.

'Composition over inheritance' presupposes inheritance as an alternative. C has no inheritance; the tradeoff the principle addresses does not exist as a language construct in C, and cppcheck has no such rule.

'Encapsulate what varies' is a design-level OO heuristic (hide varying behaviour behind abstraction). C lacks the class/interface machinery this principle targets; cppcheck has no rule for it in any language.
Cppcheck has no rules for detecting loose/tight coupling between modules or adherence to GoF design principles; it focuses on memory safety and UB, not structural design quality.
Whether code passes its tests is a runtime/test-execution concern entirely outside cppcheck's static analysis scope.
Intention-revealing naming and expressiveness require semantic/contextual judgment; cppcheck has no rules for naming quality or code clarity.
DRY / code duplication detection is not part of cppcheck's rule set; dedicated clone-detection tools (CPD, SonarQube) are needed.
YAGNI (removing unnecessary elements) requires architectural/contextual judgment about intent; cppcheck has no rule for speculative or superfluous design.
Clean Architecture's Dependency Rule (inner layers must not depend on outer) requires module/layer topology analysis; cppcheck has no such architectural constraint checking.
Separation of concerns into layers is an architectural design principle; cppcheck performs per-file/per-function bug checks and has no concept of architectural layers.
Whether inner layers know nothing of outer layers is a dependency-direction constraint; cppcheck has no rules for enforcing layered architecture boundaries.
The Boy Scout Rule is a human/team practice with no mechanical definition; cppcheck has no rule for it.
Hexagonal Architecture port definitions are architectural design decisions; cppcheck performs no architectural analysis.
Whether an adapter correctly implements a port is a semantic/architectural judgment; cppcheck has no rule for this.
Core domain purity (no I/O or infrastructure calls leaking in) requires understanding layer boundaries; cppcheck has no architectural layer model.
Driving-side (primary adapter / use-case initiator) correctness is an architectural concern; cppcheck has no rule for hexagonal driving-side patterns.
Driven-side (secondary adapter / infrastructure) correctness requires architectural understanding; cppcheck has no rule for this.
Testability by design is a structural/architectural property; cppcheck does not analyze whether code is structured for testability.
Dependency inversion / inward-pointing dependencies require module-level dependency graph analysis; cppcheck has no architectural dependency-direction rule.

Reuse/Release Equivalence is a package-cohesion principle (group reusable components together and release them as a unit). Cppcheck has no concept of packages, release units, or cohesion metrics — it only checks code-level bugs in C/C++.

Common Closure Principle (classes that change together belong together) requires semantic understanding of change reasons and component ownership. Cppcheck performs no architectural or cohesion analysis.

Acyclic Dependencies Principle operates at the component/package level (not file-include cycles). Cppcheck's internal Type::hasCircularDependencies() detects class-inheritance cycles in its symbol database but exposes no user-facing rule that flags component-level dependency cycles; no documented cppcheck --errorlist entry covers ADP violations.
Stable Dependencies Principle requires computing instability metrics (afferent/efferent couplings) across components. Cppcheck has no coupling metrics or component-level dependency analysis.
Stable Abstractions Principle requires measuring abstractness (ratio of abstract classes/interfaces) per component and correlating with stability. Cppcheck has no such architectural metric.

Bounded Context is a DDD strategic design concept (linguistic/model boundaries between subdomains). C has no module, namespace, or type-system construct that maps to bounded contexts, and cppcheck has no architectural analysis whatsoever.

Ubiquitous Language (consistent domain terminology in code) requires semantic understanding of business language vs. identifier names. C has no construct that encodes domain language, and cppcheck performs no naming-convention or semantic analysis at this level.

Domain Model integrity (rich domain objects encapsulating business rules) is a design-pattern concern requiring understanding of business logic structure. C lacks OOP constructs that domain models typically rely on, and cppcheck has no design-pattern or architectural analysis.
DDD Repository is an OOP architectural pattern (domain object persistence abstraction via a class/interface). C has no classes or interfaces, so the construct does not exist in the language.
DDD Service Layer is an OOP/architectural pattern organizing business logic in class-based services. C has no classes or service objects; the construct does not exist in the language.

Encapsulate Variable/Record/Collection is a refactoring smell (direct access to data instead of via accessors). C has structs and global variables but cppcheck has no documented rule detecting unencapsulated data exposure as a design smell.

Indecent Exposure (unnecessarily public/exported symbols) requires understanding intended API boundaries. Cppcheck has no documented rule for excess symbol visibility or unnecessary external linkage as a design issue.

Tell Don't Ask is an OOP principle about avoiding querying object state and then acting on it externally. Cppcheck has no documented rule for this semantic pattern; it is inherently contextual and requires semantic judgement.
Replace Subclass with Delegate is an OOP refactoring involving class inheritance and delegation. C has no subclassing or inheritance; the construct does not exist in the language.
Collapse Hierarchy is an OOP refactoring addressing unnecessary inheritance depth. C has no classes or inheritance hierarchies; the construct does not exist in the language.
Refused Bequest is an OOP smell where a subclass does not use inherited members. C has no subclasses or inheritance; the construct does not exist in the language.
Cppcheck has no rule to assess whether a name is intention-revealing; even its namingng.py addon only enforces regex patterns (casing/prefix), not semantic meaningfulness.
C has no classes; the 'classes are nouns, methods are verbs' principle does not apply to a language with only functions and structs. Structs have no methods.
Cppcheck cannot detect conceptual consistency across identifiers (e.g., mixing 'get'/'fetch'/'retrieve' for the same concept); no rule addresses semantic vocabulary uniformity.
Detecting disinformation in names (e.g., a variable named 'list' that is not a list) requires semantic reasoning about types and usage context; cppcheck has no such rule.
Analyzing whether a function mixes abstraction levels requires architectural/contextual judgement that cppcheck does not perform.
Command-Query Separation requires semantic understanding of intent; cppcheck has no rule for this principle.
Cppcheck detects narrow UB-related side effects (e.g., side effects in assert, order-of-evaluation issues) but has no rule enforcing that functions must be free of side effects as a design principle.
Detecting boolean/enum flag arguments used to select behaviour inside a function is a design smell requiring semantic analysis of call sites and branching logic; cppcheck has no such rule.
Cppcheck has no rules for detecting long parameter lists or suggesting introduction of a Parameter Object; it is a defect finder, not a refactoring advisor.
Cppcheck does not detect deeply nested conditionals or suggest guard-clause refactoring; no complexity or nesting-depth rules exist in its rule set.
Replacing conditional dispatch with polymorphism is a design-level refactoring; cppcheck has no rules for detecting type-switch patterns or suggesting polymorphism.
Cppcheck has no rules for detecting imperative loops that could be expressed as pipeline/functional transformations; this requires semantic understanding beyond its defect-detection scope.
Test execution speed (FIRST: Fast) is a runtime property; cppcheck performs static analysis only and has no rules for test performance.
Test independence (FIRST: Independent) requires understanding inter-test state dependencies and execution ordering; cppcheck has no test-quality rules.
Test repeatability (FIRST: Repeatable) requires detecting non-determinism sources like time, randomness, or external I/O in test contexts; cppcheck has no such test-scoped rules.
Self-validating tests (FIRST: Self-validating) requires detecting tests that lack assertions; cppcheck has no assertion-coverage or test-quality rules.
Timely is a TDD practice principle (write tests before/as you write production code). Cppcheck is a C static analyzer with no concept of test-writing timing or methodology; no rule exists for this.
Red-Green-Refactor is a TDD workflow cycle. No static analysis tool can detect whether a developer followed this process; requires process/human judgment entirely outside cppcheck's scope.

Fake It Till You Make It is a TDD implementation strategy (hardcode return values first, then generalize). Detecting this pattern requires semantic understanding of test intent vs. implementation strategy; cppcheck has no such rule.
Triangulation is a TDD technique (add multiple test cases to force generalization). Cppcheck cannot reason about test design strategy or whether triangulation was applied; no rule covers this.
Assert First is a TDD heuristic (write the assertion before the setup). Cppcheck does not analyze test structure or authoring order; no rule exists for this.

Whether code is 'legacy' (lacks tests) requires understanding the broader test suite relationship to production code. While test-coverage tools exist, cppcheck itself has no rule that flags untested code or absence of characterization tests.

Characterization Tests are tests written to document existing behavior of legacy code. This is a test-authoring strategy judgment; cppcheck has no rule to detect presence or absence of characterization tests.
Sprout Method/Class is a refactoring technique for adding behavior to legacy code. Detecting whether this pattern was used requires architectural/design-level judgment; cppcheck has no rule for this.
C has no classes; Wrap Method/Class is an OOP refactoring pattern with no equivalent construct in C and no cppcheck rule.
C has no interfaces or classes; Extract Interface is an OOP refactoring that cannot apply to C and cppcheck has no such rule.
C has no constructors; Parameterize Constructor/Method is an OOP refactoring concept that does not map to C and cppcheck has no corresponding rule.
Gradual typing is a feature of dynamically-typed languages (Python, JS/TS). C is statically typed; the construct and issue class do not exist.

Cppcheck covers specific narrowing-conversion patterns (truncLongCastAssignment, truncLongCastReturn, signConversion, floatConversionOverflow) but misses narrowing in function arguments, pointer arithmetic, and conditional expressions.
Prototype pollution exploits JavaScript prototype chains; the construct does not exist in C.
Global scope pollution via implicit globals (var hoisting, undeclared assignments) is JS-specific. C has explicit linkage with no dynamic global-creation mechanism.
Avoiding implicit request-context threading through domain models is an architectural discipline requiring semantic judgement; cppcheck has no rule for this class of issue.
C has no eval() or dynamic code execution construct as a language feature; the concept applies to dynamic languages (Python, JS, etc.) only.
Monkey patching requires runtime replacement of methods on objects/classes; C has no classes or runtime method dispatch, so the construct does not exist in the language.
Duck typing requires a dynamic type system; C is statically typed at compile time, so the concept does not apply.
Whether lint failures are enforced as build failures is a CI/build-process policy concern, not a source-code pattern; no static analyzer can detect this.
Test coverage being non-optional is an organizational/process policy; cppcheck performs source-level static analysis and has no capability to measure or enforce test coverage requirements.
Pinning and validating external dependency contracts is a build/dependency management concern; cppcheck analyses C source code and has no rules for dependency versioning or API contract conformance.
Whether log calls emit structured (JSON/key-value) vs. free-form output is a semantic/architectural concern; cppcheck has no rules about logging patterns or output format.
Whether log or trace calls include a correlation/request ID is a domain-semantic concern; cppcheck has no rules for tracing or observability patterns.
Health checks and readiness probes are an operational/infrastructure concern; no construct exists in C source code for cppcheck to analyze.
Alerting on symptoms vs. causes is a monitoring/observability design concern, not a detectable C source-code pattern.
Postel's Law (robustness principle) is an API/protocol design heuristic with no mechanically detectable counterpart in C source code.
Semantic versioning is a release/artifact management concern, not a C code construct; cppcheck has no versioning rules.
Backwards compatibility is an API/ABI design concern requiring cross-version comparison; outside the scope of single-file C static analysis.
Error contracts (documenting/enforcing which errors a function may return) exist as a C pattern but cppcheck has no rule checking for explicit error contract documentation or enforcement.
Pinning transitive dependencies is a build-system/package-manager concern; cppcheck analyzes C source, not dependency manifests.
Minimal dependency footprint is an architectural/build concern; cppcheck has no rules for auditing which libraries are included or whether they are necessary.

Dependency auditing is a supply-chain/process concern (checking package registries, CVE databases, lockfiles); cppcheck analyses C source code patterns and has no dependency-audit capability whatsoever.
Single-responsibility at the package level is an architectural design principle; cppcheck has no concept of packages, modules, or responsibility boundaries and cannot detect violations.

source_label: ListOfChecks – Other: 'variable can be declared const'
rationale: Cppcheck's 'Other' and 'Class' check categories include a 'variable can be declared const' suggestion that flags local variables and member variables that could be const-qualified — a syntactic proxy for immutability — but it does not enforce immutability as an architectural principle and misses heap-allocated data, pointer-target constness in many cases, and design-level immutability of objects.
Share-nothing architecture is a distributed-systems design pattern; cppcheck has no rule that detects shared mutable state across architectural boundaries or enforces isolation between components.

Idempotency is a behavioral/semantic property requiring understanding of side effects and their repeatability; cppcheck's structural static analysis has no rule that can verify or flag non-idempotent operations.

Making state machines explicit is a design-pattern recommendation; cppcheck does not analyse whether implicit state dispersed across variables/flags should be refactored into an explicit state machine.

Ownership semantics over shared or global resources requires understanding of program intent and API contracts; cppcheck covers null pointer and memory leaks but has no rule for incorrect ownership assumptions over shared/global resources.
Comment quality (documenting 'why' decisions vs. 'what' mechanics) is a natural-language judgement that no static analysis rule can evaluate; cppcheck has no comment-content checks.
Cppcheck has no rules about documentation placement or proximity to code; this is a process/convention concern requiring human or LLM judgement.
Executable documentation (e.g., doctests, example-as-test patterns) is a toolchain/workflow concern with no corresponding cppcheck rule.

Least privilege is a security architecture principle (file permissions, capability sets, process privileges); cppcheck performs syntactic/data-flow analysis and has no rules covering privilege scoping.
Defence in depth is a system-design strategy; cppcheck has no rules that assess whether multiple independent security layers are present.
Fail-secure requires semantic understanding of failure modes and default states; cppcheck has no rules targeting insecure default/fallback behaviour after errors.
No clang-tidy rule detects semantically redundant comments (comments that merely restate the code). google-readability-todo only enforces TODO comment formatting, not semantic redundancy.
Clang-tidy is a static analyzer with no ability to measure runtime test coverage of new or changed code; that requires instrumentation-based coverage tools (e.g. llvm-cov/gcov).

readability-function-size and readability-function-cognitive-complexity flag functions that are too large or too complex, which are common proxies for SRP violations, but they cannot detect semantic responsibility mixing — a short function can violate SRP and a large one may not.
No clang-tidy rule analyzes whether a file has a single responsibility; file-level SRP requires semantic/architectural judgement that is beyond static pattern matching.
Clang-tidy has no rules that check test file structure or naming/organization conventions for test suites.

clang-analyzer-deadcode.DeadStores detects dead store assignments, and misc-unused-parameters / misc-unused-using-decls catch some unused declarations, but general dead code (unreachable branches, unused private methods, etc.) is not fully covered.
Changelog updates are a process/workflow concern entirely outside the scope of static analysis; clang-tidy has no mechanism to check whether a changelog file was updated.
Explicit test coverage metrics require runtime instrumentation; clang-tidy is a compile-time static analyzer and cannot determine whether code paths are exercised by tests.
Clang-tidy has no rule that evaluates whether a test exercises observable requirements vs. internal implementation details; this requires semantic/design judgment.

readability-magic-numbers flags unlabeled numeric literals and nudges toward named constants (catching one form of duplicated literals), but does not detect that the same literal appears in multiple places or that constants are duplicated across locations.
Removing ambiguity through data/code normalization is a semantic design decision; no clang-tidy rule addresses this class of issue.
Distinguishing heuristic logic from deterministic alternatives requires domain-level semantic judgment that is beyond static pattern matching; clang-tidy has no rule targeting this.

bugprone-branch-clone catches one class of brittle pattern (duplicate/clone branches in if-else/switch/ternary), but brittle patterns in general (fragile ordering assumptions, tight coupling to internals) are not covered.
Judging whether code is over-specific vs. appropriately generalized requires contextual/intent-level reasoning; clang-tidy has no rule for this.
Clang-tidy has readability-function-size for oversized functions but no equivalent class-size check; Large Class detection is absent from all bundled rule sets.

bugprone-easily-swappable-parameters touches on primitive overuse for adjacent same-typed parameters and recommends strong/opaque types, but only catches the narrow parameter-swappability symptom, not the broader Primitive Obsession pattern (e.g. primitives used as domain concepts throughout a class).
Data Clumps require recognizing that recurring groups of parameters/fields belong together as an object — a semantic grouping judgment clang-tidy has no rule for.

clang-tidy's bugprone-switch-missing-default-case and hicpp-multiway-paths-covered catch coverage gaps and missing defaults, but neither detects the OOP design smell of using switch for type-based dispatch that should be polymorphism — the core of this code smell.

Temporary Field (a field only set in certain code paths/states) requires tracking field assignment patterns across control flow and object lifetime — no clang-tidy rule exists for this semantic smell.

Refused Bequest (a subclass ignores or stubs out inherited methods/data) requires understanding of intentional inheritance relationships and interface contracts — beyond clang-tidy's pattern-matching scope.

Alternative Classes with Different Interfaces require recognizing that two classes are semantically equivalent but have divergent method signatures — a cross-class semantic judgment clang-tidy has no rule for.

Divergent Change (a single class absorbs unrelated reasons to change) requires understanding of responsibility boundaries and change causality — architectural analysis far beyond static pattern rules.
Shotgun Surgery (a single logical change forces edits in many unrelated classes) requires cross-file change-coupling analysis — no clang-tidy rule exists for this systemic smell.

Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies (adding a subclass to one hierarchy forces a matching subclass in another) requires detecting cross-hierarchy structural coupling — no clang-tidy rule exists for this architectural smell.
Detecting a class with too little responsibility requires semantic judgment about design intent; no clang-tidy check measures class-level responsibility or flags under-abstracted types.

No clang-tidy rule identifies classes that only hold data with no meaningful behavior. misc-non-private-member-variables-in-classes enforces encapsulation style but does not flag the data-class smell (it ignores classes where all members are public).

Identifying unused abstractions or hooks added for hypothetical future use requires understanding design intent; clang-tidy has no rule for this smell. misc-unused-parameters only flags unreferenced parameters, not speculatively added design.
Detecting whether comments compensate for unclear code rather than adding genuine value is a semantic/editorial judgment; clang-tidy has no rule for comment over-reliance.
Feature envy (a method using more data from another class than its own) requires cross-class data-flow analysis beyond any clang-tidy check; the tool has no rule for this smell.
Detecting excessive coupling between two classes requires whole-program semantic analysis of access patterns; no clang-tidy check targets this smell.
Identifying long chains of method/member dereferences (Law of Demeter violations) requires structural analysis of call-expression depth that no clang-tidy check performs.
Detecting classes that merely delegate most of their methods to another class requires whole-class behavioral analysis; clang-tidy has no rule for this smell.

Clang-tidy has no check for God Object / Blob class. readability-function-size targets functions, not classes. Detecting a class that does too much requires semantic/architectural judgment that no bundled clang-tidy rule provides.

Anemic Domain Model requires understanding that a class holds data but lacks meaningful behavior — a semantic/design-level judgment. No clang-tidy rule inspects class method-to-field ratios or domain logic presence.

Poltergeist (a class with little state that briefly orchestrates other objects and disappears) requires understanding object lifetime and purpose. No clang-tidy rule detects this structural anti-pattern.

Yo-Yo Problem involves excessive depth or zigzagging in inheritance hierarchies. fuchsia-virtual-inheritance flags virtual inheritance (a different concern) and misc-multiple-inheritance flags multiple concrete bases; neither measures hierarchy depth or detects yo-yo navigation. No rule targets this pattern.

Sequential Coupling (methods must be called in a specific order) is a runtime behavioral contract. Clang-tidy has no rule that can statically detect missing initialization guards or enforced call-order requirements across methods.

Constant Interface anti-pattern (implementing an interface solely to inherit its constants) requires recognizing intent. No clang-tidy rule detects interfaces composed purely of static/const members being inherited for namespace pollution purposes.

Interface Bloat (an interface/abstract class with too many methods) requires measuring interface width and judging cohesion. No clang-tidy rule counts pure-virtual methods per class or flags overly wide interfaces.

Singleton Overuse requires judging whether a singleton is appropriate in context. While fuchsia-singleton (404, does not exist as a bundled check) was investigated and the check list confirms no singleton-related rule; detecting overuse is a design-level judgment beyond any clang-tidy rule.

Combinatorial explosion is a design-level concern about exponential state/condition growth; clang-tidy complexity checks (cognitive-complexity, function-size) measure local control-flow weight but cannot detect the cross-cutting combinatorial nature of this anti-pattern.

Tramp data means parameters that are merely forwarded through a function without local use; misc-unused-parameters only flags parameters with zero usage — forwarded params register as 'used' and are never flagged, so the anti-pattern is undetectable.

readability-function-cognitive-complexity flags functions with high cognitive complexity (nested control flow, many branches) which is a proxy symptom of spaghetti code, but the check only catches the most severe local manifestations and cannot detect inter-function tangled control flow or architectural spaghetti.

bugprone-branch-clone detects identical consecutive branches in if/else chains and switch statements (a copy-paste proxy), but cannot detect duplicated logic spread across different functions, files, or non-adjacent blocks — the general copy-paste programming anti-pattern.

Golden Hammer is an architectural/design anti-pattern (over-relying on a familiar solution); no clang-tidy rule can detect overuse of a particular abstraction or pattern across a codebase — this requires semantic/contextual judgement.

Lava Flow involves dead/obsolete code left in the codebase; clang-tidy catches specific dead declarations (misc-unused-parameters, misc-unused-alias-decls, misc-unused-using-decls) but cannot detect semantically dead logic, obsolete business rules, or commented-out code — the broader anti-pattern.

readability-magic-numbers catches unexplained numeric literals (integer and floating-point), covering a major class of hard-coding, but hard-coded string literals, URLs, file paths, and configuration values embedded as string constants are not detected.

Action at a Distance involves non-local effects via global/shared mutable state; no clang-tidy rule specifically detects the anti-pattern of side-effects that invisibly alter distant program state in ways callers cannot predict.

No clang-tidy rule targets boolean/flag parameters used to switch function behavior. bugprone-easily-swappable-parameters explicitly excludes bool types by default; no other rule addresses this antipattern.

cppcoreguidelines-avoid-non-const-global-variables catches mutable global variables, one common mechanism of hidden dependency, but does not analyze implicit coupling via shared state, singletons, or static locals — so only a subset of hidden dependency forms is caught.

All performance-* checks flag genuine inefficiencies and recommend optimizations; none detects that an optimization is premature (i.e., obscures intent without measurable gain). That judgment requires contextual/semantic reasoning beyond static pattern matching.
No clang-tidy rule detects reimplementation of standard library, OS, or framework functionality (inner-platform effect). The construct exists in C++ but the tool has no rule for this antipattern.

Big Ball of Mud is a whole-system architectural antipattern (lack of structure, pervasive dependencies). clang-tidy operates at the syntactic/AST level and has no rule for detecting this class of issue.
Stovepipe System is an architectural antipattern about siloed subsystems with duplicated, non-shared infrastructure. No clang-tidy rule exists for this; it requires system-level design analysis.
Database as IPC requires recognizing that a database is being used as a message-passing channel between processes — a semantic/architectural judgment that no clang-tidy rule addresses.

Vendor lock-in requires recognizing coupling to vendor-specific APIs or libraries at a design level. No clang-tidy rule targets this; abseil-no-internal-dependencies is Abseil-specific and not a general vendor-lock-in detector.
No clang-tidy rule detects classes with multiple responsibilities; SRP requires semantic/architectural judgment that static AST analysis cannot perform.

No clang-tidy rule detects violations of the Open/Closed Principle; determining whether a class is open for extension but closed for modification requires design-level reasoning unavailable to AST checkers.

cppcoreguidelines-slicing catches object-slicing (one narrow LSP symptom) and bugprone-derived-method-shadowing-base-method catches name hiding, but neither detects the general LSP contract that subtypes must be behaviorally substitutable for their supertypes. True LSP violations require semantic/behavioral reasoning.
No clang-tidy rule detects fat interfaces that force clients to depend on methods they do not use; ISP requires judgment about client coupling that is beyond static AST analysis.

No clang-tidy rule detects DIP violations (high-level modules depending on concrete low-level modules rather than abstractions); dependency direction is a design-level concept not captured by any documented check.
No clang-tidy rule enforces programming to an interface (abstract type) rather than a concrete implementation; this requires semantic understanding of design intent.

misc-multiple-inheritance discourages multiple inheritance and fuchsia-virtual-inheritance discourages virtual inheritance, but neither enforces composition-over-inheritance as a design principle; they are narrow syntactic restrictions, not detectors of inappropriate inheritance vs. composition trade-offs.

No clang-tidy rule detects failure to encapsulate what varies (GoF principle of isolating volatile behavior behind stable interfaces); this requires architectural/design reasoning beyond AST analysis.

Loose coupling (GoF) is a semantic architectural property — clang-tidy has no rule that detects tight coupling between classes or enforces dependency inversion at the design level; it has no GoF or coupling-related checks.
"Passes the tests" is a runtime/testing outcome, not a static-analysis property; clang-tidy cannot determine whether tests pass.

Intention-revealing names require semantic judgement about whether an identifier communicates its purpose; readability-identifier-naming/readability-identifier-length enforce mechanical style rules (case, minimum length) but cannot assess semantic clarity.

DRY/no-duplication requires clone-detection across semantic units; clang-tidy has readability-duplicate-include (trivial header dedup only) and no check for duplicated logic or structural code clones.

YAGNI/fewest-elements requires judgement about whether code is needed; clang-tidy has misc-unused-parameters and misc-unused-alias-decls for trivially unused identifiers but cannot assess over-engineering or unnecessary abstractions.

The Clean Architecture Dependency Rule (inner layers must not depend on outer) is an inter-module architectural constraint; clang-tidy has no checks for layer membership or cross-layer import direction.
Separating concerns into layers is a whole-system architectural judgement; no clang-tidy check detects mixed-layer responsibilities or enforces SoC/layering boundaries.

"Inner knows nothing of outer" (no inner-layer type referencing outer-layer types) is equivalent to the Dependency Rule enforcement — requires architecture-level include/dependency graph analysis not performed by any clang-tidy check.

The Boy Scout Rule ('leave code cleaner than you found it') is a process/discipline norm, not a mechanical code pattern. Clang-tidy has no check that detects whether a changeset improved or degraded code quality relative to its prior state.

Clang-tidy has no check that enforces hexagonal-architecture port semantics (e.g. that a boundary type must be an abstract interface). Architecture layer constraints are not representable as AST-level rules.
Whether a class is an 'adapter' that correctly implements a 'port' interface is a domain-model concept; clang-tidy has no rule that understands or enforces this adapter-implements-port relationship.

Clang-tidy cannot determine which translation units belong to the 'core' hex layer vs. infrastructure, so it cannot enforce that the core is free of framework or I/O dependencies. Requires architectural boundary knowledge.

The distinction between driving-side (primary) adapters/ports and domain logic is an architectural labelling concern; clang-tidy has no concept of hex-architecture sides and no check that enforces correct usage patterns on that side.
Driven-side (secondary/infrastructure) adapter correctness — e.g. that a repository adapter talks only outward through its port — is not a syntactic property. Clang-tidy has no check for this.
Testability-by-design (seams, mockable ports) is a design quality judgement. Clang-tidy has no check that measures or enforces testability of architectural seams.

Inward-pointing dependency enforcement (dependency rule) requires knowing the layer each file belongs to; clang-tidy has no layer-aware dependency-direction check. The cppcoreguidelines module contains no such rule.
Reuse/Release Equivalence is an architectural component-cohesion principle; clang-tidy has no rule that measures or enforces grouping of reusable units for release.
Common Closure Principle (classes that change together belong together) is a semantic cohesion judgment across a codebase; no clang-tidy rule addresses it.

misc-header-include-cycle detects cyclic #include chains between header files, which is a syntactic proxy for cyclic component dependencies, but it operates at the file/header level only and cannot reason about component- or package-level dependency graphs.
Stable Dependencies Principle requires computing instability metrics (fan-in/fan-out) across component boundaries; clang-tidy has no such architectural metric rule.
Stable Abstractions Principle requires measuring abstractness (ratio of abstract classes/interfaces) per component; no clang-tidy rule computes or enforces this.
DDD Bounded Context is a domain-partitioning architectural concept; clang-tidy operates on AST-level code patterns and has no rule related to context boundaries.

Ubiquitous Language consistency requires matching identifiers against a domain vocabulary — a semantic/domain judgment that no clang-tidy rule can perform; readability checks only enforce code-style naming conventions.
Domain Model correctness (aggregates, entities, value objects, invariants) is a semantic/architectural concern; clang-tidy has no rule that reasons about domain model integrity.
DDD Repository is a pure architectural pattern (data-access abstraction layer); clang-tidy has no rules for DDD concepts or layered-architecture enforcement.
DDD Service Layer is an architectural/organizational construct; clang-tidy has no rules for DDD concepts or service-layer separation.
This check flags all non-private data members in classes that also have member functions, directly detecting the mechanical pattern of unencapsulated variables/fields.
By default (IgnorePublicMemberVariables=false), the check diagnoses public data members in classes with methods — exactly the 'indecent exposure' of internal state pattern.
Tell Don't Ask requires understanding that a caller queries object state and then makes decisions on its behalf; this is a semantic/behavioral pattern with no corresponding clang-tidy rule.
Replacing a subclass with delegation is a structural refactoring decision requiring semantic understanding of class relationships; clang-tidy has no rule for this pattern.
Collapsing a class hierarchy when the distinction adds no value is a design-level judgment requiring semantic understanding; no clang-tidy rule detects this.

Refused bequest (subclass ignores or throws away inherited interface/behavior) requires semantic reasoning about inheritance intent; misc-override-with-different-visibility catches only visibility narrowing, which is a distinct and narrower issue.

clang-tidy's readability-identifier-naming enforces casing/prefix/suffix style only; readability-identifier-length enforces minimum character counts only. Neither can evaluate whether a name actually reveals intent — that requires semantic/conceptual judgement no static rule can provide.

No clang-tidy rule distinguishes noun-named classes from verb-named methods. Identifier naming checks are purely pattern/style based (casing, prefix, suffix) and have no understanding of grammatical part-of-speech or semantic role.

Enforcing one-word-per-concept (consistent synonyms across a codebase) requires cross-identifier semantic understanding. clang-tidy has no rule that tracks conceptual consistency of terminology; its naming checks operate on per-identifier patterns only.

Detecting disinformation (e.g. a variable named 'accountList' that holds a Map, or a name that contradicts its actual semantics) requires understanding the relationship between a name and the type/behaviour it describes. No clang-tidy rule performs this semantic cross-check.

readability-function-size and readability-function-cognitive-complexity flag size/complexity proxies but cannot determine whether a function mixes abstraction levels — that is a semantic design judgement beyond any static syntactic rule.

modernize-use-nodiscard identifies member functions with no observable side effects and suggests [[nodiscard]], which is a mechanical proxy for the query side of CQS. However it does not detect command functions that silently return values, and CQS violations in general are not fully covered.

cppcoreguidelines-avoid-non-const-global-variables catches a narrow class of side effects (writes to mutable global state). General side effects (hidden mutations of arguments, I/O, static locals, etc.) are not detected. No clang-tidy rule covers the full semantic scope of 'no side effects'.

No clang-tidy rule detects boolean/enum flag arguments that select function behaviour (the 'Remove Flag Argument' refactoring smell). bugprone-easily-swappable-parameters flags adjacent same-type params for swap risk, not the design smell of behaviour-controlling flags.

No clang-tidy rule detects parameter proliferation warranting a Parameter Object refactoring; bugprone-easily-swappable-parameters flags adjacent swappable params but not the general 'too many parameters' smell.

readability-else-after-return flags else-after-return/break/continue/throw, which is the syntactic proxy for the guard-clause pattern, but misses deeply nested conditionals that don't use else-after-return and does not cover all forms of guard clause opportunities.
No clang-tidy rule detects type-switch or repeated conditional dispatch on type tags that should be replaced with polymorphism; this requires semantic understanding of class hierarchies.

modernize-loop-convert only rewrites indexed/iterator loops to range-based for (not to algorithms); modernize-use-ranges upgrades existing algorithm calls to ranges but does not detect hand-written loops that should become pipeline/algorithm calls.
FIRST 'Fast' is a runtime performance property of test suites; no clang-tidy rule detects slow tests or tests with expensive I/O — requires profiling or semantic judgment.
FIRST 'Independent' (tests must not depend on each other or shared mutable state) requires understanding of test ordering and shared state semantics; no clang-tidy rule covers this.
FIRST 'Repeatable' (tests produce same result regardless of environment) requires reasoning about environment dependencies and nondeterminism; no clang-tidy rule covers this.
