FIRST 'Self-validating' (tests must have a boolean pass/fail assertion) requires semantic understanding of test structure; no clang-tidy rule detects missing assertions in tests.
Cppcheck is a C/C++ static analyzer focused on code bugs; it has no rule that evaluates comments for redundancy or quality.
Cppcheck performs static analysis only; it has no ability to measure test coverage of new or changed code — that requires instrumented execution.
Cppcheck has no rule for Single Responsibility Principle violations at the function level; SRP is a design-level judgment that requires semantic/contextual analysis.
Cppcheck has no rule for SRP at the file level; file-level cohesion is an architectural concern beyond its static analysis scope.
Cppcheck has no rules for test file structure or test organization conventions.

Cppcheck detects several syntactic forms of dead code — unreachableCode (CWE-561), unusedFunction, unusedVariable, unreadVariable — but unusedFunction requires --enable=unusedFunction (non-default), and semantically dead code (logically unreachable due to business logic) is not caught.
Cppcheck performs static code analysis only; it has no rule to verify that a changelog file has been updated.
Explicit test coverage measurement requires runtime instrumentation; cppcheck is a static analyzer with no coverage-tracking capability.
Cppcheck performs C/C++ static analysis for bugs and undefined behavior; it has no rules for test quality, test design semantics, or whether tests target requirements vs implementation.

Cppcheck's duplicate-related checks (duplicateExpression, duplicateBranch, duplicateValueTernary) catch the same expression on both sides of an operator or identical if/else branches — not repeated magic number literals or duplicated constants scattered across a codebase. No magic-number or repeated-literal rule is documented.
Normalizing data/code to remove ambiguity is a design-level judgment requiring semantic understanding; cppcheck has no rule that addresses this class of issue.
Identifying and replacing heuristic logic with deterministic equivalents requires semantic and domain understanding; cppcheck has no rule for this.
Brittle patterns (e.g., fragile coupling, assumption-laden code) require contextual/design judgment; cppcheck has no rule that detects brittleness as a class of issue.
Over-specificity vs. generalization is a design-quality judgment; cppcheck has no rule for detecting when code is too narrowly specific.

Cppcheck's Class checks cover constructor correctness, memory safety, virtual destructors, and constness — not class size or complexity metrics; Large Class detection is absent from the documented check list.
Primitive Obsession (using primitives where domain types should be used) is a design smell requiring semantic/domain judgment; no cppcheck rule addresses this pattern.

source_label: Cppcheck homepage — 'focus on bugs instead of stylistic issues'
rationale: Data Clumps is an OOP design smell requiring semantic recognition of recurring parameter groups across methods/classes. Cppcheck explicitly focuses on bugs and undefined behaviour, not design smells, and has no rule for detecting data clumps.

source_label: cppcheck ListOfChecks — no design-smell switch rule listed
rationale: Fowler's 'Switch Statements' smell flags overuse of type-dispatching switch/if-else chains as a sign polymorphism should be used instead. Cppcheck only checks for syntactic switch anomalies (e.g. unusedLabelSwitch, fallthrough) — it has no rule detecting this design-level smell.

source_label: Cppcheck homepage — scope limited to bugs/undefined behaviour
rationale: Temporary Field (fields meaningful only under certain object states) is a semantic OOP design smell. Cppcheck detects uninitialized member variables, but not the architectural pattern where a field is intentionally left unset for most of an object's lifetime.

source_label: cppcheck ListOfChecks — class checks cover destructor/override, not bequest refusal
rationale: Refused Bequest (subclass ignores inherited members/interface) requires understanding inheritance intent. Cppcheck checks virtual destructor and override keyword usage, but has no rule for detecting a subclass that deliberately refuses inherited behaviour.

source_label: Cppcheck homepage — no cross-class semantic equivalence checks
rationale: Alternative Classes with Different Interfaces (classes doing the same thing under different names) is a cross-class semantic comparison. No static analyser can detect this without cross-class semantic equivalence analysis; cppcheck has no such rule.

source_label: Cppcheck homepage — scope limited to bugs/undefined behaviour
rationale: Divergent Change (a class changes for multiple unrelated reasons) is a high-level SRP/cohesion smell requiring architectural judgement about responsibilities. Cppcheck has no cohesion or change-reason analysis.

source_label: Cppcheck homepage — no coupling or change-impact analysis
rationale: Shotgun Surgery (one change requires many small edits across classes) is a coupling/change-impact smell detectable only by cross-module coupling analysis. Cppcheck performs no such analysis.

Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies (adding a subclass in one hierarchy forces adding one in another) requires cross-hierarchy structural pattern recognition. Cppcheck has no rule for detecting mirrored inheritance structures.
Cppcheck is a correctness/safety analyzer with no rules for detecting classes with too little responsibility (Lazy Class smell). This requires semantic/design judgment beyond cppcheck's scope.
Cppcheck has no rules for detecting Data Class smell (classes with only fields and accessors, no behavior). This is a design-level judgment cppcheck does not make.

Speculative Generality (unused abstractions/parameters added for hypothetical future use) requires intent inference. Cppcheck does detect some unused functions/variables as style warnings, but cannot distinguish intentional generality from accidental dead code at the design level.
Cppcheck performs no analysis of comment density or over-reliance on comments to compensate for unclear code. Comment quality judgment is outside its scope.
Feature Envy (a method more interested in data of another class than its own) requires cross-class semantic analysis of method access patterns. Cppcheck has no such rule.
Inappropriate Intimacy (classes that access each other's private/internal details excessively) requires relationship-level design analysis. Cppcheck does not model inter-class coupling at this level.
Message Chains (long chains of calls like a.getB().getC().getD()) are a structural pattern cppcheck does not detect. Its analysis does not flag chained member access as a design smell.
Middle Man (a class that merely delegates all its work to another class) requires understanding of delegation ratios across a class's methods. Cppcheck has no rule for this design smell.
SpotBugs is a bytecode-level static analyzer focused on bug patterns; it has no rules for detecting redundant, outdated, or low-quality comments — that requires natural-language/semantic judgment.
SpotBugs does not measure or enforce unit test coverage for new/changed code; that is the domain of coverage tools (JaCoCo, Cobertura). No SpotBugs rule addresses this.
SRP at the function/method level (too many responsibilities) requires semantic reasoning about intent; SpotBugs has no rule for method-level SRP violations.
SRP at the file/class level is a design concern requiring semantic judgment; SpotBugs has no rule for class-level SRP violations.
Test file structure (naming, organisation, arrange-act-assert layout) is a convention/style concern; SpotBugs has no rules targeting test structure or layout.

SpotBugs detects several dead-code patterns (e.g. DLS_DEAD_LOCAL_STORE, UCF_USELESS_CONTROL_FLOW, RV_RETURN_VALUE_IGNORED) but cannot detect all forms of dead code — e.g. unreferenced private methods/fields missed by bytecode analysis, or code made unreachable by logic — so coverage is partial.
Changelog updates are a process/documentation requirement entirely outside SpotBugs' scope; no rule exists for this.
Explicit test coverage thresholds and enforcement are handled by coverage tools, not SpotBugs; SpotBugs has no coverage measurement capability.

SpotBugs has no rule that can distinguish tests verifying requirements from tests that merely probe internal implementation details; this requires semantic/intent-level judgement. JUA_DONT_ASSERT_INSTANCEOF_IN_TESTS catches one narrow test anti-pattern but is unrelated to the implementation-vs-requirement distinction.

HSC_HUGE_SHARED_STRING_CONSTANT detects large string literals duplicated across multiple class files, and CNT_ROUGH_CONSTANT_VALUE flags approximate use of known mathematical constants; together they cover some duplication cases, but small repeated literals within a single class, numeric magic numbers, and non-huge cross-file duplicates are not detected.

SpotBugs has no rule that identifies ambiguous or un-normalised data/code structures and suggests canonical form; this requires semantic understanding of the domain, not a mechanical bytecode pattern.

SpotBugs does not have any detector that identifies heuristic or probabilistic logic and suggests replacing it with a deterministic algorithm; distinguishing 'heuristic' from 'deterministic' requires semantic judgement beyond static analysis.

Although SpotBugs catches specific fragile bug patterns (e.g. RC_REF_COMPARISON_BAD_PRACTICE, EQ_OVERRIDING_EQUALS_NOT_SYMMETRIC), there is no general 'brittle pattern' detector; the broad class of brittleness (magic literals, fragile string matching, overly tight coupling to implementation details) is not covered systematically.

SpotBugs has no rule for over-specificity or lack of generalisation; detecting when code is needlessly specialised versus appropriately generalised requires design-level judgement beyond static bytecode analysis.
SpotBugs has no detector for class size, method count, or field count; 'Large Class' is a design smell requiring metrics-based analysis not present in SpotBugs out of the box.

PA_PUBLIC_PRIMITIVE_ATTRIBUTE flags public primitive fields on access-visibility grounds, which is a narrow proxy for Primitive Obsession. It does not detect the core smell of using raw primitives instead of domain-meaningful value objects, so most instances of Primitive Obsession are missed.
Data Clumps is a structural design smell requiring cross-method/cross-class field-group analysis; SpotBugs has no bytecode-level rule for it.

SpotBugs detects SF_DEAD_STORE_DUE_TO_SWITCH_FALLTHROUGH (missing break causing dead store) — a mechanical switch defect — but does not flag the broader 'replace switch with polymorphism' design smell, so only a syntactic subset is covered.
Temporary Field (fields set only in certain scenarios) requires inter-procedural data-flow analysis across object state; SpotBugs has no documented rule for this design smell.

Refused Bequest (subclass ignores inherited members) is a semantic OO design smell; SpotBugs has no rule that detects when a subclass overrides methods only to throw UnsupportedOperationException or otherwise refuses inherited behavior.
Alternative Classes with Different Interfaces is a design smell requiring cross-class API comparison; SpotBugs performs intra-class/bytecode analysis and has no rule for this.
Divergent Change (a class changes for multiple unrelated reasons) is a cohesion/SRP smell requiring high-level change-history and semantic reasoning; SpotBugs has no such rule.
Shotgun Surgery (one change ripples across many classes) requires cross-class coupling and change-impact analysis; SpotBugs has no rule for this design smell.
Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies (adding a subclass in one hierarchy forces adding one in another) requires cross-hierarchy structural analysis; SpotBugs has no documented rule for this.
SpotBugs has no rule for Lazy Class (a class that does too little to justify its existence); this requires structural/semantic judgment across the codebase.
SpotBugs has no rule for Data Class (anemic object with only fields/getters/setters and no behaviour); detecting this requires semantic analysis of method responsibility distribution.
SpotBugs has no rule for Speculative Generality (unused abstractions/hooks added for hypothetical future use); requires contextual judgment about intent and actual usage.
SpotBugs performs no analysis of comment quality or over-reliance on comments as a substitute for clear code; this is a human/LLM judgment concern.
Feature Envy (a method that uses data from another class more than its own) is a cross-class coupling smell requiring semantic analysis; SpotBugs has no detector for it.
Inappropriate Intimacy (two classes overly coupled via excessive access to each other's internals) requires whole-program coupling analysis; SpotBugs has no such rule.
Message Chains (a.getB().getC().getD() Law of Demeter violation) require AST/call-chain analysis; SpotBugs has no documented rule for this pattern.
Middle Man (a class that delegates almost all its methods to another) requires structural analysis of delegation ratios; SpotBugs has no detector for this design smell.

SpotBugs has no rule that detects oversized/overly-responsible classes (God Object / Blob). Its detectors focus on correctness, concurrency, and bad practice patterns at the method/expression level, not holistic class-size or responsibility metrics.

Anemic Domain Model (domain classes that are pure data containers with logic pushed into services) requires semantic understanding of domain intent. SpotBugs has no rule that distinguishes intentionally thin domain objects from anemic ones.
Poltergeist detection (a class with transient existence and little behaviour that just passes data to another class) requires cross-class semantic analysis. SpotBugs documents no such rule.

The Yo-Yo Problem (deep inheritance hierarchies forcing readers to bounce up and down the tree) is an architectural smell requiring inheritance-depth metrics. SpotBugs has no documented rule for inheritance depth or Yo-Yo detection.

Sequential Coupling (API methods that must be called in a specific order) requires temporal/semantic reasoning about method preconditions. SpotBugs has no rule for enforcing or detecting unconstrained call-order dependencies.

The Constant Interface anti-pattern (an interface whose sole purpose is to hold constants, later implemented by classes to import those constants) is not flagged by any documented SpotBugs rule. SpotBugs' BAD_PRACTICE category covers serialisation, clone, and equals/hashCode idioms but not this pattern.
Interface Bloat (an interface declaring too many unrelated methods) requires a count/cohesion metric. SpotBugs has no documented rule measuring interface method count or cohesion.

SpotBugs' SING_ detector family (SING_SINGLETON_HAS_NONPRIVATE_CONSTRUCTOR, SING_SINGLETON_GETTER_NOT_SYNCHRONIZED, etc.) flags implementation flaws *within* an existing singleton class, not the architectural smell of using too many singletons. 'Singleton Overuse' as a design smell has no corresponding SpotBugs rule.

source_label: SpotBugs bug descriptions — no matching rule
rationale: Combinatorial Explosion is a design-level anti-pattern (too many cases/branches due to factorial interactions of conditions) requiring semantic understanding of domain intent. SpotBugs has no rule targeting this; its correctness/complexity rules focus on concrete bugs, not architectural overdesign.

source_label: SpotBugs bug descriptions — no matching rule
rationale: Tramp Data (passing data through layers that don't use it, just to deliver it elsewhere) requires cross-layer call-graph dataflow analysis. SpotBugs has no rule identifying unnecessary parameter threading; its dataflow checks address null/uninitialized reads, not coupling smells.

source_label: SpotBugs bug descriptions — no matching rule
rationale: Spaghetti Code (tangled control flow, lack of structure across classes/methods) is an architectural smell requiring holistic judgement. SpotBugs has no cyclomatic-complexity or structural-coupling rules; it detects concrete bytecode-level bugs, not design quality.

RpC_REPEATED_CONDITIONAL_TEST catches one narrow copy-paste symptom — identical conditional expressions duplicated back-to-back — but general code duplication across methods/classes is not detected; SpotBugs is not a clone-detection tool.

source_label: SpotBugs bug descriptions — no matching rule
rationale: Golden Hammer (overusing a familiar tool/pattern regardless of fit) is a design-level judgement about technology choice and context-appropriateness. No SpotBugs rule addresses this anti-pattern.

Lava Flow (dead/obsolete code frozen in place) is partially addressable: UWF_UNWRITTEN_FIELD and UWF_NULL_FIELD flag fields never meaningfully written, and ISC_INSTANTIATE_STATIC_CLASS flags dead instantiations. However, general dead/zombie code spanning methods and classes is not detected; coverage is very narrow.

CNT_ROUGH_CONSTANT_VALUE flags hard-coded numeric literals that should use standard library constants (e.g. Math.PI). This covers a narrow slice of hard-coding. General magic numbers for business logic, hard-coded strings, URLs, or credentials are not detected.

source_label: SpotBugs bug descriptions — no matching rule
rationale: Action at a Distance (change in one place causes non-obvious effects elsewhere via global/shared state) requires reasoning about intent and implicit coupling. SpotBugs has no rule detecting this design smell; it does not reason about surprising side-effects through global state.
SpotBugs has no rule that detects boolean/flag parameters used to branch behavior; requires semantic understanding of call-site intent.
SpotBugs does not detect undeclared reliance on global/static state; requires data-flow/architectural analysis beyond its bug-pattern model.
Intent-based design decision; no SpotBugs rule targets this class of issue.
No SpotBugs rule detects re-implementation of platform/framework features; requires contextual/semantic judgement.
Whole-system architectural anti-pattern; SpotBugs has no metric-based or inter-class structural rules.
Inter-module isolation concern; SpotBugs has no coupling or dependency-graph rules.
SpotBugs only flags unclosed DB resources (ODR_*), not the architectural misuse of a DB as a message bus.
No SpotBugs rule detects coupling to vendor-specific APIs; a strategic/architectural concern outside its scope.

PMD's CommentRequired rule enforces presence/absence of Javadoc comments on code elements, and CommentContent flags forbidden terms, but neither rule detects semantically redundant comments (e.g., comments that merely restate the code). CommentSize limits comment dimensions but not redundancy. No rule judges whether a comment adds value vs. parrots the code.

PMD is a static AST analyser; it has no coverage instrumentation and cannot measure whether new/changed code is covered by unit tests. Test-structure rules (UnitTestShouldIncludeAssert etc.) check test code shape, not coverage metrics.

PMD offers size/complexity proxies — CyclomaticComplexity, CognitiveComplexity, NcssCount, ExcessiveParameterList — that flag overly large or complex methods, which correlates with SRP violations. However, these are thresholds on metrics, not a semantic check that a function has a single responsibility; they miss short-but-mixed-responsibility functions and require tuning.

PMD's GodClass rule detects classes that do too much using weighted method count, access-to-foreign-data, and cohesion metrics; TooManyMethods and TooManyFields add further size signals. These are structural proxies for file-level SRP violations but cannot reason semantically about whether disparate responsibilities are present in a small class.

PMD has several rules enforcing specific test structural conventions: UnitTestShouldUseBeforeAnnotation, UnitTestShouldUseAfterAnnotation, UnitTestShouldUseTestAnnotation, JUnitJupiterTestShouldBePackagePrivate, UnitTestContainsTooManyAsserts. These cover a subset of structural norms but not project-specific conventions (naming, file layout, grouping).

PMD covers several dead-code categories: UnusedPrivateField, UnusedPrivateMethod, UnusedLocalVariable, UnusedFormalParameter, UnusedAssignment, UnnecessaryImport, UnusedLabel. This is solid for private/local scope, but public/protected unused members are outside PMD's reach without whole-program analysis, so general dead-code detection is incomplete.

PMD analyses Java source code ASTs and has no awareness of changelog files (CHANGELOG.md, release notes, etc.). Whether a changelog was updated is a repository-level process check that no PMD rule addresses.

PMD cannot measure test coverage (line, branch, or mutation). Coverage requires runtime instrumentation (e.g., JaCoCo). PMD's test-related rules check structural code patterns, not whether production code is exercised by tests.
PMD has no rule checking whether tests exercise requirements vs. implementation; UnitTestShouldIncludeAssert only verifies an assertion exists, not what semantic property it tests.
AvoidDuplicateLiterals catches repeated string literals (default: ≥4 occurrences, ≥3 chars) but does not cover numeric literals or symbolic-constant duplication.
Normalizing data/code to remove ambiguity is a semantic design judgment; PMD has no rule targeting ambiguous representations or equivalent-but-inconsistent encodings.
Replacing heuristics with deterministic logic requires understanding programmer intent; PMD has no rule that identifies approximation/heuristic code paths.

AvoidLiteralsInIfCondition flags hard-coded literals in conditionals (a common brittleness symptom), but PMD has no general brittle-pattern detector; magic numbers in other contexts or fragile string comparisons are not covered.
Distinguishing over-specific from appropriately general code is a semantic judgment; PMD has no rule for detecting over-specificity or missed generalization opportunities.

PMD has no rule named LargeClass; closest proxies are TooManyFields (>15 fields), TooManyMethods (>10 methods), NcssCount (NCSS thresholds), and GodClass (WMC+ATFD+TCC) — overlapping but not identical facets of large class.

TooManyFields catches classes with excessive primitive fields (a primitive-obsession symptom) and DataClass detects data-holder classes, but PMD has no rule that directly flags primitives that should be replaced by value objects.

ExcessiveParameterList flags long parameter lists (a proxy for data clumps in method signatures) and TooManyFields flags overloaded classes, but neither detects the semantic pattern of recurring groups of fields/parameters that should be extracted into a dedicated object.

PMD has NonExhaustiveSwitch (missing default/enum coverage), ImplicitSwitchFallThrough (missing break), and SwitchDensity (overloaded switch body) — these catch structural switch defects but not the higher-level smell of using switch instead of polymorphism.

SingularField detects private fields that are always assigned before use in every method and should therefore be local variables — a structural proxy for temporary fields — but it only covers private fields and misses fields used conditionally or only in some execution paths.

No PMD rule detects subclasses that override inherited methods only to throw UnsupportedOperationException or simply do nothing with inherited behaviour; requires semantic inheritance-graph analysis absent from PMD's rule set.
PMD has no rule that identifies two classes performing equivalent roles but exposing inconsistent interfaces; detecting this smell requires cross-class semantic comparison that PMD does not perform.

Divergent Change (a class modified for multiple unrelated reasons) requires understanding of change history and responsibility boundaries; PMD's GodClass and complexity rules are weak structural proxies but do not directly detect this smell.
Shotgun Surgery (one logical change triggers edits across many classes) requires cross-class change-impact analysis that is beyond PMD's per-file static rule approach; no bundled rule addresses it.
No PMD rule detects mirrored inheritance hierarchies that must be extended in lockstep; this requires cross-hierarchy structural analysis not present in PMD's rule set.

source_label: PMD Java Design rules — complete rule list
rationale: No PMD rule named LazyClass or equivalent exists. The full design rule list has no rule targeting classes that are too small or do too little to justify their existence. UseUtilityClass catches one narrow structural sub-case (class with only static members) but does not address the general 'class not pulling its weight' smell.

PMD has a dedicated DataClass rule that uses four OO metrics (WMC, WOC, NOPA, NOAM) to flag classes with many public fields/accessors and little behaviour. It catches metric-threshold cases but misses intentional DTOs/records and cases where the metric combination falls below the threshold despite the smell being present; it is a heuristic proxy rather than a semantic detector.

source_label: PMD Java Design rules — complete rule list
rationale: No PMD rule targets Speculative Generality (unused abstractions, parameters, or hooks added for hypothetical future use). Detecting it requires understanding design intent and usage context across the codebase, which is beyond PMD's purely syntactic/metric analysis.

PMD's Documentation rules (CommentRequired, CommentSize, CommentContent, DanglingJavadoc) all enforce the presence or formatting of comments; none flag over-reliance on comments or penalise excessive commenting as a substitute for clear code. The smell requires semantic judgement about whether a comment is compensating for unclear logic.

source_label: PMD — GodClass rule (uses ATFD as component metric)
rationale: No dedicated Feature Envy rule exists. GodClass uses ATFD (Access To Foreign Data) as one of three metrics, but it targets god classes (high WMC + high ATFD + low TCC) rather than individual methods that envy another class's data. A focused feature-envy method in an otherwise normal class is not caught.

No PMD rule targets Inappropriate Intimacy (two classes that know too much about each other's internals bidirectionally). CouplingBetweenObjects counts outgoing coupling from one class but does not detect the reciprocal, bidirectional intimacy pattern that characterises this smell.

PMD's LawOfDemeter rule directly detects chained method/getter calls exceeding a configurable trustRadius, which is the syntactic form of Message Chains. However, it is not included in the default or quickstart ruleset and must be explicitly opted in; it also flags all LoD violations (not just long chains), producing noise that leads teams to disable it.

UselessOverridingMethod catches the narrow case where an override merely calls super with identical arguments — one syntactic sub-form of Middle Man. It does not detect the general pattern of a class that mostly delegates to a wrapped object via its own non-override methods, leaving most Middle Man instances undetected.

PMD's GodClass rule uses WMC/ATFD/TCC metrics to detect classes doing too much, but relies on metric thresholds rather than deterministic structural detection, and may miss god objects that score well on metrics yet still centralize too much responsibility.

PMD's DataClass rule uses WMC/WOC/NOPA/NOAM metrics to flag data-heavy classes lacking behaviour, which is a proxy for Anemic Domain Model, but it cannot assess whether missing domain logic semantically belongs in the class.

No PMD rule detects Poltergeist classes (short-lived objects that merely delegate to another object and add no real value); this requires semantic/contextual judgement about object lifecycle and responsibility.

PMD has no rule for the Yo-Yo Problem (excessively deep or wide inheritance hierarchies that fragment logic across many layers); no inheritance-depth metric rule was found in the design or other categories.
Sequential Coupling (methods that must be called in a specific order to avoid runtime failure) requires data-flow or temporal analysis; PMD has no rule for this pattern.

PMD's ConstantsInInterface rule in the Best Practices category directly and deterministically flags interfaces that declare only constants (fields) without methods, covering essentially all instances of the Constant Interface anti-pattern.

PMD's TooManyMethods rule flags classes with excessive method counts (default threshold 10), which can act as a proxy for bloated interfaces, but it targets classes not interfaces specifically, so it misses interface bloat that doesn't also manifest as a large implementing class.

PMD has no rule that detects overuse of the Singleton pattern; detecting whether a singleton is architecturally inappropriate requires contextual judgement about design intent and dependency management.

NPathComplexity counts acyclic execution paths and grows exponentially with decision points, flagging methods where path counts explode—but it measures the symptom (high path count) rather than identifying the combinatorial explosion design anti-pattern directly; it misses cases where explosion arises from interactions across multiple methods.

ExcessiveParameterList flags methods with too many parameters (default ≥10), which is a surface proxy for tramp data, but it cannot detect data that is passed through intermediate call layers unused—the core semantic of the tramp data smell.

CyclomaticComplexity, NPathComplexity, and AvoidDeeplyNestedIfStmts catch structural symptoms of spaghetti code (excessive branching, nesting), but the broader pattern—unstructured, tangled control flow across classes—requires semantic judgment PMD cannot make.

PMD's CPD (Copy-Paste Detector) is a separate standalone tool not part of the standard rule-set; within the analyzer, AvoidDuplicateLiterals catches repeated string literals and IdenticalConditionalBranches catches identical branch bodies, but structural code duplication across methods or classes is not covered by any standard PMD rule.

The golden hammer anti-pattern (over-applying a familiar solution inappropriately) is a design-intent judgment; no PMD rule captures the concept of misapplied patterns or inappropriate solution reuse.

PMD detects unused private methods, fields, and local variables (dead code), which is the mechanical subset of lava flow, but it cannot detect code that is reachable yet effectively obsolete or kept out of fear—the broader lava flow phenomenon requiring historical/contextual judgment.

PMD covers several hard-coding sub-cases: duplicate string literals (AvoidDuplicateLiterals), literals in conditionals (AvoidLiteralsInIfCondition), and hard-coded IP addresses (AvoidUsingHardCodedIP), but there is no MagicNumber rule in current PMD for arbitrary numeric literals, and hard-coded configuration values in general are not fully covered.

MutableStaticState catches non-private non-final static fields (mutable global state) and LawOfDemeter catches method-chaining access to distant objects—both are partial proxies for action at a distance, but neither detects the full semantic: effects triggered by side-channel state changes at non-obvious distance without direct coupling.
PMD has no rule targeting boolean flag arguments (a parameter that switches behaviour inside a method); ExcessiveParameterList only counts parameter count, not their boolean nature.

PMD has no rule that detects hidden dependencies (e.g., static singleton access, ThreadLocal, global state pulled inside a method instead of injected); this requires semantic understanding of dependency graphs.
PMD's Performance ruleset targets genuine inefficiencies (string concatenation, object creation in loops); it has no rule that flags premature or unnecessary optimisation as a design smell.
PMD has no rule for the Inner-Platform Effect (reimplementing abstractions already provided by the language/platform); detecting this requires understanding what the platform already offers.

GodClass, CouplingBetweenObjects, and ExcessiveImports are class-level metric proxies that can surface individual mud-ball classes, but Big Ball of Mud is a system/module-level architectural anti-pattern that PMD cannot detect holistically.
Stovepipe System is an architectural anti-pattern about siloed subsystems lacking shared abstractions; PMD has no rule that operates at the inter-module/subsystem level to detect this.
Database as IPC (using a DB table as a message queue between processes) is a runtime/architectural pattern that PMD cannot detect from static analysis of Java source code.
PMD has no rule that identifies vendor-specific API usage as a lock-in risk; LooseCoupling (bestpractices) only flags concrete collection types, not vendor lock-in in the general architectural sense.

GodClass (WMC+ATFD+TCC metrics), TooManyMethods, and TooManyFields catch oversized/over-responsible classes, but SRP violations in small, cohesive-looking classes with mixed responsibilities are purely semantic and missed entirely.

No PMD rule targets the Open/Closed Principle (open for extension, closed for modification). The principle is about design intent and extensibility strategy — not mechanically detectable from syntax or metrics.

LSP violations (behavioral substitutability — subtype breaks preconditions, postconditions, or invariants of the supertype) are semantic and require runtime or deep semantic analysis. DoNotExtendJavaLangError is too narrow and tangential to count as LSP detection.

ExcessivePublicCount flags interfaces/classes with too many public members (default threshold 45), which is a proxy for ISP violations, but it cannot detect whether clients are forced to depend on methods they don't use — the core ISP concern.

DIP (depend on abstractions, not concretions) is not addressed by any PMD rule. CouplingBetweenObjects measures coupling breadth but does not distinguish abstract from concrete dependencies — the heart of DIP.

No PMD rule checks whether variables, fields, or parameters are declared as interface types rather than concrete class types. This GoF principle requires tracking declaration types against the type hierarchy — absent from PMD's ruleset.
PMD has no rule that discourages deep or unnecessary inheritance in favour of composition. There is no 'prefer composition over inheritance' detector in the standard or Design ruleset.

The GoF principle 'encapsulate what varies' is a design-intent concept (identify variation points and isolate them behind abstractions). PMD's ImmutableField and DataClass rules touch encapsulation hygiene but do not address this principle.

PMD's LooseCoupling flags concrete collection types instead of interfaces, CouplingBetweenObjects measures coupling thresholds, and LawOfDemeter restricts chained calls — these are syntactic proxies for loose coupling but do not detect GoF-pattern usage (strategy, observer, dependency injection) or general interface-over-implementation design.
Whether code passes tests is a runtime/CI concern; static analysis cannot determine test correctness or coverage sufficiency. PMD has no rule for this.

PMD's LinguisticNaming catches mismatches between name and type (e.g. boolean getter not using 'is'), BooleanGetMethodName and MethodNamingConventions enforce camelCase conventions — these cover narrow mechanical naming violations but cannot judge whether a name is semantically intention-revealing.

CPD (Copy-Paste Detector), bundled with PMD, detects textual/structural code duplication across files. It misses semantic duplication (same logic written differently) and does not address data or knowledge duplication, so it covers only a subset of DRY violations.

PMD's UnnecessaryConstructor, UnnecessaryImport, UnnecessaryModifier, UnnecessaryReturn, and similar rules catch narrow mechanical cases of unnecessary code elements, but cannot detect speculative abstractions, over-engineered frameworks, or unused features — the core of YAGNI violations.

PMD's LoosePackageCoupling can enforce that classes from specified package hierarchies are not referenced outside permitted boundaries, approximating a unidirectional dependency rule — but requires explicit package configuration and does not auto-detect layer violations without it.

PMD has no rule that detects whether concerns are properly separated into architectural layers (presentation, business logic, data). This requires semantic/architectural judgment that static pattern rules cannot provide.

PMD's LoosePackageCoupling can be configured to prevent inner-layer packages from importing outer-layer packages, enforcing the Clean Architecture rule that inner circles know nothing of outer ones — but only when packages and allowed classes are explicitly configured; no automatic layer detection.
Roslyn has no rule that detects redundant or stale comments; comment quality requires semantic/contextual judgement that static analysis does not provide.
Roslyn performs static analysis only; test coverage for new/changed code is a runtime/CI metric with no corresponding Roslyn rule.

CA1505 flags methods/types with a low maintainability index (derived from cyclomatic complexity + volume), which is a proxy for overly large/complex functions that may violate SRP, but it is not enabled by default and does not directly detect responsibility cohesion.
Roslyn has no rule that evaluates whether a file or type has a single responsibility; SRP at the file level is a design-level concern beyond static pattern matching.
Roslyn has no rule governing test file structure conventions (e.g. Arrange/Act/Assert layout, one class per file for tests, naming of test classes); this is a human/LLM review concern.

source_label: IDE0035 — Remove unreachable code; IDE0051/IDE0052 — Remove unused private members; IDE0060 — Remove unused parameter
rationale: IDE0035 detects unreachable executable code; IDE0051/IDE0052 detect unused private members; IDE0060 detects unused parameters — covering several dead-code patterns. However, IDE0035 does not run on build by default (IDE-only), publicly-accessible dead code is not covered, and logic-level dead paths are missed.
Changelog maintenance is a project/process concern entirely outside the scope of static code analysis; Roslyn has no rule for it.
Explicit test coverage metrics are a runtime/CI concern; Roslyn performs static analysis only and has no rule enforcing test coverage thresholds.
Roslyn has no rule that detects whether tests exercise requirements vs. internal implementation details; this requires semantic/contextual judgement about test design intent.

source_label: CA1507 – Use nameof in place of string
rationale: CA1507 flags string literals that duplicate a symbol name where nameof should be used, covering one narrow class of duplicated literals. No rule covers the general case of repeated magic numbers or arbitrary duplicated constants across a codebase. CA1507 is also only a suggestion (not a warning) by default.
No Roslyn rule targets semantic ambiguity or normalization of data/code representations; this is a design-level concern requiring contextual judgement beyond static syntax/type analysis.
Roslyn has no rule that identifies heuristic logic and suggests deterministic replacements; this requires deep semantic understanding of algorithm intent.

CA1508 detects dead conditional code via dataflow analysis (conditions that always evaluate true/false), which is a mechanically detectable subset of brittle patterns. However, brittle patterns in general (fragile string matching, hardcoded assumptions, etc.) are not covered by any Roslyn rule. CA1508 is not enabled by default.
No Roslyn rule distinguishes over-specific implementations from appropriate generalizations; this requires judgement about design intent and anticipated variation.

CA1505 (Avoid unmaintainable code) uses a composite maintainability index (lines of code + cyclomatic complexity + program volume) that can flag oversized types, but it is not a direct member-count or line-count threshold for Large Class and is not enabled by default. No rule directly measures class size in isolation.
Roslyn has no documented rule that detects Primitive Obsession (overuse of primitives instead of domain/value types). No CA rule covers this refactoring smell.
Roslyn has no rule that detects groups of fields/parameters that always appear together and should be extracted into an object; this requires cross-member semantic analysis beyond Roslyn's rule set.

source_label: IDE0010 – Add missing cases to switch statement
rationale: IDE0010 only flags incomplete switch statements (missing enum cases/default) and IDE0066 nudges toward switch expressions — neither detects the design smell of using switch/type-dispatch instead of polymorphism.

Roslyn can flag unused private fields (IDE0051/IDE0052) but has no rule that detects fields used only under certain object states (the Temporary Field smell), which requires understanding of object lifecycle.
Roslyn has no rule detecting subclasses that ignore or throw on inherited members; CA1501 only flags overly deep hierarchies, not semantic misuse of inheritance.
Detecting two classes that do the same thing but expose different interfaces requires cross-class semantic comparison that no Roslyn rule performs.
Divergent Change (a class changed for multiple unrelated reasons) is a class-level cohesion smell requiring semantic and historical analysis; Roslyn has no rule for it.
Shotgun Surgery (one change requires edits scattered across many classes) is a coupling smell detectable only through change-impact analysis; no Roslyn rule addresses it.

Detecting two mirrored inheritance hierarchies that must grow in tandem requires cross-hierarchy structural pattern recognition that Roslyn's rule set does not provide; CA1501 only measures depth, not parallelism.

CA1812 flags internal classes that are never instantiated (a subset of lazy-class cases), but is disabled by default, scoped to internal types only, and does not cover classes that exist but do negligible work.
No Roslyn/CA rule detects classes that contain only public data members (properties/fields) with no meaningful behaviour; determining 'anemic' intent requires semantic judgement.

CA1040 (empty interface) and CA1052 (static-only class not marked static) each catch narrow syntactic sub-cases of speculative generality, but neither is enabled by default and the general pattern—unused abstract methods, unnecessary parameters, over-engineered hierarchies—has no Roslyn rule.
No Roslyn or .NET CA rule targets excessive, redundant, or explanatory comments; comment quality is a semantic/contextual judgement with no static detector.

No Roslyn/CA rule measures whether a method uses data from another class more than its own; detecting cross-class data affinity requires cross-symbol semantic analysis not performed by any standard rule.

CA1506 (Avoid excessive class coupling) measures fan-out type-reference count and fires when a threshold is exceeded, acting as a structural proxy; however it is disabled by default, uses a high default threshold (95 for types), and does not capture the bidirectional mutual-dependency pattern that defines inappropriate intimacy.
No Roslyn/CA rule detects chained method/property access sequences; identifying this pattern reliably requires heuristics or AST chain-length analysis absent from the standard rule set.

No Roslyn/CA rule detects classes that do nothing except delegate every call to another object; recognising delegation-only classes requires cross-method semantic analysis not present in any standard rule.

Roslyn has proxy metrics (CA1502 cyclomatic complexity, CA1505 maintainability index, CA1506 class coupling) but none directly detects the God Object / Blob pattern — a class accumulating too many unrelated responsibilities. These metric rules are also off by default.
Anemic Domain Model (data classes with no domain logic) requires semantic understanding of whether business logic belongs in the entity; no Roslyn rule targets this architectural anti-pattern.
Poltergeist (short-lived class that merely delegates to another) requires understanding call graphs and object lifetimes; no Roslyn rule targets this pattern.

CA1501 flags types five or more levels deep in the inheritance hierarchy (off by default), which is a proxy for the Yo-Yo Problem's excessive hierarchy depth, but it does not detect the navigation-difficulty aspect or horizontal scatter of logic across many layers.
Sequential Coupling (methods must be called in a specific order) requires understanding implicit state contracts between calls; no Roslyn rule detects this temporal coupling pattern.

source_label: CA1040 – Avoid empty interfaces (does not cover constants-only interfaces)
rationale: CA1040 flags empty interfaces but not constants-only interfaces (which have members — the constants — and thus are not empty). No Roslyn rule targets the Constant Interface anti-pattern specifically.
No Roslyn rule measures the number of members declared in an interface or flags interfaces with too many members; interface bloat detection is absent from the standard rule set.
No Roslyn rule detects the Singleton pattern, let alone its overuse; whether a singleton is appropriate requires architectural judgement that static analysis does not encode.

CA1502 measures cyclomatic complexity and flags methods exceeding a threshold (default 25), which serves as a proxy for combinatorial explosion in branching, but it counts linear paths rather than the exponential input-space combination count that defines this antipattern, so many true instances are missed.
Tramp data (passing parameters through layers that do not use them) requires interprocedural dataflow and architectural-layer analysis; no standard Roslyn rule detects this pattern.

CA1502 (cyclomatic complexity) and CA1505 (maintainability index) are metric proxies that correlate with spaghetti code, but neither detects the full structural disorder that defines the antipattern, and both rules are disabled by default.
No built-in Roslyn/NetAnalyzers rule detects duplicate or copy-pasted code blocks; clone detection is not part of the standard CA or IDE rule set.
Golden Hammer (over-applying a single familiar solution/pattern) is a design-intent antipattern requiring contextual judgement; no Roslyn rule addresses it.

IDE0035 (unreachable code), IDE0051 (unused private members), CA1823 (unused private fields), and CA1508 (dead conditional code) together catch a subset of lava-flow symptoms—locally dead code—but cannot detect intentionally-kept obsolete logic that is still syntactically reachable or architecturally vestigial.

CA1802 flags static readonly fields that could be const, encouraging named constants over certain runtime-initialized values, but does not flag inline magic number or string literals embedded directly in logic (the primary hard-coding smell); that pattern has no standard Roslyn rule.

Action at a Distance (behavior altered through shared/global mutable state at a non-obvious call site) requires whole-program dataflow and aliasing analysis; no standard Roslyn rule targets this pattern.
Roslyn has no rule detecting boolean flag arguments that bifurcate method behavior; this requires semantic understanding of what the parameter controls, beyond syntactic type-checking.

Hidden Dependency (e.g. grabbing singletons or global state inside a method instead of injecting them) is a semantic/design smell requiring contextual understanding; no Roslyn rule targets this pattern.
Premature Optimization is a judgment call about whether a complexity trade-off is justified at the current stage; no static rule can determine intent or necessity, and Roslyn has no such rule.
Inner-Platform Effect (re-implementing framework/language features inside the codebase) requires understanding architectural intent and what the platform already provides; beyond Roslyn's rule set.
Big Ball of Mud is a whole-system architectural anti-pattern (lack of structure, uncontrolled growth); Roslyn operates at file/symbol level and has no rule for this systemic property.
Stovepipe System (isolated subsystems with no shared abstractions) is an architectural-level integration pattern; Roslyn has no rule for cross-subsystem coupling or lack of shared interfaces.
Database-as-IPC (using DB tables/queues as a message bus between services) is an architectural deployment pattern requiring knowledge of system topology; no Roslyn rule can detect this.
Vendor Lock-in (tight coupling to a specific vendor's APIs/services) requires reasoning about abstractions and replaceability across the codebase; no Roslyn rule targets this architectural concern.

SRP requires understanding a class's conceptual responsibility boundaries; no Roslyn rule captures this. CA1502 (cyclomatic complexity) and CA1505 (maintainability index) are crude proxies measuring method complexity, not cohesion of responsibilities.
OCP (open for extension, closed for modification) requires understanding intent and future change axes; no Roslyn rule models this concept.
LSP violations require semantic reasoning about behavioral contracts of subtypes; Roslyn has no rule that checks whether a subclass preserves its superclass's invariants or postconditions.

ISP (no client forced to depend on methods it doesn't use) requires understanding usage patterns across callers; Roslyn has no rule for bloated interfaces. CA1040 only flags empty interfaces, the opposite problem.
DIP (depend on abstractions, not concretions) requires reasoning about whether a dependency is an abstraction or a concrete type in context; no Roslyn rule checks this design principle.
'Program to an interface' is a design guideline requiring contextual judgement about whether a field/parameter should use an interface type; no Roslyn rule enforces this.

CA1501 flags excessive inheritance depth (≥5 levels by default) but does not detect the principle of preferring composition over inheritance; a 2-level inheritance hierarchy that should be composition is invisible to CA1501.
'Encapsulate what varies' is a design heuristic requiring understanding of change axes; no Roslyn rule captures this concept.

CA1506 (Avoid excessive class coupling) flags types/methods with too many unique type references, catching extreme tight-coupling cases, but cannot detect whether coupling is loose by design (e.g. via interfaces/DI) — it is a raw count metric, not a GoF design-intent check. Also disabled by default.
Beck's 'Passes the tests' is a runtime/test-suite property; Roslyn performs static analysis on source AST and has no rules that verify test suite coverage or correctness.

Roslyn naming rules enforce .NET casing conventions (PascalCase, camelCase, Hungarian-prefix avoidance) but cannot judge whether a name actually reveals the intention of the code — that requires semantic/domain understanding beyond mechanical pattern matching.

Roslyn has no duplication-detection rule; DRY violations (copy-pasted logic, repeated magic literals beyond single-occurrence checks) require structural comparison across code blocks that Roslyn's per-symbol rule model does not perform.

YAGNI (no unused/speculative abstractions) requires reasoning about design intent; Roslyn can flag unused private members (IDE0051) but not whether a public API or abstraction is premature — that is a design judgement call.

Clean Architecture's Dependency Rule requires knowledge of which namespace/assembly belongs to which layer; Roslyn has no built-in rule encoding Clean Architecture layer topology or enforcing dependency direction across layers.

Separating concerns into layers (UI/Application/Domain/Infrastructure) is an architectural constraint that cannot be inferred from AST patterns alone; Roslyn has no rule that identifies or enforces concern separation across project structure.

The constraint that inner layers must not reference outer-layer types requires layer identity metadata (which namespace = which layer); Roslyn's standard ruleset has no such rule, as this is an architectural convention, not a syntactic pattern.

The Boy Scout Rule ('leave code better than you found it') is a practice principle requiring contextual judgement about prior state and improvement — not a mechanical pattern any static analyzer can detect. Roslyn has no such rule.

Whether ports are defined as interfaces is an architectural design constraint. Roslyn has no rule enforcing that domain boundary types be interfaces; CA1040 discourages empty interfaces but does not enforce port semantics.

Verifying that adapter classes implement the correct port interfaces requires knowledge of the hexagonal layer assignment of each type — architectural context Roslyn does not have. No Roslyn rule covers this.

Whether the hexagonal core is 'pure' (free of infrastructure/framework imports) requires layer-aware dependency analysis. Roslyn has no built-in rule for namespace/layer purity. Third-party tools (e.g. NsDepCop, ArchUnitNET) address this but are not Roslyn out-of-the-box.

Whether driving-side adapters (controllers, CLI, tests) are correctly placed and wired to primary ports is an architectural concern requiring knowledge of the project's hexagonal role assignment. No Roslyn rule exists for this.

Whether driven-side adapters (DB, messaging, external APIs) implement secondary ports correctly is an architectural judgement. Roslyn has no rule that identifies or validates driven-adapter structure.

Testability by design (core injectable without infrastructure) is a design-level property, not a syntactic pattern. Roslyn cannot detect whether the architecture allows seam-based testing. No applicable rule exists.

Enforcing inward-only dependency direction across hexagonal layers requires cross-project layer-graph analysis. Roslyn's built-in rules have no concept of architectural layers; this requires external tools like ArchUnitNET or NsDepCop.

Reuse/Release Equivalence Principle (REP) is a package-design principle about grouping classes that are reused together into a releasable unit; Roslyn has no rule that checks whether assemblies/namespaces satisfy REP — this requires architectural judgement across project/package boundaries.
Common Closure Principle (CCP) requires that classes changed for the same reason be co-located in the same component; no Roslyn rule reasons about co-change cohesion across class/namespace groupings.

Acyclic Dependencies Principle (ADP) requires no cycles in the component (assembly/project) dependency graph; Roslyn analyzes within a compilation, not across assembly dependency graphs, and has no rule detecting inter-assembly cycles. CA1506 counts class-level coupling but does not detect cyclic dependency graphs between components.

Stable Dependencies Principle (SDP) requires that a component depend only on components more stable than itself; Roslyn has no rule that computes or compares component stability metrics (fan-in/fan-out ratios) across assembly boundaries.
Stable Abstractions Principle (SAP) requires that stable components be abstract and instable components be concrete; Roslyn has no rule correlating assembly-level abstractness with stability metrics.
DDD Bounded Context is a strategic design concept about domain model scope and team/service boundaries; this is a sociotechnical architectural concern with no representation in Roslyn's rule set.

Ubiquitous Language violations (e.g., naming divergence from domain vocabulary) require domain knowledge to detect; Roslyn has no rules about domain-aligned naming conventions or terminology consistency.

Domain Model integrity (e.g., anemic models, missing invariants, domain logic leaking to application layer) requires semantic understanding of DDD patterns; Roslyn has no rules for detecting domain model violations.
DDD Repository pattern conformance requires architectural/semantic judgement; Roslyn has no rule for DDD Repository conventions.
DDD Service Layer conformance requires architectural judgement about layering and responsibility; Roslyn has no rule covering DDD service layer conventions.

source_label: CA1051 — Do not declare visible instance fields
rationale: CA1051 flags publicly visible instance fields and recommends wrapping them in properties, covering the most mechanical form of 'Encapsulate Variable'; however it does not cover collection encapsulation (returning mutable internal collections) or record-level encapsulation decisions, and is disabled by default in .NET 10.

source_label: CA1051 — Do not declare visible instance fields
rationale: CA1051 catches the most common form of indecent exposure (public instance fields), but detecting over-broad visibility on methods, properties, or types that are never used outside their assembly requires whole-program analysis not performed by Roslyn's standard rules.
Tell Don't Ask is a semantic/design-intent smell (callers query state then decide instead of delegating to the object); no Roslyn rule detects this pattern.
Replace Subclass with Delegate is a refactoring smell requiring semantic understanding of inheritance vs. composition trade-offs; no Roslyn rule covers this.
Collapse Hierarchy requires understanding the whole inheritance tree and usage patterns; no Roslyn rule detects this.
Refused Bequest (subclass ignores or throws on inherited members) requires cross-class semantic analysis of member usage; no standard Roslyn rule detects this smell.
Roslyn's IDE1006 naming rules enforce casing/prefix/suffix conventions but cannot determine whether an identifier name reveals its intent — that requires semantic/contextual judgement.
No Roslyn rule checks the part-of-speech (noun vs. verb) of class or method identifiers; all naming rules only enforce surface patterns like casing, prefix, or suffix.

No Roslyn rule detects inconsistent vocabulary (e.g., both 'fetch' and 'get' used for the same concept); CA1704 checks spelling against a dictionary but does not enforce cross-symbol vocabulary consistency.

source_label: CA1720: Identifiers should not contain type names
rationale: CA1720 catches the specific case of type names embedded in identifiers (e.g., naming a parameter 'int32'), which is one narrow form of disinformation, but does not detect general misleading names.
No Roslyn rule measures or enforces a single level of abstraction within a function body; this is a design principle requiring contextual/semantic analysis.
No Roslyn rule enforces Command-Query Separation (methods either return a value or mutate state, but not both); this requires semantic understanding of method intent.

CA1021 (Avoid out parameters) and CA1045 (Do not pass types by reference) flag structural side-effect proxies (out/ref parameters), but neither detects general side effects such as global state mutation, I/O, or hidden state changes.
No Roslyn rule flags boolean parameters specifically as flag arguments that should be decomposed into separate methods; CA1021 targets out parameters only, not boolean control-flow parameters.
Roslyn has no rule that detects long parameter lists and suggests introducing a parameter object; no such pattern is covered by any documented CA or IDE rule.

CA1502 flags high cyclomatic complexity (a proxy for deep nesting) but does not specifically detect nested-conditional vs guard-clause patterns; the refactoring opportunity requires semantic/structural judgement Roslyn does not encode, and CA1502 is disabled by default.
No Roslyn rule detects type-dispatching switch/if-else chains and recommends replacing them with a polymorphic design; this is a design-level refactoring that requires contextual judgement.
IDE0120/IDE0121 simplify already-written LINQ expressions but no Roslyn rule flags imperative foreach loops as candidates for replacement with LINQ/pipeline style.
Roslyn has no rule that detects slow tests or tests that call external systems, databases, or sleep APIs in a way that flags them as violating the 'Fast' FIRST property.
Roslyn has no rule that detects inter-test dependencies (shared static state, ordering assumptions) to flag violations of the 'Independent' FIRST property.
Roslyn has no rule that detects environment-sensitive code paths (network calls, date/time dependencies, file system reliance) within tests to flag 'Repeatable' violations.

Roslyn has no rule that checks whether test methods contain assertions or produce a clear pass/fail signal; the 'Self-validating' FIRST property requires semantic analysis of test intent beyond Roslyn's documented rules.
Timely refers to the TDD practice of writing tests before production code; this is a process/workflow concept with no code-level pattern Roslyn can detect.
Red-Green-Refactor is a TDD cycle discipline (write failing test, make it pass, refactor); it is a development workflow, not a static code pattern detectable by Roslyn.
Fake It Till You Make It is a TDD strategy for incrementally evolving implementations; it describes intent and process, not a syntactic pattern Roslyn can flag.
Triangulation is a TDD technique of adding multiple test cases to drive generalization; it is a test-authoring strategy, not a detectable code structure.
Assert First is a TDD practice of writing assertions before test setup/action; test authoring order is not observable in the final code by a static analyzer.
Identifying 'legacy code' as code lacking tests requires semantic coverage analysis across the whole codebase, which is outside Roslyn's static analysis rule set; no Roslyn rule addresses this.
Characterization Tests are tests written to document existing behavior of legacy code; recognizing this intent is a semantic/contextual judgment Roslyn cannot make.
Sprout Method/Class is a refactoring pattern for adding behavior to legacy code via new methods/classes; detecting the absence of this practice is a design-level judgment, not a Roslyn rule.
Wrap Method/Class is a refactoring pattern suggesting adding a decorator or wrapper layer; no Roslyn rule detects the absence of such a pattern.

Extract Interface is a refactoring suggestion (program to an interface rather than a concrete class); Roslyn has no rule that flags classes missing an extracted interface. CA1040 penalizes empty interfaces, which is the inverse concern.
Parameterize Constructor/Method is a refactoring pattern to replace hard-coded dependencies with injected parameters; no standard Roslyn rule detects this class of issue.

C# is statically typed by default; 'gradual typing' as a concept (progressively adding type annotations) does not map to C# idioms, and Roslyn has no rule promoting or detecting opportunities for gradual typing.
Type narrowing (guards that refine a union/unknown type) is a JS/TS pattern; C# is statically typed and Roslyn has no rule flagging missed narrowing opportunities.
Prototype pollution is a JavaScript-specific vulnerability exploiting JS prototype chains; C# has no prototype chain, so this construct does not exist in the language.
Global scope pollution is a JavaScript-specific issue arising from implicit global variable declaration; C# uses mandatory namespaces and type-scoped members, so the construct does not exist.
Passing HTTP request context through domain models is an architectural anti-pattern; detecting it requires semantic understanding of layer boundaries that no standard Roslyn rule addresses.
C# has no eval() keyword; analogues are CSharpScript, reflection-based invocation, and dynamic compilation. No standard Roslyn CA rule targets these patterns as dynamic execution risks.

Monkey patching (runtime method replacement without prior declaration) is not a supported language construct in C#; the language's static type system and CLR prohibit it without unsupported hacks, so there is nothing for Roslyn to detect.
C# has a 'dynamic' keyword enabling duck-typed calls at runtime, but no Roslyn CA rule audits or enforces duck typing discipline or discourages use of dynamic.
Treating lint warnings as build failures is a project-level CI/MSBuild configuration concern (TreatWarningsAsErrors), not a code pattern that Roslyn static analysis rules can detect in source.
Test coverage enforcement is a build/CI tooling concern (coverlet, ReportGenerator thresholds), not something detectable by Roslyn source-code analysis rules.
Pinning and validating external API contracts (e.g., NuGet version pinning, schema validation) is an architectural/dependency management concern; no Roslyn CA rule addresses this.

source_label: CA1727 - Use PascalCase for named placeholders
rationale: CA1727 enforces PascalCase naming on ILogger structured log placeholders (a structured logging convention), but does not detect use of string concatenation instead of structured logging templates, so it covers only a narrow subset of the structured-logging discipline.
No Roslyn CA rule checks for the presence or propagation of correlation IDs (e.g., ActivityId, TraceId) in logging calls; this requires semantic/contextual judgment.
Roslyn has no rule that checks whether a service implements or registers health check / readiness endpoints; this is an architectural concern requiring runtime knowledge.
Whether an alert fires on symptoms vs. causes is a monitoring/operational design decision; no Roslyn rule addresses this.
Postel's Law (be liberal in what you accept, conservative in what you send) is a semantic API design principle; no Roslyn rule exists for it.
Semantic versioning compliance is a release/packaging policy; Roslyn has no rule that validates SemVer usage or enforcement.
Detecting API-level breaking changes requires diffing against a prior version; Roslyn has no built-in backwards-compatibility diff rule.

Explicit error contracts (documenting which exceptions a method throws and under what conditions) require semantic reasoning; CA1065 only flags exceptions thrown in inherently wrong locations, not missing or implicit contracts, and no rule requires <exception> XML doc coverage.

Pinning transitive NuGet dependencies is a project-file/packaging concern; no shipping Roslyn analyzer rule enforces this (a GitHub issue in roslyn-analyzers #7696 proposed such detection but it is not implemented).
Minimising the dependency footprint is an architectural review concern; no Roslyn quality rule detects unused or excessive NuGet dependencies.
Dependency auditing (checking for outdated/vulnerable packages) is a supply-chain/tooling concern handled by `dotnet list package --vulnerable` or NuGet audit tooling, not a Roslyn analyzer rule.

Single-responsibility at the package/project level is an architectural boundary decision that Roslyn has no rule to enforce; it requires human or architectural judgement about cohesion across project files.

IDE0044 flags private fields that are never reassigned after initialization and should be marked `readonly`; IDE0250/IDE0251 flag structs and struct members that can be `readonly`. These cover the mechanical `readonly` field/struct pattern but miss mutable properties, collections, record mutation, or higher-level architectural immutability of types.

Share-nothing architecture is a distributed-systems design principle about avoiding shared mutable state across components/services; Roslyn has no rule that detects violations of this architectural pattern.
Idempotency is a semantic/behavioral property (repeated invocations produce the same result) that cannot be verified by static pattern matching; Roslyn has no rule targeting it.

Roslyn has no rule that detects implicit ad-hoc state management (boolean/enum flags scattered across a class) and suggests replacing it with an explicit state machine; this requires semantic/design judgement.

CA2002 flags locking on weak-identity objects (a narrow threading sub-case), but there is no Roslyn rule covering the broader principle of not assuming exclusive ownership over shared or global resources.
Roslyn has no rule that distinguishes comments explaining decisions/rationale ('why') from comments merely restating mechanics ('what'); comment quality and intent are beyond static analysis.

Roslyn has CS1591 for missing XML doc comments, but no rule enforces that documentation stays close to or in sync with the code it describes; doc proximity/currency is a semantic concern beyond syntactic checks.

Executable/runnable documentation (doctest-style) has no native construct in C# and Roslyn has no rule for it; XML doc comments are not executable and no standard analyzer verifies code samples compile or run.

source_label: CA1051: Do not declare visible instance fields
rationale: Roslyn has several rules that partially enforce minimum visibility (CA1051 flags public instance fields, CA2119 flags overridable public methods on internal interfaces, CA1822 suggests static over instance, CA1812 flags uninstantiated internal classes), but none comprehensively enforces 'use the narrowest access modifier possible' across all member kinds.
Defence in depth is an architectural principle requiring layered security checks; no Roslyn rule models or enforces this pattern — it requires semantic understanding of the overall system design.

source_label: CA1031: Do not catch general exception types
rationale: CA1031 ('Do not catch general exception types') flags broad catch blocks that can silently swallow failures, which is a common fail-insecure anti-pattern; however, it does not reason about whether the resulting code path is actually secure, and the rule is not enabled by default in .NET 10.

SonarC# has S125 (commented-out code), S1135 (TODO tags), and S1185 (useless overrides), but no rule detects semantically redundant or trivial comments (e.g. comments that merely restate the code they annotate); that requires human/LLM judgement.

SonarQube exposes coverage as a quality-gate metric derived from external test-runner data, but no SonarC# rule statically detects whether new or changed production code lacks corresponding unit tests.

source_label: csharpsquid:S3776 – Cognitive Complexity of methods should not be too high
rationale: Rules S3776 (cognitive complexity), S138 (too many lines), S1541 (cyclomatic complexity), and S107 (too many parameters) proxy for functions doing too much, but they use size/complexity thresholds rather than detecting semantic single-responsibility violations, so the general SRP concept is only partially covered.
No SonarC# rule checks whether a file contains multiple unrelated classes or violates file-level single-responsibility; this is a semantic/architectural concern beyond static pattern matching.

source_label: csharpsquid:S2187 – Test classes should contain test methods
rationale: S2187 (test class should contain test methods) enforces one structural invariant, but broader test-file structure concerns (AAA pattern, naming conventions, one-class-per-file for tests) are not covered by any standard SonarC# rule.

source_label: csharpsquid:S1144 – Unused private types or members should be removed
rationale: SonarC# provides dedicated rules covering the main dead-code categories: S1144 (unused private types and members), S1481 (unused local variables), S1068 (unused private fields), and S1854 (dead stores); together these deterministically flag the standard dead-code patterns.
Whether a CHANGELOG file was updated is a process/workflow concern; no SonarC# rule (nor any static analyzer) can detect this from source code alone.

SonarQube treats coverage as an external metric fed from test-runner reports, not as a rule; no SonarC# rule statically detects whether production code is covered by tests. S2699 (tests must have assertions) is the closest proxy but measures test quality, not production-code coverage.
SonarC# has no rule that detects whether tests exercise external requirements vs internal implementation details; this requires semantic/contextual judgment beyond static pattern matching.

source_label: S1192 – String literals should not be duplicated
rationale: S1192 flags duplicated string literals (default threshold: 3+ occurrences) and S109 flags magic numbers, together covering the class of duplicated literal/constant issues deterministically.
Removing ambiguity through data/code normalization is a semantic design concern requiring contextual judgment; SonarC# has no rule targeting this class of issue.
Replacing heuristics with deterministic logic is a semantic/algorithmic design concern that static analysis cannot assess; SonarC# has no rule for this.

source_label: S109 – Magic numbers should not be used
rationale: S109 (magic numbers) and S1192 (duplicated string literals) catch a narrow syntactic subset of brittleness (hard-coded literals), but the broader class of brittle patterns (fragile ordering assumptions, environment coupling, etc.) is not covered.

Generalization vs over-specificity is a design-level semantic judgment (e.g., choosing a concrete type vs an abstraction, hardcoded special-cases vs general logic); SonarC# has no rule detecting this.

SonarC# lacks a dedicated Large Class rule — S1448 (classes should not have too many methods) is an open, unimplemented idea for C# as of 2025. Proxy coverage exists via S104 (file too many lines) and S138 (method too many lines), but neither directly targets class-level bloat.

Primitive Obsession (using raw primitives where a value object should be introduced) is a semantic design smell; SonarC# has no rule that detects it — no RSPEC rule for this pattern exists in the C# analyzer.

No SonarC# rule detects groups of fields or parameters that recurrently appear together and should be extracted into a dedicated class; this requires cross-site semantic analysis beyond static pattern matching.

source_label: RSPEC-1301: "switch" statements should have at least 3 "case" clauses
rationale: S1301 flags switch statements with fewer than 3 cases (preferring if/else), a syntactic proxy for the Switch Statements smell, but does not detect the deeper OO smell of switching on a type tag that should be replaced with polymorphism.
No SonarC# rule targets fields that are only meaningful under certain conditions (Temporary Field smell); null-check rules address different concerns and do not detect this OO anti-pattern.

source_label: RSPEC-1185: Overriding members should do more than simply call the same member in the base class
rationale: S1185 catches the narrow case where an override does nothing but call the base method (trivial passthrough), a weak proxy for Refused Bequest, but misses the general case where a subclass silently ignores large parts of its inherited interface.

No SonarC# rule detects two classes performing equivalent roles but exposing different interfaces; recognising this requires cross-class semantic understanding that static pattern matching cannot provide.

Divergent Change is a change-history/SRP smell. S1200 (class coupled to too many others) is a loosely related structural proxy but does not detect Divergent Change directly; the smell requires contextual/semantic judgment.
Shotgun Surgery requires cross-class change-coupling analysis that SonarC# does not perform; no documented rule targets this smell.
Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies require detecting that two class hierarchies must grow in lockstep; no SonarC# rule performs this structural cross-hierarchy analysis.
No csharpsquid rule targets Lazy Class (a class doing too little to justify its existence). The third-party Fowler-smell plugin that detects this is Java-only and not part of SonarC#.

No csharpsquid rule targets Data Class (a class with only fields/getters/setters and no real behaviour). S1448 (too many methods) was requested as a rule idea for C# dotnet but never implemented; no rule detects the inverse (too few meaningful methods / pure data holder).

No csharpsquid rule detects Speculative Generality (unused abstractions, interfaces, or parameters added for hypothetical future use). S1144 catches unused private members but does not cover the broader architectural smell of over-designed abstractions.

source_label: csharpsquid:S125 – Sections of code should not be commented out
rationale: csharpsquid:S125 detects commented-out code sections — one manifestation of over-reliance on comments — and is active in the default Sonar Way profile. It does not detect the broader smell of using explanatory prose comments to compensate for unclear code (where rename/extract-method would suffice), so coverage is a narrow subset of the full smell class.
No csharpsquid rule detects Feature Envy (a method more interested in another class's data than its own). SonarSource has no rule for this coupling smell in any language including C#.

source_label: SonarSource community: no rule for inappropriate intimacy (confirmed by Sonar staff)
rationale: SonarSource explicitly confirmed in their community forum that they have no rule for Inappropriate Intimacy in any language, noting the heuristic would be 'tricky to get right'. S1200 counts total class dependencies but does not identify bidirectional or private-access intimacy between specific class pairs.

No csharpsquid rule detects Message Chains (long chains of method calls violating the Law of Demeter). The Fowler-smell plugin that detects this exists but is Java-only and third-party; it is not part of SonarC#.

No csharpsquid rule detects Middle Man (a class that does nothing but delegate all work to another). No evidence of any proxy/delegation pass-through detection rule in SonarC#'s standard rule set was found.

source_label: csharpsquid:S1200 – Classes should not be coupled to too many other classes
rationale: SonarC# has S1200 ('Classes should not be coupled to too many other classes') which is a structural proxy for God Object/SRP violations via efferent coupling count, but S1448 ('Classes should not have too many methods') — the more direct God Object signal — is an open rule idea never implemented for C#.

Anemic Domain Model (domain objects with no behaviour, only data) requires semantic reasoning about the relationship between data and behaviour across the codebase; no SonarC# rule addresses this pattern.
Poltergeist (short-lived class that only passes information to another class) requires cross-class lifecycle and call-graph analysis; no SonarC# rule targets this anti-pattern.
Yo-Yo Problem (excessive inheritance depth / alternating abstraction) requires inheritance hierarchy analysis; no SonarC# rule for this anti-pattern was found.
Sequential Coupling (methods that must be called in a specific order) requires understanding temporal/state dependencies between method calls; no SonarC# rule addresses this.
Java has S1214 ('Constants should not be defined in interfaces') but no equivalent rule exists in SonarC#; C# interfaces can hold constants and the tool does not flag this anti-pattern.
No SonarC# rule limits the number of members in an interface; S4023 (empty interfaces) addresses the opposite problem and there is no 'too many members' rule for interfaces in the SonarC# ruleset.

Java has S6548 ('The Singleton design pattern should be used with care') but no C# equivalent exists in SonarC#; S3453 (classes with only private constructors) detects a structural pattern but is not a Singleton overuse detector.

No SonarC# rule targets the combinatorial explosion anti-pattern (boolean flag parameters creating exponential execution paths); S107 (too many parameters) is a distant proxy that does not address the combinatorial logic problem.
No SonarC# rule detects tramp data (parameters threaded through intermediary methods that do not use them); the pattern requires semantic data-flow analysis beyond what the rule set offers.

source_label: RSPEC-1541: Methods should not be too complex
