Minimal dependency footprint is an architectural/build concern about which libraries are included; cppcheck does not analyze build manifests or flag unnecessary #include dependencies at a semantic level.
Dependency auditing (checking for outdated/vulnerable packages) is a supply-chain/package-manager concern entirely outside cppcheck's scope; cppcheck has no rule for this.
Single-responsibility per package is an architectural design principle requiring semantic judgment; cppcheck performs no module-level cohesion analysis.

Cppcheck can suggest adding 'const' to variables that are never modified (constVariable check), covering local immutability hints, but it does not enforce immutability as a design principle across interfaces or data structures.
Share-nothing architecture is a distributed-systems design principle; cppcheck has no rule for detecting shared mutable state across service/process boundaries.
Idempotency is a semantic property of operations requiring domain-level reasoning; cppcheck has no rule for detecting non-idempotent operations.
Making state machines explicit is a design pattern principle; cppcheck does not analyse control-flow for implicit state machine anti-patterns.

Ownership assumptions about shared/global resources require semantic understanding of ownership conventions; cppcheck's memory-leak checks cover allocation/deallocation mechanics but not ownership-assumption violations at a design level.
Distinguishing decision-documenting comments from mechanic-explaining ones requires natural-language comprehension; cppcheck has no documentation quality rules.
Cppcheck has no rules for documentation proximity or quality; it performs static code analysis, not documentation coverage analysis.
Cppcheck does not check for executable/testable documentation; this requires semantic/tooling judgement beyond its scope.
Principle of least privilege is an architectural/design concern; cppcheck has no rules covering privilege scope or access-control design.
Defence in depth is a multi-layer security architecture principle; cppcheck performs single-file/single-pass static analysis with no concept of layered security controls.
Fail-secure behaviour requires semantic understanding of error paths and security intent; cppcheck has no rules for this class of issue.

Java has no eval() or dynamic script execution construct as a first-class language feature; the 'eval' pattern is a scripting-language concept absent from Java. SpotBugs has no documented rule for this.
Monkey patching (runtime modification of classes/methods) is not a standard Java pattern; Java's nominal type system does not support it, so there is no construct for SpotBugs to detect.
Duck typing is a dynamic-language concept. Java is statically typed with nominal typing, so 'duck typing discipline' does not exist as a construct in Java.
Whether a linter is wired as a build failure is a CI/build tooling policy, not a source-code pattern. SpotBugs has no rule to detect missing lint-as-build-failure configuration.
Test coverage enforcement is a build/CI policy concern. SpotBugs performs bytecode analysis and has no rule for detecting missing or insufficient test coverage configuration.
Pinning and validating external contracts is a dependency-management and architecture concern. SpotBugs has no documented rule for this.
SpotBugs has no documented rules for structured logging. The bug descriptions page contains no LOG/SLF/LG category rules.
Correlation ID propagation is an observability design pattern requiring semantic context understanding. SpotBugs has no documented rule detecting absent or inconsistent correlation ID usage.
SpotBugs has no detector for health check or readiness probe implementation patterns; this is an operational/architectural concern outside its scope.
SpotBugs has no detector for alerting strategy (symptoms vs. causes); this is an observability design concern outside its scope.
Postel's Law is a semantic API design principle; SpotBugs has no rule for input leniency or output strictness patterns.
Semantic versioning compliance is a release/packaging policy concern; SpotBugs has no rule for versioning conventions.
Backwards compatibility is an architectural/contract concern; SpotBugs has no detector for API breakage across versions.
Explicit error contracts are a design convention; SpotBugs detects some dropped exceptions but does not enforce contract completeness.
Pinning transitive dependencies is a build/package management concern; SpotBugs operates on compiled bytecode and has no dependency version pinning rules.
Minimal dependency footprint is an architectural/build policy concern; SpotBugs has no detector for unused or excessive dependencies.
SpotBugs has no rule for dependency auditing (checking third-party library versions or known vulnerabilities); this requires external tooling (OWASP Dependency-Check, Snyk, etc.).
SpotBugs has no rule enforcing single-responsibility or cohesion at the package level; package structure is an architectural concern beyond static bytecode analysis.

SpotBugs catches specific mutability exposures (EI_EXPOSE_REP, EI_EXPOSE_REP2, MS_EXPOSE_REP, JCIP_FIELD_ISNT_FINAL_IN_IMMUTABLE_CLASS, PA_PUBLIC_MUTABLE_OBJECT_ATTRIBUTE) but does not enforce general immutability design — only flags leaks of mutable references or missing @Immutable field finality.
Share-nothing architecture is an architectural/design principle; SpotBugs has no rule that enforces or detects violations of this pattern at the component or service boundary level.
Idempotency is a semantic/behavioral property of operations; SpotBugs performs bytecode-level pattern matching and has no rule for detecting non-idempotent method implementations.
Making state machines explicit is a design/refactoring principle; SpotBugs has no rule that detects implicit or ad-hoc state machine implementations and recommends formalizing them.

SpotBugs flags some shared/global resource ownership issues (EI_EXPOSE_REP for returning mutable internal state, PA_PUBLIC_MUTABLE_OBJECT_ATTRIBUTE for public mutable fields), but does not cover the general concept of incorrectly assuming ownership of shared/global resources in concurrent or distributed contexts.
Commenting quality (documenting decisions vs. mechanics) is a human/LLM judgment concern; SpotBugs does not analyze comment content or documentation style.
SpotBugs has no rules for detecting whether documentation is co-located with code or kept up to date; this is a human/process concern, not a mechanical code pattern.
Executable documentation (e.g. runnable examples, doctests) is a process/tooling choice — SpotBugs has no rules that detect the absence or staleness of executable docs.

SpotBugs flags publicly exposed fields (PA_PUBLIC_* rules, referencing SEI CERT OBJ01-J), which partially enforces least privilege at the field level, but does not cover broader privilege-minimisation concerns such as overly permissive method visibility, excessive API surface, or OS/runtime privilege.
Defence in depth is an architectural/layering principle; SpotBugs has no rules that evaluate whether multiple independent security controls are present or correctly layered.

Fail-secure (defaulting to denial on error) requires semantic reasoning about control-flow and error-handling intent; SpotBugs has no rules that check whether exception/error paths default to a secure state.
The Boy Scout Rule is a process/culture discipline (leave code better than you found it); no static analysis rule can determine whether a developer improved touched code. SonarC# has no such rule.

Whether 'ports' are defined as interfaces is an architectural intent requiring knowledge of which types are designated as ports. SonarC# has no standard rule that identifies hexagonal port/interface contracts out of the box.

Whether a class is an 'adapter' implementing a 'port' interface is a domain-intent label, not a syntactic pattern. SonarC# cannot distinguish an adapter from any other class that implements an interface.

Whether the hexagonal core is 'pure' (no infrastructure references) requires knowing which namespaces/assemblies are infrastructure. SonarC# has no out-of-the-box rule enforcing core-layer purity; the deprecated architecture-as-code feature required manual declaration of layers.
Identifying the 'driving side' (UI/test harness initiating calls through a port) requires architectural labeling of components. No SonarC# standard rule detects this.
Identifying the 'driven side' (repositories/messaging adapters called by the core via ports) requires architectural labeling. No SonarC# standard rule detects this.
Testability by design in hexagonal architecture (ability to swap adapters for fakes) is a structural design property requiring semantic understanding of design intent. No SonarC# rule detects this.

Whether dependencies point inward (toward the domain core) requires knowing which modules are core vs. outer rings. SonarC# has no built-in rule enforcing hexagonal dependency direction; the deprecated architecture-as-code feature required teams to manually declare the architecture first.

Reuse/Release Equivalence (REP) requires tracking which types are grouped into releasable units and verifying cohesion of release packages — a semantic/architectural judgment with no corresponding SonarC# rule.
Common Closure Principle (CCP) requires reasoning about which classes change together for the same reasons — a design-level judgment that SonarC# has no rule to detect.

source_label: SonarQube Server 2025.6 Design & Architecture Overview — Java only
rationale: SonarQube's cycle detection (Design & Architecture feature) is explicitly documented as Java-only; C# is not a supported language. RSPEC-1200 counts class-level dependencies but does not detect cycles. No SonarC# rule enforces the Acyclic Dependencies Principle.
Stable Dependencies Principle (SDP) requires computing per-component instability (Ce/(Ca+Ce)) and verifying dependency direction — no SonarC# rule computes or enforces this metric.
Stable Abstractions Principle (SAP) requires computing per-component abstractness (abstract types / total types) and correlating with instability — no SonarC# rule implements this.
Bounded Context is a DDD architectural concept about semantic boundaries between models; no SonarC# rule detects violations of bounded context isolation.
Ubiquitous Language consistency requires matching code identifiers against a domain glossary — inherently semantic and human/LLM-level; no SonarC# rule addresses this.
Domain Model integrity (rich vs. anemic, aggregate invariants, etc.) requires semantic understanding of business rules; no SonarC# rule detects domain model violations.

SonarC# has no rule that enforces DDD Repository pattern correctness (e.g., repositories confined to infrastructure, domain only seeing interfaces). The only known SonarQube DDD plugin targets Java, not C#.
SonarC# has no rule that enforces DDD Service Layer conventions (e.g., domain services vs application services separation). No architectural DDD enforcement exists in the standard C# ruleset.

source_label: csharpsquid:S1104 – Fields should not have public accessibility
rationale: S1104 ('Fields should not have public accessibility') and S2357 ('Fields should be private') directly and deterministically flag public instance fields, enforcing the encapsulate-variable principle as a mechanical syntactic check.

S1104/S2357 catch over-exposed fields and S2376 catches write-only properties (a specific exposure sub-case), but SonarC# has no rule that detects over-broad visibility on methods, types, or properties in general (e.g., public setter when internal/private would suffice, or public class that should be internal).

No SonarC# rule detects Tell Don't Ask violations (querying object state externally to make decisions rather than delegating behaviour to the object). This requires semantic/contextual analysis beyond static pattern matching.
No SonarC# rule identifies inheritance hierarchies that should be refactored to delegation/composition. This is a semantic design smell requiring contextual judgement.

S110 flags overly deep inheritance trees (depth metric) but does not detect a redundant intermediate class whose functionality is identical to its parent and should be collapsed. No dedicated Collapse Hierarchy rule exists in SonarC#.

No SonarC# rule specifically detects Refused Bequest (a subclass overriding inherited members only to stub or throw NotImplementedException). S1186 flags empty methods generically but does not target the inheritance-refusal pattern.
SonarC# naming rules (S100, S101, S2342, S3376) only enforce casing patterns and suffix conventions — none check whether identifiers semantically reveal intent or carry meaningful domain concepts.

source_label: S100 – Methods and properties should be named in camelCase
rationale: S100 enforces camelCase on method names and S101 enforces PascalCase on type names, but neither rule checks whether class names are nouns or method names are verbs — that requires semantic/linguistic analysis beyond regex-based pattern matching.

No SonarC# rule checks for conceptual consistency across names (e.g. using both 'fetch' and 'retrieve' for the same concept). This requires cross-symbol semantic reasoning not supported by any documented rule.

SonarC# has no rule that detects misleading or disinforming names (e.g. a list named 'accountList' that is not a List, or a name implying wrong behaviour). Avoiding disinformation is a semantic judgement with no mechanical proxy rule.

SonarC# has complexity rules (S3776 cognitive complexity, S138 method length, S107 parameter count) but none check that a function operates at a single level of abstraction — that is a structural/semantic concern requiring human or LLM review.

source_label: S2201 – Return values from functions without side effects should not be ignored
rationale: S2201 flags ignored return values of pure functions but does not enforce Command-Query Separation (i.e. it does not detect queries that mutate state or commands that return values as a general CQS violation). No documented CQS-specific rule exists for SonarC#.

source_label: S2696 – Instance members should not write to static fields
rationale: S2696 flags instance methods writing to static fields (a specific side-effect class), and S2201 catches ignoring pure-function return values; but the general principle 'no side effects' (unexpected mutation of any external state, shared objects, parameters, etc.) has no complete detector — only narrow proxy rules exist.

source_label: S2360 – Optional parameters should not be used
rationale: No SonarC# rule flags boolean flag parameters that control branching behaviour inside a method. S2360 flags optional parameters generally (not specifically boolean flags used as control-flow switches). Detecting the flag-argument anti-pattern requires semantic analysis of parameter usage inside the method body.

source_label: S107 – Methods should not have too many parameters
rationale: S107 flags methods exceeding a configurable parameter count (default 7), which is a proxy trigger for the Introduce Parameter Object smell, but it does not require or suggest the refactoring itself and misses cases where a long list is already partially grouped.

source_label: S1541 / S3776 – Cyclomatic/Cognitive Complexity of methods should not be too high
rationale: Cognitive complexity (S3776) and cyclomatic complexity (S1541) rules flag overly complex methods that often contain the nested conditionals guard clauses would replace, but the rules flag aggregate complexity rather than identifying the nested-conditional pattern specifically or recommending guard clauses.

No SonarC# rule was found that flags type-discriminating conditionals (if-chains or switch on type) and recommends replacing them with polymorphism; this is a semantic design refactoring requiring human or LLM judgment.

source_label: S3267 – Loops should be simplified with LINQ expressions
rationale: S3267 directly flags loops that can be rewritten as LINQ pipeline expressions, but covers only syntactically simple cases and has documented false negatives/positives for Span, IAsyncEnumerable, and by-ref struct scenarios, missing the general semantic form.

source_label: S2925 – Thread.Sleep should not be used in tests
rationale: S2925 is implemented for C# and detects explicit Thread.Sleep calls in tests (a common cause of slowness), but cannot detect slow tests caused by real I/O, database calls, HTTP requests, or other non-sleep delays.

No SonarC# rule was found that detects test-to-test dependencies, shared mutable state between tests, or ordering dependencies; test independence requires semantic reasoning about test lifecycle and state.

RSPEC-5977 (tests should use fixed data instead of randomized data) exists in Java but no evidence was found of a C# implementation; RSPEC-2245 is a security hotspot for insecure random usage, not test repeatability; no SonarC# rule covers non-deterministic inputs like DateTime.Now or environment variables in tests.
S2699 is implemented for C# and mechanically detects test methods (across NUnit, xUnit, MSTest) that contain no assertions, which is the defining failure mode for a non-self-validating test.

Timely (write tests before production code) is a TDD workflow heuristic — a process-level practice. Static analysis cannot determine whether tests were written before or after the code they cover; no SonarC# rule exists for this.

Red-Green-Refactor is a TDD development cycle (write failing test, make it pass, refactor). This is a developer workflow concept; static analysis cannot observe the order in which code was written or the test-failure state at commit time.

Fake It Till You Make It is a TDD implementation strategy (return hardcoded values first, then generalize). No static rule can distinguish intentionally hardcoded stub returns from production code; SonarC# has no such rule.
Triangulation is a TDD strategy for generalizing code by adding test cases. It is a cognitive/process heuristic with no detectable syntactic fingerprint; no SonarC# rule exists for it.

Assert First is a TDD design heuristic (write assertions before test setup/act steps). Static analysis cannot determine the intended composition order of test code; no SonarC# rule exists for this pattern.

Legacy code = code without tests is a definitional/conceptual heuristic. While SonarC# tracks coverage metrics, determining whether code qualifies as legacy due to missing tests requires semantic and project-context judgement beyond static rule matching.

Characterization Tests are tests written to document existing behaviour of legacy code. This is a testing strategy/intent classification; static analysis cannot distinguish characterization tests from regular regression tests based on code structure alone.

Sprout Method/Class is a refactoring technique for adding behaviour to hard-to-test legacy code via new methods/classes. It is an architectural design decision requiring contextual understanding; SonarC# has no rule to detect or enforce it.

Timely (writing tests before production code) is a TDD process/timing concern requiring commit-history analysis; PMD has no rule that can detect whether tests were written before or after production code.
Red-Green-Refactor cycle adherence is a development process metric, not a static code pattern; PMD cannot observe or reason about the sequence in which code was written.

Fake It Till You Make It (intentionally returning hardcoded values in the red phase) is a deliberate TDD strategy indistinguishable from accidental hardcoding; PMD has no rule targeting this as a TDD practice.
Triangulation (using multiple tests to drive generalization of production code) is a cross-test design pattern requiring semantic understanding of test intent; PMD has no rule for this.

Assert First (writing assertions before setup/exercise code in a test) is an ordering discipline within test methods; PMD's UnitTestShouldIncludeAssert only checks that assertions exist, not their position relative to setup code.
Detecting code lacking test coverage requires a coverage tool (JaCoCo, Cobertura); PMD is a static AST analyzer with no rule to flag production classes or methods that have no corresponding tests.
Characterization Tests (tests capturing existing legacy-code behavior) are semantically distinct from TDD tests only by intent and history; PMD has no rule to identify or enforce their presence.
Sprout Method/Class is a legacy-code refactoring pattern that requires understanding change context and code history; PMD has no rule to detect or encourage this technique.
Wrap Method/Class is a refactoring intent pattern requiring semantic understanding of design goals; PMD has no rule that identifies when a method or class should be wrapped.

Extract Interface is a refactoring opportunity requiring judgement about abstraction goals; PMD rules like GodClass or ExcessivePublicCount hint at large classes but do not identify missing interface extractions.
Parameterizing hardcoded values in constructors/methods requires semantic judgement; PMD has no rule that detects when a literal should be lifted to a parameter.
Gradual typing is a concept from dynamically-typed or optional-type languages (Python, TypeScript); Java is statically typed and the construct does not apply.

PMD has AvoidInstanceofChecksInCatchClause (flags instanceof in catch blocks) and SimplifyConditional (flags redundant null check before instanceof), but these cover only narrow edge cases, not the general type-narrowing review concern.
Prototype pollution is a JavaScript-specific attack surface; the construct does not exist in Java.
Global scope pollution via implicit globals is a JavaScript construct; Java has no equivalent since all declarations are scoped to classes/packages.
No request context leaking into models is an architectural/layering concern requiring semantic understanding of the codebase structure; PMD has no rule for this pattern.

PMD's AvoidAccessibilityAlteration rule catches reflection-based encapsulation bypass (setAccessible), but there is no rule covering ScriptEngine, Runtime.exec, ProcessBuilder, or other dynamic execution mechanisms — the general class of eval/dynamic execution is only partially covered.

Monkey patching — runtime replacement of methods or class behaviour — is not possible in standard Java; the JVM's static class loading model and final bytecode make this construct absent. No PMD rule exists for it and the construct itself does not apply to Java.

Duck typing is a concept from dynamically-typed languages; Java is statically typed and the compiler enforces interface contracts at compile time. The discipline has no equivalent in Java and PMD has no relevant rule.

Whether lint failures are treated as build failures is a build-tool / CI configuration concern (Maven failOnError, Gradle task wiring), not source-code pattern PMD can detect. PMD analyses Java source only and cannot inspect pipeline or build configuration.

Test coverage measurement and enforcement (e.g. minimum line/branch thresholds) is outside PMD's scope; PMD is a static linter with no coverage-analysis capability. Dedicated tools such as JaCoCo are required.

Dependency version pinning and external API contract validation are handled by build-tool plugins (Dependency-Check, Snyk, enforcer rules) rather than source-code linting. PMD has no rules that inspect pom.xml/build.gradle or validate external interface contracts.

PMD has several logging-adjacent rules (GuardLogStatement, InvalidLogMessageFormat, SystemPrintln, AvoidPrintStackTrace) that push code toward using a proper logger with parameterised messages, but none explicitly enforces structured/machine-readable log output (JSON, key-value pairs). The closest structural concern — using string concatenation vs. parameterised placeholders — is addressed only indirectly.

PMD has no rule that checks for the presence or propagation of correlation/trace IDs in log statements or request handling. Detecting this pattern requires semantic, domain-specific analysis beyond PMD's rule-based scope.
Health check / readiness probe presence is a runtime/architectural concern; PMD has no rule covering it.
Alerting strategy (symptoms vs. causes) is an operational/observability concern; PMD has no rule for it.

Postel's Law (liberal input acceptance, conservative output) is a semantic API design principle; PMD has no rule that captures it. AvoidCatchingGenericException touches exception breadth but is not a proxy for the robustness principle.
Semantic versioning is enforced at the build/release level (pom.xml, release tooling); PMD analyses Java source and has no rule for version string semantics.
Backwards-compatibility detection requires cross-version comparison; PMD analyses a single snapshot and has no rule for breaking API changes.

PMD's SignatureDeclareThrowsException flags vague 'throws Exception' declarations and AvoidThrowingRawExceptionTypes enforces specific exception types, but there is no rule that validates a complete documented error contract.
Pinning transitive dependencies is a build-file concern (pom.xml / build.gradle); PMD analyses Java source code and has no rule for dependency version pinning.

PMD's UnnecessaryImport flags unused and redundant imports in Java source (import-level footprint), but cannot detect unused Maven/Gradle dependency declarations — the broader meaning of minimal dependency footprint.

PMD is a source-code static analyzer with no dependency audit capability; checking third-party dependencies for vulnerabilities or staleness requires external tools (e.g., OWASP Dependency-Check, Snyk).

PMD has no rule that evaluates package-level single responsibility; GodClass and CouplingBetweenObjects operate at the class level, not the package level, and LoosePackageCoupling only restricts which packages may be imported, not whether a package is cohesive.

PMD has ImmutableField (design) for non-final fields whose value never changes, LocalVariableCouldBeFinal / MethodArgumentCouldBeFinal (codestyle), and ArrayIsStoredDirectly / MethodReturnsInternalArray (bestpractices) — together covering several immutability patterns but not enforcing fully immutable class design holistically.

PMD has no rule that enforces a share-nothing architectural pattern; multithreading rules (UseConcurrentHashMap, NonThreadSafeSingleton, UnsynchronizedStaticFormatter) flag specific narrow antipatterns but do not detect general shared mutable state or enforce architectural isolation.
Idempotency is a semantic/behavioral property of business logic that cannot be inferred from syntax; PMD has no rule addressing it.

PMD has no rule for detecting implicit state machines or requiring explicit state machine patterns; ExceptionAsFlowControl is tangentially related but targets a narrow anti-pattern, not state machine design.

PMD's multithreading rules (UnsynchronizedStaticFormatter, NonThreadSafeSingleton, UseConcurrentHashMap) flag some unsafe shared/global resource access patterns, but there is no general rule about assuming or verifying ownership semantics of shared resources.

PMD's documentation rules (CommentRequired, CommentContent, CommentSize) enforce comment presence, forbidden words, and length limits, but cannot distinguish decision-rationale comments from mechanical description comments — that requires natural language understanding.

CommentRequired enforces Javadoc presence on public API elements; DanglingJavadoc (PMD 7.17+) flags misplaced Javadoc that won't attach to its intended element. Together they cover formal Javadoc proximity, but PMD has no rule for semantic co-location of inline comments with the code they describe, nor for stale/distant documentation.

PMD's documentation category (CommentContent, CommentRequired, CommentSize, DanglingJavadoc, UncommentedEmptyConstructor/MethodBody) has no rule for executable/testable documentation patterns such as runnable snippets, doctests, or verifiable examples. The concept is entirely outside PMD's rule scope.

Several rules address over-broad access: PublicMemberInNonPublicType, MutableStaticState, AvoidProtectedFieldInFinalClass, MethodReturnsInternalArray, ArrayIsStoredDirectly, AvoidAccessibilityAlteration. However, PMD has no general rule that enforces minimum necessary visibility on methods/classes (e.g., flagging a public method only called within its own package), so coverage is incomplete.

PMD has no rule modelling defence-in-depth as a pattern. No rule checks for re-validation at trust boundaries or detects missing layered validation. The security category contains only HardCodedCryptoKey and InsecureCryptoIv. This is an architectural concern beyond AST-level static analysis.

EmptyCatchBlock detects silently swallowed exceptions (a surface form of fail-insecure), and BrokenNullCheck detects boolean logic errors in guard conditions. Neither targets fail-secure semantics directly — PMD cannot detect a catch block that actively grants access on exception, only one that is empty.
Wrap Method/Class is a refactoring recommendation (adding a wrapper to inject behavior); SonarC# has no rule that detects missing method wrappers or suggests this pattern.

SonarC# has no rule that detects when a class should extract an interface; interface-related rules (e.g. RSPEC-1206) only enforce correct implementation of already-declared interfaces, not recommend extraction.

source_label: RSPEC-109: Magic numbers should not be used
rationale: RSPEC-109 flags magic numbers and recommends named constants, which overlaps with parameterizing hardcoded values, but does not detect the broader 'Parameterize Constructor/Method' refactoring (extracting any hardcoded dependency into a parameter).
Gradual typing means adding optional type annotations to a dynamically-typed language; C# is fully statically typed at compile time, so the concept does not apply.

source_label: RSPEC-2219: Runtime type checking should be simplified
rationale: RSPEC-2219 directly flags redundant or clumsy runtime type checks and requires using idiomatic 'is'/'as' pattern matching; RSPEC-1944 flags invalid or unsafe casts. Together these cover type-narrowing correctness mechanically.
Prototype pollution is a JavaScript-specific vulnerability tied to prototype-based inheritance; C# uses class-based static typing and has no prototype chain, so the construct does not exist.
Global scope pollution via implicit variable hoisting is a JavaScript-specific mechanism; C# has no implicit global variable creation, so the construct does not exist in this language.

source_label: RSPEC-6932: Use model binding instead of reading raw request data
rationale: RSPEC-6932 flags direct access to raw HTTP request data in ASP.NET controllers and recommends model binding, which partially captures the 'no request context in domain models' principle, but the rule is scoped to controller actions and does not detect HttpContext leaking into domain/model layer classes generally.

source_label: S5334 – Dynamic code execution should not be vulnerable to injection
rationale: S5334 is a taint-analysis vulnerability rule that tracks user-controlled input reaching dynamic code execution sinks in C#, but it requires SonarQube Developer Edition (not Community/standard). No eval() equivalent exists in C#; S1523 was added then removed for C# with the explicit note 'no eval or exec method in C#'. S5334 covers injection paths but not general dynamic execution APIs like CSharpScript or unrestricted Assembly.Load patterns.

Monkey patching (runtime replacement of methods on existing types) is not a C# language construct. Extension methods are compile-time additive only, and IL weaving happens at build time via third-party tools — neither constitutes runtime monkey patching as the construct exists in Python/Ruby. Nothing for a static analyzer to detect.

C# has the `dynamic` keyword which defers type checking to runtime, enabling duck-typing behavior. Exhaustive search found no SonarC# rule that flags use of `dynamic` to bypass the static type system. S1523 and S5334 address code injection, not the `dynamic` type; community discussions confirm no dedicated 'avoid dynamic' rule exists.

Enforcing lint/analysis as a build-breaking gate is a CI/CD process configuration concern, not a detectable code pattern. SonarQube Quality Gates can enforce this at the pipeline level, but that is platform configuration, not a static analysis rule — no rule can fire on absent CI configuration.

Test coverage thresholds are enforced via SonarQube Quality Gates (platform-level configuration), not through any per-file or per-project static analysis rule. There is no SonarC# rule that detects 'coverage not enforced' in source code.

No SonarC# rule covers pinning or validating external API/serialization contracts in the general sense (contract tests, schema snapshots, version pinning). S5766 addresses deserialization constructor validation (a narrow correctness pattern), not external contract discipline. The broader checklist item has no off-the-shelf detector.

source_label: S2629 – Logging templates should be constant
rationale: SonarC# has comprehensive structured logging coverage: S2629 flags string interpolation/concatenation in log calls (requires named placeholders instead), S6667 flags dropped exception objects in catch blocks, S6673 flags placeholder-argument order mismatches, S6674 flags syntactically malformed templates. Covers ILogger, Serilog, NLog, and log4net.
No SonarC# rule detects missing or improper correlation/trace ID propagation through async call chains. This requires architectural and dataflow reasoning beyond current SonarC# rule coverage.
SonarC# has no rules about health check endpoints or readiness probes — these are infrastructure/framework concerns outside static analysis scope.
SonarC# has no rules about alerting philosophy (symptoms vs. causes); alerting configuration is an operational concern beyond static analysis.
No SonarC# rule addresses Postel's Law (liberal in input, strict in output); this is a semantic API design principle not detectable by pattern matching.
SonarC# has no rules enforcing semantic versioning conventions on assemblies or NuGet packages.

SonarC# has no rules that detect backwards-compatibility violations as a class; rules about field visibility or method-override defaults only incidentally guard against some breakages, far too narrow to count as coverage.
SonarC# has no rules requiring explicit declaration or documentation of error contracts; RSPEC-3871 (exception types must be public) is about accessibility, not contract explicitness.
SonarC# has no rules about pinning transitive dependencies or enforcing lock-file usage; dependency version management is outside its analysis scope.
SonarC# has no rules detecting unused or excessive NuGet package references; RSPEC-1128 covers unused 'using' directives (namespace imports), not package-level dependencies.

No native SonarC# rule for dependency vulnerability audits. Vulnerable NuGet detection requires the Enterprise-only SCA add-on (2025.3+) or a third-party OWASP Dependency-Check plugin — neither is a standard out-of-the-box static analysis rule.

RSPEC-1200 measures per-class fan-out (>30 class dependencies), which is class-level coupling — not namespace/package cohesion or single-responsibility at the package boundary. No SonarC# rule analyses whether a namespace or assembly groups semantically unrelated concerns.

source_label: RSPEC-2933: Fields that are only assigned in the constructor should be 'readonly'
rationale: RSPEC-2933 flags private fields that are written only in the constructor and should be marked readonly, and RSPEC-2386 flags mutable public static fields. Together they cover narrow immutability sub-patterns but miss broader design (immutable classes, record/init-only properties, mutable ctor-injected collections), leaving the general 'prefer immutability' principle largely undetected.

source_label: RSPEC-2445: Blocks should be synchronized on read-only fields
rationale: SonarC# has several threading rules (S2445, S3998 lock-on-weak-identity, S2996 ThreadStatic initialization) that flag specific shared-state anti-patterns, but these are point fixes for known threading bugs rather than enforcement of a share-nothing architectural boundary. Architectural isolation between components cannot be checked statically.

No SonarC# rule addresses idempotency — neither HTTP-method idempotency (PUT/DELETE semantics) nor operation-level idempotency (safe-to-retry writes). Idempotency requires semantic understanding of side effects that static pattern matching cannot provide.

No SonarC# rule detects implicit state machines or enforces that state transitions be made explicit (e.g. via a dedicated state-machine class or pattern). State machine design is a structural/architectural concern with no static proxy rule in SonarC#.

source_label: RSPEC-2386: Mutable fields should not be 'public static'
rationale: RSPEC-2386 flags publicly exposed mutable static fields — the most obvious form of assuming shared-resource ownership — and S3887 flags readonly mutable collection fields that should be private. These cover specific anti-patterns but not the general design principle of not assuming exclusive access to shared or global resources.

SonarC# has no rule about comment content quality. S125 detects commented-out code and S1135 flags TODO markers, but neither distinguishes between mechanical 'what' comments and decision-rationale 'why' comments. Comment quality is a semantic/editorial judgement with no static proxy in SonarC#.

source_label: SonarSource Community: C# Documentation Rules request
rationale: SonarC# has no rule for keeping documentation close to code (outdated comments, orphaned XML docs, stale doc comments). S1135 tracks TODO tags but is unrelated to doc proximity; the community has requested C# doc rules but none are shipped.
C# has no built-in doctest convention and SonarC# has no rule for executable documentation or example-code validation; this concept is outside the scope of its static-analysis rule set.

S2357 enforces that fields be private (access minimization), and S1118 flags utility classes with public constructors. These cover least privilege at the access-modifier level but do not address OS/network/IAM permission scopes or runtime privilege escalation.

SonarC# has individual injection and authorization rules (S5145, S5808, etc.) but no rule that enforces layered/redundant controls as an architectural principle; defence-in-depth as a design property requires contextual judgement beyond static pattern matching.

source_label: S2486: Exceptions should not be ignored
rationale: S2486 flags empty catch blocks that silently swallow exceptions, which covers one fail-secure anti-pattern, but the general case—default-allow on error paths, missing authorization checks in exception handlers, or catch-and-continue logic—requires semantic reasoning not covered by the rule.

No ESLint rule detects semantically redundant comments (comments that merely restate what the code does). The no-warning-comments rule only flags TODO/FIXME markers, which is unrelated to redundancy. Semantic content analysis is beyond static AST inspection.

ESLint has no rules for verifying that new or changed code is accompanied by unit tests. Test existence and coverage are measured by testing frameworks and coverage reporters (Jest, Istanbul/NYC), not by a JavaScript linter.

ESLint offers size/complexity proxies for function-level SRP violations: `complexity` (cyclomatic complexity), `max-statements` (statement count), and `max-lines-per-function` (line count). These catch large/complex functions that often violate SRP but cannot detect semantic multi-responsibility in small functions.

The `max-lines` rule limits file length as a heuristic proxy for file-level SRP violations. ESLint docs note that large files 'tend to do a lot of things', but this is purely size-based — a long file may still be cohesive, and a short file can have many responsibilities.

Core ESLint has no rules governing test file structure (describe/it nesting, assertion patterns, file organization). Plugins like eslint-plugin-jest or eslint-plugin-mocha cover this, but they are third-party and not bundled with ESLint.

`no-unreachable` (in recommended set) flags code after return/throw/break/continue; `no-unused-vars` (recommended) flags unused variables and functions; `no-unused-labels` and `no-useless-assignment` cover additional dead-code patterns. Together these deterministically catch the primary dead-code classes.
ESLint is a JavaScript linter and has no mechanism to inspect non-code files such as CHANGELOG.md. Changelog enforcement requires Git hooks, CI scripts, or commit-lint tooling.

ESLint cannot measure or enforce test coverage thresholds. Coverage is computed by instrumented test runs (Istanbul/NYC, Jest --coverage) and enforced via their own threshold configuration, not by a linter.
No ESLint rule can statically distinguish tests that verify behavior/requirements from those that verify implementation details; this requires semantic judgement about test intent.

The built-in `no-magic-numbers` rule flags bare numeric literals that should be named constants, catching the most common form of duplicated literals, but does not detect duplicated string literals or enforce that the same value be unified into one constant.
Data normalization and ambiguity removal are semantic/design-level concerns; ESLint has no rule that detects redundant or ambiguous data representations.
Distinguishing heuristic approximations from deterministic logic requires understanding algorithmic intent, beyond ESLint's structural/syntactic analysis.
Brittleness is a semantic property (e.g. fragile coupling, ordering dependencies); ESLint complexity and style rules do not detect this class of issue.
Over-specificity vs. appropriate generalization is a design-level abstraction judgment; no ESLint rule targets this pattern.

The built-in `max-lines` rule limits file length and `max-lines-per-function` limits method length as indirect proxies for class size, but there is no rule counting total methods or properties per class.
Primitive obsession requires understanding domain semantics (when a primitive should be a value object); ESLint has no rule for this code smell.
No ESLint rule detects repeated parameter groups across functions; identifying data clumps requires cross-function semantic analysis beyond ESLint's scope.

ESLint has rules governing switch correctness (no-fallthrough, default-case) but no built-in rule that flags switch statements as a code smell. The complexity rule indirectly penalises heavy switch use via cyclomatic complexity, but does not target switch statements specifically.

no-unused-vars catches declared-but-never-read variables, but cannot detect fields that are only assigned in some code paths and null/undefined in others — the defining characteristic of Temporary Field.

class-methods-use-this flags methods that never access instance state, a weak syntactic proxy for Refused Bequest, but it does not detect unused inherited properties or methods and is not in the recommended config.
Detecting two classes that fulfil the same role under different method names requires cross-class semantic understanding; no ESLint rule exists for this.
Divergent Change is an architectural smell about a class absorbing unrelated concerns; ESLint has no rule that detects unrelated responsibility groupings in a class.
Shotgun Surgery requires tracking which files must change together across the codebase; ESLint is a file-level linter with no cross-file coupling analysis rule.
Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies require mapping mirrored class hierarchies across files; no ESLint rule performs cross-hierarchy structural analysis.

No ESLint rule detects 'Lazy Class' semantically. The closest proxy (class-methods-use-this) flags instance methods that don't use `this`, but that is an entirely different signal and misses the core concept of a class not pulling its weight architecturally.

No ESLint rule identifies a class that only holds data with no meaningful behavior. ESLint cannot distinguish a data-only class from a legitimate value object or DTO; doing so requires domain-level semantic reasoning.

Speculative generality manifests as code that exists syntactically and may even be reachable, so no-unused-vars / no-unused-expressions only catch the narrow case of literally unreferenced declarations. The broader pattern of unnecessary abstraction or over-engineering for hypothetical futures is invisible to ESLint.

no-warning-comments flags TODO/FIXME markers (a sign of deferred explanation), and no-inline-comments / capitalized-comments enforce comment style, but no rule measures comment density or detects comments that compensate for unclear code — the core of over-reliance on comments.

Feature Envy requires measuring the ratio of accesses to another class's data vs. the method's own class. ESLint has no rule that tracks cross-class member access patterns; class-methods-use-this only checks whether `this` is used at all, not the access balance.

Inappropriate Intimacy requires detecting that one class reaches into the private/internal state of another. JavaScript's lack of enforced access modifiers makes this inherently hard; TypeScript's compiler handles visibility, and no ESLint rule audits cross-class coupling depth.

The newline-per-chained-call rule only enforces newline formatting within chains (default: allows depth ≤ 2 without a newline); it does not flag chains as a code smell or enforce a maximum chain depth. No ESLint rule detects excessively long message chains as a quality issue.

Middle Man requires detecting that a class's methods are predominantly thin wrappers delegating to another object. ESLint has no rule that analyzes method delegation ratios; this pattern is invisible to syntactic analysis.

ESLint core has size/complexity proxies (max-lines, max-lines-per-function, max-statements, complexity) that can flag bloated files/functions, but no rule measures class-level responsibility count or method count directly. These are crude heuristics, not true God-Object detection.

No ESLint core or widely-bundled plugin rule detects classes that are pure data bags lacking domain behavior. Detecting an anemic domain model requires semantic understanding of class method presence/purpose — beyond static pattern matching.

Poltergeist detection requires tracking object lifetime and whether an object exists solely to delegate to another — a dataflow/scope analysis ESLint does not perform. no-unused-vars catches completely unused identifiers but not short-lived delegate objects.

ESLint has no rule that measures or limits inheritance chain depth. Constructor-related rules (no-this-before-super, constructor-super) check correctness, not depth. Yo-Yo problem detection requires traversing the extends chain across files.

Sequential coupling (temporal coupling between method calls) requires interprocedural dataflow analysis to detect that method B must be called after method A. ESLint has no such analysis; require-await only catches missing await in async functions, which is a different concern.

JavaScript has no interface keyword; a 'constant interface' pattern (class/object used only as a namespace for constants) is not flagged by any ESLint core rule. class-methods-use-this and max-classes-per-file are tangentially related but do not detect this pattern.

JavaScript has no native interface construct; TypeScript interfaces are outside ESLint-core scope. Even with @typescript-eslint, there is no rule that limits interface member count. The construct the smell targets does not exist in plain JS.

ESLint has no rule that detects or quantifies singleton pattern usage. no-restricted-properties can ban access to a known singleton property by name, but that requires manual enumeration and does not detect the pattern itself.

ESLint has no rule that detects combinatorial explosion of parameter combinations or exponential branching paths; `complexity` measures cyclomatic complexity and `max-params` limits parameter count, but neither flags the combinatorial explosion pattern specifically.

No ESLint rule detects tramp data (parameters threaded through multiple call layers without being consumed by intermediaries); `no-unused-vars` only catches variables never referenced at all, not variables passed-through unnecessarily.

`max-depth`, `complexity`, `max-statements`, and `max-nested-callbacks` flag structural proxies (nesting depth, cyclomatic complexity, statement count) that correlate with spaghetti code, but they are opt-in metric thresholds, not a semantic detector of tangled control flow.

ESLint has no duplicate-code-block detector; `no-duplicate-imports` only flags repeated import declarations, not copy-pasted logic. Detecting copy-paste programming requires dedicated CPD/clone-detection tooling.
No ESLint rule detects over-application of a single pattern or construct across unrelated problems; identifying the Golden Hammer anti-pattern requires semantic/architectural judgment.

`no-unreachable` and `no-unused-vars` catch some dead code (unreachable statements, unused identifiers), but lava flow specifically includes code that still executes but serves no current purpose — a semantic distinction ESLint cannot make.

`no-magic-numbers` reliably flags unexplained numeric literals, but ESLint has no built-in rule for hardcoded strings, URLs, or credentials; full hard-coding coverage requires additional plugins or tools.

No ESLint rule detects action at a distance (functions that silently mutate global or distant state); `no-global-assign` only prevents reassigning global identifiers, not detecting side-effectful mutations through object references.

ESLint has no rule that detects boolean flag arguments used to branch function behavior; max-params counts parameters but does not analyze whether they are boolean flags controlling internal branching.

ESLint rules (no-global-assign, no-undef, no-restricted-globals) address global assignment or undeclared names, but none flag a function that silently reads from external/global state as a hidden dependency — the semantic dependency relationship is not modelled.
No ESLint rule exists for premature optimization; detecting it requires understanding developer intent and performance trade-off judgements, which is beyond AST-level static analysis.

ESLint has no built-in rule to detect reimplementation of language or platform built-ins; identifying that user-written code duplicates Array/Object/String methods requires semantic library knowledge not present in core ESLint.
Big Ball of Mud is a whole-codebase architectural anti-pattern; ESLint operates per-file at the AST level and has no mechanism for holistic structural analysis of codebase organisation.
Stovepipe System is an inter-module/cross-system architectural concern requiring analysis of duplication and sharing across subsystems; no ESLint rule addresses this level of abstraction.

Database-as-IPC is a runtime architectural pattern (polling a DB instead of using message queues); ESLint has no rule to distinguish database polling intent from normal queries, so it cannot detect this statically.

The built-in no-restricted-imports rule can enforce bans on specific vendor packages, but only catches what is explicitly configured — it does not auto-detect vendor lock-in and requires the reviewer to enumerate prohibited imports in advance.
SRP is semantic (one reason to change); ESLint's size/complexity proxies (max-lines, complexity, max-statements) correlate loosely but cannot determine whether a class has distinct responsibilities.
OCP requires reasoning about whether future changes require modification vs. extension — pure design-intent analysis beyond any ESLint syntactic rule.
LSP requires verifying behavioral contracts (preconditions, postconditions, invariants) across class hierarchies; no ESLint rule approaches behavioral substitutability.
ISP requires detecting overly-broad interfaces; JavaScript has no formal interfaces, and no ESLint rule checks whether a type/object forces unused method dependencies.

DIP (depend on abstractions, not concretions) requires semantic understanding of abstract vs. concrete relationships; eslint-plugin-boundaries can enforce directory-level import rules but does not detect abstract-vs-concrete dependency violations.
JavaScript lacks formal interfaces; no ESLint or typescript-eslint rule flags violations of the 'program to an interface' principle — it requires semantic reasoning about coupling to concrete types.

no-restricted-syntax can ban the 'extends' keyword entirely as a blanket rule but cannot judge whether a specific inheritance use violates composition preference; no standard rule covers this semantically.
Identifying which code paths represent variability vs. stable logic requires semantic reasoning about design intent; no ESLint rule or known plugin targets this GoF principle.

ESLint has no built-in rule that detects tight coupling or enforces dependency injection patterns. Third-party plugins (eslint-plugin-boundaries, eslint-plugin-import no-cycle) exist but are opt-in and only catch circular imports — not general coupling as a design quality.
ESLint is a static linter; it cannot run tests or verify test results. No ESLint rule determines whether tests pass or what coverage exists.

ESLint naming rules (camelcase, id-length, id-match) enforce syntactic naming conventions, not semantic intention clarity. A misleadingly named but syntactically valid identifier is invisible to ESLint.

ESLint built-in rules detect narrow structural duplicates: no-duplicate-imports (same module imported twice), no-dupe-keys (duplicate object keys), no-dupe-args, no-duplicate-case. These cover trivial cases but miss any logic or code-block duplication — the bulk of DRY violations.

YAGNI requires judging whether an abstraction or feature is justified by current requirements — a design-intent decision no static linter can make. no-unused-vars catches dead code after the fact but not over-engineering at design time.

no-restricted-imports can block specific import paths but requires complete manual configuration of every layer boundary per project — it is not a general Clean Architecture detector. No built-in rule enforces the dependency rule automatically.

Layer separation is an architectural design judgment. ESLint has no built-in rule that understands or enforces concern/layer boundaries; dedicated plugins (eslint-plugin-boundaries) are entirely opt-in and not standard.

The inner-knows-nothing-of-outer rule requires knowing which layer a file belongs to and whether its imports cross outward — context ESLint lacks without explicit project-specific configuration. no-restricted-imports cannot do this without heavy manual setup.

Boy Scout Rule is a process/meta principle (leave code cleaner than you found it) — ESLint has individual code-smell rules (complexity, no-unused-vars, etc.) but no rule that detects 'code was made worse by this change' as a class.

ESLint cannot verify that ports are defined as interfaces/abstract contracts; no rule checks whether a directory's exports are interface-typed vs concrete-class-typed in a semantically meaningful way.
ESLint has no rule that verifies a class implements a specific port interface; TypeScript's compiler checks 'implements' clauses but ESLint itself cannot enforce that adapters fulfill port contracts.

eslint-plugin-boundaries or import/no-restricted-paths can enforce that core/domain directories have no imports from infrastructure layers, which is the mechanical proxy for 'core is pure'; requires opt-in configuration.
ESLint cannot verify that driving-side adapters only invoke port interfaces rather than concrete implementations; this requires semantic knowledge of which types are ports vs implementations.
ESLint cannot verify that driven-side adapters properly implement port interfaces; verifying implementation contracts requires type-system analysis beyond what ESLint rules provide.
Testability by design is an architectural quality attribute, not a detectable code pattern; ESLint has no rule that evaluates whether a design is inherently testable.

eslint-plugin-boundaries and import/no-restricted-paths can enforce unidirectional import flow (outer layers may not import inner layers), which is the structural proxy for inward dependency direction; requires opt-in zone configuration.
REP requires understanding which components are released together as reusable units — a release/versioning semantic judgment; no ESLint core or plugin rule addresses this.
CCP requires identifying which classes change together over time (temporal coupling) — a dynamic/historical analysis; no static ESLint rule exists for this.

eslint-plugin-import's no-cycle rule detects circular import chains, catching the most common form of cyclic dependencies, but misses higher-level component-graph cycles and requires opt-in configuration.
SDP requires computing stability metrics (fan-in/fan-out ratios) across the whole codebase and comparing them across dependency edges — no ESLint rule performs this analysis.
SAP requires measuring abstractness (abstract class/interface ratio) per component and correlating with stability scores — no ESLint rule computes or enforces this metric.

eslint-plugin-boundaries can enforce folder/layer import policies as a proxy for bounded context boundaries, but only catches cross-boundary imports, not the full semantic definition of a bounded context.
Ubiquitous Language consistency requires matching identifiers against a domain glossary — a semantic/naming convention check that no ESLint rule supports out of the box.
Detecting anemic domain models (entities with no behavior, logic displaced into services) requires structural semantic analysis of class method distribution — no ESLint rule addresses this.

DDD Repository pattern enforcement requires understanding architectural boundaries and import layering — ESLint has no built-in rule for this. `no-restricted-imports` can be manually configured per-project to block direct data-layer access, but that requires bespoke setup and is not an out-of-the-box detection of the pattern violation class.

DDD Service Layer violations (business logic leaking into wrong layer) require semantic understanding of domain boundaries. ESLint's `no-restricted-syntax` and `no-restricted-imports` can be configured per-project, but there is no standard rule that detects this class of issue out of the box.

Encapsulating mutable internal state (e.g. returning direct references to internal arrays) is a semantic concern. `accessor-pairs` only ensures getter/setter pairing exists, not that returned values are copies vs. references. No ESLint rule detects exposure of mutable internals.

In TypeScript projects, `@typescript-eslint/explicit-member-accessibility` can flag class members lacking an explicit access modifier, nudging all public surface to be intentional. However, in plain JS there is no equivalent; the rule is opt-in and only applies to classes, not module-level exports or object literals.

Tell Don't Ask violations require recognising that external code queries object state to make a decision that the object itself should make — a semantic/design concern ESLint has no rule for. `class-methods-use-this` is about a different issue (static-like methods) and does not detect this pattern.

Replace Subclass with Delegate is a refactoring judgement call requiring understanding of the class hierarchy and whether delegation fits better. ESLint has no rule that identifies when an inheritance relationship should be replaced by composition.
Collapse Hierarchy (flattening unnecessary inheritance levels) requires semantic analysis of the whole class tree to determine if a middle class adds no behaviour. ESLint has no rule for this.

Refused Bequest (a subclass inherits but ignores or overrides nearly all parent behaviour) requires cross-class semantic analysis. `no-unused-private-class-members` only flags private members declared in the same class, not inherited members that go unused.
ESLint naming rules (id-match, id-length, camelcase) enforce syntactic patterns only; no rule can judge whether a name meaningfully reveals intent.
new-cap enforces PascalCase for constructor/class names (partial noun enforcement), but ESLint has no rule requiring method names to be verb-prefixed; the verb half is undetectable.
ESLint cannot enforce cross-function terminological consistency; id-denylist can block specific tokens but cannot detect that get/fetch/retrieve are used inconsistently for the same concept.
No ESLint rule validates semantic accuracy of names against actual runtime types or data structures; detecting that accountList is actually a Set requires type-aware semantic analysis.
complexity and max-lines-per-function measure size/branching, not abstraction level; ESLint has no rule that detects mixed high-level and low-level operations within a single function.
consistent-return only checks return-value consistency within one function path; no ESLint rule detects the CQS violation of a function that both mutates state and returns a value.
no-param-reassign detects one class of side effect (parameter mutation), but ESLint has no general pure-function or hidden-side-effect rule covering global mutations, I/O, or external calls.
max-params counts all parameters uniformly without distinguishing boolean flags; ESLint has no rule that specifically targets boolean flag arguments that alter function control flow.

ESLint's `max-params` rule flags functions exceeding a parameter count threshold (default 3), catching the symptom that motivates the refactoring, but it does not detect or suggest the Parameter Object pattern itself — it only reports the violation.

`max-depth` flags blocks nested beyond a configurable threshold (default 4), detecting the symptom. `no-lonely-if` catches the specific case of a lone `if` inside `else` and auto-fixes it to `else if`, but neither rule recommends or enforces the guard-clause/early-return pattern in the general case.

ESLint has no rule that identifies switch statements or if-else chains as candidates for polymorphic dispatch. `complexity` measures cyclomatic complexity numerically but does not suggest replacing conditionals with polymorphism; this is an architectural judgment beyond static syntactic analysis.

No ESLint core or widely-bundled rule detects imperative for-loops that could be replaced with map/filter/reduce pipelines. `no-restricted-syntax` can ban all for-loops syntactically but has no semantic analysis to distinguish replaceable from non-replaceable loops; `array-callback-return` only enforces return statements inside array method callbacks.

Neither ESLint core nor eslint-plugin-jest has rules that detect slow test practices such as real timers, real network calls, or real file I/O. Rules like `max-expects` and `max-nested-describe` address test structure, not execution speed.

ESLint cannot detect test interdependencies such as shared mutable state across tests or ordering dependencies. eslint-plugin-jest's `require-hook` and `no-duplicate-hooks` enforce structural hook conventions but cannot track state mutations or cross-test data pollution.

ESLint has no rules that flag non-deterministic constructs (Date.now(), Math.random(), etc.) specifically within test bodies. eslint-plugin-jest's `no-conditional-expect` and `no-conditional-in-test` prevent conditional branching in tests but do not detect randomness or time-dependency sources.

eslint-plugin-jest's `expect-expect` rule (part of the plugin's recommended config) flags test bodies containing no assertion calls, catching the most common form of self-validation failure. However, it requires the jest plugin (not core ESLint) and can miss assertions made through indirect helper calls unless explicitly configured via `assertFunctionNames`.

Timely (writing tests before production code) requires temporal analysis of commit order, which is outside ESLint's static-analysis scope. No ESLint rule detects when tests were written relative to production code.

Red-Green-Refactor cycle adherence is a development workflow concept requiring commit-sequence analysis across multiple files. ESLint analyzes static snapshots of code and has no rule for TDD cycle compliance.

Fake It Till You Make It is a TDD progression technique: intentionally returning hard-coded values as a first pass. ESLint rules like no-magic-numbers flag unexplained literals generally, but cannot distinguish a deliberate TDD 'fake' implementation from any other hard-coded value. No rule targets this semantic pattern.

Triangulation (adding test cases to force generalization) is a reasoning/design practice that requires understanding test intent and whether implementation has been properly generalized. ESLint has no rule that can detect this.

Assert First (writing assertions before setup) is a TDD style discipline. While eslint-plugin-jest has expect-expect (ensures assertions exist) and prefer-hooks-on-top (hooks before tests), no rule enforces or detects assertion ordering within a test body.

eslint-plugin-square's no-missing-tests rule enforces that each source file has a corresponding test file, catching the file-level 'no tests at all' case. However, it cannot detect untested code within a file or measure actual coverage.

Characterization Tests (tests that document existing legacy behavior) are distinguished by intent, not structure. ESLint cannot semantically differentiate a characterization test from any other test. No rule targets this pattern.

Sprout Method/Class is an architectural refactoring technique (isolating new code from untouched legacy). Detecting whether this technique was followed requires understanding structural intent and legacy isolation, which is beyond static-analysis rule matching.
Wrap Method/Class is a refactoring pattern (wrapping a class in an adapter/decorator). ESLint has no rule that detects this architectural smell; it requires semantic understanding of design intent.

Extract Interface is a refactoring pattern. JavaScript has no native interface construct, so there is no syntactic hook for ESLint to flag; the pattern requires human/semantic judgement about when a shared interface should be extracted.

ESLint's no-magic-numbers flags hardcoded literals but cannot infer that a literal should become a parameter rather than a named constant; parameterisation is a refactoring decision that requires contextual judgement beyond literal detection.

The @typescript-eslint package provides rules like no-explicit-any and explicit-function-return-type that encourage gradual typing, but these are not bundled with core ESLint and require separate installation plus explicit opt-in configuration; core eslint:recommended has no typing rules.

ESLint's valid-typeof only validates that typeof comparisons use legal type strings (syntax correctness); no rule enforces that developers must narrow types before use, which is the semantic pattern at issue.

no-proto blocks __proto__ access and no-extend-native blocks direct Object.prototype mutation, but dynamic-key prototype pollution (obj[userControlledKey] = val where key is '__proto__') is not caught; the most dangerous injection vector is missed.

no-implicit-globals prevents top-level var/function declarations from leaking to global scope, and no-global-assign prevents reassigning read-only globals, but mutation of writable global properties (e.g. window.myVar = x in module files) is not fully covered.

Detecting that request-context objects (e.g. Express req) are passed into domain/model layers is an architectural layering concern; ESLint has no rule for this and it requires project-specific structural analysis.

no-eval blocks direct/indirect eval calls, no-new-func blocks the Function constructor, and no-implied-eval blocks setTimeout/setInterval with string arguments — together covering all mainstream dynamic-execution patterns.

no-extend-native catches direct prototype assignment and defineProperty on native prototypes, but the docs explicitly note it misses variable indirection, window-prefixed references, eval-based mutation, and with-statement approaches.

ESLint has no rule enforcing duck-typing discipline generally; valid-typeof only guards against typos in typeof string literals — neither it nor any standard rule addresses intentional duck-typing patterns or instanceof discipline.
This is a CI/build-pipeline configuration concern, not a source-code pattern any lint rule can detect; ESLint is itself the enforcement mechanism, so no rule can flag its own absence from CI.
ESLint has no built-in rules for coverage thresholds; enforcement requires separate tools (jest --coverageThreshold, nyc, c8). No standard or recommended ESLint rule addresses this.

ESLint cannot validate external API schemas or pin contract versions semantically; no standard rule verifies type correctness or schema conformance of external data — that requires TypeScript, Zod/Joi, or runtime validators.

no-console can ban raw console.* calls (pushing teams toward a dedicated logger), but does not enforce structured/JSON logging format or mandate any specific logging library — the positive requirement is not checkable by any standard rule.
Correlation-ID propagation across async chains requires runtime data-flow tracking; ESLint has no rule that can statically verify request context is threaded through async call chains.
Health check / readiness probe presence is a runtime infrastructure concern; ESLint has no rules that detect whether an application exposes health endpoints.
Distinguishing symptom-based from cause-based alerting is a runtime observability design concern; ESLint performs static code analysis and has no rules for alerting semantics.
Postel's Law (liberal input acceptance, conservative output) is a semantic design principle; ESLint has no rule that detects whether a function validates or narrows its inputs permissively.
ESLint core has no semver-validation rule; niche plugins like eslint-plugin-node-dependencies exist but are not widely-used bundled rules, and none enforce semver policy in source code.

Detecting backwards-incompatible API changes requires semantic understanding of public contracts across versions; ESLint has no rule for this. eslint-plugin-import's no-deprecated targets usage of deprecated symbols, not compatibility breakage in authored APIs.

eslint-plugin-jsdoc's require-throws rule enforces that throw statements have a corresponding @throws JSDoc tag, enforcing documentation of error contracts; it misses Promise rejections and implicit error propagation, and only fires when a literal throw is present.

Pinning transitive (nested) dependencies cannot be enforced at the source-code level; it requires lockfile analysis (e.g. npm-shrinkwrap / package-lock.json). ESLint has no rule for lockfile inspection or transitive dep pinning.

eslint-plugin-import's no-extraneous-dependencies rule flags imports of packages not listed in package.json dependencies, catching obviously extraneous deps at import-site; it does not measure overall footprint size or detect redundant/duplicate packages with overlapping functionality.
ESLint is a static code linter with no built-in or widely-bundled rule to detect outdated/vulnerable dependencies; dependency auditing requires npm audit or dedicated SCA tools.
No ESLint rule measures module-level single responsibility; `complexity` measures per-function cyclomatic complexity, not module cohesion or import sprawl at the package boundary.

Core rules `prefer-const`, `no-var`, and `no-param-reassign` (with props:true) enforce immutable bindings and prevent param/property mutation, but deep object/array immutability requires opt-in config or third-party plugins.

`no-implicit-globals` prevents top-level declarations from leaking into global scope and `no-var` reduces mutable module-level bindings, but neither comprehensively detects shared mutable state patterns across modules.
No ESLint core or widely-bundled rule detects non-idempotent operations; idempotency is a semantic property requiring contextual reasoning about external state that AST-based rules cannot determine.
No standard ESLint rule enforces explicit state machines; `switch-exhaustiveness-check` exists only in typescript-eslint and requires type info, not available in plain JS ESLint.

`no-global-assign` (in eslint:recommended) blocks reassignment of native globals and `no-extend-native` (opt-in) blocks prototype extension, but indirect modifications via aliases or user-defined shared resources are not caught.

ESLint rules only enforce comment presence or surface-level lexical non-redundancy; no rule can distinguish 'decision rationale' comments from 'mechanics description' comments, which requires semantic judgment.

ESLint deprecated `require-jsdoc` and `valid-jsdoc` in v5.10 and removed them from core; no standard rule enforces that documentation lives near the code it describes. Documentation proximity is a structural/editorial concern outside ESLint's scope.
Executable/testable documentation (doctest-style) is entirely outside ESLint's scope. ESLint has no rule that verifies code examples in comments compile or run correctly.

`no-var` and `prefer-const` enforce minimal variable scope (block-scoped, immutable where possible), and `no-shadow` prevents unintended scope capture — these cover the JS scoping dimension of least privilege. The broader security principle (minimal API surface, token scopes, permission grants) is semantic and beyond static analysis.

Defence in depth is an architectural property requiring multiple independent validation layers. ESLint has no rule that verifies layered defences exist; individual rules like `no-eval` reduce attack surface but cannot confirm redundant guards are in place.

`no-unsafe-finally` flags control-flow in finally blocks that silently suppress exceptions, and `no-useless-catch` catches rethrow-only catch blocks — these detect specific fail-insecure structural patterns. Whether a default or error path is actually 'secure' requires semantic judgement ESLint cannot perform.
No SonarJS rule detects comments that merely restate what the code says; only formatting rules (e.g. trailing comment position) exist.
SonarJS has no rule that tracks whether new or changed code has unit test coverage; coverage metrics are a SonarQube platform quality gate, not a per-rule JS detector.

source_label: RSPEC-3776: Cognitive Complexity of functions should not be too high
rationale: RSPEC-138 (too many lines) and RSPEC-3776 (cognitive complexity) flag overly large/complex functions as size/complexity proxies, but neither enforces single-responsibility semantically.

source_label: RSPEC-104: Files should not have too many lines of code
rationale: RSPEC-104 flags files with too many lines of code, which is a size proxy for SRP violations in files but does not detect mixed responsibilities semantically.
No SonarJS rule enforces test file naming conventions, describe/it block structure, or test organization patterns in JavaScript.

source_label: RSPEC-1763: All code should be reachable
rationale: RSPEC-1763 detects unreachable code and RSPEC-1481 detects unused local variables and functions; together these cover the standard dead-code patterns deterministically.
SonarJS performs static analysis of source code and has no mechanism to detect whether a changelog file was updated alongside a change.

SonarQube exposes coverage as a project-level quality gate (requires an external coverage reporter like Istanbul/nyc), but there is no SonarJS lint rule that enforces a coverage threshold on its own; it depends on integration setup.

SonarJS has rules requiring test assertions (RSPEC-2699) and exception checks (RSPEC-5958), but cannot semantically distinguish tests that validate requirements/behavior from tests that verify internal implementation details — that distinction requires contextual judgment beyond static pattern matching.

source_label: RSPEC-1192: String literals should not be duplicated
rationale: RSPEC-1192 detects duplicated string literals; RSPEC-109 flags magic numbers (any unnamed numeric literal, not strictly duplicates). Together they cover the two most common literal types, but boolean/null/object-literal duplication are not covered, and RSPEC-109 does not require the number to appear more than once.
No SonarJS rule addresses data/code normalization or removal of semantic ambiguity in the general sense; this requires domain-semantic judgment beyond static pattern detection.
Detecting use of heuristics/approximations where deterministic logic should be used is a semantic design concern; no SonarJS rule addresses this pattern class.

SonarJS has narrow rules that overlap with brittle sub-patterns (e.g. RSPEC-1192 for hardcoded strings), but no rule addresses the general class of brittle patterns such as instanceof type checks, error-message string matching, or fragile conditional chains as a unified concern.
Over-specificity vs. appropriate generalization is a high-level design judgment; no documented SonarJS rule targets this pattern.

RSPEC-1448 ('Classes should not have too many methods') exists for Java/C# in SonarSource but is not documented for JavaScript in SonarJS. SonarJS has function-level size rules (RSPEC-138) but no class-level Large Class detector.

Primitive Obsession (overuse of primitives where domain value objects should be used) is a design smell requiring semantic understanding; no SonarJS rule detects this pattern. RSPEC-1533 (wrapper objects for primitives) is the inverse concern and unrelated.

SonarJS has no rule that detects data clumps (recurring groups of fields/parameters that should be encapsulated together); this design smell requires semantic, cross-location analysis beyond SonarJS's structural rules.

source_label: S1301 – switch with too few cases
rationale: S1301 flags switch statements with fewer than 3 case clauses as a code smell (prefer if-else), which is a syntactic proxy for switch overuse, but SonarJS has no rule recommending polymorphism over switch as a design principle.

No SonarJS rule detects temporary fields (class fields that are only meaningful in certain execution paths); this requires data-flow / lifecycle analysis that SonarJS does not perform for this pattern.

No SonarJS rule detects refused bequest (subclass ignoring or overriding inherited behaviour with empty/throwing methods); the S110 inheritance-depth rule exists for other languages but is not implemented for JavaScript.

No SonarJS rule detects alternative classes with different interfaces; while no-identical-functions catches duplicate implementations, there is no rule for structurally similar classes that should share an interface.
Divergent change is a module-cohesion smell requiring cross-commit or multi-reason change analysis; SonarJS has no rule for this architectural pattern.
Shotgun surgery requires tracking that a single logical change forces edits across many unrelated classes; this cross-file dependency smell is beyond SonarJS's per-file structural analysis.
No SonarJS rule detects parallel inheritance hierarchies; S110 (inheritance depth) is not implemented for JavaScript, and even if it were it would not detect the cross-hierarchy parallelism pattern.
