SonarJS has no rule targeting classes that do too little (Lazy Class). This is a design-level smell requiring semantic judgment about whether a class's abstraction is warranted; no SonarJS rule covers it.
SonarJS has no rule for Data Class (a class with only fields/getters/setters and no behavior). While S2376 enforces getter/setter pairing, no rule flags pure data holders as a smell.
SonarJS has no rule for Speculative Generality (over-engineering for hypothetical future needs). Detecting intent-to-over-engineer requires contextual judgment beyond static pattern matching.

SonarJS S125 (no-commented-code) flags commented-out code blocks, which is one symptom of over-reliance on comments. However, it does not flag excessive explanatory prose comments or comments compensating for unclear code.
SonarJS has no rule for Feature Envy (a method that uses another class's data more than its own). This requires cross-class data-flow analysis beyond SonarJS's rule set.

SonarJS has no rule for Inappropriate Intimacy (two classes with excessive knowledge of each other's internals). Detecting bidirectional coupling at the design level is not covered by any SonarJS rule.
SonarJS has no rule for Message Chains (long a.getB().getC().getD() chains). S6523 covers optional-chaining safety, not chain depth. No chain-length or Law-of-Demeter rule exists in SonarJS.

SonarJS has no rule for Middle Man (a class that mostly delegates to another). Detecting delegation-only classes requires semantic analysis of method bodies across classes, which SonarJS does not provide.
SonarJS has no class-level 'too many methods' or 'too many responsibilities' rule; S1448 exists for Java/PHP/C++ but has no JavaScript equivalent.
No SonarJS rule detects domain objects that contain only data (getters/setters) with no behavior; this requires semantic/design-intent reasoning beyond static pattern matching.

source_label: S1533 - Wrapper objects should not be used for primitive types
rationale: S1533 catches only primitive wrapper objects, not the general Poltergeist pattern (a class with no real state/behavior that acts as a thin transient wrapper); general detection requires semantic reasoning.
S110 (inheritance tree depth) exists for Java/PHP/C# but has no JavaScript implementation in SonarJS; the Yo-Yo problem goes undetected.
No SonarJS rule detects methods that must be called in a specific order without enforcement; this is a semantic/temporal coupling issue beyond static pattern matching.

S1214 ('Interfaces should not solely consist of constants') exists only for Java; JavaScript/TypeScript has no direct equivalent in SonarJS, and the construct manifests differently in JS (objects/enums rather than interfaces).
No SonarJS rule targets interface bloat (too many methods on an interface/class contract); no JavaScript equivalent of a method-count threshold rule exists in the SonarJS catalog.
S6548 ('Singleton design pattern should be used with care') exists only for Java; SonarJS has no equivalent rule for detecting Singleton overuse in JavaScript.

source_label: S107 – Functions should not have too many parameters
rationale: No rule targets combinatorial explosion directly. S107 catches the parameter-count proxy (too many parameters enabling combinatorial call patterns), and S3776/S1541 catch complexity from deeply nested conditions, but the boolean-combination explosion pattern itself is not explicitly addressed.

No SonarJS rule detects tramp data (data threaded through intermediaries that don't use it). S1172 (unused parameters) is not equivalent — tramp data is used by the eventual recipient, not unused. Interprocedural data-flow analysis for this pattern is absent.

source_label: S3776 – Cognitive Complexity of functions should not be too high
rationale: S3776 (Cognitive Complexity) and S1541 (Cyclomatic Complexity) directly target tangled control flow that characterises spaghetti code, but they only catch the complexity symptom, not structural coupling or lack of modularity. Coverage is strong for the control-flow dimension.

source_label: S4144 – Functions should not have identical implementations
rationale: S4144 detects functions with identical implementations, S1871 detects duplicated branch bodies in conditionals, S1192 detects repeated string literals, and SonarJS runs a CPD engine for file-level duplication metrics — together covering the copy-paste smell comprehensively.

No SonarJS rule detects Golden Hammer (over-reliance on a single pattern or library). This requires semantic intent modelling that AST-level static analysis cannot produce; no such rule exists in the SonarJS RSPEC database.

source_label: S1763 – All code should be reachable
rationale: SonarJS catches lava-flow symptoms: S1763 (unreachable code), S1481 (unused variables/functions), S1854 (dead stores), S125 (commented-out code), S1068 (unused private members). It cannot detect why code is frozen in place or that it cannot be safely removed — the defining characteristic of lava flow.

source_label: S109 – Magic numbers should not be used
rationale: SonarJS has purpose-built rules for all major sub-types: S109 (magic numbers), S2068 and S6437 (hardcoded credentials), S1313 (hardcoded IP addresses), S4507 (debug code in production). Together these cover the hard-coding smell comprehensively.

source_label: S2703 – Variables should be declared explicitly
rationale: S2703 (implicit global creation via undeclared variables) and S3798 (explicit global scope declarations) address the creation side of global state that enables action at a distance, but SonarJS does not perform cross-function taint/alias analysis to detect when a distant function mutates shared state in a surprising way.

source_label: S2301 — Methods should not contain selector parameters
rationale: S2301 ('no-selector-parameter') directly flags functions/methods with a boolean parameter that controls which code path executes — the canonical Flag Argument smell. Introduced in SonarJS 10.15.0 for both JS and TS.

S4328 catches imports of packages not declared in package.json (implicit/hidden package dependencies), and S2703 flags accidental global variable creation. Both cover narrow syntactic subsets of hidden dependency; the broader semantic form (relying on singletons, ambient state, or injected globals that are visible in the signature) is not detected.
Premature optimization is a semantic/intent judgment (clarity sacrificed for speculative performance gains). SonarJS has no rule targeting this pattern; it requires human or LLM review.

The Inner-Platform Effect (reimplementing interpreter/framework features inside application code) requires recognizing equivalence between custom code and built-in constructs — beyond static pattern matching. No SonarJS rule covers this.

Big Ball of Mud is a whole-system architectural anti-pattern (pervasive coupling, absence of layering). SonarJS operates at the file/function level and has no rule or built-in detector for this architectural smell.

Stovepipe System (duplicated, siloed subsystems with no shared infrastructure) is an inter-module architectural pattern. SonarJS has code-duplication metrics but no rule that identifies subsystems failing to share common abstractions.
Database-as-IPC (polling a DB table as a message queue) requires understanding the runtime intent of database access patterns. SonarJS has no rule for this architectural anti-pattern.

Vendor lock-in (tight coupling to a specific vendor API without an abstraction layer) requires understanding abstraction boundaries and vendor specificity — a semantic judgment SonarJS has no rule for.

SonarJS has no SRP-specific rule. RSPEC-3776 (cognitive complexity) and RSPEC-107 (too many parameters) are complexity proxies that correlate weakly with SRP violations but do not detect the principle itself — a large class/function can be low-complexity yet violate SRP, and vice versa.

No SonarJS rule addresses the Open/Closed Principle. Detecting OCP violations requires understanding design intent (whether a class should be extended vs. modified), which is beyond static pattern matching.

No SonarJS rule checks Liskov Substitution. RSPEC-110 (inheritance depth) is not implemented for JavaScript/TypeScript (confirmed absent in SonarSource community), and even depth limits do not enforce LSP behavioral contracts.

No SonarJS rule addresses Interface Segregation. JavaScript lacks formal interface constructs, and SonarJS has no rule about overly broad duck-typed or TypeScript interfaces forcing unnecessary method implementation.

No SonarJS rule enforces Dependency Inversion. Detecting that a high-level module directly depends on a concrete low-level module (rather than an abstraction) requires semantic understanding of module roles that static analysis cannot provide.

No SonarJS rule enforces programming to an interface rather than a concrete implementation. This pattern requires understanding design intent and type abstraction choices, not mechanical syntax patterns.

No SonarJS rule encourages composition over inheritance. RSPEC-110 (deep inheritance trees) is not implemented for JavaScript/TypeScript per SonarSource community, and even if it were, capping depth is only a weak proxy for the composition principle.
No SonarJS rule detects failure to encapsulate what varies. This principle requires understanding which behaviors are likely to change — a design-level judgment that static analysis cannot make.

SonarJS has no rule that detects coupling between modules. S3776 measures cognitive complexity within a function, not structural coupling between components. Loose vs tight coupling is an architectural property requiring semantic judgment.
Whether tests pass is a runtime/CI outcome, not a static code property. No static analyzer can detect this class of issue; it is outside the domain of what SonarJS or any linter can judge.

SonarJS has naming-convention rules (e.g. S100 for function naming patterns) but none assess whether names are semantically intention-revealing vs opaque. That judgment is contextual and requires human or LLM evaluation.

source_label: S1192 – String literals should not be duplicated
rationale: S1192 flags repeated string literals and S4144 (no-identical-functions) flags functions with identical implementations, both in the recommended profile. These catch verbatim/mechanical duplication but miss semantic duplication (copy-paste with minor variation, or logically equivalent code in different form).

source_label: S1481 – Unused local variables and functions should be removed
rationale: SonarJS detects the unused-code facet of YAGNI via S1481 (unused local variables), S1172 (unused function parameters), and S6960 (unused imports). However, YAGNI also covers speculative abstractions and over-engineering that no static rule can reliably detect.

source_label: SonarQube Cloud – Design and Architecture overview
rationale: SonarQube's Architecture as Code feature can enforce dependency-direction constraints for JS/TS matching the Clean Architecture Dependency Rule, but only in paid SonarQube Cloud Team/Enterprise plans with explicit YAML configuration. Standard out-of-the-box SonarJS has no such rule.

SonarJS has no rule enforcing separation of concerns into architectural layers. The Architecture as Code feature requires paid SonarQube Cloud with explicit configuration; it is not part of the standard out-of-the-box SonarJS rule set.

source_label: SonarQube Cloud – Design and Architecture overview
rationale: SonarQube Cloud Architecture as Code can detect forbidden cross-layer dependencies (inner depending on outer) for JS/TS when layer constraints are configured, but only in paid Team/Enterprise plans. Out-of-the-box standard SonarJS has no rule for this pattern.

No 'Boy Scout Rule' rule exists, but SonarJS partially covers the spirit via dead-code (S1144, S1763), empty-function (S1186), duplication (S1192), and complexity (S1541) rules — these catch some 'leave it worse' patterns but not the general obligation to improve surrounding code.

SonarJS has no rule enforcing that hexagonal-architecture ports be defined as interfaces/abstract types; architectural layer intent is not detectable by SonarJS for JS/TS (the Architecture module is Java-only).
SonarJS cannot verify that adapter classes implement specific port interfaces in an architectural sense; no rule exists for hexagonal adapter-to-port conformance in JS/TS.
SonarJS has no rule that identifies domain-core files and enforces absence of I/O or framework imports; dependency-direction enforcement for JS/TS is not supported by SonarJS.
No SonarJS rule distinguishes or enforces the driving-side adapter pattern in hexagonal architecture; architectural boundary detection for JS/TS is unavailable in SonarJS.
No SonarJS rule enforces that driven-side adapters (DB, external services) properly implement port contracts; the Architecture module covering this exists only for Java.
Testability-by-design in hexagonal architecture requires structural reasoning about port seams and dependency injection; SonarJS has no rule for this architectural property in JS/TS.
SonarJS for JS/TS has no forbidden-dependency or inward-dependency-direction rule; the SonarQube Architecture module that enforces layer dependency direction is restricted to Java projects.
Reuse/Release Equivalence Principle (package cohesion) is a design-level architectural judgment; SonarJS has no rule that checks whether components grouped for reuse are also grouped for release.
Common Closure Principle (classes that change together belong together) requires semantic reasoning about change reasons; SonarJS has no rule targeting this component cohesion principle.

SonarJS has a circular-dependency rule (S7197) that detects cyclic imports between source files, but it is deprecated, available only on SonarQube Cloud, and covers file-level cycles rather than full component-level Acyclic Dependencies Principle analysis.
Stable Dependencies Principle (stable components should not depend on unstable ones) requires computing instability metrics across the component graph; SonarJS has no such rule.
Stable Abstractions Principle (stable components should be abstract) requires correlating stability and abstractness metrics; SonarJS has no rule for this relationship.
DDD Bounded Context (enforcing domain boundary isolation) is a semantic architectural concept; SonarJS has no rule that identifies or enforces bounded context boundaries.
DDD Ubiquitous Language (consistent domain terminology in code) requires natural-language/semantic understanding of naming against a domain glossary; SonarJS has no such rule.
DDD Domain Model integrity (entities, aggregates, value objects, invariants) requires semantic understanding of domain modelling; SonarJS has no domain-model-aware rules.
SonarJS has no rules for DDD Repository pattern violations; architectural layer enforcement exists as a config feature but there are no automated rule detectors for repository pattern misuse.
SonarJS has no rules detecting DDD Service Layer design violations; no automated rule analyzes service layer responsibilities or cross-layer dependencies in a DDD-specific way.

source_label: S1444 – public static fields should be readonly
rationale: S1444 flags public static fields that aren't readonly (TypeScript only); no JS rule enforces encapsulation of instance fields or collections behind accessor methods — the general refactoring class is not covered.

source_label: S1068 – unused private class members should be removed
rationale: S1068 (unused private members) catches dead API surface, but SonarJS has no rule proactively flagging public members that should be private/unexported — unnecessary exposure is mostly missed.
SonarJS has no rule detecting Tell-Don't-Ask violations; detecting external state queries that should be delegated to the object requires semantic reasoning beyond static pattern matching.
SonarJS has no rule suggesting replacement of inheritance with delegation/composition; no analyzer examines class hierarchies to recommend this structural refactoring.
SonarJS has no equivalent to Java's S110 (deep inheritance tree) for JavaScript/TypeScript; collapsing unnecessary class hierarchies is not covered by any standard rule.
SonarJS has no rule detecting Refused Bequest; detecting which inherited methods are never overridden or used in a subclass requires cross-file inheritance analysis not present in the rule set.

SonarJS naming rules (e.g. RSPEC-2430, RSPEC-3317) enforce syntactic conventions like camelCase/PascalCase; no rule evaluates whether a name actually reveals the programmer's intent vs. hiding it behind abbreviations or meaningless labels.
SonarJS has no rule that enforces classes to be named with nouns and methods with verbs; its naming convention rules check casing patterns, not grammatical part-of-speech.
Enforcing one word per concept (no mixing of 'fetch'/'get'/'retrieve' for the same operation) requires cross-codebase semantic consistency that SonarJS has no rule to detect.
Detecting disinformation in names (e.g. accountList that holds a Map) requires semantic type-vs-name reasoning; SonarJS naming rules only check syntactic casing patterns, not semantic accuracy.

SonarJS RSPEC-3776 (Cognitive Complexity) is a proxy for overall complexity but does not specifically detect mixing of abstraction levels within a function; the clean-architecture concept of one level of abstraction per function is not represented by any SonarJS rule.
Command-Query Separation is not addressed by any SonarJS rule; the tool has no CQS-specific detector for functions that both mutate state and return a value.

SonarJS RSPEC-2201 flags ignored return values of known-pure functions (the inverse problem), but no SonarJS rule detects hidden side effects in functions; general side-effect enforcement requires semantic analysis beyond the tool's scope.

source_label: RSPEC-2301 - Methods should not contain selector parameters
rationale: SonarJS S2301 directly targets the boolean flag-argument code smell, but covers only boolean-typed selectors and has explicit exceptions (object-property flags, event-handler callbacks), so non-boolean selector parameters and excepted patterns are missed.

S107 ('Functions should not have too many parameters') flags functions exceeding a configurable parameter count and explicitly cites grouping parameters into an object as the remedy — a direct mechanical proxy for this refactoring.

S3776 (cognitive complexity) flags deeply nested conditionals that exceed a threshold, which is the symptom guard clauses fix, but no SonarJS rule specifically detects nested-if structures and recommends guard-clause / early-return refactoring. S1126 ('prefer-single-boolean-return') is only about boolean return simplification, not general guard clauses.

No SonarJS rule detects type-dispatch switch/if-else chains (using typeof/instanceof) and suggests replacing them with polymorphism. Searches of the SonarJS rule catalog and community forums found no such rule for JavaScript.

No SonarJS rule detects imperative for-loops that could be replaced with map/filter/reduce pipelines. Searches found no such rule; S7728 (use for...of instead of forEach) is unrelated to this refactoring direction.
No SonarJS rule detects slow-running tests or tests with performance issues. No equivalent of Java's RSPEC-5973 ('Tests should be stable') exists for JavaScript/TypeScript in SonarJS.
No SonarJS rule detects test interdependencies or shared mutable state between tests. The SonarJS test-quality rule set does not cover test isolation or ordering dependencies.
No SonarJS rule detects non-deterministic or flaky tests. No JavaScript equivalent of Java's test-stability rules exists in SonarJS.

S2699 ('Tests should include assertions' / assertions-in-tests) exists and flags test cases with no assertions, covering chai/sinon by default. However, it has documented false negatives with vitest and some other modern frameworks, and cross-file assertion detection is limited — so it misses a meaningful subset of assertion-free tests.

Timely is a TDD discipline (write tests before or alongside code, not after); SonarJS has no rule that can detect when tests were written relative to implementation — it only analyzes code structure, not commit order or authoring sequence.

Red-Green-Refactor is a workflow cycle discipline; static analysis cannot observe the sequence of test writing, test failure, implementation, and refactoring — SonarJS has no rule targeting this practice.

Fake It Till You Make It (starting with hardcoded return values to pass a test) is a transient TDD strategy; SonarJS has no rule that distinguishes a hardcoded stub value left as a TDD stepping stone from any other constant expression.
Triangulation (adding multiple test cases to force a general solution) is a TDD design technique observable only in the test-writing process over time; SonarJS has no rule for this pattern.

Assert First (write the assertion before the code under test) is a TDD authoring-order discipline; SonarJS rules check that assertions exist (S2699) and are well-formed, but cannot detect whether assertions were written before or after the implementation.

SonarJS can surface test coverage metrics (via imported coverage reports) but that is a CI metric, not an off-the-shelf static-analysis rule; there is no SonarJS rule that flags a class or module as 'legacy' due to lack of tests.

Characterization Tests (tests that pin existing legacy behavior) are a human-intent concept; SonarJS has no rule that can classify a test as characterization vs. specification, as both look identical syntactically.
Sprout Method/Class (adding new well-tested methods/classes alongside legacy code) is a refactoring strategy; SonarJS has no rule that identifies this structural pattern or its absence.

Wrap Method/Class is a refactoring pattern (adding behavior by wrapping existing methods or classes); SonarJS has no rule that detects when this pattern should be applied — it requires semantic/design judgement.

JavaScript has no formal interface construct (unlike Java/C#), so 'Extract Interface' as a refactoring category does not apply to JS. TypeScript has interfaces but SonarJS has no rule suggesting when to extract one.

source_label: javascript:S109 – Magic numbers should not be used
rationale: RSPEC-109 flags magic number literals, which is a syntactic proxy for values that should become parameters, but it covers only numeric literals and does not detect the general case of hard-coded constructor/method arguments that should be parameterized.

source_label: typescript:S4204 – The 'any' type should not be used
rationale: RSPEC-4204 (TypeScript-only) flags use of the `any` type, discouraging the escape hatch from typing and nudging toward explicit types — one facet of gradual typing adoption — but does not enforce a broader gradual-typing strategy and only applies to TypeScript files.

RSPEC-4322 (TypeScript-only, prefer-type-guard) recommends replacing ad-hoc type checks with explicit type guard functions, covering a subset of narrowing scenarios, but is TypeScript-only and misses incorrect narrowing patterns in plain JS.

No SonarJS rule targets prototype pollution attacks (e.g., unsafe assignment to __proto__, Object.prototype, or constructor). Taint-based detection for this class of vulnerability is a confirmed gap in SonarJS's rule set.

source_label: javascript:S3798 – Variables and functions should not be declared in the global scope
rationale: RSPEC-3798 (declarations-in-global-scope) directly flags variables and functions declared at global scope in JavaScript, mechanically catching this entire class of pollution.

Implicitly threading request context through globals or thread-locals into model/domain layers is an architectural anti-pattern requiring design-level judgement; SonarJS has no rule targeting this pattern.

source_label: S1523 – Dynamically executing code is security-sensitive
rationale: S1523 directly flags all uses of eval() and other dynamic code execution APIs (Function constructor, setTimeout/setInterval with string arguments). Covers the class comprehensively.

source_label: S3525 – Class methods should be used instead of 'prototype' assignments
rationale: S3525 flags prototype assignments for methods (e.g. Foo.prototype.method = function…) and nudges toward class syntax, which catches the most common monkey-patching pattern. Does not cover adding arbitrary new properties to built-in prototypes (Array.prototype.foo = …) or dynamic property injection patterns, so coverage is partial.

Duck typing discipline (checking that objects carry expected properties/methods before use) is inherent to JavaScript's dynamic nature and has no corresponding SonarJS rule. TypeScript-specific type-guard rules exist but SonarJS plain-JS analysis has no mechanism to enforce structural type discipline.

Whether lint failures are wired as a hard build gate is a CI/process configuration concern, not a code pattern. No static-analysis rule can detect that a project is or is not failing its build on linter errors — this is outside SonarJS's scope.

SonarQube Quality Gates can be configured to fail a build when coverage drops below a threshold, providing enforcement. However this is opt-in platform configuration, not an always-on rule, so it is partial rather than complete.

Pinning and validating external contracts (dependency version pinning, API schema validation, contract testing) is not addressed by any SonarJS rule. S6774 (React prop-types) is scoped to component interfaces only and does not generalise to external contract validation or dependency pinning.

SonarJS has no rule enforcing structured (machine-parseable) log output. S5757 is about preventing sensitive data from appearing in logs — a privacy rule, not a log-format rule. No SonarJS rule requires or checks for JSON/key-value structured logging.

No SonarJS rule exists for correlation ID propagation or instrumentation patterns. This is an observability concern requiring semantic understanding of request context flow, well beyond static pattern matching.
SonarJS has no rule for health check or readiness probe implementation; this is an operational/infrastructure concern outside static code analysis scope.
Alerting on symptoms vs causes is an observability design principle requiring semantic judgment; no SonarJS rule covers this.
Postel's Law (robustness principle) is an API design philosophy requiring semantic understanding; SonarJS has no rule for it.
Semantic versioning enforcement is a release/package management convention; SonarJS has no rule that validates semver compliance in version strings or changelogs.
Backwards compatibility requires semantic reasoning about API surface changes across versions; no SonarJS rule detects breaking API changes.

Explicit error contracts (stable error codes, structured error responses) require reasoning about API contract design; SonarJS error-handling rules cover promise/exception mechanics but not error response schema conventions.

source_label: S6957 – Use ranges, don't pin dependency versions in package.json
rationale: SonarJS rule S6957 flags pinned exact versions in package.json and recommends ranges, touching the version-pinning surface but not transitive lock-file pinning strategies; coverage is narrow and inverted in framing.
SonarJS has no rule for detecting unused or excess npm dependencies (minimal dependency footprint); tools like depcheck handle this, not SonarJS.

SonarJS is a static code analyser; dependency vulnerability/freshness auditing is a separate SCA capability (SonarQube Advanced Security / Dependency-Check integration) — no SonarJS rule scans package manifests for outdated or vulnerable dependencies.
No SonarJS rule enforces single-responsibility at the package or module boundary; cohesion analysis at that granularity is beyond static AST-level checking and no documented rule targets it.

source_label: S3353 – Unchanged local variables should be 'const'
rationale: S3353 flags `let` variables that are never reassigned (prefer `const`) and S3504 bans `var` in favour of `let`/`const`, nudging toward immutability for local bindings — but neither rule covers object mutation (Object.freeze, readonly fields, deep immutability), so the general 'prefer immutability' principle is only partially enforced.

Share-nothing architecture is an architectural/deployment concern (no shared mutable state across service boundaries); SonarJS has no rule that detects cross-module or cross-service shared state at this level — S2703 only catches accidental implicit globals, a narrow syntactic subset unrelated to architectural isolation.
No SonarJS rule detects whether an operation is idempotent or not; idempotency is a semantic/behavioural property that cannot be inferred from local AST patterns.

SonarJS has no rule that requires state machines to be modelled explicitly (e.g. with XState or enum-guarded transitions); this is an architectural design pattern beyond the scope of any documented SonarJS rule.

source_label: S2703 – Variables should be declared explicitly (no-implicit-global)
rationale: S2703 (BLOCKER) flags implicit global variable creation and S2424 prevents overriding built-in globals — together they catch two narrow forms of unsafe global/shared resource access, but the broader principle (unguarded writes to process.env, shared caches, singleton side-effects) is not covered.

S125 removes commented-out code but does not analyse whether comments explain intent ('why') vs. mechanics ('what'); SonarJS has no rule that evaluates comment quality or flags comments that merely restate the code.
SonarJS has no rule enforcing that documentation is kept physically close to the code it describes. S125 only flags commented-out code blocks, not misplaced or orphaned doc comments.
No SonarJS rule exists for executable documentation or doctests. JavaScript has no native doctest convention and SonarJS ships no rule in this space.

source_label: S2612: Setting loose POSIX file permissions is security-sensitive
rationale: S2612 covers POSIX file permissions (chmod-style overly broad modes) and S5604 covers intrusive browser permission APIs (geolocation, camera, mic). Both are narrow, domain-specific subsets of least privilege; broader violations such as over-permissioned tokens, excessive API scopes, or general access control over-grants are not covered.

No SonarJS rule detects missing layered security controls or single-point-of-failure validation. Defence in depth is referenced as a conceptual framework in SonarQube documentation but has no corresponding detector rule.

source_label: S2486: Exceptions should not be ignored (empty catch blocks)
rationale: S2486 flags empty catch blocks (swallowed exceptions), a syntactic proxy for some fail-open patterns, but does not detect the broader semantic class: catch blocks that actively grant access, return true, or enable functionality that should be denied on error. The dangerous fail-open pattern requires semantic reasoning SonarJS cannot perform.

Biome has no rule that detects semantically redundant comments (comments that merely restate what the code does); evaluating comment semantics requires natural-language understanding beyond static pattern matching.

Biome is a linter/formatter and has no coverage instrumentation or change-aware test-gap detection; identifying untested new/changed code requires a coverage tool (Istanbul, c8, Vitest coverage) external to Biome.

Biome has noExcessiveCognitiveComplexity and noExcessiveLinesPerFunction as complexity-proxy rules that can surface functions doing too many things, but both are opt-in (not recommended/default) and catch length/complexity symptoms rather than true SRP violations.
Biome has no rule for detecting file-level SRP violations (e.g. too many classes or concepts per file); this requires semantic/architectural judgement unavailable to a static linter.

Biome has noSkippedTests and noFocusedTests (both recommended) to catch .skip/.only misuse, but has no rules enforcing describe-block structure, test naming conventions, or broader test organization patterns.
Biome ships noUnreachable, noUnusedVariables, noUnusedImports, and noUnusedFunctionParameters all in the recommended ruleset, covering the main mechanical classes of dead code reliably.
Biome is a JS/TS linter and has no awareness of non-code files like CHANGELOG.md; changelog update checks require CI scripts or git hooks outside Biome's scope.
Biome has no coverage instrumentation or threshold enforcement; explicit test coverage metrics require dedicated coverage tools (Jest, Vitest, c8) configured independently.
Biome has no rule that distinguishes tests verifying public behavior from tests verifying internal implementation details; this requires semantic/contextual judgment.
noMagicNumbers (opt-in, v2.1.0) flags repeated numeric literals; Biome has no equivalent rule for duplicated string literals across a codebase, so string-constant duplication is missed.
Normalizing data/code to remove ambiguity is a semantic design concern with no corresponding Biome rule.
Replacing heuristics with deterministic logic requires semantic understanding of algorithmic intent; Biome has no rule targeting this class of issue.
Brittle patterns require contextual judgment about fragility; Biome has no rule detecting brittleness as a class.
Over-specificity vs. appropriate generalization is a design-level concern requiring semantic judgment; Biome has no rule for this.
Biome has no rule measuring class size by method count or lines; noExcessiveClassesPerFile counts classes per file, not size of individual classes.
Biome has no rule detecting over-reliance on primitives as a domain modeling smell; noBannedTypes only flags boxed wrapper types, not primitive obsession in general.
Data Clumps require identifying groups of parameters/fields that habitually co-occur across multiple call sites or class definitions — a cross-location semantic pattern that Biome has no rule for.

source_label: noFallthroughSwitchClause (representative switch rule — none covers the polymorphism smell)
rationale: Biome's switch-related rules (noFallthroughSwitchClause, noUselessSwitchCase, useDefaultSwitchClause, useExhaustiveSwitchCases) address correctness/style concerns, not the design-smell of using switch instead of polymorphism. No rule flags switch-as-OO-smell.

Temporary Field (a class field only meaningful under certain conditions) requires understanding conditional initialization patterns across methods. noUnusedPrivateClassMembers catches never-used fields but not conditionally-used ones; no Biome rule targets this smell.

Refused Bequest (subclass that ignores inherited methods/fields) requires cross-class semantic analysis of inheritance usage. No Biome rule analyzes inheritance hierarchies for unused inherited members.

Alternative Classes with Different Interfaces requires comparing method signatures across semantically equivalent but unrelated classes — a cross-file semantic pattern entirely outside Biome's rule set.

Divergent Change (a class modified for many unrelated reasons) is a design-level smell requiring understanding of a class's responsibility surface over time. Static analysis cannot detect this; no Biome rule addresses it.

Shotgun Surgery (one logical change scattered across many classes) requires change-coupling analysis across the codebase. Even Biome v2.5's cross-file linting targets specific patterns (unused exports etc.), not change-coupling smells.
Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies require detecting structural mirroring between two class hierarchies — a cross-file architectural pattern with no corresponding Biome rule.

noStaticOnlyClass flags classes used as static namespaces (all-static members), which is one narrow manifestation of Lazy Class, but does not detect the general case of a class with minimal responsibilities or too few methods/fields.
Biome has no rule targeting classes with only fields/getters/setters and no real behavior (Data Class). useGroupedAccessorPairs enforces accessor ordering, not detection of data-only classes.

Speculative Generality is a design-intent smell requiring semantic judgment about whether abstractions/parameters are premature. noUnused* rules catch incidentally dead code but cannot detect over-engineered-but-used constructs.
Biome has no rule that detects over-reliance on comments to explain code; its comment-related rules (noCommentText, useSingleJsDocAsterisk) address formatting, not explanatory overuse.
Feature Envy requires cross-class data-access frequency analysis, which is a semantic/architectural check Biome does not perform; no relevant rule exists.

Inappropriate Intimacy requires detecting when two classes access each other's internals excessively. Biome's encapsulation rules (useConsistentMemberAccessibility, noUnusedPrivateClassMembers) enforce modifiers but do not detect cross-class coupling patterns.
Biome has no rule detecting long method/property chains (a.getB().getC().getD()). noExcessiveNestedCallbacks targets nested callback depth, not chained call expressions.

Middle Man (a class that mostly delegates to another) requires semantic analysis of delegation ratios across methods. No Biome rule targets this pattern; useThisInClassMethods (nursery, opt-in) is an indirect proxy at best and does not reliably identify it.
Biome has no rule that measures class size, method count, or responsibility scope — God Object detection requires semantic class-level analysis beyond Biome's syntax-oriented ruleset.
Detecting Anemic Domain Model (data classes with no behavior) requires understanding object intent and domain semantics; Biome has no rule for this pattern.
Poltergeist detection requires recognising that a class is a trivial pass-through intermediary; Biome has no structural or semantic rule for delegation patterns of this kind.
Biome has no rule tracking inheritance depth or flagging deep class hierarchies; Yo-Yo Problem analysis is beyond its scope.
Sequential Coupling (enforced method-call ordering) requires inter-procedural call-graph analysis; Biome performs no such analysis and has no related rule.
Biome has no rule that detects interfaces or abstract classes composed solely of constants (Constant Interface anti-pattern).
Biome has no rule that caps the number of members in a TypeScript interface or flags Interface Segregation Principle violations.
Biome has no rule that recognises the Singleton pattern or flags its overuse; singleton detection requires structural class analysis not present in Biome.
Biome has no rule that detects combinatorial explosion — exponential branching from parameter/condition combinations is a semantic pattern requiring data-flow analysis beyond Biome's scope.
No Biome rule detects tramp data. noUnusedFunctionParameters flags truly unused params but cannot distinguish an unused param from one that is only forwarded deeper in a call chain.

noExcessiveCognitiveComplexity (since v1.0.0) catches high-complexity functions; noNestedTernary catches deeply nested ternaries — both require explicit opt-in and cover only structural proxies, not general spaghetti control flow.
Biome has no general code-duplication detector. Rules like noDuplicateObjectKeys only catch duplicate keys in object literals, not copy-pasted logic or code blocks.
Golden Hammer (systematic overuse of one pattern throughout a codebase) requires cross-file semantic analysis; Biome has no such rule.

noUnusedVariables (recommended/default since v1.0.0) and noUnreachable (recommended/default since v1.0.0) catch dead local variables and unreachable statements, but miss dead exported symbols and intentionally-kept legacy code that remains reachable.

noMagicNumbers (since v2.1.0) catches numeric literals but requires explicit opt-in, has many exceptions (0,1,2, array indices, enum values, etc.), and no Biome rules cover hardcoded strings, URLs, or credentials.

noGlobalAssign (recommended/default since v1.5.0) prevents reassignment of built-in globals, covering the clearest form of action-at-a-distance. Arbitrary global state mutations via module-level variables or implicit side effects are not detected.

No Biome rule targets the flag-argument anti-pattern (a boolean parameter that switches function behavior); complexity rules like useMaxParams limit parameter count but do not flag this specific design smell.

useExhaustiveDependencies (default-on) catches hidden dependencies in React hooks; noProcessGlobal, noDocumentCookie, noGlobalAssign address specific global-state accesses, but no rule covers the general hidden-dependency pattern across arbitrary JS code.

Biome's performance rules (noDelete, noAccumulatingSpread, noAwaitInLoops) flag known bad-performance idioms, not the design anti-pattern of optimizing prematurely before profiling; that requires judgment beyond AST analysis.

Multiple rules (useFlatMap, useArrayFind, useIncludes, useDateNow, useStringStartsEndsWith, useMathMinMax) catch reimplementation of specific built-in constructs, covering a meaningful subset of inner-platform violations but not the general semantic case.
Big Ball of Mud is a global architectural smell requiring cross-module structural analysis; Biome operates at the AST level per file and has no rule for this pattern.
Stovepipe System is a cross-module architectural anti-pattern about siloed subsystems with no shared reuse; no Biome rule addresses this structural concern.
Database-as-IPC is a runtime/architectural pattern detectable only through understanding inter-process communication intent; Biome has no rule for this.

noRestrictedImports can be configured to block vendor-specific SDK imports (e.g. aws-sdk/*), but it is not enabled by default and requires explicit per-vendor configuration, making it opt-in rather than automatic detection.

Biome has several proxy rules: noStaticOnlyClass (recommended, flags utility classes that violate SRP by bundling unrelated statics) and opt-in noExcessiveCognitiveComplexity / noExcessiveLinesPerFunction / noExcessiveClassesPerFile flag oversized functions/files. These catch symptoms correlated with SRP violations but cannot detect the general semantic case (a short class can still violate SRP).

Biome has no rule that detects Open/Closed Principle violations. OCP requires semantic reasoning about extensibility intent — whether a class is closed for modification but open for extension — which is beyond static pattern matching.

Biome has no rule that checks Liskov Substitution. Rules like noInvalidConstructorSuper verify syntactic super() usage, not behavioral subtype conformance (whether a subclass honors the superclass contract). LSP violations require semantic/type-flow analysis beyond Biome's scope.

noEmptyInterface catches one narrow anti-pattern (empty interface) but does not detect fat interfaces or ISP violations in general. No Biome rule identifies interfaces with too many unrelated members or clients forced to depend on methods they don't use.

noImportCycles (opt-in) detects circular imports, which can be a symptom of DIP violations, but does not detect the core DIP problem (high-level modules importing concrete low-level implementations rather than abstractions). No Biome rule enforces depending on abstractions.

Biome has no rule enforcing the GoF 'program to an interface' principle. Detecting whether code binds to a concrete type instead of an abstraction requires type-flow/semantic analysis that Biome does not perform.

Biome has no general rule preferring composition over inheritance. React-specific rules (useReactFunctionComponents) encourage functional style in that narrow context but cannot generalize to detecting inappropriate inheritance hierarchies across a codebase.

Biome has no rule for the GoF 'encapsulate what varies' principle. Identifying which behavior should be extracted and encapsulated requires understanding of design intent and variability, which is a semantic/architectural judgment beyond static analysis.

Biome has no rules for measuring coupling strength or enforcing loose coupling between modules. The closest rule (noImportCycles) only detects circular imports, not coupling degree or dependency inversion.
Biome has no rules that verify tests exist, pass, or have adequate coverage. Test passage is a runtime/CI concern, entirely outside the scope of static linting.

Biome enforces naming conventions (useNamingConvention) and flags magic numbers (noMagicNumbers), which partially address intention-revealing names, but cannot detect semantically poor or misleading names, opaque logic, or unclear abstractions.
Biome has no code-duplication detection rules. DRY violations (repeated logic blocks, copy-pasted code) require cross-location semantic comparison that Biome does not perform.

Biome detects locally unused code (noUnusedVariables, noUnusedImports, noUnreachable, noUselessConstructor, etc.) which catches a narrow subset of YAGNI violations, but cannot detect speculative features, over-engineered abstractions, or premature generalisation.

Biome has no concept of architectural layers or the Dependency Rule (outer layers must not be imported by inner layers). noRestrictedImports can enforce import bans but requires manual per-project configuration of every forbidden path, not a built-in architectural rule.

Biome has no rules for detecting mixed concerns or enforcing separation of layers. A few React-specific rules (noNestedComponentDefinitions, useComponentExportOnlyModules) are narrow UI-framework heuristics, not general separation-of-concerns enforcement.

Biome cannot detect inner-layer knowledge of outer layers (e.g. domain code importing from infrastructure). This requires understanding of architectural boundaries that Biome does not model; noImportCycles only catches circular references, not directional dependency violations.
Boy Scout Rule ('leave code cleaner than you found it') is a human/process discipline about incremental improvement; no static-analysis rule can enforce it. Biome has no such rule.
Whether ports are defined as interfaces is a semantic architectural convention; Biome has no rule enforcing hexagonal port definitions.
Verifying that adapters implement the correct port interfaces requires understanding of architectural role assignments; Biome has no such structural rule.
Enforcing that the hexagonal core is pure (no I/O, no framework imports) requires semantic knowledge of which modules are 'core'; Biome has no domain/layer purity rule.
Identifying and enforcing the driving side (primary adapters/actors) of hexagonal architecture is a semantic architectural concept; Biome has no such rule.
Identifying and enforcing the driven side (secondary adapters) of hexagonal architecture is a semantic architectural concept; Biome has no such rule.
Testability by design (ensuring the core is independently testable via ports) is an architectural quality judgment; no Biome rule covers this.

Biome's noImportCycles detects circular imports but does not enforce directional dependency constraints (inward-only in hexagonal architecture); layer-aware dependency direction rules do not exist in Biome.
Reuse/Release Equivalence is a package-cohesion design principle (components reused together should be released together). Biome has no rule that measures or enforces release-unit cohesion.

Common Closure Principle (classes that change together belong in the same component) requires semantic change-coupling analysis. Biome has no rule addressing component-level closure or co-change grouping.

Biome's noImportCycles detects circular import chains in the module graph (ADP violations), but the rule is not recommended/default — it must be explicitly enabled in biome.json, and it only flags cycles, not the broader dependency-direction discipline ADP implies.
Stable Dependencies Principle requires computing instability metrics (efferent vs. afferent coupling) across the module graph. Biome has no rule that measures or enforces dependency stability ratios.

Stable Abstractions Principle requires measuring abstractness (abstract classes/interfaces vs. concrete classes) per component and correlating with instability. Biome has no rule for abstractness-vs-stability analysis.

noPrivateImports (recommended, on by default) enforces JSDoc @private/@package visibility tags to block cross-boundary imports — a syntactic proxy for bounded-context isolation. noRestrictedImports can add explicit cross-context import bans with config. Neither understands DDD bounded-context semantics; enforcement requires manual annotation/configuration.

Ubiquitous Language requires that code identifiers match the domain vocabulary, which is a semantic judgement Biome cannot make. useNamingConvention enforces syntactic patterns (casing, prefixes) but has no awareness of domain terminology correctness.

Domain Model structural patterns (entities, aggregates, value objects, domain services) require semantic understanding of domain design intent. Biome has no rules targeting domain model structure or DDD tactical patterns.
Biome has no rules for DDD Repository pattern concerns (e.g., business logic leaking into persistence layer); these require architectural/semantic analysis beyond static linting.
Biome has no rules for DDD Service Layer violations (domain logic misplaced in service classes); requires understanding of domain model structure beyond Biome's scope.

Biome has no rule that flags public fields that should be private or detects direct external mutation of collections; useReadonlyClassProperties only enforces readonly on never-modified properties, not encapsulation of access.
Biome has no rule for detecting unnecessary public API surface (indecent exposure); noDefaultExport concerns module exports style, not the broader concern of exposing internal implementation details.
Biome has no rule for Tell Don't Ask / feature envy patterns; detecting state-query-then-act sequences requires inter-object semantic analysis not available in Biome.
Biome has no rule suggesting composition over inheritance or flagging subclasses that should be replaced with delegation; this is a structural design smell beyond static linting.
Biome has no rule detecting overly deep or superfluous class hierarchies (collapse hierarchy); inheritance depth analysis is not provided by Biome.
Biome has no rule detecting Refused Bequest (subclasses that throw in inherited methods or do nothing, violating LSP); LSP-level behavioral analysis is outside Biome's capabilities.

Biome's useNamingConvention enforces casing and format patterns but cannot evaluate whether a name is semantically intention-revealing — that requires understanding the identifier's purpose in context, which is a semantic/LLM judgement.

Biome has no rule that enforces classes to use noun names or methods to use verb names; useNamingConvention only enforces casing formats (camelCase, PascalCase, etc.), not grammatical/semantic categories.

source_label: Biome rules index (no matching rule found)
rationale: Biome has no rule that detects inconsistent or synonymous terminology across a codebase (e.g., using both 'fetch' and 'get' for the same concept); this requires semantic/contextual judgement.

source_label: Biome rules index (no matching rule found)
rationale: Avoiding disinformation in names (e.g., naming a non-list variable 'accountList') requires understanding the named entity's actual type or behaviour — beyond what any static pattern matcher can do.

Biome's noExcessiveCognitiveComplexity caps raw complexity but does not detect mixed abstraction levels within a single function; recognising when low-level detail is interleaved with high-level orchestration requires semantic understanding.

source_label: Biome rules index (no matching rule found)
rationale: Biome has no rule for Command-Query Separation; determining whether a function both mutates state and returns a meaningful value without side-effect intent requires semantic analysis not present in the rule set.

Biome has no general no-side-effects rule; noAssignInExpressions only flags assignments embedded inside expressions (a narrow syntactic anti-pattern), not the broader principle that functions should not produce observable side effects.

source_label: Biome rules index (no matching rule found)
rationale: Biome has no rule that detects boolean flag parameters used to toggle function behaviour; identifying this design smell requires understanding the parameter's role across call sites.
Biome has no rule that detects functions with too many parameters as a signal to introduce a parameter object; parameter-count thresholds are entirely absent from its rule set.

noUselessElse removes redundant else blocks after early returns but is non-recommended (opt-in only) and covers only that narrow syntactic case, not the general 'replace nested conditionals with guard clauses' pattern; no other Biome rule targets deep nesting or guard-clause promotion.
Biome has no rule that detects type-discriminating switch/if-else chains as candidates for polymorphic dispatch; this is a semantic refactoring pattern beyond static rule coverage.

useForOf (non-default) suggests for-of over index-based for loops but does not recommend map/filter/reduce pipelines; no Biome rule detects imperative loops as candidates for functional pipeline refactoring.
Biome has no rule that detects slow tests or excessive async timeouts; test performance is a runtime concern outside static analysis scope.
Biome has no rule detecting tests that share state or depend on execution order; noDuplicateTestHooks catches duplicate hook declarations but not inter-test dependencies or shared mutable state.
Biome has no rule detecting tests that depend on wall-clock time, randomness, or environment variables; repeatability violations require semantic/runtime analysis.
Biome has no rule that flags test functions lacking assertions (no expect()/assert() call check); this would require test-framework-aware analysis not present in Biome's rule set.
Biome has no rule that can detect whether tests were written in a timely manner relative to the code they cover; this is a process/workflow concern, not a static code pattern.
Red-Green-Refactor is a TDD workflow discipline; Biome cannot observe commit sequencing or test-vs-production code ordering — no static rule exists for this.
Fake It Till You Make It (returning hardcoded values initially) is a TDD implementation micro-tactic not detectable by any Biome rule; no such rule exists.
Triangulation (adding test cases to force generalization) is a TDD process pattern requiring semantic understanding of test intent; Biome has no rule that models this.
Assert First is a TDD authoring strategy; Biome's useTestHooksOnTop enforces hook ordering but not assertion-first writing order within a test body.
Detecting code without tests requires cross-file coverage analysis; Biome is a syntax/AST linter with no rule linking production code to the absence of test files or coverage.
Characterization tests are a human-intent category indistinguishable from ordinary tests at the AST level; Biome has no rule for this pattern.
Sprout Method/Class is a legacy-code refactoring technique; Biome has no rule that detects whether new methods were introduced as sprouts rather than modifying existing code.
Wrap Method/Class is a refactoring heuristic requiring semantic understanding of design intent; Biome has no rule that detects or suggests this pattern.
Extract Interface is a refactoring opportunity that requires semantic analysis of class structure and usage; Biome has no rule for detecting this.
Parameterize Constructor/Method (making implicit dependencies explicit via parameters) is an architectural/DI pattern; Biome has no rule targeting this class of issue.

noExplicitAny (recommended) flags explicit `any` annotations; noImplicitAnyLet catches untyped `let` declarations — but these cover only a subset of 'move toward full typing' (e.g., missing return-type annotations, JS files, or inference gaps are not all caught).

Biome has no rule that detects missing or incorrect type narrowing; adjacent rules (noNonNullAssertion, useValidTypeof) touch related patterns but do not flag the general 'should narrow here' class of issue.
noPrototypeBuiltins only prevents calling prototype methods directly on objects (safe-usage pattern), not assignment to Object.prototype itself; no Biome rule detects prototype mutation/pollution.

noGlobalAssign (recommended) catches reassignment of existing read-only globals (e.g. undefined, Object), but does not detect adding new properties to global scope or accidental implicit globals; only a subset of global-scope pollution is covered.
Explicit over implicit (no request context in models) is an architectural design pattern requiring contextual/semantic judgement; Biome has no rule targeting this class of issue.

noGlobalEval (recommended) catches direct eval() calls; noImpliedEval (nursery, not in recommended set) catches new Function() and string-arg setTimeout/setInterval. Together they cover most dynamic execution, but noImpliedEval requires opt-in and noGlobalEval alone misses new Function().

noPrototypeBuiltins flags unsafe *calls* to Object.prototype methods (e.g. foo.hasOwnProperty), and noProto flags __proto__ access — neither detects assignment to built-in prototypes (Array.prototype.x = ...) which is the core monkey-patching pattern.
Duck typing discipline is an architectural convention enforced by type-system design or code review; Biome has no rule that detects or enforces structural-vs-nominal type discipline choices.
Lint-as-build-failure is a CI/CD pipeline configuration concern, not a code pattern a linter rule can detect in source files; no static analysis rule applies here.

Biome has test-quality rules (noSkippedTests, noFocusedTests, useExpect) but none enforce coverage thresholds or require tests to exist; coverage enforcement requires dedicated tools like Istanbul/c8.
noUndeclaredDependencies checks that imports reference declared deps, but no Biome rule enforces version pinning in package.json or validates external API contracts/schemas.

noConsole (not recommended by default, requires opt-in) discourages console usage and can nudge toward structured loggers, but it does not enforce structured/JSON logging patterns or validate logger API usage.
No Biome rule exists for correlation ID propagation in logging; this is a semantic/architectural concern requiring contextual judgment.
Health check / readiness probe presence is an infrastructure and runtime concern; no Biome rule exists that can statically detect whether a service exposes a health endpoint.
Alerting strategy (symptoms vs causes) is a monitoring/ops concern entirely outside static code analysis; Biome has no rule that can distinguish symptom-based from cause-based alert configurations.
Postel's Law (robustness principle) is an architectural/design principle about API input leniency and output strictness; Biome has no rule that enforces or detects violations of this principle.
Semantic versioning correctness lives in package.json metadata and release tooling; Biome analyses source code syntax and has no rule for validating semver strings or version bump correctness.
Detecting backwards-compatibility breaks requires comparing the current API surface to a prior version; this is a cross-revision semantic analysis that static per-file linting cannot perform.

Biome has useThrowOnlyError, useThrowNewError, useErrorMessage, and useErrorCause which enforce disciplined error creation (throw proper Error objects with messages and cause chains), covering injectable cases like throwing non-Error values; however the broader notion of 'explicit error contract' (e.g. documenting all thrown error types via @throws or a typed error union) is not mechanically enforced.
Pinning transitive (sub-)dependencies is a package-manager and lockfile concern; Biome does not analyse package.json dependency trees or lockfiles and has no rule for this.

Biome's noBarrelFile, noNamespaceImport, and noReExportAll rules catch import patterns that bloat bundles and inflate dependency surface, covering the injectable namespace/barrel anti-patterns; they do not detect unused package.json dependencies or over-broad third-party imports that are individually named.
Dependency auditing (CVE scanning, npm audit) is a runtime/CI workflow concern. Biome has no rule that checks for known-vulnerable package versions or triggers security advisories.

Single responsibility at the package level (what belongs in a package.json) is an architectural design concern that Biome cannot evaluate statically; its complexity rules target functions and classes, not package scope.

Biome enforces mechanical immutability patterns via useConst (recommended, flags let-declared variables that are never reassigned) and opt-in rules noParameterAssign and useReadonlyClassProperties, but cannot detect semantic immutability violations on objects, arrays, or mutable data structures passed by reference.
Share-nothing architecture is an architectural principle about mutable state isolation across modules or processes; Biome has no rule that enforces or detects cross-module shared state.
Idempotency is a behavioral/semantic property of functions that cannot be determined by static pattern matching; Biome has no rule targeting idempotency.
Making state machines explicit is a design-pattern enforcement concern; Biome has no rule that requires or detects state machine implementations.

Assuming ownership of shared or global resources is a semantic concurrency/design concern; while noParameterAssign discourages mutating function arguments, it does not cover shared globals or the broader ownership principle.
Biome can enforce JSDoc formatting (useSingleJsDocAsterisk) but cannot judge whether a comment documents a decision rather than mechanics — that requires semantic understanding of intent.
Biome has no rules enforcing documentation co-location or detecting docs/code drift; only JSDoc formatting rules (e.g. useSingleJsDocAsterisk) exist, which are unrelated to proximity.
Biome has no rules for executable documentation or doctests; its test-related nursery rules only validate existing test code, not whether documentation examples are runnable.

Biome catches specific over-privileged patterns (noGlobalEval, noDangerouslySetInnerHtml, noBlankTarget, noScriptUrl) but has no general rule for over-broad permission grants or unnecessary capability requests at the application level.

Defence in depth is an architectural principle requiring multiple independent controls; Biome enforces individual defensive rules (noUnsafeFinally, useStrictMode, noSecrets) but has no rule that verifies layered or redundant security controls are present.

Biome flags swallowed errors (noUselessCatch), unhandled promises (noFloatingPromises), and missing switch defaults (useDefaultSwitchClause), catching some fail-insecure patterns, but cannot detect the semantic case where an error path grants access or returns a permissive value.
tsc has no rule for detecting redundant or unnecessary comments; it can only strip comments from output via removeComments, not analyze their quality.
tsc is a type checker, not a test coverage tool; it has no concept of which code paths are covered by tests.
tsc does not analyze function complexity or responsibility distribution; SRP is an architectural principle outside the scope of type checking.
tsc does not validate module cohesion or file-level responsibility; project references are an organizational tool, not a violation detector.
tsc does not validate test file structure or naming conventions; multiple tsconfig files support organization but enforce nothing.

tsc detects unused locals (noUnusedLocals), unused parameters (noUnusedParameters), and syntactically unreachable code (allowUnreachableCode:false), but all three are opt-in and the unreachable-code check only covers provable control-flow cases, missing semantic dead code.
tsc has no awareness of changelog files; changelog validation is a process/CI concern entirely outside the compiler.
tsc does not instrument or measure test coverage; that requires external tools such as Istanbul/c8.
tsc is a type checker with no test analysis capability; it cannot distinguish tests verifying requirements from tests verifying implementation details.

source_label: TypeScript TSConfig Reference (82 options, none for duplication)
rationale: tsc has no compiler option or built-in rule for detecting duplicated string/number literals or repeated constants; duplication detection is outside its scope.
tsc validates type correctness but cannot detect whether data or code should be normalized to remove ambiguity; normalization analysis requires semantic understanding beyond type checking.
tsc performs type narrowing via control-flow analysis but cannot identify heuristic logic or recommend algorithmic improvements; algorithmic analysis is outside its scope.
tsc has defensive options (noImplicitReturns, noFallthroughCasesInSwitch) that catch type/control-flow issues but no capability to detect brittle coupling or fragility to change.
tsc checks that code is type-correct but has no mechanism to detect over-specific implementations or suggest appropriate generalizations; design abstraction analysis is out of scope.
tsc has no compiler option for class size, method count, or complexity metrics; size/complexity analysis requires separate tools like SonarQube or ESLint plugins.

tsc validates correct use of primitive types but cannot flag primitive obsession (where primitives should be domain objects); this is a design-level smell requiring semantic analysis beyond type checking.

Data Clumps require detecting groups of fields/parameters that consistently co-occur across the codebase — a semantic usage-pattern analysis that tsc's type system cannot perform. No compiler flag addresses this.

tsc's noFallthroughCasesInSwitch flags accidental case fallthrough bugs in switch statements, but does not flag the design smell of overusing switch statements instead of polymorphism. Only a narrow bug-class is caught, not the OOP code smell.

Temporary Field means a class field is only meaningful under certain conditions. strictPropertyInitialization only ensures fields are initialized in constructors — it does not detect fields that are conditionally populated post-construction and undefined the rest of the time.

Refused Bequest requires detecting that a subclass inherits but does not use parent methods/properties. tsc enforces type contracts and noImplicitOverride tracks intentional overrides, but has no mechanism to flag inherited members that are never invoked.

Alternative Classes with Different Interfaces requires comparing semantic intent across unrelated classes with different method names. tsc has no way to determine that two classes are functionally equivalent but inconsistently named.

Divergent Change is a class-responsibility/cohesion smell detectable only by analyzing reasons a class changes over time. tsc performs static type analysis at a single snapshot and cannot reason about change history or responsibility separation.

Shotgun Surgery requires detecting that one logical change propagates across many unrelated classes. tsc does not track cross-class coupling or impact radius; it resolves types within a snapshot, not across change boundaries.

Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies require detecting that two separate class hierarchies evolve in lockstep. tsc enforces inheritance contracts within a hierarchy but has no mechanism to detect correlated growth between independent hierarchies.
tsc has no diagnostic for classes with insufficient responsibility; the type system checks correctness, not design intent.
tsc cannot detect classes that hold only data with no behavior; pure data containers are valid TypeScript and produce no diagnostics.

noUnusedLocals and noUnusedParameters catch unused variables and parameters (dead code artifacts of speculative generality), but unused interfaces, abstract classes, and over-engineered generics are not flagged.
tsc has no analysis of comment quantity, quality, or accuracy; removeComments only strips comments at emit time.
tsc does not track method-to-member access ratios across objects; feature envy requires semantic analysis beyond type checking.
private/protected access modifiers prevent some violations but tsc has no diagnostic that detects inappropriately intimate class relationships through public APIs.
tsc validates that each call in a chain is type-correct but has no diagnostic for chain depth or message chain length.
tsc has no diagnostic for classes that primarily delegate to another class; delegation patterns are not tracked by the type system.
tsc has no rule checking class size, method count, or responsibility scope; type-checking flags like noUnusedLocals flag dead members but cannot detect oversized classes.
tsc cannot distinguish data-only classes from behaviorally rich ones; the compiler has no concept of 'intended domain behavior' vs. pure data holders.
tsc has no object-lifetime or usage-frequency analysis; detecting short-lived controller classes requires dataflow/runtime reasoning beyond type checking.
Deep inheritance chains are structurally valid TypeScript; noImplicitOverride only enforces override keyword hygiene, not hierarchy depth or navigability.
tsc cannot enforce or detect implicit method-call ordering constraints; sequential coupling requires semantic/runtime reasoning that is outside the type system's scope.
Interfaces holding only constants are syntactically valid TypeScript; tsc has no flag distinguishing constant-container interfaces from behavioral ones.
tsc has no rule checking interface method count or enforcing Interface Segregation Principle; interface size is not a type-safety concern.
tsc performs no architectural pattern detection; identifying singleton overuse requires cross-module usage analysis that is beyond the compiler's type-checking scope.
tsc is a type checker with no rule detecting functions with combinatorially many boolean parameters or branching paths; no tsconfig flag covers this class of issue.

--noUnusedParameters flags parameters that are never used at all, catching a narrow slice of tramp data where an intermediate parameter is wholly unused; it misses the general case where the parameter is merely forwarded without being consumed at the intermediate layer.
tsc has no rule for tangled or overly-complex control flow; --noFallthroughCasesInSwitch targets a narrow correctness bug in switch statements, not general spaghetti structure.
tsc performs no duplicate-code detection; identifying copy-pasted blocks requires separate tooling (e.g. jscpd, ESLint). No tsconfig flag or built-in checker covers this.
Golden hammer is an architectural anti-pattern (over-reliance on one solution); tsc has no rule that tracks pattern-usage frequency or flags design monotony.

tsc can flag provably unreachable code (--allowUnreachableCode), unused locals (--noUnusedLocals), and unused parameters (--noUnusedParameters), catching the mechanically-dead subset of lava flow, but cannot detect reachable-yet-obsolete logic or commented-out cruft.
tsc has no magic-number or magic-string rule; it enforces types but does not flag inline literals that should be named constants. No tsconfig option covers this.
tsc's type system cannot track side effects or detect non-local state mutation that is non-obvious; action-at-a-distance requires data-flow / effect analysis beyond what tsc provides.
tsc has no rule to detect boolean parameters used as control-flow switches; this is a design smell requiring semantic judgement beyond type checking.
tsc can enforce explicit imports via module resolution but has no rule to detect implicit global-state or singleton access that constitutes a hidden dependency.
Premature optimization is a judgement about cost/complexity tradeoffs; tsc performs no performance or complexity analysis.
Detecting reimplementation of existing platform/language features requires semantic and domain knowledge; tsc only type-checks, not pattern-of-use semantics.
Big Ball of Mud is an architectural concern about coupling and structure; tsc performs no architectural analysis.
Stovepipe detection requires cross-module duplication analysis; tsc has no such capability.
Database-as-IPC is an architectural usage pattern; tsc cannot distinguish database calls used for messaging from normal data access.
Vendor lock-in detection requires domain knowledge about abstraction layers and proprietary APIs; tsc has no such heuristic.
SRP requires semantic judgment about cohesion and responsibilities; tsc has no rule that detects classes with too many concerns — it only type-checks structure.
OCP requires architectural reasoning about whether modification vs. extension was appropriate; tsc has no flag for this class of violation.

tsc's strictFunctionTypes enforces contravariance on function parameters and noImplicitOverride catches accidental method mismatches, but these cover only narrow structural forms of LSP violation; behavioural substitutability is not checked.

ISP (fat interfaces) requires counting and evaluating which members callers actually use — tsc has no rule for overly broad interfaces; noUnusedLocals does not detect unused interface members on implementors.

DIP (depend on abstractions, not concretions) requires dependency-flow analysis; tsc's structural typing facilitates interface-based code but does not flag violations where concrete types are depended on directly.
Programming to an interface rather than an implementation is a design-time choice; tsc does not flag variable declarations typed to concrete classes instead of interfaces.
Composition over inheritance is an architectural preference; tsc does not detect deep or excessive inheritance hierarchies or flag where delegation would be preferable.
Encapsulate-what-varies requires identifying variation points and evaluating encapsulation strategy — a semantic/design concern tsc has no rule for.

source_label: tsconfig type-checking options (no coupling-detection flag exists)
rationale: tsc enforces type correctness but has no rule or flag that detects whether dependencies are wired through abstractions vs. concretions; coupling degree is a semantic design property invisible to the type checker.
tsc performs static type analysis only; it has no mechanism to execute, discover, or evaluate test results — test passage is entirely outside the type checker's scope.
No tsconfig option or tsc diagnostic corresponds to intention-revealing code; naming quality and readability are human judgments beyond static type analysis.

source_label: noUnusedLocals / noUnusedParameters (scope: unused refs, not duplication)
rationale: tsc's noUnusedLocals/noUnusedParameters catch dead references only; no tsc option performs semantic clone detection or detects copy-pasted/duplicated logic across files.

source_label: noUnusedLocals / noUnusedParameters (misses unused exports and unnecessary abstractions)
rationale: noUnusedLocals/noUnusedParameters are weak proxies that miss unused exports and cannot assess whether abstractions are necessary; tsc has no concept of over-engineering or speculative generality.

source_label: TypeScript Project References (TS6202 for circular project refs only, no layer semantics)
rationale: tsc has no built-in layer/zone awareness; Project References can coarsely model inter-package direction (TS6202 for circular refs) but require explicit setup and don't enforce architectural direction intra-project.
No tsc diagnostic or tsconfig option relates to concern separation, layer boundaries, or module organization; this requires architectural linting tools like dependency-cruiser or ArchUnitTS.

source_label: TypeScript Project References (structural only, no inner/outer layer semantics)
rationale: tsc has no inward/outward layer model; Project References can coarsely constrain cross-package imports with explicit setup but provide no intra-project or semantic layer enforcement.
Boy Scout Rule is a process discipline (leave touched code cleaner than found); tsc has no rule that detects whether a change improved surrounding code quality.
tsc's type system can represent interfaces but has no rule enforcing that hexagonal ports must be declared as interfaces rather than abstract classes or concrete types.
tsc structural typing verifies that a class satisfies an interface at the call-site, but cannot detect whether the architectural adapter→port relationship is correctly established.
tsc has no module-boundary or import-restriction rule; it cannot prevent the core domain from importing infrastructure/framework code.
tsc has no concept of primary (driving) vs secondary (driven) adapters; there is no compiler flag or rule that classifies or validates adapter roles.
tsc has no mechanism to classify driven-side (secondary) adapters or verify they conform to the hexagonal driven-adapter pattern.
Testability by design is a structural design judgement; tsc cannot assess whether seams are designed for test-double substitution or that the architecture enables isolation.
tsc project references only prevent circular dependencies; they do not enforce directional dependency constraints (inward-only) required by hexagonal architecture.

Reuse/Release Equivalence (REP) is a design-level principle about grouping reusable units for release together. tsc has no compiler rule or tsconfig option that can detect violations of this packaging principle.

Common Closure Principle (CCP) is a design-level principle about co-locating code that changes for the same reason. tsc has no compiler rule or tsconfig option that can detect violations of this packaging principle.

Acyclic Dependencies Principle: tsc Project References enforces acyclic project-level dependency graphs (circular references between projects cause a build error), but does not detect circular module-level imports within a single project — the more common case.
Stable Dependencies Principle (SDP) requires measuring component instability metrics (afferent/efferent couplings). tsc has no rule or metric for this; it has no concept of component stability.
Stable Abstractions Principle (SAP) requires computing the ratio of abstract types in a component and correlating it with stability. tsc does not measure or enforce this metric.

Bounded Context is a DDD strategic pattern about semantic boundaries between domain models. tsc has no concept of bounded contexts and no compiler option that enforces or detects violations of this pattern.

Ubiquitous Language violations (mismatched naming between domain and code) require semantic/business knowledge to detect. tsc enforces type consistency but has no rule for naming alignment with domain vocabulary.

Domain Model integrity (aggregates, invariants, value object immutability) requires contextual/semantic understanding of domain rules. tsc's type system can enforce some structural constraints if explicitly typed, but cannot detect violations of DDD domain model patterns as a class.
tsc enforces type structure and access modifiers but has no concept of DDD Repository pattern boundaries or violations thereof.
tsc has no rules for DDD Service Layer conventions; it cannot detect when domain logic leaks into or out of a service layer.
tsc enforces declared private/readonly modifiers but cannot detect when a developer should have encapsulated a field or collection but chose not to — that requires design intent judgement.
