rationale: golangci-lint has no documented rule that identifies heuristic or approximate logic and suggests replacing it with deterministic equivalents; this requires semantic understanding that static analysis tools do not provide.

errorlint catches one specific brittle pattern — matching errors by identity instead of using Go 1.13 wrapping-aware Is/As — and gocritic covers a narrow set of additional bug/style patterns, but the broad class of brittle patterns (magic strings in tests, fragile string matching on error messages, order-dependent assertions) has no general detector.

source_label: golangci-lint linters index (no matching rule found)
rationale: dupl detects copied code blocks (a symptom that could prompt generalization), but golangci-lint has no rule that identifies over-specific implementations or recommends broader abstractions — this is a design-level judgment beyond static pattern matching.

funlen flags overly long functions (an analog for Large Method), but golangci-lint has no documented linter for oversized structs (too many fields) or types with too many methods — the function-length proxy catches only part of the Large Class smell.

source_label: golangci-lint linters index (no matching rule found)
rationale: No golangci-lint linter is documented that detects Primitive Obsession — cases where raw primitives (string, int) are used in place of proper domain types. This is a DDD/design-pattern concern not addressed by any available rule.

golangci-lint has no rule that detects groups of parameters/fields that repeatedly co-occur and should be extracted into a struct; this requires cross-call-site semantic analysis beyond static pattern matching.

The 'exhaustive' linter flags non-exhaustive enum switches, and go-critic's 'singleCaseSwitch'/'ifElseChain' checks flag trivial switch misuse, but the core smell (switching on type tags instead of using polymorphism/interfaces) has no dedicated detector; exhaustive coverage is opt-in and only covers enum completeness, not the design-smell aspect.

No golangci-lint rule detects the Temporary Field smell (struct fields set/used only in certain code paths); 'unused' catches truly unreferenced fields but not conditionally-used ones, and 'exhaustruct' checks initialization completeness, not conditional usage patterns.

Refused Bequest (a subtype ignoring inherited methods) presupposes class inheritance; Go has no subclassing — only embedding and interfaces — so the construct as classically defined does not exist in Go.

Alternative Classes with Different Interfaces requires cross-type semantic similarity analysis; 'iface' identifies identical interface method sets or opaque return types but does not compare types for functional equivalence with different naming.
Divergent Change (one module changed for multiple unrelated reasons) is an architectural/coupling smell requiring change-history and responsibility analysis that no static linter can perform.
Shotgun Surgery (one logical change scattered across many files/types) requires understanding logical change units across the codebase, which is beyond the scope of any golangci-lint rule.
Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies presupposes class-based inheritance trees; Go has no class inheritance, so this smell cannot arise in idiomatic Go code and there is nothing for the tool to detect.
No golangci-lint rule detects structs that do too little to justify their existence; identifying a 'lazy class' requires behavioral and usage analysis that no available linter performs.

No golangci-lint rule specifically detects pure data-container structs (data classes); in Go, data-only structs are a legitimate and common pattern (e.g. for JSON marshaling), and no bundled linter flags them as a smell.

The `unused` linter (and related `deadcode`, `revive`'s `unused-parameter`/`unused-receiver` rules) catch unused exported symbols, interfaces, and parameters — the mechanical symptoms of speculative generality — but miss over-abstracted code that is referenced but never truly needed.

gocritic's `commentedOutCode` checker flags commented-out code, and `godox` flags TODO/FIXME markers; but no linter detects compensatory over-commenting that substitutes for clear code — only narrow comment-style sub-cases are covered.
Feature envy requires cross-type data-access pattern analysis; no golangci-lint rule performs this semantic check.

Inappropriate intimacy requires coupling/dependency graph analysis; Go's package visibility enforces some boundary at the language level, but no golangci-lint rule specifically detects cross-type intimate coupling within a package.
No golangci-lint rule detects long method chains (Law of Demeter violations); complexity metrics like `gocognit`/`cyclop` measure control-flow depth, not expression-level call-chain depth.

No golangci-lint rule detects middle-man structs that primarily delegate to another; gocritic's `wrapperFunc` is the closest but flags unnecessary stdlib wrappers, not delegation-heavy types in general.

No golangci-lint rule counts methods or fields on a concrete struct; function-level complexity linters (gocognit, maintidx, funlen) catch behavioral bloat inside functions but not structural bloat across a type.
Detecting structs that are pure data bags with logic split elsewhere requires semantic/architectural judgment; no golangci-lint rule models domain behavior distribution.
No golangci-lint rule identifies pass-through wrapper structs (Poltergeist); detecting structs whose sole purpose is to delegate to another requires intent analysis beyond syntax.
nestif targets deeply nested if-statements, not struct embedding chains; no golangci-lint linter measures embedding hierarchy depth or the Yo-Yo Problem pattern.
Enforcing mandatory method call ordering requires data-flow or protocol analysis; no golangci-lint rule models temporal coupling between method calls.
Go interfaces can only contain method signatures, not constants or fields, so the Constant Interface anti-pattern (an interface used as a namespace for constants) cannot exist in Go.
The bundled interfacebloat linter directly counts methods on interfaces and enforces a configurable maximum, but it is opt-in (not enabled by default) so requires explicit configuration.

gochecknoglobals (opt-in) flags all package-level variables, catching the global-var form of singletons, but is broader than singleton detection and misses sync.Once singletons held in unexported vars without init-time assignment.

No golangci-lint rule targets the combinatorial explosion anti-pattern (exponential code-path growth from excessive flag/option combinations). Parameter-count proxies like revive's argument-limit or funlen exist but do not detect this design-level smell.

golangci-lint bundles context-specific linters (containedctx, contextcheck, fatcontext) that catch one common Go tramp-data pattern (context stored in structs or ferried redundantly), but there is no general detector for arbitrary data passed through unrelated layers.

Multiple bundled linters (cyclop, gocognit, nestif, gocyclo, funlen) measure cyclomatic/cognitive complexity and nesting depth, which are reliable proxies for spaghetti code, but they require opt-in config and miss structural coupling or goto-style flow that doesn't inflate complexity scores.

The bundled dupl linter performs token-based code-clone detection and directly targets copy-paste duplication, but it is not enabled by default, requires threshold tuning, and misses semantic duplication where code is paraphrased rather than literally copied.

No golangci-lint rule detects the golden-hammer anti-pattern (over-applying a favourite tool/pattern to every problem). Incidentally related rules like gochecknoinits or gochecknoglobals catch specific global-state smells but not architectural over-reliance on a single abstraction.

Several bundled linters (unused, deadcode, unparam, ineffassign, wastedassign) reliably flag unused symbols, unreachable code, and wasted assignments — covering the dead-code facet of lava flow — but they cannot detect the broader smell of abandoned experimental code that is still reachable and syntactically used.

The bundled mnd linter (Magic Number Detector) flags unnamed numeric literals and encourages named constants, but it covers only numeric literals; there is no equivalent rule for hardcoded string literals, URLs, credentials, or other non-numeric magic values.

gochecknoglobals and gochecknoinits detect global variable declarations and init()-based side effects — the most common Go mechanisms for action-at-a-distance — but neither rule detects indirect mutation propagated through function arguments, closures, or reflection, so many spooky-action patterns are missed.
No golangci-lint rule targets boolean flag arguments (functions that take a bool to switch behavior). Nearest linters (unparam, inamedparam) address unused/unnamed parameters, not this anti-pattern.

gochecknoglobals (flags global variables) and gochecknoinits (flags init functions) catch some hidden-dependency forms, but both are opt-in and miss runtime singletons or package-level state accessed inside functions without init.

Complexity linters (cyclop, gocyclo, gocognit) measure structural complexity but cannot determine whether that complexity constitutes premature optimization — a valid high-performance algorithm would also flag. Requires human/LLM judgement.

No golangci-lint rule detects reimplementation of existing language or standard-library features. Identifying inner-platform effect requires semantic knowledge of what already exists in the stdlib/framework.

Big Ball of Mud is an architectural topology concern. depguard can enforce import boundaries only if explicitly configured with allowed-package lists, and even then cannot detect pervasive coupling; no rule identifies the pattern inherently.

Stovepipe System (isolated silos with duplicated logic but no shared abstractions) requires intent-level architectural analysis. dupl detects syntactic code duplication but cannot reason about whether silos should share abstractions.

No golangci-lint rule can detect the Database-as-IPC pattern; recognising that a database table is being used as a message queue between services requires understanding of runtime communication intent, not syntactic analysis.

depguard and gomodguard can block specific import paths when manually configured with a vendor's package list, but they cannot infer vendor lock-in semantically — they only enforce explicit deny/allow lists set by the user.
No rule detects SRP violations semantically; complexity proxies (cyclop, funlen, gocognit) catch functions that are 'too large' but not the architectural concept of mixed responsibilities.

OCP is a semantic architectural principle; golangci-lint has no rule that can detect whether a type is closed for modification or open for extension. Go's lack of traditional inheritance makes this even less mechanically checkable.

LSP violations (e.g. an interface implementation that silently changes behaviour/pre/post-conditions) require semantic reasoning; no golangci-lint rule detects this. The `iface` linter finds interface structural issues (identical/unused) but not substitutability violations.

`interfacebloat` flags interfaces that exceed a configurable method-count threshold, catching 'fat interface' cases, but it is not enabled by default and a count limit is only a proxy — it misses logically unrelated small-method interfaces.

`ireturn` enforces 'accept interfaces, return concrete types', which catches one facet of DIP (depending on abstractions at call boundaries), but it does not detect concrete-type field dependencies inside structs or missing abstraction layers.

`ireturn` partially enforces 'program to an interface' by requiring interface types at function boundaries, but does not cover struct fields typed as concrete implementations or other non-boundary usage.

Go has no inheritance (no class hierarchy to inherit from), so 'composition over inheritance' is not a meaningful violation class in Go — the language already enforces composition as the only structural reuse mechanism.
'Encapsulate what varies' is a design-level judgement about which concerns should be isolated behind an abstraction; no golangci-lint rule detects violations of this principle.

golangci-lint has no direct DI-pattern detector, but `ireturn` flags functions returning concrete types instead of interfaces (pushing toward DI-friendly design), and `depguard` can restrict which packages couple to which — indirect, syntactic proxies that catch some coupling violations but miss the general 'dependency should be injected' principle.
golangci-lint is a static analysis tool and cannot run tests or measure test pass/fail status; it has no mechanism to verify that code passes its test suite.

Naming-convention linters (`revive` with `var-naming`, `confusing-naming`, `unexported-naming`; `varnamelen`; `errname`; `predeclared`) enforce mechanical naming rules and catch some obviously non-revealing names, but cannot judge whether a name semantically reveals the programmer's intention — a fundamentally semantic, context-dependent quality.

`dupl` does token-based duplicate fragment detection and `gocritic` catches logic-level duplicates (`dupArg`, `dupBranchBody`, `dupCase`, `dupSubExpr`); `goconst` catches repeated string literals. Together they cover structural/syntactic DRY, but semantic duplication (same concept expressed differently) is not detectable statically.

`unused` (default-on) catches unused symbols; `unparam` flags unused parameters; `ineffassign` flags dead assignments — these mechanically detect artifacts of YAGNI violations, but cannot judge whether a present, used feature was added speculatively without current need, which is the core YAGNI concern.

`depguard` can enforce package-import allow/deny lists per file-glob, approximating Clean Architecture dependency rules, but it requires full manual configuration of every layer boundary and has no built-in understanding of Clean Architecture layers — any coverage depends entirely on user-supplied config.

golangci-lint has no linter that detects whether concerns are properly separated into architectural layers; complexity linters (`funlen`, `gocognit`, `nestif`) flag structural proxies of mixed concerns but cannot determine whether two distinct concerns reside in the wrong layer.

`depguard` with manually configured file-glob rules can flag inner-layer packages importing outer-layer packages, but only if every layer boundary is explicitly encoded in config; without that config it detects nothing, and it has no inherent model of what 'inner' vs 'outer' means.

The Boy Scout Rule (leave code cleaner than you found it) is a human discipline about incremental improvement; no golangci-lint rule detects whether changed code was improved or degraded relative to its prior state.

Whether a hexagonal port is expressed as a Go interface requires understanding architectural intent; golangci-lint has no rule that identifies which types are ports or enforces that they must be interfaces.
Verifying that an adapter struct implements the correct port interface requires knowing which types are adapters and which are ports — architectural semantics that no golangci-lint rule encodes.

Whether the core domain is 'pure' (no infrastructure imports) is an architectural boundary rule; depguard can enforce import blocklists but requires per-project configuration naming the specific packages, so no built-in rule covers this class generically.
Identifying and validating the driving side (primary adapters / inbound) of a hexagonal architecture requires semantic knowledge of architectural roles that golangci-lint does not model.
Identifying and validating the driven side (secondary adapters / outbound) of a hexagonal architecture requires semantic knowledge of architectural roles that golangci-lint does not model.
Testability by design (e.g. injecting dependencies through ports so the core can be tested without infrastructure) is an architectural intent that golangci-lint has no rule to evaluate.

Enforcing that dependencies point inward (toward the core) is an architecture-layer rule; depguard can block specific imports when configured per-project, but golangci-lint ships no built-in rule that knows about hexagonal layers or enforces inward-only dependency direction generically.

Reuse/Release Equivalence Principle (REP) is a package cohesion principle requiring that reusable units align with release units. No golangci-lint linter measures or enforces this; depguard/gomodguard enforce import allowlists but have no concept of release-unit coherence.

Common Closure Principle (CCP) asks whether classes that change together are packaged together — a semantic/historical coupling judgment no static rule can make. No golangci-lint linter addresses this.

Go's compiler hard-rejects import cycles, and golangci-lint's govet linter inherits this; literal import cycles are caught. However, ADP's broader concern (dependency direction stability, layering discipline) is not addressed by any named rule — only the degenerate cycle case is covered.

Stable Dependencies Principle requires measuring package instability (efferent/afferent coupling ratios) and verifying dependencies flow toward more stable packages. No golangci-lint linter computes or enforces stability metrics.
Stable Abstractions Principle requires correlating a package's stability with its level of abstraction. No golangci-lint linter measures abstractness or enforces the stability-abstraction tradeoff.

DDD Bounded Context enforcement requires knowing which packages belong to which context and detecting cross-context coupling. golangci-lint has no concept of bounded contexts; depguard can manually block imports but requires exhaustive hand-configuration of every boundary.

DDD Ubiquitous Language consistency requires comparing identifier names against a domain glossary — a semantic task. golangci-lint naming rules (revive, errname, etc.) enforce syntactic conventions only, with no domain vocabulary awareness.

DDD Domain Model violations (business logic in the wrong layer) require understanding architectural layer semantics and intent. No golangci-lint linter has layer-awareness or can distinguish domain logic from infrastructure code.

DDD Repository pattern compliance (ensuring data access is abstracted through repository interfaces) is an architectural/semantic concern. golangci-lint has no linter that validates DDD layer conventions or repository pattern adherence.

DDD Service Layer conventions (proper separation of business logic into service objects) require semantic/architectural judgement. No golangci-lint rule enforces or detects service layer design violations.

Encapsulating variables/collections (replacing direct field access with accessor methods) is a semantic refactoring smell. golangci-lint has no rule that flags 'this exported field should be hidden behind a method'; unused/revive only flag missing docs or truly unused exports.

revive's unexported-return rule warns when an exported function returns an unexported type (a specific leaky-abstraction case), and exported enforces naming/doc conventions on exported symbols — together they catch some unnecessary exposure. They do not detect the general case of an identifier that is exported but only used internally.
Tell Don't Ask is a behavioral/architectural principle (prefer methods that act over getters whose results the caller acts on). No static linter rule in golangci-lint detects this pattern.

Go has no subclasses; the analog smell is over-using struct embedding where delegation/composition suffices. golangci-lint has no rule that detects misuse of embedding vs. explicit delegation; embeddedstructfieldcheck only enforces field ordering, not design appropriateness.

Collapsing unnecessary interface/type hierarchy layers requires semantic judgement about whether abstraction adds value. interfacebloat limits interface size but does not detect redundant or collapsible hierarchies.

Refused Bequest (implementing an interface with panic stubs or empty no-ops for methods the type doesn't meaningfully support) requires semantic analysis of method bodies. No golangci-lint rule detects this code smell.

golangci-lint naming rules (revive:var-naming, stylecheck:ST1003) enforce Go conventions (initialisms, casing) but cannot judge whether a name actually reveals intent — that requires semantic understanding beyond mechanical pattern matching.

No golangci-lint rule enforces that type names be nouns and method names be verbs; revive and stylecheck naming rules cover casing and initialism conventions only, not grammatical category of identifiers.

revive:confusing-naming only detects struct-member names that differ solely by capitalization; it cannot detect when the same semantic concept is referred to by different words across a codebase (e.g. 'fetch' vs 'get' vs 'retrieve').

revive:time-naming catches a narrow case (unit suffix on time.Duration), but no golangci-lint rule detects the general class of semantically misleading names (e.g. a slice named 'list', a map named 'array').

Complexity linters (funlen, gocognit, cyclop, nestif) measure length/branching/nesting as proxies for function quality but cannot detect the specific violation of mixing abstraction levels within a single function.

No golangci-lint rule enforces Command-Query Separation; revive:modifies-value-receiver detects receiver mutation but does not check whether a mutating method also returns a meaningful result, which is the CQS concern.

revive:modifies-parameter and revive:modifies-value-receiver (opt-in) detect parameter and receiver mutation side effects, but miss global-state writes, I/O side effects, and any side effect not expressed as direct parameter/receiver modification.

revive:flag-parameter directly targets boolean parameters used to branch function control flow, matching the 'Remove Flag Argument' smell; however revive is not enabled by default in golangci-lint and the rule requires opt-in configuration, and it only covers bool parameters (not arbitrary enum/int flags).

revive's `argument-limit` rule flags functions exceeding a configurable parameter count (default 8), catching the symptom that motivates a Parameter Object refactoring, but it does not suggest the grouping refactoring itself and requires opt-in configuration.

revive's `early-return` rule explicitly detects if-then-else where the predicate can be inverted to reduce nesting (guard clause pattern), and `nestif` flags deeply nested conditionals; both require opt-in configuration and are not enabled by default.

No golangci-lint rule detects repeated type-dispatch conditionals (switch/if-else chains on a type tag) and suggests replacing them with polymorphism/interface dispatch; `exhaustive` only checks for incomplete switch coverage, not the refactoring opportunity.
Go idioms favour explicit loops, and no golangci-lint linter detects imperative for-range loops that could be expressed as functional pipelines (map/filter/reduce) and suggests the transformation.

paralleltest flags tests missing `t.Parallel()`, catching one common cause of slow test suites (lack of parallelism), but does not detect other slowness causes such as time.Sleep, blocking I/O, or unnecessarily sequential subtests.

No golangci-lint rule detects shared mutable global state between tests or test-order dependencies; `testpackage` enforces package separation as a structural proxy but does not detect actual state-sharing or ordering issues.

No golangci-lint rule detects non-deterministic patterns in tests such as unguarded time.Now(), unseeded math/rand calls, or map-iteration order dependence; this class of issue requires semantic/contextual analysis.

testifylint checks for incorrect testify assertion usage but does not detect tests that contain zero assertions; no other golangci-lint linter enforces the presence of at least one assertion in a test function.

"Timely" (writing tests before production code, i.e. test-first discipline) is a process/workflow practice; golangci-lint has no rule that can detect whether tests were written before or after the code they cover.
Red-Green-Refactor is a TDD workflow cycle; it exists only in the developer's process, not in the resulting source code, so no static analyzer including golangci-lint can detect violations of it.

"Fake It Till You Make It" (returning hardcoded values to pass a test before generalising) is a temporary TDD technique visible only during the iterative cycle, not in committed code; golangci-lint has no rule for it.
Triangulation (adding test cases to force generalisation of hardcoded fakes) is a TDD design discipline; golangci-lint has no rule that detects under-triangulated test suites.

"Assert First" (writing the assertion before the setup/act in a test) is a test-authoring style guideline with no mechanical syntactic invariant; golangci-lint has no rule that enforces or detects it.

Detecting code that lacks any corresponding test (legacy-code-as-untested-code) requires coverage analysis, not static linting; golangci-lint has no coverage-threshold or missing-test rule — go test -cover is the dedicated tool.

Characterization Tests (golden-master tests written to capture existing behaviour of untested code) are a testing strategy; there is no structural code pattern golangci-lint could inspect to detect their presence or absence.

Sprout Method/Class (extracting a new, testable method from untestable legacy code) is a refactoring technique; golangci-lint has no rule that detects opportunities for or failures to apply this pattern.
Wrap Method/Class is a structural refactoring pattern requiring semantic understanding of desired interface changes; no golangci-lint rule detects or suggests this.

The opt-in `iface` meta-linter (analyzers: opaque, identical, unused) can surface interface-extraction opportunities, but it is not enabled by default and only catches indirect signals, not a direct 'extract interface' recommendation.
Parameterize Constructor/Method (DI refactoring: pass deps as params instead of hardcoding) requires semantic dependency-graph analysis; no golangci-lint rule covers this.
Go is a statically, mandatorily typed compiled language — the concept of gradual/optional type annotations does not exist in Go, so the construct is absent.

`forcetypeassert` (flags unsafe type assertions lacking the ok-check) and `gocritic`'s `typeAssertChain` checker (suggests type switches over chained assertions) address safe type assertion usage in Go, but neither proactively identifies narrowing opportunities; they enforce correctness, not completeness of narrowing.
Prototype pollution is a JavaScript-specific vulnerability tied to prototype-based inheritance; Go uses explicit struct types with no prototype chain, making the construct absent.

Global scope pollution via implicit property assignment to a shared global object is a JavaScript construct; Go has package-scoped symbols with no equivalent dynamic global namespace, so the construct does not exist in Go.

`containedctx` detects `context.Context` stored as a struct field (the canonical anti-pattern of implicit context in models), and `contextcheck` flags non-inherited context propagation — together they cover the explicit-over-implicit context rule. However, broader 'no request state in models' (e.g. storing DB handles or loggers in domain structs) is not detected, so coverage is partial.

Go has no native eval(). Dynamic execution requires third-party libraries (expr, govaluate, etc.). forbidigo can be configured with custom regex patterns to forbid calls to such libraries, but this requires explicit opt-in configuration — there is no default rule targeting dynamic execution.
Go has no language-level monkey patching. While unsafe-based patching libraries exist (bouk/monkey, gomonkey), golangci-lint has no rule that detects or forbids their use.

The iface linter detects interface quality issues (identical method sets, unused interfaces, single-implementation interfaces) but does not enforce explicit compile-time interface satisfaction declarations (the var _ I = (*T)(nil) idiom). Structural typing conformance is implicit in Go.
Enforcing lint-as-build-failure is a CI/build configuration concern, not a source-code pattern golangci-lint can detect. This is meta-level policy outside any linter's scope.

golangci-lint has no bundled linter that enforces minimum test coverage thresholds. Coverage enforcement requires external dedicated tools (go-test-coverage, gocheckcov) used separately alongside golangci-lint.

golangci-lint includes no rules for API contract validation, schema pinning, or protobuf compatibility checking. Such checks require dedicated tools (buf, protolint) outside the golangci-lint ecosystem.

sloglint enforces consistent structured slog usage (no fmt.Sprintf in messages, key naming conventions, context-aware logging, attribute consistency) and loggercheck validates logr/klog/zap patterns. Both are available as golangci-lint linters covering the major structured logging libraries.

contextcheck detects functions that use non-inherited contexts (e.g. fresh context.Background() instead of propagating the caller's context), which is a prerequisite for correlation ID propagation. However, it does not specifically verify that a correlation/trace ID is present in the context — only that context inheritance is maintained.
Health check and readiness probe presence is an architectural/deployment concern; no golangci-lint rule can detect whether an HTTP service exposes a /healthz or /ready endpoint.
Alerting strategy (symptom- vs cause-based) is a runtime observability design decision with no static code representation that any linter rule addresses.

gosec and staticcheck detect specific unsafe input handling (injection sinks, malformed format strings, invalid regexes), which proxies part of Postel's Law, but no rule enforces the general principle of liberal acceptance / conservative output.
No golangci-lint rule checks whether a module's own version tag or go.mod version field follows semver conventions; semver compliance is a release-process concern, not detectable by static analysis.

SA1019 flags uses of deprecated symbols (catching one class of compatibility break when consuming libraries), but no rule detects whether your own exported API introduces breaking changes for downstream consumers.

errcheck enforces that returned errors are handled, wrapcheck enforces cross-package error wrapping, and errorlint enforces correct Go 1.13 error formatting — together these enforce error-handling discipline, but none can verify that a function's documented error contract (e.g. 'returns ErrNotFound or ErrUnauthorized') is actually upheld.

gomodguard and depguard only enforce policies on direct dependencies; Go's go.sum file pins transitive dependencies mechanically but no linter enforces explicit pinning or auditing of the transitive graph.

depguard and gomodguard can block or allowlist specific packages/modules as a policy gate, and unused catches dead code, but no rule measures or flags overall dependency tree size or flags individually superfluous transitive imports.

govulncheck (bundled since golangci-lint v1.54) scans Go module dependencies against the Go vulnerability database for known CVEs, but does not enforce a regular audit process, check for license issues, or flag outdated-but-safe dependencies.
No golangci-lint linter measures package cohesion or enforces single-responsibility at the package level; this is an architectural/design judgement that static analysis cannot mechanically detect.

Go lacks const structs/immutable value types, and no golangci-lint linter flags mutable state where an immutable alternative would be preferable; this is a design-pattern judgement beyond static analysis.

gochecknoglobals flags all package-level variables, acting as a blunt proxy for shared mutable state, but does not detect shared state passed via function args, struct fields, or channels, and does not reason about goroutine ownership boundaries.
Idempotency is a semantic/behavioural property (same inputs always produce same side-effects); no golangci-lint linter can detect non-idempotent operations without runtime or domain knowledge.
No golangci-lint linter detects implicit or ad-hoc state machines or requires them to be modelled explicitly; this is a structural design concern beyond static pattern matching.

While gochecknoglobals discourages globals, no linter reasons about ownership semantics or detects unsafe assumptions about who may mutate a shared or global resource; this requires contextual/semantic judgement.

golangci-lint can enforce godoc presence/format (godot, godclint) and flag FIXME/TODO markers (godox), but no linter can distinguish comments that explain WHY (decisions) from those that describe WHAT (mechanics) — comment quality is a natural-language judgement.

golangci-lint (via revive's `exported` rule and godot) can flag exported symbols missing godoc comments, but it cannot verify that documentation is *kept close* to the code it describes or that docs remain accurate/co-located as code evolves — that is a semantic/proximity concern, not a syntactic one.

The `testableexamples` linter flags Go Example functions that lack an `// Output:` comment (meaning they compile but are never executed by `go test`), which directly enforces runnable doc examples. However it only covers the Go-specific Example function pattern and does not enforce that examples exist at all — only that existing ones are executable.

Principle of least privilege as a design principle (minimal permissions, scoped access, interface segregation) has no direct golangci-lint rule. `interfacebloat` flags large interfaces and revive's `unexported-return` catches accidental type exposure, but these are narrow stylistic checks, not a principled enforcement of least-privilege access scoping — the general semantic principle requires human/LLM judgement.

Defence in depth is an architectural/design principle requiring multiple independent security controls; no golangci-lint rule evaluates whether layered defences are present or sufficient. gosec catches specific known-bad patterns (injection, weak crypto) but cannot verify that a system has defence-in-depth as a property.

`errcheck` (enabled by default) flags unhandled errors, and `nilerr` catches returning nil instead of propagating an error, both of which address common fail-insecure patterns. However, they cannot verify that the *default outcome* of an error path is safe/denied rather than permissive — that requires semantic reasoning about what the fallback state means in context.

No Clippy rule detects semantically redundant comments (those that merely restate what the code already makes obvious); detecting comment redundancy requires semantic judgment beyond AST-based linting.
Clippy is a static linter and cannot determine whether specific functions have tests; coverage tracking requires runtime instrumentation tools like cargo-tarpaulin or cargo-llvm-cov.

Heuristic proxy rules exist (too_many_lines, cognitive_complexity, too_many_arguments) but all are in the restriction/pedantic group (off by default) and detect size proxies, not multi-responsibility semantics.
No Clippy rule checks file-level size or responsibility scope; a too_many_lines_in_file lint was proposed in 2026 but remains unmerged.
No Clippy rule enforces or validates test file/module organizational patterns such as placement in tests/ directories or naming conventions.

source_label: rustc dead_code / unused_imports / unused_variables (warn by default)
rationale: rustc's built-in dead_code, unused_imports, and unused_variables lints fire by default during cargo clippy; Clippy adds supplementary lints (unused_async, collection_is_never_read) for additional dead-code patterns.
Changelog updates are a process/workflow check entirely outside the scope of static code linting; no Clippy rule can detect whether CHANGELOG.md was modified in a commit or PR.
Test coverage enforcement requires runtime instrumentation (cargo-tarpaulin, cargo-llvm-cov); Clippy has no such capability and cannot track which code paths are exercised by tests.

Clippy has no lint that detects whether a test exercises requirements vs. implementation internals. `assertions_on_result_states` and `cfg_not_test` are tangential style lints, not behavioural-vs-structural test design detectors. This requires semantic/design judgment.

clippy::approx_constant flags float literals that approximate stdlib math constants (π, e, etc.) and suggests using std::f*::consts; no general magic-numbers or duplicate-constant lint exists in Clippy.

Clippy's style/complexity lints (collapsible_if, redundant_closure, etc.) address syntactic simplification, not the semantic concern of normalizing data representations or removing ambiguity from domain logic. The item class is a design-level concern beyond Clippy's scope.
Clippy has no rule for detecting heuristic or approximate logic that should be replaced with a deterministic algorithm. This is an architectural/semantic concern requiring human or LLM judgement.

clippy::unwrap_used and clippy::expect_used flag panic-prone patterns, but both are in the restriction group (not enabled by default) and cover only panic-path brittleness, not broader brittle patterns like hardcoded values or fragile string matching.

clippy::ptr_arg flags &String/&Vec<T> arguments and suggests &str/&[T] — a concrete case of over-specificity — but Clippy has no general lint for preferring trait bounds (e.g. impl IntoIterator) over concrete types, so coverage is only partial.
Clippy has no lint for Large Class (struct or impl with too many fields/methods). The cognitive_complexity and too_many_arguments lints apply to functions, not to struct/impl size as a whole.
Clippy has no lint for Primitive Obsession (using raw primitives where a newtype or domain type would be appropriate). This is a higher-level type-design concern that requires semantic judgment.

Clippy has no lint for detecting groups of data that recur together across parameter lists or structs and should be extracted into a dedicated type; this requires cross-site semantic analysis beyond Clippy's scope.

Clippy flags some match-related style issues (match_same_arms, match_bool, collapsible_match, single_match) but does not detect the OOP smell of using match/dispatch chains where polymorphism/trait dispatch would be more appropriate.

Clippy has no lint for struct fields that are only meaningful in certain states; detecting this smell requires understanding conditional initialization patterns, which is a semantic concern beyond Clippy's rule set.

Rust has no classical inheritance, so the OOP form of Refused Bequest (subclass ignores inherited methods) does not directly map; Clippy has no lint for trait impls whose methods are entirely unused or delegated to panic!, which would be the closest Rust analogue.

Clippy has no cross-type analysis to identify two types that serve the same role but expose differently-named interfaces; this requires semantic understanding of intent that is beyond static pattern matching.

Divergent Change is an architectural smell (one module changed for many unrelated reasons) requiring change-history and responsibility analysis; Clippy operates only on the current AST and has no such lint.
Shotgun Surgery requires cross-file change-impact analysis that Clippy does not perform; it has no lint for detecting that a single logical change forces edits scattered across many modules.
Rust has no class inheritance hierarchies, so parallel inheritance hierarchies as an OOP antipattern cannot exist in the language; the construct the smell describes is absent.
Rust has structs and modules but Clippy has no rule detecting types/modules that do too little to justify existence; this requires semantic judgment about design intent.
Clippy's struct_excessive_bools (pedantic, off by default) flags one narrow symptom, but no rule detects the general 'data class' pattern of structs that only hold data with no meaningful behavior.

The Rust compiler's dead_code lint and Clippy's related lints catch unused functions, fields, and parameters, covering the 'dead abstractions' symptom of speculative generality, but cannot detect over-engineered abstractions that are technically used or parameterised for hypothetical callers.
Clippy has no rule that detects over-reliance on comments as a code smell; the only comment-adjacent rule (allow_attributes_without_reason) concerns lint-suppression annotations, not prose comments.
Detecting feature envy requires cross-struct call-graph analysis to compare how often a method references its own type vs. another's; no Clippy rule performs this analysis.
Inappropriate intimacy (two types accessing each other's internals excessively) requires coupling metrics across module boundaries; no Clippy rule detects this pattern.

Method chaining is idiomatic Rust (iterators, builder pattern) and Clippy has no rule flagging long chains; specific chain-simplification lints (bind_instead_of_map, etc.) target correctness/perf, not chain length.
Detecting middle-man types requires identifying that most methods simply forward calls to a wrapped type; no Clippy rule analyses delegation ratio or method-forwarding patterns.

Clippy has no lint that counts methods or responsibilities per struct/impl block. Complexity proxies like `cognitive_complexity` and `too_many_lines` fire on individual functions, not on the aggregate size/scope of a type, so God Object as an architectural anti-pattern goes undetected.

Clippy has no rule that warns when a struct carries only data fields with no meaningful methods. Detecting an Anemic Domain Model requires semantic judgement about intended design — well outside Clippy's mechanical lint model.

Clippy has no lint for types that are instantiated, perform a tiny task, and are immediately discarded (Poltergeist). Detecting this requires cross-call-site lifetime and usage analysis that Clippy does not perform.

Rust has no class inheritance hierarchy; traits provide polymorphism without subtyping chains. The Yo-Yo Problem (deep inheritance forcing readers to jump up and down a hierarchy) cannot arise structurally, so there is nothing for a tool to detect.

Clippy has no lint that detects methods which must be called in a specific order but lack compile-time enforcement (e.g. typestate). Detecting Sequential Coupling requires understanding protocol invariants — a semantic concern outside Clippy's scope.

Rust traits cannot hold associated constants as a primary use-case in the same problematic way as Java constant interfaces (implementing a trait just to inherit its constants is idiomatic in some crates). Clippy has no lint targeting traits whose sole content is associated constants used to 'inherit' values.

Clippy has no lint that counts the number of required methods on a trait and warns when that count is excessive. `too_many_arguments` applies to individual function signatures, not to trait surface area as a whole.

Clippy's `static_mut_refs` and the `clippy::mut_key` family warn against unsafe mutable statics, and `clippy::declare_interior_mutable_const` / `borrow_interior_mutable_const` flag unsafe global-state patterns. However, safe singleton patterns via `lazy_static`, `once_cell`, or `std::sync::OnceLock` are not flagged, so overuse of the singleton pattern in its idiomatic Rust forms goes undetected.

Combinatorial Explosion is a design-level problem (exponential state/branch growth) requiring semantic understanding of domain logic. Clippy's cognitive_complexity lint measures nesting/branching depth as a proxy for readability but explicitly acknowledges it cannot detect true cognitive complexity, and it is disabled by default (restriction/allow). No rule targets the combinatorial explosion anti-pattern specifically.

Tramp Data — parameters threaded unnecessarily through call chains — requires understanding data-flow intent across multiple call sites. Clippy's too_many_arguments lint flags functions with a large number of parameters but cannot distinguish intentional APIs from tramp data threading. No Clippy rule detects the semantic anti-pattern.

Spaghetti Code is a holistic structural smell (tangled control flow, poor modularity) that requires human/LLM judgment. Clippy's excessive_nesting and collapsible_if lints address specific structural sub-patterns but do not and cannot detect spaghetti code as a general class.

Clippy has several rules that catch specific forms of copy-paste duplication: branches_sharing_code (shared code in if/else blocks), match_same_arms (duplicate match arms), and if_same_then_else (identical branches). These cover common syntactic duplication patterns but miss copy-paste across functions, modules, or non-trivial structural variants.
Golden Hammer is a design judgment call — it requires understanding project intent and whether a pattern is being over-applied. No Clippy rule addresses this.

Lava Flow (dead/obsolete code nobody dares remove) is partially addressed: Rust's built-in dead_code lint and Clippy's collection_is_never_read (nursery/allow) flag unused items. However, Lava Flow also covers code that is technically reachable but serves no real purpose — a semantic judgment no static tool can make fully.

Clippy's approx_constant rule (correctness/deny) flags hardcoded floating-point approximations of stdlib math constants (π, e, etc.). This covers one narrow class of hard-coding but does not detect hardcoded strings, magic integers, configuration literals, or other general hardcoded values.

Action at a Distance (non-local, surprising side effects) requires understanding of causality across far-apart code paths. Clippy's await_holding_lock/await_holding_refcell_ref cover specific async side-effect hazards, but the general anti-pattern — where state changes in one place invisibly alter behavior elsewhere — requires semantic/contextual analysis beyond static pattern matching.

clippy::fn_params_excessive_bools flags functions with too many boolean parameters (default threshold ≥3), catching the multi-flag case but missing the single-bool flag argument anti-pattern by default.

Hidden Dependency (implicit global state / service-locator coupling) is a design-level smell requiring semantic understanding of what a dependency is; Clippy has no rule that detects implicit coupling to global state in function signatures.

Premature Optimization (sacrificing readability for unvalidated performance gains) is a judgement call about intent and necessity; Clippy has no rule targeting this pattern — its manual_* lints address stdlib reimplementation, not optimization trade-offs.

Clippy's extensive suite of manual_* lints (manual_map, manual_strip, manual_clamp, manual_ilog2, manual_retain, etc.) catches reimplementation of stdlib functionality, which is the most common form of Inner-Platform Effect, but misses higher-level reimplementation of language/framework abstractions.

Big Ball of Mud is an architectural anti-pattern (lack of module boundaries, pervasive coupling); Clippy operates at the expression/statement level and has no rule for detecting module-level architectural chaos.

Stovepipe System (isolated subsystems with brittle point-to-point connections, no shared infrastructure) is an architecture-level pattern that requires cross-module dependency graph analysis; Clippy has no such rule.
Database as IPC requires semantic understanding of which types represent database operations used as a messaging channel; Clippy has no domain-aware rule for this pattern.

Vendor Lock-in (absence of abstraction over vendor-specific APIs) requires knowledge of which APIs are vendor-specific and whether abstraction layers exist; Clippy has no rule for this architectural concern.

Clippy has opt-in proxy metrics for SRP violations: `cognitive_complexity` (threshold-based, Restriction category), `too_many_lines`, `too_many_arguments`, and `too_many_fields`. These catch symptoms of doing too much but cannot detect mixed concerns semantically.
No Clippy rule targets the Open/Closed Principle. Detecting whether code is open for extension but closed for modification requires architectural intent analysis that static linting cannot perform.

Clippy cannot detect LSP violations. Verifying that a trait implementation preserves the behavioral contract of the trait requires semantic reasoning about pre/postconditions that is beyond static pattern matching.

No Clippy rule detects overly fat trait interfaces or methods that trait implementors are forced to provide but never use. Detecting ISP violations requires cross-crate usage analysis beyond what Clippy performs.

Clippy's `borrowed_box` (Complexity/Warn, default-on) flags `&Box<T>` and suggests `&T`, nudging toward trait-based abstractions over concrete types. No rule directly enforces depending on traits rather than concrete structs in general.

`borrowed_box` (default Warn) encourages `&T` over `&Box<T>`, promoting programming to traits rather than concrete types. `single_fn_generic_bound` (Pedantic, opt-in) guides toward better generic abstraction. Neither rule enforces the principle systematically.

Rust has no class inheritance, so the classic composition-over-inheritance smell does not apply as in OOP languages. Clippy has no rule that detects inappropriate delegation patterns or overuse of trait default methods as a substitute for inheritance. The na verdict is tempting but the GoF principle still manifests in Rust (e.g., wrapping structs vs. blanket impls); Clippy simply has no detector for it.

No Clippy rule detects failure to encapsulate volatile behavior. The nursery lint `branches_sharing_code` flags duplicated code across match arms but does not reason about whether varying behavior is properly isolated behind an abstraction.
Coupling is an architectural/design-level concept requiring whole-system judgement; Clippy has no rule that detects tight coupling or excessive inter-module dependencies.
Whether code passes tests is a runtime property; static analysis cannot verify it. Clippy is a linter and has no mechanism to confirm test outcomes or coverage.

Clippy's style lints catch some surface-level intent signals (disallowed placeholder names like 'foo'/'bar', non-idiomatic boolean comparisons, blocks in conditions) but cannot assess semantic clarity or design intention broadly.

Clippy detects structural/syntactic duplication via branches_sharing_code and collapsible_* lints, but misses semantic duplication (duplicate logic with different variable names or split across functions).

Rustc's dead_code lint (surfaced in Clippy's recommended set) catches unused functions, types, and fields — the mechanical subset of YAGNI — but unnecessary abstractions or over-engineered design are beyond static detection.
The Clean Architecture Dependency Rule (inner layers must not depend on outer layers) requires crate/module boundary analysis; Clippy has no rule for enforcing architectural layer constraints.
Separation of concerns into layers is an architectural judgement requiring contextual understanding of domain boundaries; no Clippy rule addresses layered architecture or mixed-concern detection.
Clippy cannot determine which modules constitute 'inner' vs 'outer' layers in a given architecture, so it cannot detect when inner-layer code imports outer-layer types.

The Boy Scout Rule ('leave code cleaner than you found it') is a process/culture heuristic with no static representation; Clippy has no rule for detecting whether a commit improved or degraded surrounding code quality.
Clippy has no rule enforcing that ports (boundaries) be declared as traits/interfaces; this is an architectural intent that requires design-level reasoning beyond AST analysis.
Whether a struct correctly implements a port (adapter role) is an architectural decision; Clippy has no rule that verifies adapter-to-port conformance at the hexagonal architecture level.

Clippy cannot determine whether domain/core code is 'pure' in the hexagonal sense (free of I/O, infrastructure concerns); this requires semantic understanding of module roles and dependency intent, not pattern matching.
The concept of 'driving side' (initiating side of hexagonal architecture) is a design-level classification with no syntactic marker; Clippy has no rule for this.
The concept of 'driven side' (infrastructure/secondary adapters in hexagonal architecture) is a design-level classification; Clippy has no rule for detecting or enforcing it.

Testability-by-design (e.g. dependency injection through ports) is an architectural property; Clippy does not evaluate whether code structure enables substitution or test isolation at the design level.

Clippy has no understanding of architectural layer boundaries or intended dependency direction; enforcing 'dependencies point inward' requires module-graph analysis with user-defined layer annotations, which Clippy does not support.

Clippy's 828 rules focus on code correctness, style, and performance at the function/module level; it has no rules for the Reuse/Release Equivalence Principle, which requires reasoning about component release and reuse cohesion across crates.

Clippy has no rules for the Common Closure Principle; detecting whether classes/modules that change together are packaged together requires change-history and semantic analysis far beyond static pattern matching.

Clippy has no lint for detecting cycles in the component (crate/module) dependency graph; the Acyclic Dependencies Principle requires cross-crate dependency graph analysis which is outside Clippy's scope.

Clippy has no rules for the Stable Dependencies Principle; determining component stability and whether dependencies point in the direction of stability requires architectural-level analysis unavailable in Clippy.
Clippy has no rules for the Stable Abstractions Principle; enforcing that stable components be abstract requires component-level metrics (abstractness, instability) that Clippy does not compute.
Clippy has no concept of DDD Bounded Contexts; enforcing context boundaries requires architectural knowledge about domain decomposition that is entirely outside static pattern-matching lint rules.
Clippy has no domain-language naming rules; detecting violations of Ubiquitous Language requires knowing the domain's vocabulary, which cannot be encoded in a general-purpose static analyzer.

Clippy has no rules for DDD Domain Model integrity; detecting domain logic leaking into wrong layers requires understanding the intended layered architecture, which is a semantic/contextual judgment Clippy cannot make.
DDD Repository pattern is an architectural abstraction; Clippy has no rules for detecting violations of layering or data-access separation.
DDD Service Layer boundaries are architectural concerns; Clippy operates at the syntactic/stylistic level and has no rules for service-layer violations.

unreachable_pub flags pub items invisible outside the crate (suggesting they should be private/pub(crate)); partial_pub_fields (restriction lint) flags structs mixing public and private fields — together these catch some over-exposed fields, but neither detects the general 'this collection/record should be encapsulated behind an accessor' smell.

unreachable_pub mechanically flags items declared pub that cannot be reached from outside the crate, directly catching the 'over-broad pub' form of Indecent Exposure; however it misses cases where an item is reachable but its visibility is still wider than necessary within a large codebase or module hierarchy.
Tell Don't Ask (query-then-decide patterns, feature envy) requires semantic understanding of data flow and decision logic; Clippy has no rule that detects this principle violation.
Rust has no classical inheritance or subclasses; the 'Replace Subclass with Delegate' refactoring has no direct analogue, making this class of issue inapplicable in Rust.
Rust does not have inheritance-based type hierarchies, so the 'Collapse Hierarchy' smell (unnecessary superclass/subclass levels) does not exist as a construct in the language.

Refused Bequest (implementor ignores most of a parent's behavior) maps poorly to Rust traits, which are structural contracts without inherited default state; Clippy has no rule detecting under-utilised trait implementations.
Clippy enforces casing/format conventions (snake_case, etc.) but has no rule that evaluates whether an identifier semantically reveals intent — this requires human or LLM judgement.

wrong_self_convention enforces specific Rust method-naming prefixes (as_*, into_*, is_*, to_*, new, from_*) which partially implements 'methods as verbs', but does not enforce general noun/verb naming for all types and methods.
No Clippy rule checks for consistent terminology across a codebase (e.g., detecting simultaneous use of 'fetch' and 'get' for the same concept); this requires semantic/domain analysis.

misnamed_getters catches a specific class of disinformation — getter methods whose name implies returning one field but actually return a different one — but general misleading naming is not detected.
cognitive_complexity (restriction) measures cyclomatic/structural complexity as a proxy, but does not detect mixing of abstraction levels within a function, which is a semantic design principle.
Clippy has no rule enforcing Command-Query Separation; no lint checks whether a function both returns a value and mutates state as a design principle violation.
Clippy has no general side-effect detection rule; specific symptom lints (e.g., await_holding_lock) catch narrow patterns but the broad 'no hidden side effects' principle is not enforced.

fn_params_excessive_bools (pedantic, allow-by-default) flags functions with more than a configurable number of bool parameters (default threshold typically >3), catching multi-bool flag argument patterns but not a single boolean flag argument.

too_many_arguments fires when a function exceeds the argument threshold (default 7), flagging the symptom of the smell, but it does not suggest grouping parameters into a Parameter Object struct — that refactoring insight is absent.

collapsible_if merges nested conditions with && or collapses else-if chains — a different, simpler pattern. No Clippy rule detects deeply nested conditionals that should be replaced with guard-clause early returns.

No Clippy rule detects large match/if-else chains dispatching on type tags and suggests replacing them with trait-based polymorphism; this requires semantic reasoning about dispatch intent beyond static pattern matching.

Clippy has multiple rules that catch specific loop-to-pipeline opportunities (manual_map, manual_filter_map, needless_collect, explicit_iter_loop, manual_flatten, etc.) but coverage is pattern-by-pattern; general imperative accumulation loops without a recognized shape are not flagged.
Clippy has no lint for detecting slow tests or tests that perform unnecessary I/O; test execution performance is a runtime property not addressable by Rust static analysis.
Clippy has no lint for detecting shared mutable state between tests or test ordering dependencies; independence is a runtime/concurrency semantic property that Clippy does not model.
Clippy has no lint for detecting non-determinism sources (unseeded RNG, SystemTime::now, etc.) inside test functions; repeatability requires test-context-aware semantic analysis absent from Clippy.

Clippy lints exist for malformed assertions (assertions_on_constants, missing_assert_message) but no lint detects test functions that contain zero assertions; detecting absent assertions requires understanding test intent beyond Clippy's scope.
Clippy has no rule that detects whether tests were written before production code; this is a process/workflow concern requiring commit-history analysis, not static code analysis.
Red-Green-Refactor is a TDD cycle discipline; Clippy cannot track the ordering of test vs. production code changes across commits — no Clippy rule addresses this.

Fake It Till You Make It is an intentional TDD technique (temporarily hardcoding return values); Clippy cannot distinguish deliberate TDD stubs from accidental hardcoding, and no rule targets this pattern.
Triangulation requires recognising that test cases are insufficient to force generalization; Clippy has no rule that reasons about test-case sufficiency or TDD triangulation.
Assert First is a TDD authoring style (write assertions before setup/action); Clippy has no rule that enforces or detects the order of statements within a test function relative to this technique.

Detecting that production code lacks any corresponding tests requires whole-project coverage analysis; Clippy is a lint tool with no test-coverage tracking. The cfg_not_test rule flags code hidden from test builds but does not identify untested public APIs.
Characterization tests are a semantic intent (capturing existing behaviour to enable refactoring); there is no Clippy rule that identifies or enforces this test authoring pattern.
Sprout Method/Class is a legacy-code refactoring technique applied at design time; Clippy has no rule that recognises or enforces this structural pattern.

Wrap Method/Class is a refactoring pattern suggesting you wrap existing functionality — Clippy has no rule that detects when a method or class should be wrapped; it flags code quality issues, not missing abstractions.
Extract Interface is a refactoring suggestion to derive a trait from a concrete type — Clippy has no rule that identifies when a trait should be extracted from an existing struct or impl block.

Parameterize Constructor/Method detects hardcoded values that should become parameters — Clippy's approx_constant catches approximate math constants but there is no general rule suggesting parameterization of constructors or methods.
Rust has a fully static type system with no gradual typing mechanism analogous to Python Any or TypeScript any; the construct does not exist so there is nothing to detect.

Type narrowing in the TypeScript/JS sense (runtime isinstance checks narrowing a union type) does not exist in Rust — the type system handles variant discrimination at compile time via enums and exhaustive match, not runtime narrowing.
Prototype pollution is a JavaScript-specific vulnerability exploiting the prototype chain; Rust has no prototype-based inheritance model so the construct does not exist.
Global scope pollution via window/global object is a JavaScript-specific concept; Rust has no equivalent global object namespace that user code can accidentally pollute.

Detecting implicit request context smuggled through thread-locals or globals into domain models is an architectural concern requiring semantic understanding — Clippy has no rule targeting this pattern.

Rust has no eval() or runtime dynamic execution equivalent; Clippy has no lint targeting this class. The construct barely exists in Rust (proc-macros run at compile time, not runtime), and there is no documented Clippy rule warning against dynamic execution patterns.
Monkey patching (runtime modification of existing types/methods) is impossible in Rust's static type system. The construct does not exist in the language, making this check inapplicable.
Rust uses explicit compile-time trait bounds rather than duck typing. The duck-typing discipline concept does not apply to Rust's type system, so there is nothing for a linter to enforce or detect.

Clippy itself does not lint whether a project has configured lints-as-errors. Enforcing `#![deny(clippy::all)]` or `-D clippy::all` is a build-system/CI configuration decision, not a code pattern Clippy detects. Clippy's correctness group is deny-by-default but that is a lint-level setting, not a Clippy rule that checks whether the project enforces lints as build failures.

Clippy has no lint that enforces or checks test coverage thresholds. Test coverage measurement requires separate tooling (llvm-cov, cargo-tarpaulin). Clippy's `missing_docs_in_private_items` only covers documentation, not test existence or coverage.

Clippy has a `deprecated_semver` lint (correctness-level) that validates semver strings in `#[deprecated(since = ...)]` attributes, and cargo-category lints (off by default) can flag multiple versions of the same crate. These cover narrow sub-cases but Clippy has no general rule for pinning or validating external API contracts.

Clippy has no lint that requires structured logging (e.g., enforcing use of `tracing` structured fields over bare `println!` or `log::info!` with interpolated strings). The separate `clippy-tracing` crate adds instrumentation but is not a bundled Clippy rule.
Correlation IDs are an architectural runtime pattern (passing request-scoped IDs through middleware/context). No static analysis lint can mechanically enforce this; Clippy has no rule addressing it.
Clippy operates at code correctness and style level; health check / readiness probe patterns are an application architecture concern with no corresponding Clippy lint.
Alerting strategy (symptoms vs. causes) is an operational/monitoring concern entirely outside Clippy's scope; no Clippy rule addresses it.
Postel's Law is a high-level API design principle (conservative output, liberal input); it has no mechanically detectable code pattern and no corresponding Clippy lint.

Clippy's `deprecated_semver` only validates that the `since` field in `#[deprecated]` is a valid semver string, not actual API breaking changes or semver compliance. Comprehensive semver checking requires a separate tool (cargo-semver-checks).

Clippy has no rule detecting backwards-compatibility breakage in public APIs; `avoid_breaking_exported_api` is a config that suppresses refactor suggestions, not a detector. Backward-compat detection requires cargo-semver-checks.

Clippy's `missing_errors_doc` (pedantic, non-default) flags public functions returning `Result` or `Option` that lack a `# Errors` doc section, covering undocumented error contracts. Opt-in only (pedantic group) and checks documentation presence, not correctness or completeness.

Clippy's `wildcard_dependencies` (cargo group) flags `*` version constraints in direct deps; `multiple_crate_versions` detects duplicate versions in the dependency graph. Neither enforces Cargo.lock pinning for transitive deps nor catches under-constrained ranges beyond wildcards.

Clippy has no lint for unnecessary or excessive dependencies. The rustc `unused_crate_dependencies` is a compiler lint (not Clippy), and Clippy's cargo-category lints cover metadata and version conflicts, not dependency minimality.

source_label: clippy::multiple_crate_versions (closest cargo lint, but not a security audit)
rationale: Clippy's cargo lints check metadata and duplicate crate versions, but do not perform security audits or vulnerability checks. Dependency auditing (CVE scanning) requires a separate tool such as `cargo-audit`.

Single-responsibility at the package level is an architectural judgment. Clippy's `module_inception` lint flags trivial naming anti-patterns but does not analyze whether a crate's public API cohesively covers one responsibility.

Clippy has several rules that flag unnecessary mutability (`needless_pass_by_ref_mut`, `unnecessary_mut_passed`, `mut_mut`) and interior mutability warnings (`declare_interior_mutable_const`), but these cover only syntactic/mechanical cases. They do not detect design-level decisions where a `mut` binding could be replaced by a purely functional approach.

Share-nothing architecture is a distributed-systems design principle (avoiding shared mutable state across threads/processes). While Clippy flags some unsafe shared-state patterns (`static_mut_refs`, `await_holding_lock`), it has no rule that detects or enforces a share-nothing design at the architectural level.
Idempotency is a semantic property of operations that requires understanding domain logic and state transitions. Clippy has no rule that detects whether a function or API is idempotent.

Clippy does not have a rule that detects implicit state machines or encourages encoding them as explicit enums. There are no lints that flag boolean/integer state flags that should instead be modeled as typed state enums.

Clippy warns about specific unsafe patterns involving global/shared resources: `static_mut_refs` (taking references to mutable statics), `declare_interior_mutable_const` and `borrow_interior_mutable_const` (misuse of interior mutability in consts). These cover narrow mechanical cases but do not detect the broader design concern of inappropriate ownership of shared resources.

Clippy's documentation lints (`missing_docs`, `doc_markdown`) enforce presence and formatting of documentation but have no ability to distinguish comments that explain mechanical 'what' from comments that explain 'why' (decisions, intent, constraints). That distinction requires semantic understanding.

Clippy's missing_safety_doc, missing_errors_doc, and missing_panics_doc (pedantic/style group) flag absent required doc sections on public APIs, but cannot detect stale, inaccurate, or out-of-sync documentation — only structural absence.

Clippy has needless_doctest_main and test_attr_in_doctest which detect malformed or misused doctests, but cannot detect missing doctests or failing doctest execution — that requires cargo test --doc, not static analysis.

Clippy's nursery-group redundant_pub_crate catches one narrow case (pub(crate) inside already-private modules). The general unreachable_pub lint that flags over-public items is a rustc lint, not Clippy. No usage-based analysis to determine if visibility is broader than necessary.

Defence-in-depth is an architectural/design-level property requiring understanding of threat models and security layering. Clippy has no lints for detecting missing validation layers, redundant checks, or security boundary enforcement.

Clippy's restriction-group panic_in_result_fn and unwrap_in_result flag panics/unwraps inside Result-returning functions, enforcing error propagation over crashes. However, they cannot verify whether the error path actually denies access (fail-secure semantics) vs. just avoids panicking.

PHPStan is a type-checker; it has no rule that identifies semantically vacuous code comments. It can flag missing or structurally invalid PHPDoc blocks, but that is unrelated to redundant prose comments.
Test coverage is a runtime metric collected by PHPUnit + Xdebug/PCOV. PHPStan performs static analysis only and has no mechanism to determine which code paths are exercised by a test suite.
Single Responsibility Principle violations in functions require semantic/design-level judgement. PHPStan has no rule for function-level SRP; complexity metrics live in tools like PHPMD, not PHPStan.
PHPStan has no rule to detect that a file mixes too many classes or responsibilities. This is an architectural concern beyond static type analysis.

The official phpstan-phpunit extension adds PHPUnit-aware type-safety rules (assert type correctness, mock usage), catching some structural misuse. It does not validate naming conventions, missing annotations, or broader test-class structure.

source_label: PHPStan — Detecting unused private properties, methods, constants
rationale: PHPStan level 4+ includes built-in detection of unreachable code, always-true/always-false conditions, and unused private properties/methods/constants. Comprehensive dead-code detection is a documented default capability.

Whether a CHANGELOG file was updated is a source-control/process concern. PHPStan analyzes PHP code for type errors and has no facility to inspect file modification history or enforce process-level conventions.
Explicit code coverage percentages are a runtime metric (PHPUnit + coverage driver). PHPStan performs static analysis and cannot measure what fraction of code is exercised by tests.

PHPStan's phpunit extension (phpstan-phpunit) checks test assertion style and structure (e.g., assertSame vs assertEquals, data provider types), but has no rules that detect whether tests verify requirements vs. implementation details — that is a semantic/design concern beyond static type analysis.
PHPStan has `array.duplicateKey` for duplicate keys within a single array literal, but no rules for detecting duplicated string/numeric literals or magic numbers repeated across a codebase.
PHPStan has type-narrowing rules and `cast.useless` for redundant casts, but 'normalize data/code to remove ambiguity' is a design-level concept with no corresponding PHPStan rule category.
No PHPStan rules address heuristic vs. deterministic logic; this is a semantic/architectural concern that requires human or LLM judgement, not a mechanical pattern detectable by a type-checker.

PHPStan has no rule category for 'brittle patterns' in the design sense; it detects type unsafety and some unsafe dynamic constructs (`variable.dynamicName`) but not fragility or maintainability concerns generally.
PHPStan has no rules for detecting over-specificity or insufficient generalization; this is a design-quality judgement with no mechanical static-analysis equivalent in PHPStan's rule set.

PHPStan's 28 class-related error identifiers cover inheritance, visibility, and declaration correctness, but none measure class size or method count; PHPStan is a type-checker, not a complexity/metrics tool.

PHPStan has no rules for Primitive Obsession; it validates how primitives are used type-safely but does not enforce using value objects instead of primitives — a design-pattern concern outside its scope.

PHPStan has no rule to detect groups of parameters/fields that co-occur across method signatures; detecting data clumps requires cross-method parameter-pattern heuristics outside PHPStan's analysis model.
PHPStan does not flag switch/match as a design smell; phpstan-strict-rules' switchConditionsMatchingType checks type-match correctness only, not polymorphism opportunity.
PHPStan's property.unused detects fully-unused private properties, not the Temporary Field pattern where a field is conditionally populated along certain code paths.
PHPStan verifies type-correctness of inherited method calls but has no rule to detect when a subclass fails to use inherited behavior (Refused Bequest).
Detecting semantically equivalent classes with different interfaces requires cross-class semantic similarity analysis that is entirely outside PHPStan's type-checking model.
Divergent Change is a temporal smell visible only across commits; PHPStan performs snapshot-only analysis and has no history or change-tracking model.
Shotgun Surgery is a temporal/evolutionary smell requiring cross-commit coupling analysis; PHPStan's coupling awareness is limited to type-safety at call sites, not design-smell signals.
Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies require detecting cross-hierarchy structural co-evolution; PHPStan does not model inheritance-hierarchy co-dependency patterns.
PHPStan has no rule for detecting classes that do too little (Lazy Class); this requires architectural judgment about a class's responsibility that static type analysis cannot make.
PHPStan cannot distinguish a problematic Data Class (only fields + accessors, no behavior) from a legitimate DTO; no rule targets this structural smell.
Speculative Generality (unused abstractions, dead interfaces, over-engineered hierarchies) requires intent-level reasoning; PHPStan has no rule for this design smell.
PHPStan analyses code semantics and types, not documentation density; it has no rule to flag over-reliance on comments as a code smell.
Feature Envy (a method that uses another class's data more than its own) requires cross-class data-flow analysis that PHPStan does not perform; no rule covers this.
Inappropriate Intimacy (excessive coupling between classes) is an architectural smell; PHPStan enforces access modifiers but does not detect pathological inter-class coupling patterns.
PHPStan validates that each chained call is type-correct but has no rule to flag long method chains (a.getB().getC().getD()) as a design smell.
Middle Man (a class that mostly delegates to another) requires reasoning about the ratio of delegation vs. own logic; PHPStan has no rule for this design smell.

PHPStan analyzes type safety across levels 0–10; no built-in or official extension rule measures class size (method count, field count, responsibilities). PHPMD has TooManyMethods/TooManyFields but PHPStan does not.
No PHPStan rule (core or any major extension) analyzes getter/setter ratio vs. business logic; detecting anemic domain models requires semantic heuristics absent from PHPStan's type-focused rule set.
No PHPStan rule detects a class that exists solely to delegate to another without independent logic; structural intermediary analysis of this kind is absent from all surveyed PHPStan packages.

PHPStan has no rule that measures inheritance chain depth or flags excessively deep hierarchies; neither phpstan-strict-rules nor any major community extension (symplify, ergebnis, shipmonk) adds this.
Sequential coupling requires inter-procedural state/flow analysis across call sites; PHPStan has no rule modeling method-call ordering constraints and no community extension covers this pattern.

No PHPStan core rule or documented community extension flags interfaces that contain only constants; exhaustive search of phpstan-src, phpstan-strict-rules, symplify, ergebnis, and shipmonk found no match.

No PHPStan rule counts methods declared in an interface and flags excessive count; neither core rules nor phpstan-strict-rules, symplify/phpstan-rules, shipmonk/phpstan-rules, nor ergebnis/phpstan-rules include this check.
No PHPStan core rule or surveyed community extension detects the Singleton pattern (private constructor + static getInstance()); PHPStan's scope is type correctness, not creational-pattern overuse.

PHPStan has no built-in rule for combinatorial explosion (excessive boolean-flag parameters or combinatorial branching). Complexity metrics require the third-party cognitive-complexity extension, which is not bundled.
PHPStan cannot detect tramp data (parameters threaded through call chains unused at intermediate layers). No built-in or standard rule exists for this inter-procedural coupling pattern.

PHPStan does not detect spaghetti code (tangled unstructured control flow) out of the box. Unreachable-code detection at level 4+ is a narrow proxy; general structural complexity analysis requires third-party extensions.
PHPStan has no copy-paste/clone detection rule. Duplicate code detection requires a dedicated tool (e.g. phpcpd) or a custom PHPStan extension; nothing is bundled in the standard distribution.

Golden Hammer (over-reliance on a familiar pattern or class) is an architectural/design-pattern smell with no corresponding PHPStan rule. Static type analysis cannot identify inappropriate pattern overuse without semantic context.

source_label: PHPStan: Detecting Unused Private Properties, Methods, Constants
rationale: PHPStan detects unused private properties, methods, and constants (bleeding-edge rules) and unreachable code at level 4+, catching the dead-code subset of Lava Flow. However, abandoned public API, experimental code, or dead code reachable only via reflection is not detected.

PHPStan has no built-in rule for detecting magic literals or hardcoded values that should be constants or configuration. No standard or strict-rules bundle includes magic-number/magic-string detection.
