PHPStan's purity analysis (@phpstan-pure) requires explicit annotations and only verifies annotated functions; it does not proactively detect action-at-a-distance (non-local state mutation as a side effect of seemingly unrelated calls). General detection of implicit global/shared-state side effects is outside PHPStan's scope.

The general 'boolean flag argument' code smell is a design judgment requiring semantic understanding. PHPStan's phpstan-strict-rules enforces the $strict parameter on a handful of built-in functions (in_array, array_search, etc.), which is a completely different, unrelated check and does not detect the general design pattern.

No core PHPStan rule detects hidden dependencies (service locator, static calls, globals used instead of injection). Third-party packages like symplify/phpstan-rules can enforce DI patterns via custom configuration, but nothing ships out of the box with PHPStan itself.

Premature optimization is an intent-based design judgment (code was optimized without evidence of a bottleneck) that cannot be determined from syntax or types. No PHPStan rule exists for this; it requires human or LLM review.

Inner-platform effect (reimplementing language/framework features) requires knowing what the framework already provides and recognising a custom implementation duplicates it — semantic reasoning beyond static type analysis. PHPStan has no rule for this.

Big Ball of Mud is a whole-system architectural smell (no layering, everything coupled). PHPStan analyses individual files and type correctness; it has no metric or rule for detecting absence of architecture at the system level.

Stovepipe System (siloed subsystems, no shared abstractions, duplicated logic) is an architectural/structural pattern requiring cross-module comparison and intent analysis. PHPStan does not detect this pattern.

Database as IPC (polling a DB table as a message queue between services) is a runtime architectural/integration pattern invisible at the PHP type level. PHPStan has no concept of inter-service communication topology.

Vendor lock-in (direct coupling to vendor-specific APIs without an abstraction layer) is an architectural concern. PHPStan's core ships no rule to detect or enforce layering boundaries; third-party packages can add such checks only with explicit custom configuration, not out of the box.
PHPStan has no rule that measures class responsibilities, method counts, or cohesion metrics. SRP requires design-level judgment that static type analysis cannot automate.
OCP is an architectural principle about extension points and rigidity. PHPStan is a type-safety tool; it has no concept of whether code is 'open for extension, closed for modification'.

PHPStan (level 3+) checks method override covariance/contravariance via error identifiers method.childParameterType and method.childReturnType, catching type-level LSP violations. Behavioral contract violations (same types, changed semantics) are not detectable.
PHPStan has no rule that detects fat interfaces or clients forced to implement unused interface methods. No official extension covers this.
PHPStan validates types but has no rule that flags dependence on concrete classes instead of abstractions. DIP requires architectural intent that is invisible to type checking.

PHPStan enforces that declared interface contracts are type-correct, but has no rule requiring method signatures to use interfaces rather than concrete types. 'Program to an interface' is a design preference, not a type error.
PHPStan has no rule that detects deep or unnecessary inheritance hierarchies or recommends composition. It validates inheritance when used correctly but does not flag overuse.
PHPStan has no rule detecting unencapsulated variation. Identifying 'what varies' and whether it is properly encapsulated requires design-level semantic understanding beyond static type analysis.
PHPStan has no rule for measuring coupling or enforcing loose coupling/DI patterns; architectural dependency enforcement belongs to Deptrac, not PHPStan.
Static analysis cannot execute or verify tests; 'passes the tests' is a runtime property entirely outside PHPStan's scope.
Revealing intention through naming/structure is a subjective, semantic judgment; PHPStan only checks case-sensitivity of names, not whether names communicate intent.

PHPStan detects narrow duplication cases (duplicate array keys, duplicate function arguments, duplicate declarations) but not general code-clone / copy-paste DRY violations; PHPCPD is the dedicated tool for that.

source_label: PHPStan blog: detecting unused private properties, methods, constants
rationale: PHPStan detects unused private properties/methods/constants (bleeding-edge, level 4+) and dead branches/unreachable code, but public dead code requires the third-party shipmonk/dead-code-detector extension and YAGNI at design level (unnecessary abstractions, over-engineering) is not covered.

PHPStan has no built-in or bundled rule for Clean Architecture's Dependency Rule (inner layers must not depend on outer layers); this requires a dedicated architecture-enforcement tool such as Deptrac.
Separating concerns into layers is a design-level judgment requiring architectural context; PHPStan performs type/flow analysis and has no concept of layers or SRP cohesion.

Verifying that inner layers have no knowledge of outer layers is identical to enforcing the Dependency Rule; PHPStan cannot enforce directional namespace/dependency constraints without an architecture tool like Deptrac.

PHPStan has no concept of code quality delta over time; it cannot detect whether a commit leaves code cleaner than it found it. The Boy Scout Rule requires comparing before/after state across a diff, which is outside PHPStan's static-analysis model entirely.

PHPStan has no built-in rule that enforces 'ports must be PHP interfaces'. Naming conventions and namespace checks are not included in any standard rule level (0-10); architectural structure is outside PHPStan's default scope.

PHPStan does not check whether classes in an 'adapter' namespace implement the corresponding port interface. Adapter-to-port binding is an architectural convention that PHPStan's standard rules do not model.

PHPStan cannot determine which classes belong to the 'core' domain and which are infrastructure; it has no layer concept in its standard rules. Detecting impure dependencies in the core requires layer-aware tooling (e.g. Deptrac), not PHPStan out of the box.
PHPStan has no model of 'driving side' (primary) adapters or their correct relationship to application ports. This is an architectural topology concept that falls entirely outside PHPStan's rule set.

PHPStan has no concept of 'driven side' (secondary) adapters or whether they are correctly wired as implementations of output ports. Structural hexagonal topology is outside PHPStan's standard rule set.

PHPStan cannot assess testability by design (i.e. whether the core can be exercised without infrastructure). This is a structural architecture property requiring layer analysis, not type or control-flow analysis.

PHPStan's standard rules have no layer/dependency-direction model; enforcing that dependencies point inward (adapters to core, never core to adapters) requires a separate tool such as Deptrac. PHPStan levels 0-10 cover only type safety and code correctness.

source_label: PHPStan Rule Levels (no architectural cohesion rules listed)
rationale: REP is a component cohesion principle about release packaging — requires cross-component grouping analysis PHPStan does not perform; no rule exists.

source_label: PHPStan Rule Levels (no component-grouping rules listed)
rationale: CCP requires analyzing which classes change together across releases — a historical/change-tracking concern entirely outside PHPStan's static type-analysis scope.

source_label: PHPStan Rule Levels (no dependency-cycle rules listed)
rationale: PHPStan has no built-in cycle detection across components; ADP enforcement is handled by separate tools (Deptrac, PHPat) that are not PHPStan rules.

source_label: PHPStan Rule Levels (no stability-metric rules listed)
rationale: SDP requires measuring component stability metrics and enforcing directional dependency flow — graph-level architectural analysis not supported by PHPStan.

source_label: PHPStan Rule Levels (no stable-abstractions rules listed)
rationale: SAP requires correlating component stability with abstractness across the component graph — PHPStan has no rules for component-level abstraction metrics.

source_label: PHPStan Rule Levels (no bounded-context rules listed)
rationale: Enforcing bounded context boundaries requires domain-defined namespace/layer rules; PHPStan has no built-in support — Deptrac/PHPat are separate tools.

source_label: PHPStan Rule Levels (no naming-semantics rules listed)
rationale: Ubiquitous language enforcement requires semantic domain vocabulary knowledge; no PHPStan rule can check whether names align with a domain's ubiquitous language.

source_label: PHPStan Rule Levels (no domain-layer isolation rules listed)
rationale: Domain model purity (no infrastructure leaking into domain) requires architectural layer rules not present in PHPStan core; PHPat extension covers this but is a separate tool.

PHPStan has no built-in or standard-extension rule for DDD Repository pattern violations. Detecting misuse of repositories (e.g., accessed outside the service layer) requires architectural layer-boundary analysis that PHPStan cannot perform without custom rules or a dedicated architecture-testing tool like phpat.

PHPStan has no rule for DDD Service Layer violations (domain logic leaking into controllers/other layers). Layer-boundary enforcement requires custom collectors or an external architecture tester; it is not part of PHPStan's standard or strict-rules set.

PHPStan detects unused private members and dynamic properties, but has no rule that flags public fields/arrays that should be encapsulated into private fields or collection objects. The encapsulation smell requires design-level judgment PHPStan does not perform.

PHPStan's Restricted Usage Extensions (v2.1.13+) can enforce @internal tags and namespace-visibility attributes to flag overly-public API surface, and third-party dead-code detectors surface unused public members. However, there is no built-in rule that proactively identifies unnecessarily public visibility without explicit annotation or configuration, so coverage is partial and opt-in.

PHPStan has no rule for Tell Don't Ask violations (querying object state externally then branching on it). Detecting this pattern requires inter-statement data-flow analysis that PHPStan does not perform; no built-in or widely-used extension covers it.

PHPStan has no rule that identifies subclasses used purely for behavioral variation that should instead be composition/delegation. Detecting this opportunity requires semantic understanding of inheritance intent; neither core PHPStan nor phpstan-strict-rules address it.

PHPStan has no rule for detecting unnecessarily deep or redundant class hierarchies. The sealed-classes extension controls extensibility but does not flag unnecessary hierarchy levels. Collapsing a hierarchy requires design judgment beyond PHPStan's scope.

PHPStan's phpstan-strict-rules enforces LSP covariance/contravariance and parent constructor calls, but does not detect Refused Bequest — where a subclass overrides inherited methods with empty bodies or throws to reject unwanted inherited behaviour. Detecting the full pattern requires semantic analysis not present in PHPStan.
PHPStan is a type checker with no rule evaluating whether names reveal intent; naming extensions like symplify enforce structural suffix/prefix patterns, not communicative quality.
No PHPStan core or widely-used extension rule enforces noun/verb grammar on class/method identifiers; structural suffix rules (Interface, Abstract) are unrelated.
Enforcing one word per concept (e.g. not mixing fetch/retrieve/get) requires cross-file vocabulary consistency analysis beyond PHPStan's type-checking scope.
Flagging misleading names (e.g. 'accountList' holding a HashSet) requires correlating a name's semantics against the inferred type — no PHPStan rule does this.
Single level of abstraction per function is an architectural/semantic judgement (mixing high-level orchestration with low-level detail); no PHPStan rule or extension addresses this.
PHPStan has no general CQS rule; symplify's NoReturnSetterMethodRule is a naming-convention proxy covering only set*/add*-prefixed methods and requires opt-in community package.
PHPStan has no purity/side-effect analysis; ergebnis rules restrict by-reference passing but do not detect side effects (I/O, global state, logging) in general.
PHPStan core has no boolean flag argument rule; pmarki/phpstan-rules has one but is a niche low-adoption package, not a widely-used bundled extension.

source_label: PHPStan rule levels — no parameter-object rule
rationale: PHPStan has no rule detecting when multiple parameters should be consolidated into a parameter object; this is a semantic design-level refactoring beyond static type analysis.

source_label: PHPStan rule levels — no guard-clause rule in core
rationale: PHPStan core has no nesting-depth or guard-clause rule; the only packages that detect this (e.g. sanmai/phpstan-rules) are niche third-party add-ons (~18K installs, 3 GitHub stars), not widely-used bundled rules.

source_label: PHPStan rule levels — no polymorphism-replacement rule
rationale: PHPStan checks exhaustiveness of switch/match on enums (type-safety) but has no rule that flags a conditional chain as a candidate for polymorphism replacement; that is a design-pattern judgement beyond static analysis.

source_label: PHPStan rule levels — no loop-to-pipeline rule
rationale: PHPStan provides type inference for array_map/filter/reduce but has no rule that detects an imperative loop as a candidate for pipeline replacement.
PHPStan and the official phpstan-phpunit extension perform type analysis on test code but have no rule detecting slow-test anti-patterns such as HTTP calls, DB queries, or sleep() inside tests.

Neither PHPStan core nor phpstan-phpunit detect test inter-dependencies (e.g. @depends annotations or shared mutable static state); test isolation is a runtime/design concern outside static type analysis.
PHPStan has no rule flagging use of non-deterministic sources (time(), rand(), global state) inside tests; repeatability is a semantic property requiring dataflow reasoning beyond PHPStan's scope.

PHPStan core and the official phpstan-phpunit extension do not detect assertionless tests; phpstan-phpunit adds type-aware assertion helpers but no rule requiring at least one assertion per test method.
PHPStan is a static type analyzer; it has no mechanism to detect whether tests were written before production code — temporal ordering of commits is not something it checks.
Red-Green-Refactor is a development-cycle discipline; no static type rule can detect adherence to or violation of this workflow.
Hardcoded/fake return values are syntactically valid PHP and PHPStan has no rule that flags them as TDD placeholders; it cannot distinguish a fake return from a real one by intent.
Triangulation is a TDD design strategy requiring multiple test cases to converge on a general solution; PHPStan has no rule related to test-case sufficiency or generalization strategy.
Assert First is a TDD authoring technique; PHPStan cannot detect the order in which a developer wrote assertions relative to implementation code.

source_label: PHPStan blog: Find Bugs Without Writing Tests
rationale: PHPStan does not track which production classes or methods have corresponding tests; identifying untested/legacy code requires code-coverage tooling (e.g. Xdebug + PHPUnit), not a type checker.
Characterization tests are a pattern/intent for writing tests against legacy code; PHPStan has no rule to identify whether tests serve a characterization purpose versus a specification purpose.
Sprout Method/Class is a refactoring technique for legacy code; PHPStan cannot detect whether a new method or class was introduced using this pattern versus any other refactoring approach.
Wrap Method/Class is an architectural refactoring pattern (adapter/decorator suggestion); PHPStan has no rule that recommends wrapping existing methods or classes.
Extract Interface is an architectural refactoring suggestion; PHPStan has no rule that detects when a concrete class should expose an interface.

Parameterize Constructor/Method (moving hard-coded values into parameters) is a design refactoring concern; PHPStan has no rule that flags hard-coded literals that should be constructor/method parameters.

source_label: PHPStan Rule Levels – Level 6: missing typehint reporting
rationale: PHPStan Level 6 reports missing typehints (parameters, return types, property types), which mechanically flags untyped code — the direct proxy for 'use gradual typing'. However, it only detects absent declarations; it does not advise on typing strategy or prioritise which parts of a codebase to type first.

PHPStan's type-inference engine detects always-true/false conditions (level 4+), redundant instanceof checks, and the 'Lie Detector' flags @var annotations that widen or misstate the actual type; checkTooWideReturnTypesInProtectedAndPublicMethods catches over-broad declared return types. These cover common narrowing mistakes mechanically, but incorrect domain-level narrowing logic is not caught.
Prototype pollution is a JavaScript/Node.js vulnerability tied to dynamic prototype chains; the construct does not exist in PHP, so PHPStan cannot apply.
Global scope pollution via implicit globals is a JavaScript concept (var hoisting, window properties); PHP has no equivalent mechanism, so this issue class does not apply.
Detecting request-context objects (e.g. HttpRequest) leaking into model/domain classes requires architectural understanding of layer boundaries; PHPStan has no built-in rule for this pattern.

PHPStan core has no built-in rule for eval() or dangerous execution functions. The third-party phpstan-disallowed-calls extension provides bundled configs (disallowed-dangerous-calls.neon, disallowed-execution-calls.neon) that cover eval(), exec(), shell_exec(), system(), passthru(), etc., but this requires installing and configuring a non-bundled extension.

Monkey patching (runtime class/method modification via runkit, uopz, or reflection) occurs at runtime and is fundamentally undetectable by static analysis. No PHPStan core rule or known extension addresses this pattern.

PHPStan does not have a rule that enforces explicit interface declarations when duck typing is used. It does catch calls to undefined methods on typed objects (level 0+), which detects duck typing violations after the fact, but it does not require callers to declare interface contracts proactively.

'Lint as build failure' is a CI/process policy (configuring the build pipeline to fail on lint errors), not a detectable code pattern. Static analyzers like PHPStan are the lint tool itself and cannot audit whether a project has wired them into CI as a required gate.

PHPStan does not measure or enforce test coverage. The phpstan-phpunit extension validates PHPUnit test code statically (mock types, assertions) but does not track coverage metrics or enforce coverage thresholds. This is explicitly out of PHPStan's scope.

PHPStan has no rule to detect missing version pins in composer.json or validate API contracts against installed dependency versions. It provides stub files to override type info for third-party packages, but this is for type documentation, not contract or version validation.

PHPStan has no built-in or bundled rule to enforce structured logging (e.g., PSR-3 compliance, flagging raw error_log() or echo-based logging). The phpstan-disallowed-calls extension can be manually configured to disallow specific functions, but there is no pre-built structured-logging enforcement rule.

Detecting missing correlation/trace IDs in log calls or HTTP requests requires semantic data-flow analysis across call sites, which is beyond PHPStan's type-checking scope. No PHPStan core rule or known extension addresses this pattern.
PHPStan is a source-code static analyser; health check endpoints and readiness probes are infrastructure/operational concerns with no PHP AST pattern to detect.
Alerting strategy (symptoms vs. causes) is an operational/monitoring design concern entirely outside PHPStan's scope; no rules exist for it.
Postel's Law is a high-level API design principle requiring semantic/contextual judgement; no PHPStan rule models or detects it.
PHPStan has no rules about semver compliance or version-number policy; dedicated external tools exist but are not PHPStan rules.
PHPStan does not compare two versions of a codebase to find BC breaks; the backward-compatibility page covers only PHPStan's own BC promise, not user code.

PHPStan has a PHPStan\Rules\Exceptions namespace and missingCheckedExceptionInThrows rule, but exception-contract checking is entirely opt-in via explicit config and requires classifying which exceptions are 'checked'; nothing runs by default.
PHPStan does not inspect composer.json or composer.lock for version-pinning policy; no bundled rule covers transitive dependency pinning.
PHPStan has no built-in rules for detecting unused or excessive Composer dependencies; separate standalone tools handle this outside PHPStan's standard distribution.

PHPStan is a code-level static analyzer with no knowledge of Composer dependency databases or CVE feeds; dependency vulnerability auditing is handled by separate tools (composer audit, Snyk, Dependabot) and is entirely outside PHPStan's scope.

PHPStan operates on individual PHP files and cannot reason about package/module boundaries or whether a package has a single coherent responsibility — this is an architectural/organisational concern with no representation in PHP's AST or type system.

PHPStan supports native PHP 8.1 readonly properties and @readonly/@immutable PHPDoc annotations, reporting errors when annotated properties are assigned outside the constructor, but only enforces shallow immutability on opt-in-annotated classes — it does not proactively flag all mutable-by-default classes.

No built-in PHPStan core rule flags global/static state; detection requires opt-in third-party extensions such as jonbaldie/phpstan-extension-accessing-globals or spaze/phpstan-disallowed-calls, which can ban superglobal access and the global keyword but are not bundled with PHPStan.
PHPStan has no concept of idempotency; verifying that repeated invocations produce the same result requires semantic/runtime reasoning that is beyond static type analysis.

PHPStan performs type- and flow-level analysis but has no rules to detect ad-hoc or implicit state machine patterns (e.g., bare boolean/string status flags acting as states without a formal state enum or class hierarchy).

PHPStan core does not flag misuse of superglobals or static properties by default; third-party extensions (jonbaldie/phpstan-extension-accessing-globals, spaze/phpstan-disallowed-calls) provide opt-in rules blocking the global keyword, superglobal access, and static property use, but require explicit installation and configuration.
PHPStan validates the technical correctness of PHPDoc type annotations but has no rules to assess comment quality, narrative content, or whether comments explain decisions rather than mechanics.

PHPStan validates PHPDoc accuracy (parameter.phpDocType, return.phpDocType, the 1.10 'lie detector' for @var tags) — it catches when existing docs contradict code types — but does not enforce documentation presence or detect stale/abandoned comments.
PHPStan is a static analyzer with no support for executable documentation or doctests. No rule, extension, or feature in the official or community ecosystem runs documentation examples as tests.

PHPStan (bleeding-edge mode) detects unused private properties/methods/constants, enabling removal of unnecessarily scoped members, but it does NOT flag overly broad visibility (e.g., public members that should be private/protected). The inverse direction — 'this could be narrower' — is not covered.

Defence in depth is an architectural/design principle requiring assessment of layered security controls across system boundaries — PHPStan has no rules that model or check for this; it would require custom domain-specific rules.

Via phpstan-strict-rules, PHPStan flags empty() (which silently coerces types, enabling fail-open logic) and enforces explicit boolean conditions (if.condNotBoolean, ternary.condNotBoolean), catching some fail-open patterns from type juggling. However, it cannot detect application-level fail-secure semantics — e.g., whether a default return value grants or denies access on error.
Psalm analyzes PHP code semantics and types; it has no rules that inspect comment text or detect redundant/meaningless comments.
Psalm is a static type/correctness analyzer; it does not track which code paths are exercised by tests or whether new code has corresponding test coverage.

Psalm's ComplexFunction and ComplexMethod rules flag overly complex functions/methods as a cyclomatic-complexity proxy, but they do not directly detect SRP violations (a short function can violate SRP; a long one may not).
Psalm has no rules for detecting whether a class or file violates the Single Responsibility Principle at an architectural/design level.
Psalm does not analyze test file organization, naming conventions, or conformance to testing framework structural conventions.

source_label: UnusedVariable / UnusedMethod / UnusedClass / UnevaluatedCode (and related rules)
rationale: Psalm ships comprehensive dead-code detection (UnusedVariable, UnusedParam, UnusedMethod, UnusedClass, UnusedProperty, UnusedReturnValue, UnevaluatedCode, and their Possibly* variants) enabled by default via findUnusedCode=true.
Psalm is a PHP source-code analyzer and has no capability to check whether a CHANGELOG or documentation file has been updated.
Psalm does not measure runtime test coverage; that requires separate tools such as PHPUnit with Xdebug/PCOV.
Psalm is a static type checker with no rules for test quality or whether tests exercise requirements vs. implementation details.
DuplicateConstant only catches redeclaration of the same constant name (a compile error), not repeated magic literal values scattered across code. No rule for duplicated literal values.
Psalm has no rules for data normalization or removing structural ambiguity; it checks type correctness, not semantic data-model design.
No Psalm rule targets replacement of heuristic logic with deterministic alternatives; this is a semantic design concern beyond static type analysis.
Psalm has no rules for detecting brittle or fragile code patterns in the general sense; its issue set covers type safety and structural correctness, not fragility heuristics.
Psalm has no rules for over-specificity or insufficient generalization; these are design-level judgements outside its type-checker scope.
Psalm documents ComplexMethod/ComplexFunction for cyclomatic complexity but has no Large Class metric (no rule for class size, number of methods, or responsibilities).
Psalm's type system enforces declared types but has no rule for detecting Primitive Obsession (using primitives where value objects should be used); that is a design smell, not a type error.
Psalm has no rule for Data Clumps; it is a type-safety tool and does not analyze whether groups of parameters or fields habitually travel together.
Psalm has no rule flagging large switch/match statements as a design smell; it checks type correctness inside switch but not the structural anti-pattern.
Psalm's PropertyNotSetInConstructor catches uninitialized properties but not the Temporary Field smell (fields only meaningful under certain conditions); the semantic pattern is not detected.
Psalm has no rule for Refused Bequest; UnimplementedAbstractMethod/UnimplementedInterfaceMethod flag missing overrides but not the smell of a subclass ignoring inherited behavior it received.
Psalm has no rule to detect two classes providing similar functionality under different interface names; this requires cross-class semantic comparison beyond Psalm's scope.
Divergent Change is an architectural smell requiring understanding of change reasons; Psalm performs no change-coupling or cohesion analysis.
Shotgun Surgery requires cross-class change-impact analysis; Psalm performs no such coupling analysis.
Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies is a structural design smell requiring hierarchy-shape analysis; Psalm has no rule for it.

Psalm's UnusedClass rule (enabled via --find-dead-code) detects completely unreferenced classes, which covers one narrow sub-case of Lazy Class (a class so thin it's never used). However, it cannot detect a class that IS used but has trivially little responsibility — the core lazy-class smell.

Psalm has no rule that identifies classes consisting solely of data fields and accessors with no behavioral logic. Its dead-code rules (UnusedProperty, UnusedMethod) flag unused members but cannot detect the structural pattern of a data-only class.

Psalm is a type checker with no rule for detecting over-engineered abstractions or hypothetical generality. While UnusedParam/PossiblyUnusedMethod can flag unreachable members, they do not assess whether an abstraction exists for speculative future use.
Psalm performs no analysis of comment quantity, quality, or compensatory usage. As a type-safety tool it ignores comment patterns entirely.
Psalm has no rule to measure the ratio of foreign-class vs own-class data access within a method. Feature Envy requires behavioral/semantic analysis beyond Psalm's type-checking scope.

Psalm enforces PHP visibility modifiers (InaccessibleMethod, InaccessibleProperty) but this only catches outright illegal access, not the design-level smell of classes that are excessively coupled to each other's internals while still respecting visibility rules.
Psalm validates that individual method calls are type-safe but does not track chain length or flag fluent call chains as a structural smell. No rule for message chain detection exists.

Psalm has no rule to detect a class that predominantly delegates to another. UnusedMethod/PossiblyUnusedMethod can surface unused wrapper methods but cannot identify the architectural pattern of a pass-through middle-man class.
Psalm is a type checker with no rules measuring class size, method count, or responsibility breadth; God Object detection requires structural metrics Psalm does not implement.
Psalm has no rules to analyse behavioural density (method-to-property ratio or absence of domain logic); detecting Anemic Domain Model requires semantic judgement beyond type checking.
Psalm does not track object lifetime or instantiation patterns across call sites; detecting short-lived, stateless Poltergeist classes requires dataflow/lifetime analysis absent from Psalm.
Psalm uses inheritance for type resolution but has no rule measuring inheritance depth or flagging deep hierarchies; Yo-Yo Problem detection requires a depth threshold metric not present in Psalm.
Sequential Coupling requires call-order flow analysis to detect methods that must be invoked in a specific sequence; Psalm performs no such ordering analysis.
Psalm has no rule that flags an interface containing only constants and no method contracts; the Constant Interface anti-pattern is not covered by any documented Psalm issue.
Psalm imposes no method-count thresholds on interfaces; Interface Bloat detection requires a configurable size metric that Psalm does not provide.

Psalm has no built-in rule detecting Singleton implementation or overuse; a third-party plugin (kafkiansky/service-locator-interrupter) covers a related but narrower Laravel-specific pattern, not general Singleton overuse.

Psalm has ComplexFunction and ComplexMethod rules that flag overly complex code, which is a proxy for combinatorial explosion in branching logic, but these are cyclomatic-complexity thresholds rather than a dedicated detector for exponentially-growing code paths.

Psalm has no rule detecting data passed unnecessarily through multiple call layers (tramp data); this is an architectural/design smell requiring semantic understanding that static type analysis does not cover.

Psalm's ComplexFunction/ComplexMethod rules flag functions that are too complicated and should be refactored, serving as a cyclomatic-complexity proxy for spaghetti code, but they do not detect unstructured control flow or tangled logic in general.

Psalm detects duplicate function/method/class declarations via DuplicateFunction, DuplicateMethod, and DuplicateClass rules, but these catch identical re-declarations, not copy-pasted logic blocks with slight variations across the codebase.

Psalm has no rule for detecting overuse of a single pattern, class, or abstraction across the codebase; Golden Hammer is an architectural smell requiring contextual judgment that static analysis does not address.

Psalm's dead-code detection family (UnusedVariable, UnusedClass, UnusedMethod, UnusedProperty, UnusedReturnValue, enabled via --find-dead-code) catches unreferenced code, which is a proxy for lava flow, but requires opt-in and does not detect legacy code that is technically reachable but functionally obsolete.

Psalm's InvalidLiteralArgument rule flags suspicious hardcoded literal values passed where variables are expected, but it targets type mismatches rather than the general smell of magic numbers/strings or hardcoded configuration values.

Psalm's Impure* rule family (ImpureGlobalVariable, ImpureMethodCall, ImpureStaticProperty, etc.) detects side effects in code annotated as @psalm-pure, which is a proxy for action-at-a-distance via global state mutation, but requires @psalm-pure annotations and does not detect the general case in unannotated code.

Psalm's RedundantFlag rule detects only redundant PHP built-in filter flags (e.g. FILTER_NULL_ON_FAILURE paired with a default option), not the general OOP anti-pattern of boolean arguments that control branching within a function.

Psalm's Impure* rules (ImpureGlobalVariable, ImpureFunctionCall, etc.) detect hidden global-state dependencies, but only when the function is explicitly annotated @psalm-pure — no default detection of hidden dependencies in unannotated code.
Premature optimization is a runtime/profiling concern; no static analysis rule in Psalm's documented issue set detects it.
Detecting reimplementation of platform/framework functionality requires semantic knowledge of what the framework provides; Psalm has no rule for this anti-pattern.
Big Ball of Mud is an architectural-level disorder spanning the whole codebase; Psalm's ComplexFunction rule only flags individual function complexity, not systemic structural absence.
Stovepipe System requires architectural analysis of module coupling and reuse across the codebase; Psalm has no documented rule for this pattern.
Psalm's SQL-related rules (TaintedSql etc.) address injection security, not the architectural anti-pattern of using database tables as inter-process communication channels.
Vendor lock-in requires semantic knowledge of which APIs are proprietary; Psalm has no documented rule for detecting tight coupling to a specific vendor's API.
Psalm has no rule for detecting Single Responsibility violations — SRP requires semantic judgement about class cohesion and responsibility boundaries, which is beyond static type analysis.
Psalm has no rule for Open/Closed violations. The principle (open for extension, closed for modification) requires semantic intent analysis that static type checking cannot provide.

Psalm detects type-signature LSP violations via MethodSignatureMismatch, ImplementedReturnTypeMismatch, ImplementedParamTypeMismatch, and LessSpecificImplementedReturnType, but misses behavioral/semantic LSP violations (precondition strengthening, exception narrowing, invariant changes).
Psalm has no rule for detecting fat/bloated interfaces (ISP violations). It enforces that declared interfaces are implemented, but does not flag clients forced to depend on methods they don't use.
Psalm has no rule for detecting dependencies on concrete classes instead of abstractions/interfaces. DIP violations require architectural-level analysis beyond Psalm's type-checking scope.
Program-to-an-interface violations require semantic/architectural judgement; Psalm enforces type correctness but does not flag use of concrete types where abstractions should be preferred.
Psalm has no rule for detecting over-reliance on inheritance instead of composition. This is a design-level principle requiring architectural judgement, not detectable by static type analysis.
Encapsulate-what-varies is a design-level GoF principle about isolating change points; Psalm enforces access modifiers but cannot detect when variation is not properly encapsulated.
Psalm has no built-in rule for detecting tight coupling or enforcing loose coupling; coupling enforcement requires custom plugins or separate tools like Deptrac.
Psalm is a static analysis tool and cannot execute tests or verify that a test suite passes; test-passage verification is outside its scope entirely.
Psalm has no rules for naming quality, intention-revealing identifiers, or semantic clarity; it performs type and security analysis, not code expressiveness checks.
Psalm has no clone-detection or DRY enforcement; code duplication detection requires a separate tool such as PHPCPD.

source_label: UnusedClass (and related UnusedMethod, UnusedVariable, UnusedParam, UnusedProperty)
rationale: Psalm's dead-code detection (UnusedClass, UnusedMethod, UnusedVariable, UnusedParam, UnusedProperty) covers unused/over-engineered elements, but only when the non-default --find-dead-code flag is enabled; it does not cover over-designed abstractions that are actually used.
Psalm has no built-in Dependency Rule enforcement; enforcing directional dependency constraints across architecture layers requires a dedicated tool (e.g. Deptrac) or a custom Psalm plugin.
Psalm has no standard rule for detecting separation-of-concerns violations or layer mixing; such architectural checks are outside Psalm's type-and-security focus.
Psalm has no rule that detects when an inner architecture layer imports or depends on an outer layer; enforcing inner/outer isolation requires a custom plugin or a dedicated dependency-guard tool.

The Boy Scout Rule is a process/culture guideline (leave code cleaner than you found it). Psalm has no rule that detects whether a given change improved or degraded code quality relative to its prior state; its unused-code and complexity rules are general quality checks, not a Boy Scout enforcement mechanism.

Psalm has no rule that enforces ports must be PHP interfaces rather than concrete classes; its type system validates that interface contracts are honored once declared, but does not detect that a given boundary should have been declared as an interface in the first place.

Psalm validates that a class correctly implements the methods of an interface it already declares (via ImplementedParamTypeMismatch etc.), but it cannot detect that an adapter class is missing its required port interface declaration — that architectural obligation is invisible to Psalm.

Psalm has no concept of a 'core' or 'domain' layer and no rule that flags I/O calls or framework imports inside it; detecting purity of a layer requires architectural namespace/dependency mapping that Psalm's built-in rules do not provide.
Psalm does not model hexagonal driving-side responsibilities (primary adapters calling core via ports); there is no built-in rule that detects violations of this pattern.

Psalm does not model hexagonal driven-side responsibilities (secondary adapters implementing port interfaces for DB/external services); no built-in rule enforces or detects violations of this pattern.

Testability-by-design (dependencies injected via interfaces so they can be swapped in tests) is an architectural design principle; Psalm's type checker verifies type contracts but has no rule that flags hard-coded concrete dependencies or untestable coupling.

Enforcing that outer layers depend on inner layers (and not vice versa) requires configurable layer/namespace dependency mapping. Psalm has no built-in rule for this; the third-party kvush/layer-violation-psalm-plugin provides it, but that is not part of Psalm's standard out-of-the-box rule set.
Psalm has no rules for component cohesion or the Reuse/Release Equivalence Principle; it focuses on type correctness and code quality, not package-level design.
Common Closure Principle (group classes that change together) is a design-level judgment with no corresponding Psalm rule.
Psalm's CircularReference rule detects circular class inheritance only (type-system level), not component/namespace-level dependency cycles required by ADP; no package-graph cycle detector exists.
Stable Dependencies Principle requires measuring instability metrics across component dependency graphs; Psalm has no such architectural analysis.
Stable Abstractions Principle requires computing abstraction ratios per component; Psalm has no rule or metric for this.
Bounded Context is a DDD architectural concept enforced through conventions and design; Psalm has no DDD-awareness or context-boundary rules.
Ubiquitous Language consistency requires semantic/domain knowledge to validate naming conventions; Psalm has no domain-language or naming-coherence rules.
Domain Model integrity (aggregates, entities, value objects, invariants) is a design-level DDD concern; Psalm has no domain-model pattern enforcement.
Psalm has no DDD-specific rules; Repository pattern adherence is an architectural concern beyond its 320 documented issue types.
Psalm has no Service Layer or DDD architectural rules; it performs type and purity analysis, not domain-model boundary enforcement.

Psalm enforces access to already-private members (InaccessibleProperty/InaccessibleMethod) but has no rule that flags members which *should* be encapsulated but aren't — the encapsulation-design smell itself is undetected.

Psalm does not flag over-broad visibility (public/protected when private would suffice). UnusedProperty/UnusedMethod require --find-dead-code and only catch dead code, not unnecessarily-wide visibility.
No Psalm rule maps to the Tell Don't Ask principle; Psalm does not analyse whether a caller queries object state and then acts on it externally instead of delegating to the object.

Psalm has no refactoring-catalog rules; it does not suggest converting inheritance to delegation. The inheritance-related rules (MethodSignatureMismatch, LessSpecificImplementedReturnType, etc.) check type correctness, not design suitability.
Psalm does not detect unnecessary inheritance depth. UnusedClass (--find-dead-code) flags completely dead classes but does not identify hierarchies that could be flattened.

Psalm has no rule detecting subclasses that ignore or override everything inherited from a parent (Refused Bequest). UnusedMethod with --find-dead-code catches dead methods in general but does not specifically flag inherited-but-unused members as a design smell.
Psalm has no rule for enforcing intention-revealing names; it is a type-checker, not a naming convention linter.
Psalm does not enforce that classes use nouns or methods use verbs; naming conventions of this kind are outside its rule set.
Psalm has no rule for enforcing one-word-per-concept consistency. ParamNameMismatch only flags renamed params vs. parent methods, not cross-codebase naming consistency.
Psalm has no rule that detects misleading or disinforming names (e.g., a list variable that holds a single item).
Psalm does not detect mixing of abstraction levels within a function; no documented rule covers this structural code smell.
Psalm has no Command-Query Separation rule and does not detect methods that both mutate state and return a meaningful value.

Psalm detects side effects via ImpureFunctionCall and ImpurePropertyAssignment, but only inside functions explicitly annotated @psalm-pure; unannotated functions are not checked, so coverage is opt-in only.
Psalm has no rule for boolean flag arguments; this capability exists in PHPMD (BooleanArgumentFlag) but not in Psalm's documented issue set.

Psalm's TooManyArguments rule fires on call-site arity mismatches, not on function definitions with excessive parameters. No rule in Psalm's ~250-issue list flags a signature as having too many parameters as a design smell.

source_label: Psalm issue list (no nesting rule found)
rationale: Psalm has no nesting-depth metric and no rule targeting deeply nested conditionals or suggesting early-return guard clauses. No rule named DeepNesting, NestedConditional, or similar exists in the full issue list.

source_label: Psalm issue list (no type-switch smell rule)
rationale: Psalm uses instanceof and type narrowing for inference but has no rule flagging chains of instanceof/switch(get_class()) as a design smell suggesting polymorphism. UnhandledMatchCondition is a correctness check, not a refactoring prompt.

Psalm does not flag imperative foreach loops as candidates for array_map/array_filter pipelines. LoopInvalidation detects variable shadowing inside loops, not style preference for functional pipelines.
Psalm is a static analyzer with no runtime profiling; it cannot detect slow I/O in tests. The psalm/plugin-phpunit focuses on data-provider type correctness and dead code, not test execution speed.
Psalm has no rule detecting tests relying on execution order, shared mutable state, or inter-test dependencies. psalm/plugin-phpunit does not detect test isolation issues.
Psalm cannot detect non-determinism (rand(), time(), $_SERVER, etc.) in test code as a FIRST-principle violation. Taint analysis tracks data flows for security, not environment-dependence in tests.

Psalm does not detect test methods lacking assertions. psalm/plugin-phpunit explicitly does not flag missing assertions. UnusedFunctionCall fires on unused return values generally, not on assertion absence in test methods.
Psalm is a PHP type-checker/static analyzer with no concept of when tests were written relative to production code; it cannot detect violations of test-first discipline.
Red-Green-Refactor is a development workflow discipline; Psalm analyzes static code structure and types, not development cycles or commit ordering.
Fake It Till You Make It is a transient TDD coding technique detectable only by human review of test/commit history; Psalm has no rule for hardcoded-value stubs used as TDD placeholders.
Triangulation is a TDD technique about adding test cases to drive generalization; it is a human process decision invisible to static analysis tools like Psalm.
Assert First is a TDD authoring technique (write the assertion before setup); Psalm does not analyze test structure, assertion ordering, or test authoring patterns.

Psalm's dead code rules (UnusedClass, UnusedMethod, etc.) detect unreferenced code, not untested code — these are distinct concepts; determining test coverage requires runtime instrumentation (PHPUnit + Xdebug), not static analysis.
Characterization Tests are a legacy-code testing technique; Psalm does not analyze test intent, test categorization, or whether tests capture existing behavior vs. specify new behavior.

Sprout Method/Class is a refactoring pattern for safely adding features to legacy code; Psalm analyzes type correctness of existing code, not whether a developer applied a particular refactoring technique.
Psalm has no rule that flags Wrap Method/Class as a refactoring opportunity; its refactoring support is limited to renaming/moving, not detecting wrapping candidates.
Psalm has no rule that identifies Extract Interface opportunities; it cannot suggest that a class should expose an interface.
Psalm detects constructor-related type issues (MissingConstructor, PropertyNotSetInConstructor) but has no rule that suggests parameterizing hardcoded dependencies.

Psalm provides MissingParamType, MissingReturnType, and MissingPropertyType rules that encourage adding types, but these only fire at higher strictness error levels (not the default) and do not enforce gradual-typing workflows holistically.

Psalm's flow-sensitive type inference tracks narrowed types through branches; RedundantCondition flags impossible/already-narrowed checks, and TypeDoesNotContainNull / PossiblyNullArgument catch incorrect narrowing deterministically.
Prototype pollution is a JavaScript/prototype-chain construct; PHP has no prototype chain, so the issue class does not exist in this language and Psalm (a PHP tool) cannot apply.
Global scope pollution via implicit globals is a JavaScript-specific concern; PHP's scoping rules differ and Psalm is a PHP-only tool — JS global scope pollution is not a construct it can analyse.

Psalm enforces type-level explicitness (MissingParamType etc.) but has no rule that detects domain-architectural anti-patterns such as injecting request context objects implicitly into model/domain classes.
ForbiddenCode can be configured to ban eval(), but requires explicit opt-in config; TaintedEval flags tainted user input flowing into eval but does not catch unconditional eval use.
No dedicated monkey-patching rule; UndefinedPropertyAssignment catches dynamic property additions but runtime replacement of methods or functions as a policy is not detectable by Psalm.

Psalm's core mission is nominal type enforcement; InvalidArgument, UnimplementedInterfaceMethod, ImplementedReturnTypeMismatch, MethodSignatureMismatch and related rules reliably enforce typed interface contracts.
Lint-as-build-failure is a CI/CD process concern; Psalm provides exit codes but has no rule or configuration that itself enforces linting is wired into a build gate.
Psalm has no test-coverage tracking, coverage threshold enforcement, or test-requirement rules; it performs static analysis only.
Psalm enforces internal interface contracts but has no rules for external API schema validation, OpenAPI/JSON Schema contract pinning, or third-party contract verification.
Psalm has no rules for structured logging format enforcement; it cannot detect unstructured log calls or require structured log patterns.
Psalm has no correlation ID tracking or context-propagation enforcement rules.
Health check / readiness probe patterns are infrastructure/deployment concerns; Psalm is a PHP static analysis tool with no rules covering liveness or readiness endpoint existence or correctness.
Alerting on symptoms vs. causes is an operational/observability design concern; Psalm has no rules related to monitoring strategy or alert semantics.
Postel's Law (liberal input acceptance, conservative output) is an API design principle requiring semantic judgement; Psalm has no rule that enforces or detects violations of this pattern.
Semantic versioning is a release/package management convention; Psalm analyses PHP source code and has no rules that validate or enforce version numbering schemes.

Psalm detects internal backwards-compatibility violations (incompatible method signatures in subclasses/implementations) via MethodSignatureMismatch and related rules, but cannot detect API-level or cross-package breaking changes.

Psalm's MissingThrowsDocblock rule flags functions that throw without a @throws docblock, enforcing documented exception contracts, but it requires opt-in config (checkForThrowsDocblock) and cannot enforce richer contract semantics.
Pinning transitive dependencies is a Composer/package-manager concern; Psalm analyses PHP source code and has no rules covering dependency version pinning.
Minimising dependency footprint is a package management concern; Psalm has no rules that analyse which Composer packages are used or whether a dependency is necessary.

Psalm is a code-level static analyzer with no dependency auditing capability. It has no rules for scanning installed packages for known vulnerabilities or staleness. Tools like `composer audit` serve this purpose.
Psalm has no rules for evaluating package cohesion or single-responsibility at the package/module level. Its analysis is scoped to type-checking and code-level patterns within files.

Psalm can flag classes/interfaces that could be annotated @psalm-immutable via MissingImmutableAnnotation, and enforces immutability contracts via MutableDependency/ImmutableDependency, but this is opt-in and requires annotation-driven workflow — it does not proactively detect all mutable state that could be made immutable.

Psalm's purity system (ImpureStaticProperty, ImpureGlobalVariable, ImpureStaticVariable) detects shared mutable state access inside functions annotated @psalm-pure, but undecorated code sharing state across requests/processes is not flagged — coverage requires explicit purity annotations throughout the codebase.

Psalm has no rules for detecting idempotency violations. Determining whether an operation produces consistent results across repeated calls is a semantic/behavioural property beyond static type analysis.
Psalm has no rules for detecting implicit state machines. ComplexFunction flags high cyclomatic complexity generically but does not identify or enforce explicit state machine patterns.

Psalm's ImpurePropertyAssignment, ImpureMethodCall, and ImpureGlobalVariable rules flag unsanctioned mutations of shared/global resources inside pure-annotated functions, but code not annotated @psalm-pure or @psalm-mutation-free receives no such checks — coverage is limited to the annotated surface.

Psalm enforces type annotations (MissingParamType, MissingReturnType, MissingThrowsDocblock) but has no rules that evaluate comment quality or presence of rationale/decision documentation versus mechanical description.

Psalm enforces that docblocks exist (MissingDocblock, MissingReturnType) but has no rule detecting whether documentation is physically co-located with the code it describes versus scattered in external files or misplaced; proximity is a layout/organisation principle beyond static type analysis.

Executable documentation (doctests, runnable code examples in comments) is a test-runner concern; Psalm is a static type analyser and has no mechanism to execute or verify that inline code examples actually run correctly.

Psalm's InaccessibleMethod / InaccessibleProperty rules catch access-control violations (calling private/protected members from outside scope), enforcing declared visibility. They do not detect over-permissive declarations (e.g. a method that could be private but is public), so only the enforcement half of least-privilege is covered.

Defence in depth is an architectural principle requiring multiple independent security layers; no static analyser can verify the presence or adequacy of layered controls, and Psalm has no rule targeting this design concern.

Fail-secure requires that error/exception paths default to a denied/closed state; this is a semantic security property about control-flow intent that Psalm's type and taint analysis does not model — it can track taint flows but cannot determine whether a fallback is 'secure'.

RuboCop detects only redundant RuboCop directive comments (Lint/RedundantCopDisableDirective, Lint/RedundantCopEnableDirective), not general semantically redundant comments that merely restate what the code does.
RuboCop has no rule for detecting whether new or changed code has corresponding unit tests; coverage tracking is handled by separate tools (SimpleCov, Code Climate) outside RuboCop's scope.

Metrics cops (Metrics/MethodLength, Metrics/CyclomaticComplexity, Metrics/PerceivedComplexity, Metrics/AbcSize) catch proxy signals for over-complex methods but are heuristic thresholds, not true SRP reasoning — they miss functions that are long but cohesive or short but multi-responsibility.

Naming/FileName with ExpectMatchingDefinition enforces one primary class/module per file by filename matching, but cannot detect multiple unrelated responsibilities within a file or measure general file-level cohesion.

Comprehensive test-file structure rules exist in rubocop-rspec (RSpec/DescribeClass, RSpec/HooksBeforeExamples, RSpec/MultipleDescribes, etc.), but rubocop-rspec is a separate gem that must be opted into — it is not bundled with base RuboCop.

Lint/UnreachableCode, Lint/UselessAssignment, Lint/UnreachableLoop, and related Lint/Useless* cops are built into RuboCop and deterministically flag unreachable code paths, unused variables, and other syntactic dead-code patterns.
RuboCop has no rule for verifying that a changelog file was updated; this is a workflow/process concern outside the scope of static code analysis.
RuboCop does not measure or enforce test coverage percentages; that responsibility belongs to dedicated coverage tools such as SimpleCov.

rubocop-rspec has rules like RSpec/MessageSpies and RSpec/InstanceVariable that nudge toward behavior-based testing, but no rule directly detects testing of private methods or stubbing of internal implementation details — the core of this issue.

RuboCop detects duplicate hash keys (Lint/DuplicateHashKey), duplicate set elements (Lint/DuplicateSetElement), and duplicate magic comments, but has no rule for repeated magic numbers or duplicate string literals scattered across the codebase.

RuboCop covers naming normalization (Naming/* cops) and several Lint/Ambiguous* cops (AmbiguousAssignment, AmbiguousOperator, AmbiguousRange), but semantic normalization of domain data representations (e.g. inconsistent nil/empty handling, mixed date formats) is not detected.

No RuboCop rule targets heuristic/approximation patterns and suggests deterministic replacements. Lint/Conversion (to_i vs Integer()) is the closest tangent but addresses type-conversion safety, not heuristic-vs-deterministic design.

RuboCop flags some brittle patterns (Lint/FloatComparison, Lint/AmbiguousRange, Lint/BooleanSymbol, Rails/IpAddress) but has no general detection for fragile string parsing, brittle conditionals, or edge-case-prone structural patterns.

RuboCop detects exact duplication (Lint/DuplicateMethods, Style/CombinableLoops, Rails/DuplicateAssociation) but cannot detect near-duplication or over-specific similar methods that are candidates for generalization — that requires semantic judgment.
Metrics/ClassLength directly measures class size (default 100 lines) and flags Large Class mechanically; Metrics/MethodLength, Metrics/AbcSize, and Metrics/ModuleLength cover related size concerns.

RuboCop has no rule for detecting Primitive Obsession — using primitive types (String, Integer, Hash) where a domain value object should be introduced. Lint/Conversion and Lint/BooleanSymbol are unrelated type-safety nudges, not structural design detectors.

No RuboCop rule detects data clumps (groups of parameters/fields that always travel together). This semantic pattern requires cross-method/cross-class analysis that RuboCop does not perform; Reek is the dedicated tool for it.

Metrics/CyclomaticComplexity flags methods with many branches (including case/when), and Style/CaseLikeIf (non-default) suggests converting if-elsif chains to case. Neither detects the underlying smell of replacing conditionals with polymorphism — only syntactic/complexity proxies.

No RuboCop rule detects temporary fields (instance variables set only in some code paths or only under certain conditions). This requires data-flow / live-variable analysis beyond RuboCop's AST-pattern scope.
No RuboCop rule detects refused bequest (subclasses that ignore or override most inherited behaviour). Detecting this requires cross-class inheritance analysis that RuboCop does not perform.

No RuboCop rule detects alternative classes with different interfaces (semantically equivalent classes with differing method names). This requires semantic cross-class comparison beyond RuboCop's capabilities.

Divergent change (a class that changes for multiple unrelated reasons) requires change-history or responsibility-coupling analysis. RuboCop has no rule for this; even Metrics/ClassLength is only an indirect proxy.
Shotgun surgery (one logical change forces edits scattered across many classes) requires cross-file change-coupling analysis that is outside RuboCop's per-file, AST-pattern model.
Parallel inheritance hierarchies (mirrored hierarchies that must grow in lockstep) require cross-hierarchy structural analysis. RuboCop has no rule that detects this architectural pattern.

RuboCop's Metrics/ClassLength enforces a maximum class size, not a minimum. There is no cop that flags a class for doing too little — detecting a Lazy Class requires semantic judgment about whether a class's responsibilities justify its existence.

No RuboCop cop detects Data Classes (classes with only attr_accessor/attr_reader/attr_writer and no meaningful behavior). This pattern requires semantic analysis of the class's method set that no standard cop provides.

Lint/UnusedMethodArgument and Lint/UnusedLocalVariable catch unused parameters and variables, covering a narrow slice of speculative generality. However, overengineered abstractions, unused modules, or abstract methods added 'just in case' are not detected.

RuboCop's comment cops (Style/BlockComments, Style/CommentAnnotation, Style/CommentedKeyword, etc.) enforce comment formatting and placement, not semantic purpose. No cop detects compensatory comments that exist because code is unclear or overly complex.

No RuboCop cop detects Feature Envy (a method more interested in another class's data than its own). This requires cross-class semantic analysis of data access patterns that static pattern-matching cannot reliably perform.

No general-purpose RuboCop cop detects Inappropriate Intimacy (two classes accessing each other's internals). Rails/HelperInstanceVariable covers one narrow Rails-specific case but does not address the general code smell.

Style/SafeNavigationChainLength flags safe-navigation (&.) chains exceeding a configurable max (default 2), and Style/MultilineBlockChain flags chained multi-line blocks. These cover only syntactic subsets of the Message Chains smell; plain dot-chain length and general Law of Demeter violations are not enforced.

Rails/Delegate enforces use of the Rails `delegate` macro over manual forwarding methods — it is an idiom enforcer, not a Middle Man detector. It does not flag classes where most methods are thin wrappers delegating to another object. No general cop detects the Middle Man smell.

Metrics/ClassLength flags classes exceeding a line-count threshold (default 100), which is a size proxy for God Objects, but RuboCop has no cop that counts the number of methods or measures breadth of responsibilities directly.

RuboCop has no cop to detect data-only classes (pure accessors/attributes with no domain behaviour); detecting Anemic Domain Models requires semantic understanding of method purpose that static line-pattern rules cannot provide.

No RuboCop cop detects classes with suspiciously few methods or transient lifecycle; Poltergeist detection requires reasoning about class purpose and usage patterns across the codebase, beyond static syntax rules.
RuboCop has no cop that measures inheritance hierarchy depth or width; no Metrics rule tracks ancestor chain length, so deep Yo-Yo inheritance trees go undetected.
Sequential coupling (implicit required call order between methods) is a behavioural/semantic pattern requiring control-flow and state-dependency analysis; RuboCop's syntactic rules cannot detect it.

Ruby has no 'interface' keyword or construct; the Constant Interface anti-pattern is Java/C#-specific (implementing an interface solely to inherit its constants). The analogous Ruby idiom (including a module of constants) is idiomatic and has no RuboCop prohibition, but the construct the anti-pattern describes does not exist in Ruby.

Metrics/ModuleLength and Metrics/ClassLength catch modules/classes that grow too large by line count, which correlates with interface bloat, but RuboCop has no cop that counts public methods specifically to flag an oversized public API.
RuboCop has no cop that detects use of Ruby's Singleton module (include Singleton) or class-level singleton patterns; no Style or Lint rule flags or discourages the Singleton pattern.

RuboCop's Metrics/CyclomaticComplexity and Metrics/PerceivedComplexity flag high-complexity methods that often result from combinatorial branching, and Metrics/BlockNesting caps nesting depth. These are structural proxies that catch many instances, but they measure general complexity rather than specifically identifying exponential combinatorial explosions — a combinatorial explosion in data generation would not be detected.

Tramp data is data threaded through intermediate methods that don't use it — a semantic, cross-method data-flow pattern. Lint/UnusedMethodArgument catches unused parameters within a single method but cannot trace data flowing through a call chain unused. No RuboCop cop detects the tramp-data architectural anti-pattern.

Spaghetti code is approximated by Metrics/CyclomaticComplexity, Metrics/PerceivedComplexity, Metrics/MethodLength, and Metrics/AbcSize, all enabled by default. These flag methods that are likely spaghetti, but are indirect proxies — a short method with convoluted logic may not trigger them, and a long well-structured method may.

RuboCop detects specific narrow forms: Lint/DuplicateMethods flags identically-named method definitions, Lint/DuplicateBranch flags repeated branch bodies within a single if/case. These catch some copy-paste artefacts but RuboCop has no token-similarity or cross-method/cross-file clone detection, so the broad class of copied logic is not detected.

Golden Hammer (over-applying a familiar solution regardless of fit) is a contextual, architectural judgement. RuboCop has no rule that detects this anti-pattern; individual style cops nudge toward Ruby idioms but do not identify pattern overuse.

Lava Flow is only partially addressable statically. Lint/UnreachableCode catches syntactically dead code after return/break/raise. Lint/EmptyMethod and Lint/EmptyBlock catch hollow stubs. But unused methods/classes that are still referenced, or obsolete code that is conditionally reachable, are not detected.

Hard coding (magic numbers, magic strings, hardcoded credentials) has no dedicated base RuboCop cop. Style/NumericLiterals only enforces underscore formatting in large numbers, not use of named constants. There is no built-in cop for magic strings or hardcoded secrets; rubocop-magic_numbers is a separate third-party gem, not part of the standard distribution.

Action at a Distance is partially addressed: Style/GlobalVars discourages global variables (a primary source of distant mutation), and Style/ClassVars warns against class variables shared across instances. However, RuboCop cannot track instance variable mutations, method-level side effects, or distant state changes through call chains.

Style/OptionalBooleanParameter flags methods with optional boolean positional parameters and recommends keyword args, but it only covers optional booleans (not required boolean flags) and addresses style rather than the full design-smell of flag arguments as a control-coupling issue.

No RuboCop cop detects hidden dependencies (objects secretly constructing their own collaborators). Tangentially related cops like Lint/NestedMethodDefinition or Lint/MissingSuper address different concerns and miss the general DI-violation semantic.

rubocop-performance encourages performance idioms (frozen strings, constant regexes, etc.) rather than detecting unwarranted optimizations. A linter cannot judge whether an optimization was premature vs. necessary; no cop flags this design smell.

No RuboCop cop targets reimplementation of language or framework features as a class. Scattered Style cops (e.g. Style/ComparableClamp, Style/DateTime) nudge toward specific built-ins but cover narrow named cases, not the general inner-platform pattern.

Big Ball of Mud is an architectural anti-pattern requiring whole-system judgement. Metrics cops (CyclomaticComplexity, ClassLength, etc.) are symptom proxies but do not detect or flag this architectural condition directly.

Stovepipe System requires detecting near-duplicate subsystems across modules — a semantic, cross-file analysis task. Lint/DuplicateMethods only catches same-scope same-name exact duplicates; no cop detects duplicated architectural subsystems.

Database-as-IPC is a runtime/integration architectural pattern (polling a DB as a message bus). No RuboCop cop exists for this; it requires understanding of runtime intent beyond what static AST analysis can determine.

No RuboCop cop detects vendor lock-in. rubocop-rails is a framework-specific extension, not a lock-in detector. Tight coupling to a specific vendor's API is a semantic/architectural judgement with no automated rule.

RuboCop's Metrics cops (ClassLength, MethodLength, CyclomaticComplexity, AbcSize) flag size/complexity as a proxy for SRP violations, but cannot detect when a class has multiple semantic reasons to change. Size alone is neither necessary nor sufficient for SRP compliance.

No RuboCop cop detects OCP violations. Determining whether a class is properly closed for modification yet open for extension requires understanding design intent and call-site behavior — beyond static pattern matching. No relevant cop exists in the standard or widely-bundled rule sets.

Lint/MissingSuper flags missing super calls in constructors and lifecycle callbacks (a necessary but not sufficient condition for LSP), and Lint/InheritException enforces exception hierarchy correctness. These catch narrow LSP-adjacent anti-patterns but cannot verify general behavioral substitutability.

Ruby has no formal interfaces; ISP violations (modules/classes exposing too many unrelated methods) require semantic understanding of client usage. Metrics/ParameterLists is the closest proxy but measures method arity, not interface cohesion. No cop directly addresses ISP.

No RuboCop cop detects whether high-level modules depend on concrete low-level implementations vs. abstractions. Rails cops (ApplicationController, ApplicationRecord) nudge toward base-class patterns but do not enforce DIP. Dependency directionality requires semantic analysis unavailable to the linter.

Ruby has duck typing rather than formal interfaces, and RuboCop has no cop that detects coupling to concrete types vs. abstract protocols. Programming to an interface is a semantic/design principle that requires understanding call-site contracts, which RuboCop cannot assess.

No RuboCop cop identifies when inheritance should be replaced with composition. Rails/Delegate encourages delegation patterns in Rails contexts but does not flag inheritance misuse in general. Detecting composition-over-inheritance tradeoffs requires architectural judgment beyond static pattern matching.

RuboCop enforces access modifier syntax (Style/AccessModifierDeclarations, Lint/IneffectiveAccessModifier) but cannot detect whether the right things are encapsulated — i.e., whether variation points are properly isolated. Identifying what varies vs. what stays stable is a semantic design judgment.

RuboCop has no cop that measures or enforces loose coupling as a design property; coupling metrics (Feature Envy, Control Couple, Inappropriate Intimacy) require semantic/architectural judgement. Rails-extension cops touch adjacent symptoms but not coupling as a structural principle.
RuboCop is a static linter; it cannot determine whether tests pass. 'Passes the tests' is a runtime/CI concern, not a static-analysis one — no static tool can satisfy this criterion.

RuboCop's Naming/* department enforces intent-revealing conventions (predicate ? suffix, no get_/set_ prefixes, minimum parameter-name length, snake_case methods), catching a mechanical subset. It cannot detect semantically misleading names or opaque logic that humans would flag as failing to reveal intention.

Lint/DuplicateBranch, DuplicateMethods, DuplicateHashKey, DuplicateElsifCondition etc. catch literal/syntactic duplication within a file, but miss structural or semantic duplication across files; cross-file DRY violations require tools like Flay outside RuboCop.

Lint/Unused* (UnusedMethodArgument, UnusedBlockArgument, UnusedLocalVariable) catch dead local code; Metrics/* flag over-long/over-complex code as a proxy for over-engineering. These cover only a mechanical subset; the full YAGNI principle (no speculative features, no premature abstraction) requires judgement RuboCop cannot provide.

RuboCop has no cop that understands or enforces the Clean Architecture Dependency Rule. Architectural dependency enforcement requires explicit package configuration (e.g. Packwerk), which is a separate tool not bundled with RuboCop.

RuboCop has no cop that understands layered architecture or enforces separation of concerns into layers (presentation, domain, data). Style/ClassAndModuleChildren enforces namespacing style only, with no layer semantics.

RuboCop cannot reason about which layer a class belongs to or whether an inner-layer class references an outer-layer one. This is a cross-module architectural invariant requiring explicit configuration; no standard RuboCop cop addresses it.

Boy Scout Rule is a behavioral/team practice (incrementally improve touched code), not a mechanical pattern; RuboCop has no cop for it. Quality-metric cops (Metrics/MethodLength, Lint/UnusedLocalVariable, etc.) can flag symptoms but cannot detect whether a change left code cleaner.

Hexagonal 'ports as interfaces' is an architectural design concept requiring semantic understanding of domain boundaries; RuboCop has no cop that identifies or enforces port/interface definitions in the hexagonal sense.

Whether a class is an adapter implementing a port requires understanding the full architectural intent; RuboCop can inspect inheritance/module inclusion syntactically but has no rule that identifies adapter–port relationships.

Verifying that the domain core contains no I/O or framework dependencies requires tracing transitive require/call graphs across the whole codebase; RuboCop has no bundled cop for this, and third-party gems (rubocop-dependency) only provide opt-in namespace-boundary configs, not standard rules.

Driving-side correctness (external actors entering via well-defined ports) is an architectural concept with no corresponding RuboCop cop; it requires semantic knowledge of application flow that static pattern-matching cannot supply.
Driven-side correctness (app reaches external services only through adapter layers) similarly requires whole-system architectural reasoning; no standard RuboCop rule covers this.

Testability by design is an emergent architectural property; RuboCop can flag proxies (long parameter lists, high coupling) but cannot positively verify that the hexagonal design makes the core independently testable.

Enforcing inward-only dependency direction requires whole-graph layer analysis; rubocop-dependency offers namespace-boundary enforcement but is not a standard bundled rule and requires explicit per-project configuration, so no off-the-shelf detector exists.
Reuse/Release Equivalence is a package-cohesion policy (group for reuse what you release together); RuboCop has no cop measuring or enforcing this principle.
Common Closure Principle (classes that change together belong together) requires change-coupling analysis across commits; RuboCop has no cop that detects this.
Acyclic dependency detection between components requires a cross-file dependency graph; RuboCop has no such cop. The separate tool Packwerk can enforce package boundaries but is not RuboCop.
Stable Dependencies Principle requires computing instability metrics (fan-in/fan-out) per component and checking dependency direction; RuboCop has no cop for this.
Stable Abstractions Principle requires correlating abstractness with stability across a component; RuboCop has no abstraction-ratio or stability-score cop.
DDD Bounded Context enforcement requires understanding semantic domain boundaries; RuboCop has no cop for this; even Packwerk (a separate tool) enforces package boundaries, not DDD contexts.
Ubiquitous Language compliance requires validating naming against a domain glossary; RuboCop Naming cops enforce style conventions but have no domain-semantic validation capability.
Domain Model correctness (aggregates, entities, value objects, repositories in the right places) requires semantic/architectural understanding; RuboCop has no cops for DDD structural patterns.

RuboCop has no cop for DDD Repository pattern compliance or violations; this is an architectural concern requiring semantic understanding of layer boundaries that static syntax analysis cannot provide.
RuboCop has no cop for DDD Service Layer pattern compliance; Metrics/ClassLength can flag bloated controllers indirectly but does not detect missing service layer abstractions as a pattern.

Style/TrivialAccessors flags public instance variable read/write patterns that should use attr_*, and Style/Attr discourages bare attr; these catch some encapsulation gaps but no cop detects raw @ivar exposure or collection-wrapping smells specifically.

Style/AccessModifierDeclarations enforces consistent use of private/protected/public, and Lint/IneffectiveAccessModifier catches visibility modifiers applied where they have no effect; neither detects whether a public method should semantically be private based on actual usage patterns.

No RuboCop cop detects Tell Don't Ask violations; identifying external state queries followed by external decision logic requires behavioral/semantic analysis beyond RuboCop's syntactic pattern matching.

Style/StructInheritance and Style/DataInheritance detect inappropriate inheritance from Struct/Data.define (canonical delegation-candidate cases), but the general case of subclass-should-be-delegate is not covered.
RuboCop has no cop that measures inheritance hierarchy depth or flags hierarchies that should be collapsed; Metrics cops measure class/method size, not inheritance tree depth or redundancy.

RuboCop's Lint cops detect unused parameters/variables in general but have no awareness of inheritance context; no cop detects that a subclass fails to use inherited methods or fields (Refused Bequest).

Naming/MethodParameterName and Naming/BlockParameterName enforce minimum name length (default 3 chars) and flag single-letter/short parameter names, which catches the most egregious non-intention-revealing cases. However, these cops do not cover local variables, instance variables, or class-level names, and cannot judge whether an arbitrarily-long name is still meaningless (e.g., 'temp2', 'data2'). Coverage is limited to parameter naming only.

Naming/PredicateName enforces that predicate methods end with '?' and non-predicate methods don't — a narrow verb-form rule for boolean queries. Naming/AccessorMethodName enforces correct naming for accessor-like methods. Neither cop nor any other validates that class names are nouns or that method names in general are action verbs; those are semantic judgements beyond static pattern matching.

No RuboCop cop detects inconsistent vocabulary across a codebase (e.g., mixing 'get', 'fetch', 'retrieve' for the same concept). This requires cross-symbol semantic understanding that static regex-based rules cannot provide.
