No RuboCop cop can detect misleading names (e.g., a variable named 'accountList' that holds a Set). Detecting disinformation requires understanding runtime types and domain semantics, which is beyond static lexical analysis.

Metrics/MethodLength, Metrics/AbcSize, and Metrics/CyclomaticComplexity can flag overly long or complex methods, which correlates loosely with mixed abstraction levels, but none of these cops directly measure or enforce single-level abstraction. A short method can still mix abstraction levels; the concept is fundamentally semantic.
No RuboCop cop enforces Command-Query Separation. Detecting that a method both mutates state and returns a meaningful value requires data-flow analysis that RuboCop's AST-based rules do not perform.

RuboCop has narrow Lint cops that catch specific side-effect anti-patterns (e.g., Lint/AssignmentInCondition, Lint/EnsureReturn), but these cover only a tiny subset of possible side effects. The general principle — that a function should not have hidden side effects — requires semantic/data-flow analysis unavailable in RuboCop.

No RuboCop cop specifically detects boolean flag arguments or suggests splitting methods. Metrics/ParameterLists limits total parameter count but has no awareness of boolean type or flag semantics, so it does not target this pattern.

Metrics/ParameterLists flags methods exceeding the parameter count threshold (default: 5), which is the canonical static proxy for this smell, but it cannot determine whether the parameters are semantically cohesive enough to form an object — the refactoring decision itself requires human/LLM judgement.

Style/GuardClause directly detects when a method wraps its body in a conditional that could be replaced with an early return/guard clause, and offers autocorrection — this is precisely the pattern the item targets.

No RuboCop cop detects type-checking conditionals (e.g. if obj.is_a?(TypeA)) and suggests replacing them with polymorphism; Style/ClassCheck only enforces is_a? vs kind_of? stylistic consistency, and Style/CaseLikeIf targets if-elsif→case rewrites, neither of which captures this refactoring.

Several cops catch specific sub-patterns (Performance/FlatMap for map+flatten, Performance/SelectMap for select+map, Performance/Detect for select+first, Style/EachForSimpleLoop for range iteration) but there is no general rule that detects arbitrary imperative loops that could be rewritten as functional pipelines.

No RuboCop cop detects actual slow I/O in tests (network calls, real DB hits); RSpec/ExampleLength and similar cops measure test size as a weak proxy but do not detect the root cause of FIRST 'Fast' violations.

rubocop-rspec has several cops targeting shared-state leaks (RSpec/BeforeAfterAll discourages :all hooks, RSpec/InstanceVariable flags shared instance vars, RSpec/LeakyConstantDeclaration catches constants leaking into global scope), but these cover only known RSpec anti-patterns — arbitrary ordering dependencies or inter-test state mutation via external resources are not detectable.

No RuboCop cop detects tests that depend on the system clock, random seeds, environment variables, filesystem state, or external services — the FIRST 'Repeatable' principle requires semantic understanding of what makes a test environment-dependent.

RSpec/NoExpectationExample flags any RSpec example block that contains no expectation call (reports 'No expectation found in this example'); rubocop-minitest's Minitest/NoAssertions equivalently catches Minitest test methods with zero assertions — both cover the FIRST 'Self-validating' violation deterministically.

Test timeliness (tests written before/alongside code) is a temporal workflow property requiring git history analysis; RuboCop's static analysis of code structure cannot detect whether tests were written before or after implementation.

Red-Green-Refactor is a TDD workflow process detectable only via git commit ordering and history; RuboCop has no cops that can identify whether this cycle was followed — it only analyzes final code structure.

Fake It Till You Make It involves intentional hardcoded return values later replaced by real logic — a TDD strategy indistinguishable from accidental hardcoding without commit-history and intent context; no RuboCop rule targets this pattern.

Triangulation (adding multiple test cases to drive generalization) is a TDD strategy identifiable only by understanding developer intent and test evolution sequence; no RuboCop rule exists for this pattern.

Assert First (writing assertions before test setup) is a code-authoring-order practice indistinguishable in final AST from any other test structure; RuboCop has no rule targeting assertion-placement-relative-to-authoring-order.

Detecting untested legacy code requires test coverage analysis (e.g. SimpleCov), not static AST analysis; RuboCop has no cops that cross-reference production code files against test files or measure coverage.

Characterization tests are semantically distinguished by purpose (documenting existing behavior) not structure; they are syntactically identical to ordinary tests and no RuboCop rule can distinguish them.
Sprout Method/Class is a refactoring technique requiring understanding of intent and commit history; no RuboCop rule detects this pattern.
No RuboCop cop detects opportunities to apply the Wrap Method/Class refactoring pattern; this is a semantic/architectural suggestion requiring human or LLM judgement.
Ruby has no formal interfaces or protocols (only duck typing and modules); the Extract Interface construct does not exist as a discrete language feature, so there is nothing to detect.
No RuboCop cop detects hardcoded values that should be promoted to constructor/method parameters. Metrics/ParameterLists flags the opposite (too many params). Requires semantic judgement.
Core RuboCop ships no rule enforcing or suggesting gradual typing (Sorbet/RBS annotations). The rubocop-sorbet extension can enforce annotation presence but is not bundled with standard RuboCop.
RuboCop performs no type-flow analysis; it cannot detect missing or incorrect type narrowing in conditional branches.
Prototype pollution is a JavaScript-specific vulnerability exploiting JS prototype chains; Ruby has no prototype chain mechanism, so the construct does not exist.
Global scope pollution via JS prototype/window globals is a JavaScript-only construct; it does not apply to Ruby.
No RuboCop cop detects Rails models or service objects implicitly accessing request-context globals (current_user, request, etc.); this requires semantic/architectural judgement.

Security/Eval flags Kernel#eval and Binding#eval with non-literal strings (enabled by default), but misses instance_eval/class_eval blocks, send with dynamic method names, define_method with dynamic content, and other dynamic execution vectors.

No RuboCop cop specifically detects monkey patching (reopening core/third-party classes to redefine methods). Lint/DuplicateMethods flags same-class duplicate definitions but not cross-file reopening of external classes.

source_label: Style/ClassCheck — style consistency only, not duck-typing enforcement
rationale: Style/ClassCheck, Style/CaseEquality, and Style/ClassEqualityComparison only enforce style consistency among type-checking idioms; they do not flag overuse of explicit type checks as a duck-typing discipline violation. No cop discourages respond_to? or is_a? calls as anti-duck-typing.

Lint-as-build-failure is a CI/CD process configuration concern, not a code pattern detectable in source files. RuboCop itself is the lint tool; whether its exit code fails a build is a pipeline decision outside any cop's scope.

RuboCop has no cop that enforces minimum test coverage thresholds or flags untested code paths. rubocop-rspec/rubocop-minitest add test-style cops but not coverage measurement; that requires dedicated tools (SimpleCov, etc.).

Bundler/GemVersion can enforce that version specifiers are present in Gemfiles, but is disabled by default and does not validate pinning rigor (loose ~>, missing upper bounds) or external API contract adherence.

RuboCop has no cop that enforces structured (e.g., JSON) logging format or discourages plain string interpolation in log statements. No logging-discipline rules exist in core or widely-used bundled extensions.

RuboCop has no cop that detects missing or improperly propagated correlation IDs in log calls. This is a semantic/architectural concern requiring contextual understanding of request context flow, beyond static pattern matching.
No RuboCop cop exists for health check or readiness probe patterns; this is an infrastructure/operational concern requiring semantic understanding of endpoint purpose, beyond static analysis.
No RuboCop cop addresses alerting philosophy (symptoms vs. causes); this requires semantic understanding of monitoring intent that static analysis cannot provide.
No RuboCop cop enforces Postel's Law; detecting whether a method is liberal in input acceptance and conservative in output requires architectural/semantic analysis beyond RuboCop's scope.

Bundler/GemVersion and Gemspec/DependencyVersion enforce presence of version constraints in Gemfile/gemspec but do not validate that version strings conform to semver format; both cops are disabled by default and require opt-in configuration.
No RuboCop cop detects backwards compatibility breakage in APIs; Gemspec/RequiredRubyVersion only checks Ruby version alignment, not whether public interfaces remain compatible across versions.

Lint/InheritException, Lint/DuplicateRescueException, and Lint/EnsureReturn enforce structural rescue/raise correctness, but do not detect whether error contracts are explicitly defined or communicated to callers — the semantic notion of an explicit error contract is not covered.

Bundler/GemVersion (disabled by default) can enforce that direct Gemfile dependencies have version constraints, but RuboCop has no rule detecting whether transitive dependencies are pinned (e.g., sub-dependencies bounded).
Bundler/DuplicatedGem (enabled by default) detects duplicate gem entries, but no RuboCop cop detects unused gems or unnecessary requires — the core minimal footprint concern is unaddressed.

RuboCop's Bundler cops (InsecureProtocolSource, GemVersion, DuplicatedGem) enforce Gemfile style and format but do not audit gems against known vulnerability databases — that requires a separate tool like bundler-audit.

No RuboCop rule enforces single-responsibility at the gem/package boundary. Metrics cops (ClassLength, MethodLength) apply within files; Packaging cops address require-path hygiene, not semantic cohesion of a gem's public surface.

Style/MutableConstant flags mutable objects (arrays, hashes, strings) assigned to constants without .freeze, and Style/FrozenStringLiteralComment enforces the frozen-string-literal magic comment — but neither covers local variables, instance variables, or general mutability discipline beyond these two narrow patterns.

Share-nothing architecture is a distributed-systems design principle (no shared memory/disk across processes/services). RuboCop has no rule that models or enforces process-level isolation; this requires architectural review.

RuboCop has no idempotency cop. The docs explicitly note that several cops are marked 'unsafe' precisely because they cannot determine whether method calls have side effects, confirming the tool lacks semantic idempotency analysis.
No RuboCop rule detects implicit state machines or requires use of a state machine library/pattern; this is a semantic design concern beyond static AST analysis.

Style/GlobalVars flags use of Ruby global variables ($var syntax), which is a direct proxy for assuming ownership of shared global resources. However, it misses class variables (@@var), class-level instance variables, and other shared-state patterns that the checklist item covers.

Style/CommentAnnotation only enforces the format of TODO/FIXME/etc. annotation keywords; no RuboCop rule distinguishes decision-documenting comments from mechanical 'what the code does' comments — that requires human or LLM judgement.

Style/Documentation and Style/DocumentationMethod enforce that docs exist for classes/modules/methods, but RuboCop cannot detect whether docs are co-located with, separated from, or have drifted away from the code they describe — the spatial/proximity aspect is beyond static analysis.

RuboCop has no cop for executable documentation (doctests, verifiable code examples in comments). Its documentation cops only check for presence of comment blocks, not whether examples are runnable or correct.

Style/AccessModifierDeclarations enforces consistent use of private/protected/public modifiers, Style/ConstantVisibility requires explicit constant visibility, and Lint/IneffectiveAccessModifier catches modifiers silently failing on singleton methods — together covering mechanical visibility hygiene. However, whether the chosen access level is semantically appropriate for a given design is beyond static analysis.

Defence in depth is an architectural pattern (layered redundant controls). RuboCop has no cops for detecting missing validation layers, absent compensating controls, or single-point-of-failure security checks — this requires semantic/design-level judgement.

Lint/EnsureReturn catches return-in-ensure blocks that silently swallow exceptions (a classic fail-open anti-pattern). However, RuboCop has no cop for broad rescue-all clauses that grant access on error, default-allow logic, or other fail-open patterns — covering only the ensure-return subset.
Semgrep explicitly does not parse comments in source code, so no rule can detect redundant/unnecessary comments by design.
Semgrep has no coverage-tracking or diff-aware test-coverage rules; its test tooling tests Semgrep rules themselves, not user code coverage.
Semgrep has no rules for SRP or general code-complexity/responsibility analysis at the function level; it is security-pattern focused, not SOLID-aware.
No Semgrep registry rules detect files with too many responsibilities; file-level architectural concerns are outside Semgrep's pattern-matching scope.
Semgrep's test-annotation system (ruleid:/ok: comments) governs its own rule tests, not the structure of user test files; no registry rule checks test file structure.

source_label: Semgrep: demystifying taint mode (dead code skipped)
rationale: Semgrep intentionally skips dead/unreachable code in taint analysis; community rule sets cover unused imports or variables in some languages but there is no official cross-language dead-code rule in the standard registry.
Semgrep operates on source code ASTs and has no rule or mechanism to detect whether a CHANGELOG file was updated in a diff.
Semgrep has no code-coverage metrics or rules; explicit test coverage reporting is outside its design scope entirely.
Semgrep has no rule that detects tests coupling to implementation details rather than requirements/behaviour; this is a semantic/design judgement beyond pattern matching.

Semgrep supports constant propagation internally but ships no registry rule that flags duplicated literals or magic numbers as a code-quality issue; no such rule exists in semgrep-rules or the public registry.
No Semgrep rule detects code ambiguity or data-normalisation deficiencies; path-normalisation in Semgrep config is a tooling concern, not a source-code detector.
No Semgrep rule identifies heuristic logic (e.g., regex guesses, ad-hoc scoring) that should be replaced with deterministic implementations; requires semantic understanding.
No standard Semgrep rule detects brittle patterns such as error-message string matching or fragile assumptions; these require contextual judgement.
No Semgrep rule flags over-specificity (hard-coded enumerations that should be generalised loops, etc.); the concept requires semantic reasoning unavailable to pattern matchers.
Semgrep has no built-in rule for Large Class detection (excessive method/field count); the r2c-best-practices ruleset contains no such metric-based check.
No Semgrep rule detects Primitive Obsession (using raw primitives where value objects or domain types are appropriate); no such pattern exists in the public registry.

Semgrep has no rules for Data Clumps detection; identifying recurring groups of fields/parameters that should be encapsulated requires cross-location semantic analysis that Semgrep's pattern-matching model does not support, and no registry rule covers this.
Semgrep can match switch/case syntax but has no standard rule that flags switch statements as a code smell (as a proxy for missing polymorphism); the registry contains no such code-quality rule.

Detecting Temporary Fields requires tracking which class fields are conditionally null/unset across execution paths — a data-flow/semantic analysis that Semgrep's pattern matcher cannot perform; no registry rule covers this smell.

Refused Bequest (subclass ignoring/overriding inherited members without use) requires cross-class inheritance analysis and semantic understanding of usage; Semgrep has no rule for this and its design-level scope is outside the security-focused registry.

Detecting Alternative Classes with Different Interfaces requires understanding semantic equivalence across unrelated class hierarchies — a cross-module semantic judgment that no Semgrep rule addresses.

Divergent Change is a change-history and SRP-level architectural smell; Semgrep operates on static snapshots of code with no change-pattern or responsibility analysis, and no registry rule covers this.
Shotgun Surgery requires analysing the blast radius of a change across the codebase — a cross-file coupling metric that Semgrep cannot compute; no registry rule covers this.

Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies require detecting mirrored class-hierarchy growth across separate class trees — a structural/architectural analysis beyond Semgrep's pattern-matching scope; no registry rule exists for this.

Semgrep has no rule for Lazy Class. Detecting a class that 'does too little' requires holistic class complexity metrics (method count, field count, LOC) that are outside Semgrep's AST pattern-matching design. Semgrep's own documentation explicitly states it does not track code smells or technical debt.

Semgrep has no rule for Data Class. Identifying a class composed only of fields and accessors requires whole-class structural analysis (scanning all methods to confirm absence of behavior), which Semgrep cannot perform. No rule exists in the public registry.

Semgrep has no rule for Speculative Generality. Detecting unused abstractions, dead hooks, or parameters added for hypothetical future use requires cross-file dataflow and reachability analysis beyond Semgrep's scope. No community rule covers this pattern.

Semgrep has no rule for comment over-reliance. Measuring whether comments are compensating for poor naming or complex logic requires semantic understanding of intent, not syntactic pattern matching. Semgrep explicitly does not track code quality smells of this nature.

Semgrep has no rule for Feature Envy. Detecting that a method accesses another class's data more than its own requires cross-class dataflow analysis and access-frequency counting across method bodies — well outside Semgrep's pattern-matching capabilities.

Semgrep has no rule for Inappropriate Intimacy. Detecting bidirectional coupling or internal-field access between classes requires relational/interprocedural analysis that Semgrep does not support. No registry rule addresses this smell.

Semgrep's ellipsis operator can match chained method calls syntactically in a custom rule, but no standard or widely-used bundled Semgrep rule detects message chains as a code smell. Verdicting 'none' because no off-the-shelf rule exists; a custom rule would be possible but is not provided.

Semgrep has no rule for Middle Man. Detecting that a class delegates the majority of its methods to another class requires whole-class delegation-ratio analysis across method bodies, which is beyond Semgrep's AST pattern scope.

God Object detection requires class-level metrics (method count, field count, LOC, LCOM) that Semgrep does not compute; Semgrep is a security-focused pattern matcher with no code-quality metric rules.
Anemic Domain Model detection requires semantic analysis of whether domain classes carry meaningful behavior vs. pure getters/setters across the call graph — beyond Semgrep's syntactic pattern scope.
Poltergeist detection requires cross-file lifetime and delegation analysis (class instantiated, delegates all work, discarded); Semgrep has no bundled rule for this architectural pattern.
Yo-Yo Problem requires traversing inheritance depth across multiple files; Semgrep's pattern matcher has no metric for inheritance chain depth and no bundled rule for this smell.
Sequential Coupling requires interprocedural control-flow / state-machine analysis (must call init before use); Semgrep has no bundled rule detecting mandatory call ordering.

Constant Interface (an interface containing only constants, no methods) could in principle be matched syntactically, but Semgrep ships no bundled rule for it; no evidence found in the registry or community rules.
Interface Bloat detection requires counting interface members against a threshold metric; Semgrep has no bundled rule for interface method-count checks.
Singleton pattern usage can be matched syntactically, but Semgrep ships no bundled rule detecting singleton overuse; 'overuse' also requires cross-class frequency analysis beyond pattern matching.
Semgrep has no complexity metrics or CFG path-count analysis; it cannot detect exponential path growth from nested conditionals.
Tramp data requires interprocedural analysis of which parameters are unused by intermediate call sites — beyond Semgrep's AST pattern-matching scope; no rules exist.
Spaghetti code is a holistic structural judgment requiring complexity and coupling metrics; Semgrep has no code-quality features of this kind.
Clone/duplicate-block detection requires cross-file similarity analysis; Semgrep's pattern engine matches fixed AST patterns, not similarity across arbitrary code blocks.
Golden Hammer is a design-level judgment about inappropriate pattern over-application; completely outside Semgrep's rule scope.
Semgrep catches narrow syntactic dead-code patterns (useless-assign, useless-ifelse, leftover debug calls) but cannot perform whole-program unreachable-code or unused-function analysis.

Semgrep has strong off-the-shelf coverage for hardcoded credentials/secrets (p/secrets ruleset, jwt-hardcode, express-jwt-hardcoded-secret, etc.) but misses general magic numbers, hardcoded URLs, and non-credential config literals.
Action at a distance requires interprocedural global-state side-effect analysis; no Semgrep rules address this architectural antipattern.

Semgrep has no rule for the flag-argument smell (boolean parameter that controls which of two behaviors a function performs). Semgrep explicitly excludes code-quality/code-smell detection; its registry contains no boolean-parameter or flag-argument rule.

Hidden dependencies on global/singleton state require data-flow or architectural analysis beyond Semgrep's pattern-matching scope. No rule in the Semgrep registry addresses implicit coupling to undeclared global state.

Premature optimization requires inferring developer intent (speculative vs. measured performance trade-off), which is outside the scope of Semgrep's syntactic/semantic pattern matching. No rule exists in the registry.

Detecting reimplementation of platform/framework features (inner-platform effect) requires knowing what the target platform provides and recognising equivalent custom code — an architectural judgement Semgrep cannot make with its pattern rules. No registry rule covers this.

Big Ball of Mud is a whole-codebase structural anti-pattern (absence of discernible architecture). Semgrep operates on individual files/patterns and has no metric or rule that could surface this structural property.
Stovepipe System requires cross-module analysis of reuse patterns across system boundaries — beyond Semgrep's per-file pattern matching. No registry rule exists for this architectural anti-pattern.

Database-as-IPC (polling a DB table as a message queue) requires recognising the intent behind query patterns inside loops and distinguishing them from legitimate DB reads. No Semgrep rule for this pattern was found in the registry or web sources.

Vendor lock-in detection requires knowing which APIs are vendor-proprietary and whether an abstraction layer is absent — an architectural judgement. No pre-built Semgrep rule for vendor lock-in exists; custom rules could enforce specific import bans but none ship by default.
Semgrep is a SAST/security tool; no standard or bundled rule exists for SRP violations (classes/functions doing too much). Architectural analysis is outside its scope.
No Semgrep rule detects OCP violations (modifying rather than extending). The registry has no architectural or design-principle category; this requires semantic/structural judgement.
No Semgrep rule exists for LSP violations (non-substitutable subtypes). Semgrep registry search for 'liskov' returns zero results.
No Semgrep rule covers ISP violations (fat interfaces). Registry search for 'interface segregation' returns zero results.
No Semgrep rule detects DIP violations (depending on concrete classes instead of abstractions). Registry search for 'dependency inversion' returns zero results.
No Semgrep rule enforces 'program to an interface' guidance. Semgrep rules are scoped to security patterns, not GoF design principles.
No Semgrep rule detects preferring composition over inheritance. Registry search for 'composition over inheritance' returns zero results.
No Semgrep rule covers 'encapsulate what varies'. Encapsulation/design-variance detection requires architectural context Semgrep does not provide.

Measuring coupling between classes/modules requires semantic analysis of dependency graphs and cohesion metrics; Semgrep is a syntactic pattern-matcher with no built-in rules for coupling metrics or GoF design-quality checks.
Whether code passes its tests is a runtime/CI criterion, not a static-analysis property; no SAST tool including Semgrep can determine this from source text alone.

Intent-revealing naming is a semantic/readability judgment; while Semgrep supports metavariable-regex for naming conventions, no out-of-the-box rule covers this class, and the general principle requires human or LLM judgment rather than pattern matching.

Semgrep does not detect code duplication; it matches patterns within a file/AST but has no clone-detection engine, and third-party commentary explicitly notes duplication tracking is absent from Semgrep's capabilities.

YAGNI/over-engineering is a semantic design judgment about whether features are needed; Semgrep has no built-in rules for this, and while micro-patterns like useless-eqeq exist, they do not cover the YAGNI principle broadly.

Semgrep can match import statements via patterns, but there are no out-of-the-box rules enforcing Clean Architecture's dependency rule; enforcement requires project-specific custom rule authoring with knowledge of which layers are 'inner' vs 'outer'.
Determining whether concerns are properly separated into layers requires architectural/semantic understanding of module organization; Semgrep has no built-in rules for layer-separation analysis.

While Semgrep can detect specific forbidden imports via custom rules, no out-of-the-box rule exists for the Clean Architecture principle that inner layers must not import outer/infrastructure code; this requires project-specific rule authoring.

Boy Scout Rule requires comparing code quality across commits/diffs over time; Semgrep is a single-snapshot static analyser with no ability to detect progressive degradation or improvement relative to prior state.
Semgrep has no standard rule that verifies ports are declared as interfaces/abstract types; enforcing this requires understanding domain-boundary semantics, not syntactic pattern matching.
Verifying that adapter classes implement the correct port interfaces requires cross-file type-hierarchy analysis with architectural intent; no such rule exists in Semgrep's standard registry.

Detecting that domain core is free of infrastructure/framework imports requires knowing which packages constitute 'infrastructure', a project-specific semantic boundary Semgrep has no standard rule for.
Enforcing that primary (driving) adapters call through port interfaces rather than reaching directly into the core is an architectural constraint with no corresponding standard Semgrep rule.
Verifying driven-side (secondary) adapters satisfy port contracts is a structural design concern; Semgrep's rule registry contains no standard rule targeting hexagonal driven-side compliance.
Testability-by-design is a holistic architectural property (core instantiable without infrastructure wiring); no Semgrep standard rule assesses this class of issue.

Dependency-direction enforcement (inward flow only) requires project-level import-graph analysis with labelled layers; Semgrep has no standard rule for this, and the Semgrep registry has no hexagonal or clean-architecture packs.

Reuse/Release Equivalence is an organizational/versioning principle about which classes belong in the same release unit. No Semgrep rule exists; the concept requires release-process metadata and change-history analysis, not AST pattern matching.

Common Closure Principle requires tracking which classes change together across commits — a historical/coupling metric. No Semgrep rule exists; this is fundamentally beyond syntactic pattern matching.

Acyclic Dependencies Principle requires building a global component-level dependency graph and detecting cycles. Semgrep has no dedicated cycle-detection rule; its Supply Chain dependency graph targets vulnerability reachability, not architectural cycles. No out-of-the-box rule found.

Stable Dependencies Principle requires computing per-component stability metrics (efferent/afferent coupling ratios) and verifying dependency direction. No Semgrep rule exists; quantitative stability metrics are outside pattern-matching scope.

Stable Abstractions Principle requires measuring the ratio of abstract to concrete classes per component and correlating with stability. No Semgrep rule exists; this is a quantitative architectural metric, not a syntactic pattern.

DDD Bounded Context enforcement requires understanding business-domain boundaries and detecting cross-context leakage — inherently semantic. No Semgrep rule exists in the registry for bounded context detection.

Ubiquitous Language consistency requires matching code identifiers against a project-specific domain glossary. No Semgrep rule exists; this requires semantic/domain knowledge unavailable to a generic pattern matcher.
Domain Model integrity (entity/aggregate/value-object invariants) requires semantic business-logic understanding. No Semgrep rule exists; enforcing DDD model purity is beyond AST pattern matching.
Semgrep has no rules for DDD Repository pattern violations; it is a security SAST tool with no architectural or domain-layer pattern enforcement.
No Semgrep rules exist for DDD Service Layer violations; detecting misplaced business logic across architectural layers requires semantic/contextual judgement beyond Semgrep's rule engine.
Semgrep has no rules targeting exposed mutable public fields or collection encapsulation violations; it can match structural patterns but ships no rule for this class of design smell.
Semgrep's access-control rules target missing authorization on endpoints (security), not general over-exposure of public APIs or unnecessary public visibility in class design.
No Semgrep rule detects the Tell-Don't-Ask antipattern (querying object state then acting on it externally); this requires inter-statement semantic reasoning beyond pattern matching.
Semgrep can match inheritance syntax but ships no rule for detecting when subclassing should be replaced with delegation; this is a design refactoring judgement with no documented rule.
No Semgrep rule detects near-empty or redundant class hierarchies suitable for collapsing; comparing superclass and subclass content across files is outside Semgrep's single-file pattern model.
Refused Bequest (subclass ignoring inherited interface/methods) requires cross-file usage analysis; Semgrep has no documented rule for this smell.

Semgrep is an AST pattern-matcher; determining whether a name is 'intention-revealing' requires semantic judgment about developer intent and domain context — no rule in the standard registry addresses this.
Semgrep has no standard rule enforcing the noun/verb convention for class vs method names; detecting grammatical role of identifiers requires NLP, not pattern matching.
Ensuring one word per concept (e.g. not mixing 'fetch'/'get'/'retrieve') requires cross-codebase semantic consistency analysis; Semgrep has no rule for synonym detection across identifiers.
Detecting misleading names (e.g. a name implying a type or behaviour it doesn't have) requires semantic understanding of intent vs behaviour; no Semgrep rule addresses this naming quality class.
Detecting mixed abstraction levels within a function requires understanding the semantic layer of each call-site; Semgrep's structural pattern matching has no rule for this principle.
Command-Query Separation requires data-flow/side-effect analysis to classify methods as commands or queries; Semgrep's standard registry has no CQS rule.

Detecting general side effects (mutations of external state, I/O) requires whole-program semantic analysis; Semgrep's taint mode targets security sinks, not purity enforcement, and no standard rule covers this.

The flag-argument anti-pattern (boolean parameter switching fundamentally different behaviour) is not covered by any rule in the Semgrep standard registry; confirmed by exhaustive search of semgrep/semgrep-rules and the public registry.

Semgrep is a security-focused SAST tool with no standard rules for detecting long parameter lists or the Introduce Parameter Object refactoring opportunity. Code quality metrics like parameter count are handled by dedicated linters (ESLint, Pylint, RuboCop), not Semgrep.

Semgrep has no built-in rules for detecting deeply nested conditionals or recommending guard clauses. Cyclomatic complexity and nesting-depth metrics are outside Semgrep's scope; they belong to tools like SonarQube or language-specific linters.

No standard Semgrep rule detects switch/if-else chains on type tags that should be replaced with polymorphism. This is an architectural refactoring pattern requiring semantic/contextual judgment, not a security vulnerability Semgrep targets.

Semgrep has no standard rules for detecting imperative loops that could be rewritten as functional pipelines (map/filter/reduce). This is a code-style/paradigm preference handled by language-specific linters, not a security-focused tool like Semgrep.

Semgrep has no standard rules for detecting slow tests (e.g., Thread.sleep, time.sleep, network I/O in test code). Test performance is a test-framework and CI/CD concern, not a Semgrep security pattern.

Semgrep has no standard rules for detecting test-ordering dependencies or shared mutable state between tests. Test isolation analysis requires semantic understanding of test lifecycle that Semgrep's pattern-matching does not provide.

While Semgrep has security-oriented rules flagging Math.random() in cryptographic contexts, there are no standard rules targeting non-determinism specifically in test code (Date.now(), random seeds, system-time usage inside test functions). The existing crypto-randomness rules are not scoped to test files and would not serve as a reliable detector for this FIRST-principle violation.

Semgrep has no standard rule for detecting test methods that lack assertions (the 'self-validating' FIRST principle). The only assertion-related rule in the registry (php.lang.security.assert-use) flags PHP's assert() as a security risk in production code, not missing assertions in tests.

"Timely" means writing tests before production code. Static analysis operates on a snapshot with no access to commit history or authorship order, so Semgrep cannot determine whether tests preceded the code they cover. No rule in the Semgrep registry addresses this.

Red-Green-Refactor is a process discipline (iterative TDD cycle). It requires tracking the temporal sequence of commits and test states across a development history — entirely outside the scope of static AST analysis. Semgrep has no rule for this.

"Fake It Till You Make It" is a TDD technique involving intentionally hardcoded return values as a temporary step toward generalization. While Semgrep can flag hardcoded literals in various contexts, it cannot distinguish a deliberate TDD stub from legitimate constants, and no rule targets this pattern as a TDD anti-pattern or incomplete implementation signal.

Triangulation is a TDD technique of adding test cases to force the implementation to generalize. Detecting it requires understanding the relationship between a growing test suite and corresponding implementation changes over time — a semantic and historical judgment Semgrep cannot make.

"Assert First" is a TDD authoring style (write the assertion before the setup/act code). Even if assertion position in a test body could be mechanically checked, Semgrep has no rule targeting this pattern, and the signal is ambiguous (assertions can legitimately appear anywhere).

Detecting "code without tests" requires cross-file coverage analysis (mapping production functions to test callsites). Semgrep performs intra-file or limited cross-file pattern matching but has no built-in rule or ecosystem support for identifying untested functions/classes. This is the domain of coverage tools (pytest-cov, JaCoCo, etc.), not Semgrep.

Characterization Tests are tests written to capture current (possibly buggy) behavior of legacy code. Distinguishing a characterization test from any other test requires semantic intent that cannot be inferred from code structure alone. No Semgrep rule addresses this concept.

Sprout Method/Class is a legacy-code refactoring technique (adding new behavior as a new method/class rather than modifying existing code). Semgrep cannot detect this pattern because it requires understanding design intent and comparing against prior code state — neither is available to a single-snapshot static analyzer.

Detecting when a method or class should be wrapped (Decorator/Wrapper pattern) requires architectural judgment across class relationships — outside Semgrep's syntactic pattern-matching scope. No rule in the standard registry addresses this refactoring suggestion.
Extract Interface is a design-level refactoring that requires reasoning about class hierarchies and coupling — beyond Semgrep's capability. No standard or community rule exists for this.

Semgrep can flag specific hardcoded literals (e.g., hardcoded secrets), but deciding whether a constructor/method argument should be parameterized is a design judgment, not a detectable pattern. No standard rule covers this class of refactoring suggestion.

Semgrep's type-awareness features are for matching typed metavariables, not for detecting the absence of type annotations. No standard rule flags untyped functions/variables as needing gradual typing.

No dedicated Semgrep rule for detecting missing or incorrect type narrowing patterns in TypeScript/JS was found. The TypeScript ruleset (semgrep.dev/p/typescript) targets security issues, not type narrowing correctness.

Semgrep ships prototype-pollution-assignment and prototype-pollution-loop rules that flag bracket-notation object property assignments from user input. They catch common syntactic patterns (CWE-915) but miss semantically equivalent patterns mediated through libraries or spread operators.

No dedicated Semgrep rule for detecting global scope pollution exists. Semgrep GitHub issue #10325 explicitly documents that the tool lacks reliable aliasing between global scope, `this`, and `window`, making general global-scope-pollution detection infeasible with current rules.

Semgrep's framework-native analysis (Pro/Code tier) can taint-track Flask `g` and Django request objects into security sinks, but detecting the architectural anti-pattern of implicit request context access inside domain/model classes is a design judgment not covered by any standard open-source rule.

Semgrep has explicit rules for eval()/exec() in Python, JavaScript (eval + new Function()), Ruby, and PHP, covering the most common dynamic-execution sinks. However, indirect dynamic execution paths (e.g., importlib with dynamic strings, __import__, compile()+exec chains, framework-specific loaders) are not covered, so coverage is syntactic rather than fully semantic.

Semgrep covers prototype pollution (a security-relevant subset of monkey patching) in JavaScript with dedicated rules, but has no general detection of runtime patching of built-in types or modules across languages. The coverage is narrow: security-critical JS prototype mutation only.

No Semgrep rules found for enforcing duck typing discipline, detecting missing type annotations, or flagging inappropriate isinstance/hasattr usage patterns. This requires semantic/contextual judgement beyond syntactic pattern matching.

Lint-as-build-failure is a CI/CD process policy (e.g., ensuring lint steps use exit-code enforcement in Makefile or GitHub Actions YAML). Semgrep has no rules that audit whether lint steps are configured to fail the build; this is outside Semgrep's rule scope.
No Semgrep rules found that detect missing or unenforced test coverage thresholds in CI configurations or project configs. This is an organizational policy check, not a code pattern Semgrep targets.

Semgrep Supply Chain performs dependency vulnerability scanning and can flag dependencies without pinned versions in some manifest formats, but it is SCA-mode only (not default static analysis), covers vulnerabilities rather than general pinning policy, and does not validate external API contract schemas. Coverage is narrow and opt-in.

A C#-specific rule detects string interpolation in logging calls (csharp.lang.best-practice.structured-logging.structured-logging), nudging toward structured logging. No equivalent rules exist in the standard registry for Python (print() vs logging), JavaScript (console.log), or other languages, making coverage language-narrow and not comprehensive.

No Semgrep rules found for detecting missing correlation ID propagation in HTTP middleware or request/response handlers. This is an architectural pattern check requiring context-aware analysis that Semgrep's syntactic rules cannot perform.

Semgrep has no standard rule that detects missing or misconfigured health check / readiness probe endpoints. Health check presence is an infrastructure/design concern; searching the registry and community rules returned no matching rule.
Alerting on symptoms vs. root causes is a monitoring strategy principle, not a static code pattern. Semgrep has no rule that distinguishes symptom-based from cause-based alert definitions.

Postel's Law (robustness principle — be conservative in output, liberal in input) is a high-level API design principle with no concrete syntactic pattern. No Semgrep rule encodes this check; it requires semantic/contextual judgement.
Semgrep uses semver internally for rule versioning but has no rule that detects semver violations in application version strings, package manifests, or API version identifiers.

Semgrep Supply Chain's Upgrade Guidance detects breaking changes in third-party library upgrades you consume, not breaking changes your own code/APIs introduce. No rule covers backwards-compatibility of your own API surface.

Semgrep has error-handling rules (catching vs. ignoring exceptions) but no rule that enforces consistent, explicitly documented error contracts (stable error codes, uniform response shapes) across API endpoints.

source_label: Semgrep Supply Chain — why no findings
rationale: Semgrep Supply Chain consumes existing lockfiles to scan for vulnerabilities; it does NOT flag when a lockfile is absent or when transitive dependencies are unpinned. Official docs state it simply skips unpinned deps rather than alerting on them.
Semgrep Supply Chain detects vulnerable or malicious dependencies, not unused or unnecessary ones. No rule enforces minimal dependency footprint or flags dead/redundant imports at the package level.

Semgrep Supply Chain provides 80k+ SCA rules with reachability analysis for known vulnerable dependencies, but this is a separate paid product (not the free standard rule set). The open-source `semgrep --config=auto` does not include dependency auditing; it requires opt-in to Supply Chain.

Single Responsibility Principle at the package level requires measuring module cohesion and business-logic scope — architectural analysis beyond Semgrep's AST pattern-matching and dataflow capabilities. No documented rule exists for this.

Semgrep has Python-specific rules flagging mutable default arguments (e.g., `default-mutable-dict`), a narrow proxy for immutability preference, but no general cross-language rules enforcing immutability as a design principle.

Share-nothing architecture is an architectural/design constraint requiring cross-component semantic analysis. Semgrep has no standard rules enforcing this; Trail of Bits race-condition rules (third-party, not bundled) touch a narrow subset of thread-safety patterns but not the architectural principle.
Idempotency is a semantic property of operations across invocations and external system state; static pattern matching cannot determine it. No Semgrep rules address idempotency detection.

Detecting implicit ad-hoc state machines (e.g., scattered boolean flags, stringly-typed status fields) requires control-flow graph analysis and semantic intent recognition beyond Semgrep's capabilities. No documented rules exist for this.

Semgrep Pro's cross-file taint analysis can track data flowing through global variables, and Trail of Bits rules flag concurrent write races (e.g., `racy-write-to-map` in Go), covering a narrow race-condition subset. General ownership-assumption violations are not detectable.

Distinguishing comments that explain 'why' (decisions) from comments that explain 'what' (mechanics) is a semantic/NLP judgment. Semgrep can match comment presence via generic patterns but cannot evaluate comment intent or quality.

Semgrep has no standard rule that detects documentation placed far from the code it describes. Comment matching is syntactically possible in generic mode but requires custom rules; no off-the-shelf rule covers this class.

No Semgrep rule exists for detecting missing doctests, missing runnable examples, or other executable-documentation patterns. This is a documentation-quality concern with no standard Semgrep detector.

Semgrep has domain-specific least-privilege rules (overly permissive os.chmod, Docker no-new-privileges, Kubernetes run-as-non-root, subprocess shell=True) but coverage is fragmented across domains and misses IAM wildcards and general access-control over-grants, so only partial coverage of the class.
Defence in depth is an architectural property (layered controls, no single point of failure) that cannot be reduced to a syntactic pattern; Semgrep has no rule for detecting its absence.

The p/secure-defaults ruleset and individual rules (e.g., WTF_CSRF_CHECK_DEFAULT=False, subprocess shell=True) catch specific fail-open patterns, but general fail-open logic in error handlers or conditional branches requires semantic reasoning that Semgrep cannot perform.
CodeQL has no rule for comments that merely restate code. It detects commented-out code (e.g. cpp-commented-out-code, py-commented-out-code) but not explanatory redundancy — a semantic judgment call.
CodeQL performs static analysis only; it cannot determine whether new or changed code is covered by unit tests, which requires runtime test execution and diff-aware coverage tooling.

CodeQL has no SRP-violation rule for functions. C/C++ has cpp-complex-block and related complexity hints, but no cross-language rule that flags functions doing too many things — this is an architectural judgment.
CodeQL has no rule for file-level responsibility analysis. File cohesion is a design-level concern outside CodeQL's AST/data-flow model.
CodeQL has no rules for test file organization or structure. Test infrastructure conventions are outside CodeQL's design scope.

CodeQL has extensive dead-code rules across languages (js/unreachable-statement, js/unused-local-variable, py/unreachable-statement, java/local-variable-never-read, etc.) covering unreachable statements and unused locals/imports. Coverage is uneven: exported/public symbols and unused class members are often missed, and rule availability varies by language.
CodeQL analyzes source code ASTs and data flows; it has no mechanism to check whether a CHANGELOG file was updated in a commit. This is a process/VCS-level check.
Explicit test coverage metrics require runtime test execution; CodeQL is a static analysis tool and does not measure or report coverage percentages.

No CodeQL standard rule detects tests coupling to implementation internals. The java/visible-for-testing-abuse rule flags production code accessing @VisibleForTesting members from non-test code — an access control concern, not a test-quality detector. No rule identifies tests exercising private methods or internal state rather than public API behavior.

CodeQL has language-specific rules for structural duplicates within a single construct (py/duplicate-key-dict-literal catches duplicate dict keys, js/duplicate-property catches duplicate object properties), but no general 'magic number / repeated literal' rule that detects repeated constant values across a file or codebase that should be extracted into named constants.

CodeQL covers one concrete form of code ambiguity — operator precedence — with rules like js/unclear-operator-precedence (JavaScript) and go/whitespace-contradicts-precedence (Go). Other ambiguity classes (implicit coercions, ambiguous conditional logic, data normalization) have no standard rule.

No CodeQL standard rule detects heuristic or approximate logic that should be replaced with deterministic algorithms. This requires semantic understanding of algorithmic intent that is outside CodeQL's shipped query set.

The broader 'brittle patterns' class — string-matching on error messages, hardcoded OS/path assumptions, fragile regex assumptions — has no standard CodeQL rule. CodeQL has hardcoded-credential/secret rules (security-focused) but these do not cover the design-level brittleness patterns described by this item.

No CodeQL standard rule detects over-specificity or failure to generalize. This is a design-intent judgment that requires semantic understanding of whether a pattern is appropriately specific or should be generalized; it is outside CodeQL's shipped rule set.

CodeQL provides metric infrastructure (MetricRefType, getCyclomaticComplexity, getMaintainabilityIndex) that could be used to write a Large Class detector, but no standard shipped rule flags classes with too many methods, fields, or lines. No cross-language or Java/Python Large Class rule exists in the default query suites.

No CodeQL standard rule detects Primitive Obsession (excessive use of primitives where value objects should be introduced). Detecting this anti-pattern requires design-intent judgment that is beyond CodeQL's shipped static analysis rules.

CodeQL has no query detecting groups of fields/parameters that cluster together and should be extracted into a class; its published rules target security, correctness, and basic readability — not this structural smell.
CodeQL detects duplicate switch cases (js/duplicate-switch-case) but has no rule for the design smell of using switch/if-else chains on type tags instead of polymorphism.
CodeQL has no query for fields that are only populated in certain code paths and null/empty otherwise; its unused-assignment rules target variables never read, not conditionally-used instance fields.

CodeQL's inheritance-related rules focus on correctness (missing overrides, virtual calls from constructors) and have no detector for subclasses that override parent methods while ignoring inherited state or behaviour.
Detecting two classes that do the same thing under different interface names requires cross-class behavioural equivalence analysis that is outside CodeQL's documented rule scope.

SRP violations (one class changed for many reasons) require reasoning about change history or responsibility semantics; CodeQL is a snapshot analyser with no published rule for divergent-change detection.
Shotgun surgery is an edit-coupling pattern detectable only from version-control history; CodeQL analyses a single code snapshot and has no query for cross-file change coupling.
CodeQL has no query for structural symmetry between class hierarchies; its inheritance rules address correctness issues, not the architectural smell of mirrored subclass trees.

CodeQL has no documented rule for detecting Lazy Class (a class with too little responsibility). It is a structural/architectural smell that requires judgement about class purpose, which CodeQL's security-oriented rule set does not cover.

CodeQL has no published rule targeting the Data Class smell (classes with only fields/getters/setters and no meaningful behavior). While CodeQL has library types for analyzing Kotlin data class declarations, this is structural modelling, not smell detection.

CodeQL has no rule for Speculative Generality. A tangentially related rule (java/unused-reference-type) catches some dead abstractions but does not detect over-engineering for hypothetical future use, which requires intent-level reasoning.

CodeQL comment-related rules (e.g. cpp/poorly-documented-function, cpp/commented-out-code) detect under-documentation or commented-out code in C++ only, not over-reliance on comments as a compensatory smell. No general cross-language rule for this smell exists.

CodeQL has FeatureEnvy library predicates in its Java standard library but no published, documented query rule that surfaces Feature Envy findings to end users. It does not appear in any shipped query suite.

CodeQL has cs/coupled-types (C# only, in csharp-security-and-quality.qls) which detects pairs of classes overly coupled through mutual field/method access — a close match for Inappropriate Intimacy. No equivalent rule exists for Java, JavaScript, or other languages, so coverage is language-restricted.
CodeQL has no published rule detecting Message Chains (long a().b().c() call chains). While CodeQL can model call chains via data flow, no off-the-shelf query targets this smell.

CodeQL has no published rule for the Middle Man smell (a class that mostly delegates all calls to another). This requires reasoning about delegation ratios across a class's method set, which is beyond CodeQL's shipped query catalogue.
CodeQL has no standard rule for God Object / Blob; detecting excessive class size requires architectural/metric-based analysis outside CodeQL's standard query suites.
CodeQL has no rule for Anemic Domain Model; distinguishing data-only classes from behaviorally rich domain objects requires semantic/contextual judgment not covered by standard CodeQL queries.
CodeQL has no rule for Poltergeist; detecting short-lived classes that merely delegate requires lifecycle and call-graph analysis beyond CodeQL's standard suite.
CodeQL has no rule for the Yo-Yo Problem; measuring inheritance depth and cross-hierarchy coupling requires architecture-level metrics not present in CodeQL's standard queries.
CodeQL has no rule for Sequential Coupling; detecting enforced method-call ordering requires data-flow/state-machine reasoning not covered in the standard CodeQL suite.
CodeQL has a documented Java/Kotlin rule flagging interfaces containing only constants, but coverage is Java/Kotlin-only with no equivalent for other languages in the standard suite.
CodeQL has no standard rule for Interface Bloat (interfaces with too many methods); this metric-based structural smell is not in the standard CodeQL query suites.
CodeQL has no standard rule for Singleton Overuse; identifying overuse of the pattern requires design-intent reasoning beyond CodeQL's standard queries.

CodeQL has no documented rule for detecting combinatorial explosion patterns (exponential branching, nested combination loops, or recursive paths without memoization); this requires algorithmic complexity analysis beyond CodeQL's rule set.

CodeQL's unused-parameter rules (e.g. java/useless-parameter) catch the extreme case where a parameter is never read at all, but the core tramp data pattern — data actively passed through multiple intermediate layers without local consumption — is not detected.

CodeQL provides control-flow graph infrastructure but has no standard query that flags overly tangled or deeply nested control flow as spaghetti code; detecting this pattern requires cyclomatic/structural complexity thresholds not present in the default query suites.

CodeQL includes narrow structural-duplication queries (go/duplicate-switch-case, go/duplicate-condition, js/duplicate-html-attribute) that catch copy-paste within specific constructs, but there is no general code-clone detection rule covering arbitrary duplicated blocks across files.
Golden Hammer is an architectural anti-pattern requiring judgment about design intent and solution fit; CodeQL has no rule for it and the pattern is not amenable to static pattern matching.

CodeQL reliably detects syntactically unreachable code (py/unreachable-statement, js/unreachable-statement, cpp/dead-code-goto) and calls to deprecated APIs (cs/call-to-obsolete-method), covering the dead-code facet of lava flow, but not the broader accumulation of obsolete artifacts (unused classes, commented-out code, legacy modules) that characterize the full anti-pattern.

CodeQL has strong hardcoded-credential rules across multiple languages (rb/hardcoded-credentials, java/hardcoded-credential-api-call, rust/hard-coded-cryptographic-value) covering security-sensitive literals, but has no general magic-literal rule for arbitrary hardcoded constants (port numbers, business thresholds, file paths in non-security contexts).

CodeQL has no standard query targeting action-at-a-distance — code that produces non-obvious side effects on distant or global state; while CodeQL's dataflow framework could support custom queries, no off-the-shelf rule in the default suites addresses this pattern.
No CodeQL shipped query targets flag arguments (boolean/enum parameters that toggle code paths); this design smell requires semantic intent analysis beyond CodeQL's standard rule set.

CodeQL has no rule for detecting hidden dependencies on global state or singletons not visible in a function's signature; this requires whole-program semantic reasoning not covered by standard queries.
Premature optimization requires subjective judgment about intent and readability trade-offs; no CodeQL query targets this class of code smell.
Detecting inner-platform effect requires knowledge of what the language/framework already provides to compare against custom re-implementations; no CodeQL query addresses this pattern.

CodeQL's Coupling module provides library predicates (afferentCoupling, lackOfCohesion) but no shipped query detects Big Ball of Mud as an architectural anti-pattern; the closest shipped rule (cs-coupled-types / Inappropriate Intimacy) targets localized two-class coupling, not systemic architectural decay.
Stovepipe System detection requires recognizing missing shared abstractions across subsystems, an architectural-intent judgment for which CodeQL has no standard query.
CodeQL can identify database access patterns but has no rule to detect a database or queue being misused as an inter-process communication channel; this requires architectural-level intent analysis.
CodeQL's coupling predicates are internal library infrastructure, not shipped queries; no standard CodeQL rule flags over-reliance on a specific vendor's API or assesses switching difficulty.

CodeQL has no rule that detects Single Responsibility violations (classes with too many responsibilities, excessive method counts, or mixed concerns). SRP requires semantic/contextual judgement that static pattern matching cannot provide.

CodeQL has no rule for Open/Closed Principle violations. Detecting whether a class is closed for modification but open for extension requires architectural/design-level reasoning beyond CodeQL's documented query repertoire.

CodeQL detects narrow LSP-adjacent violations: Python signature mismatches in overriding/overridden methods, and Java/C#/C++ virtual-call-in-constructor issues. These catch mechanical proxy patterns but miss the general semantic form of LSP (substitutability of behaviour).

CodeQL has no rule detecting Interface Segregation violations (fat interfaces, clients forced to depend on methods they don't use). The closest rule (java/constants-only-interface) targets a constants anti-pattern, not ISP.

CodeQL has no rule detecting Dependency Inversion violations (depending on concrete classes rather than abstractions). The Angular-specific js/angular-dependency-injection-mismatch is framework-specific plumbing, not DIP detection.

CodeQL has no rule enforcing 'program to an interface' — i.e., flagging code that depends on concrete types where an abstraction exists. This requires semantic understanding of the design intent, beyond documented CodeQL queries.

CodeQL has no rule recommending composition over inheritance. While cpp/undisciplined-multiple-inheritance flags problematic multiple-inheritance patterns, it does not detect single-inheritance cases where composition would be preferable.

CodeQL's java/internal-representation-exposure detects a concrete instance of encapsulation failure (returning mutable internal references), which is a mechanical proxy for 'encapsulate what varies'. It misses the broader principle (e.g., not exposing variation points via public fields or direct concrete-type coupling).

CodeQL's standard libraries expose coupling metrics (afferent/efferent coupling counts) for measurement, but no shipped query flags tightly-coupled designs or enforces the GoF loose-coupling principle; metrics are not detectors.
"Passes the tests" is a runtime property requiring test execution; CodeQL is a static analyzer and has no mechanism to evaluate whether code satisfies a test suite.

CodeQL has a handful of narrow, language-specific naming rules (e.g. cpp/short-global-name, js/suspicious-method-name-declaration) but no general cross-language rule covering intention-revealing naming as a class; the general principle requires semantic/contextual judgment.

CodeQL ships duplicate-condition and duplicate-branch rules across several languages that catch narrow syntactic duplication, but has no general clone-detection or copy-paste DRY enforcement across method/class boundaries.

CodeQL has mature unused-variable, dead-code, and unused-type rules across Java, Python, C++, C#, and JS/TS — a meaningful subset of YAGNI — but YAGNI also covers over-abstraction and speculative generality, which no CodeQL rule addresses.

CodeQL's cyclic-import rule catches bidirectional dependency loops but does not enforce the directional constraint of Clean Architecture's Dependency Rule (outer layers must not be imported by inner layers); cycles are a subset, not a superset, of Dependency Rule violations.
CodeQL has no built-in rule for detecting layer-separation violations; enforcing separate concerns into layers requires layer topology knowledge that cannot be expressed without custom queries.

No standard CodeQL rule enforces that inner architectural layers must not import outer layers; the cyclic-import rule catches only cycles, not the unidirectional violation where an inner module references an outer one without a cycle.

The Boy Scout Rule (leaving code cleaner than found — better naming, removed dead code, minor refactors) is a holistic human judgement call; CodeQL's dead-code / unused-variable rules in the security-and-quality suite cover a narrow slice (removal of dead code) but do not flag naming improvements, structural cleanup, or the broader intent of continuous incremental improvement.

CodeQL has no rule for enforcing that ports in a hexagonal architecture are expressed as interfaces/protocols; it performs data-flow and AST analysis for security bugs, not architectural contract enforcement.
CodeQL cannot detect whether adapter classes correctly implement port interfaces in the hexagonal sense; it has no built-in rule for architectural adapter/port conformance.

Verifying that the domain core imports no framework/infrastructure packages requires understanding architectural layer boundaries; CodeQL has no rule for this, though a custom QL query could be written to track import dependencies.
Classifying and validating driving-side (primary) adapters is an architectural design concern; CodeQL has no rule for detecting hexagonal driving-side violations.
Classifying and validating driven-side (secondary) adapters is an architectural design concern; CodeQL has no built-in rule for hexagonal driven-side conformance.
Testability by design (e.g. dependency injection, no hard-coded singletons at port boundaries) is a structural/architectural judgement; CodeQL has no rule targeting this pattern.

Enforcing inward-pointing dependencies (dependency inversion / dependency rule) requires resolving module-layer membership, which CodeQL's standard query suites do not model; there is no built-in rule for this architectural invariant.
CodeQL has no rule for Reuse/Release Equivalence; detecting whether components are released together because they are reused together requires version/release history analysis outside CodeQL's scope.
CodeQL has no rule for Common Closure; detecting whether co-changing classes are co-packaged requires change-history correlation, which is beyond CodeQL's static AST/data-flow analysis.

CodeQL detects cyclic imports in Python (py/cyclic-import, py/unsafe-cyclic-import) covering module-level dependency cycles, but no equivalent rules exist for Java, JavaScript/TypeScript, C#, Go, or other supported languages.

CodeQL has no rule for Stable Dependencies; checking that dependencies flow toward more stable components requires computing stability metrics across the component graph, which is outside CodeQL's standard query packs.
CodeQL has no rule for Stable Abstractions; correlating a component's abstraction level with its stability metric is an architectural analysis not represented in any standard CodeQL query.
CodeQL has no DDD-aware rules; validating bounded-context boundaries requires domain knowledge that cannot be inferred from code structure alone.
CodeQL has no rule for Ubiquitous Language consistency; enforcing uniform domain terminology across a codebase requires semantic/naming analysis beyond CodeQL's vulnerability-focused scope.
CodeQL has no DDD domain-model rules; detecting misuse of entities, value objects, or aggregates requires pattern recognition tied to domain conventions that CodeQL does not model.

CodeQL has no rules for DDD Repository pattern violations (business logic in repositories, bypassed repositories, persistence leaking into domain). Architectural layering is outside CodeQL's detection scope.
CodeQL has no rules for DDD Service Layer violations (anemic domain model, domain logic in application services). It does not analyse architectural layering or DDD principles.

Java and C# each have a rule detecting mutable internal fields/collections returned directly from getters or assigned without defensive copying — a key subset of this class. Public primitive fields and broader record/struct exposure are not covered. Applies only to Java and C#, not all CodeQL languages.

The mutable-field-exposure rules (java/internal-representation-exposure, cs/expose-implementation) catch one concrete form of indecent exposure, and java/constants-only-interface catches constants polluting public API. No rule broadly detects an oversized public API surface or unnecessary public methods.
CodeQL has no rule for Tell-Don't-Ask / Law of Demeter violations (getter chaining, feature envy, querying state externally then acting on it). Requires semantic/contextual judgement.

CodeQL has no rule detecting when subclassing should be replaced with delegation/composition. Inheritance-related rules focus on correctness (shadowing, virtual calls), not architectural composition choices.

CodeQL has no rule detecting unnecessary inheritance hierarchies where parent and child could be merged. Detecting this requires understanding class purpose and relationships beyond syntactic analysis.
CodeQL has no rule for Refused Bequest (subclasses that inherit but throw, provide empty overrides, or leave inherited members unused). No documented query targets this anti-pattern.
CodeQL has no rule for evaluating whether identifiers reveal intent; this requires domain-semantic judgement that static pattern matching cannot supply.
CodeQL has no rule enforcing class-as-noun / method-as-verb conventions; the java/confusing-method-name rule only flags capitalization ambiguity, not part-of-speech naming.
CodeQL has no rule detecting inconsistent synonyms across similar operations (fetch vs retrieve vs get); consistent-concept naming requires cross-codebase semantic reasoning.
CodeQL has no rule detecting misleading names (e.g. a name implying one behaviour while the body does another); this is a semantic intent mismatch that static pattern analysis cannot resolve.
CodeQL has no rule detecting abstraction-level mixing within a function body; this architectural principle requires contextual reasoning beyond what CodeQL's query model provides.
CodeQL has no rule for Command-Query Separation violations; detecting methods that both mutate state and return a meaningful value as an anti-pattern is not in its standard rule set.

CodeQL's useless-expression / useless-assignment rules flag the absence of expected side effects, not the presence of unexpected ones in otherwise pure functions; no rule targets unexpected side-effect design violations.
CodeQL has no rule flagging boolean flag arguments as a design anti-pattern; no standard query targets this Clean Code principle.

CodeQL has no rule detecting long parameter lists or suggesting parameter object refactoring. The C# rule cs/too-many-ref-parameters targets only excessive ref/out parameters specifically, not general parameter count; no equivalent exists for other languages or the general case.

CodeQL has no rule detecting deeply nested conditionals or guard-clause refactoring opportunities. cs/nested-if-statements detects only a narrow case (adjacent ifs combinable with &&), not general nesting depth or early-return opportunities. No cyclomatic complexity threshold rule exists in the standard packs.

java/chained-type-tests detects chains of instanceof checks that should use polymorphism; csharp/cs-chained-type-tests covers the C# 'is' equivalent. Misses switch-on-type-tag patterns, modern switch expressions with pattern matching, and languages other than Java/C#.
CodeQL has no rule detecting imperative loops that could be replaced with functional pipeline operations (map/filter/reduce/streams). No such refactoring suggestion exists in any standard query pack.

CodeQL has no rule detecting slow tests (e.g., tests containing Thread.sleep, network calls, or database connections). java/sleep-with-lock-held is a concurrency bug rule, not a test-quality rule, and does not target test code specifically.

CodeQL has no rule detecting test isolation violations such as shared mutable static state between tests or order-dependent test execution. Test independence is a design property requiring contextual reasoning beyond CodeQL's standard rule set.

CodeQL has no rule detecting non-deterministic tests (e.g., use of unseeded Random, System.currentTimeMillis, or wall-clock dependencies in test code). Repeatability is a contextual test-design property with no standard CodeQL detector.

CodeQL has no rule detecting tests that lack assertions entirely. Python rules like py/imprecise-assert and py/asserts-tuple address assertion quality or misuse, not absence of assertions; no language pack detects empty or assertion-free test methods.

source_label: CodeQL query help — no test-timing category exists
rationale: TDD timing discipline (tests written before production code) requires analysing commit history and development workflow; CodeQL is a static AST/dataflow analyser with no concept of authoring order, and no standard query addresses test-first timing.

source_label: CodeQL query help — no TDD-cycle category exists
rationale: Red-Green-Refactor is a development-process cycle requiring observation of test states across commits; CodeQL analyses a single snapshot of source code and has no queries related to this cycle.

source_label: CodeQL query help — no TDD-stub category exists
rationale: Detecting hardcoded return values used as TDD stubs would require distinguishing deliberate stubs from production constants; no such query exists in CodeQL's standard suite.

source_label: CodeQL query help — no test-sufficiency queries
rationale: Triangulation (adding test cases to force generalisation) is a TDD authoring strategy detectable only by reasoning about test-suite evolution over time; CodeQL has no queries for test-case sufficiency or generalisation pressure.

source_label: CodeQL query help — no assert-ordering queries
rationale: Assert-First style is a within-test ordering convention; CodeQL does not analyse internal structure of test methods for assertion-placement patterns, and no such query exists in the standard suite.

The only adjacent CodeQL artefact is js/test-in-files ('Number of tests'), a @kind treemap metric query that counts tests per JavaScript file — it produces no code-scanning alerts, is not in the default/security/quality suites, and does not flag untested files as violations.

source_label: CodeQL query help — no test-classification queries
rationale: Characterisation tests is a testing strategy concept; CodeQL cannot distinguish a characterisation test from any other test, and no standard query addresses this pattern.

source_label: CodeQL query help — no refactoring-pattern queries
rationale: Sprout Method/Class is a refactoring design pattern; CodeQL analyses code correctness and security, not refactoring quality, and has no queries detecting whether a sprout pattern was applied.

Wrap Method/Class is a refactoring pattern (adding a wrapper/decorator around an existing class or method), not a detectable defect. CodeQL has no rule for this and cannot have one — the construct is not a bug class.
Extract Interface is a refactoring improvement (pulling a shared interface out of a concrete class), not a bug. CodeQL has no rule for this pattern — it is outside the tool's defect-detection scope.

Parameterizing constructors/methods (replacing hardcoded literals with parameters) is a refactoring discipline. CodeQL has no rule for this general pattern; the closest rule (js-hardcoded-data-interpreted-as-code) targets hardcoded data fed to eval/exec — a security issue, not a parameterization smell.

Gradual typing (incrementally adding type annotations to dynamically-typed code) is a team/architectural practice, not a detectable defect class. CodeQL has no rule encouraging or enforcing type annotation adoption.

CodeQL catches narrow type-narrowing failures: js-useless-type-test flags always-true/false type guards, and js-type-confusion-through-parameter-tampering flags missing runtime narrowing between string/array on HTTP inputs. These cover specific degenerate cases but not the general pattern of using a union-typed value without narrowing it first.

CodeQL ships two default-suite rules specifically for prototype pollution: js-prototype-polluting-assignment (attacker-controlled key writes to __proto__) and js-prototype-pollution (unsafe merge/extend calls with untrusted data). Both are high-precision and included in javascript-code-scanning.qls.

CodeQL's js-missing-variable-declaration (default suite, high precision) detects variables used inside functions without being declared, which causes them to implicitly become globals — the core global scope pollution pattern.

The architectural practice of passing request context explicitly rather than accessing it via global/thread-local in the model layer requires semantic understanding of call graphs and layering conventions. CodeQL has no general rule for this; the only related rules (js-angular-missing-explicit-injection and variants) are AngularJS-specific DI checks, not a general architectural enforcement.

CodeQL's js/code-injection and py/code-injection rules flag eval/exec only when tainted user-controlled data flows into them — they do not enforce a blanket 'no eval' policy. Unconditional use of eval with safe/literal arguments is not flagged.

CodeQL detects prototype pollution (a security-exploit form of monkey-patching) via js/prototype-pollution and js/prototype-polluting-assignment, but does not detect intentional monkey-patching of built-in objects as a code smell in the general case.
No CodeQL rule audits duck-typing discipline or checks whether code validates assumed interfaces before use; this requires semantic/design-level judgement.
Whether lint is wired as a build/CI failure gate is a process/configuration concern, not a source-code pattern; static analysis of application source cannot detect it.
Enforcing test-coverage thresholds is a CI/build configuration concern; CodeQL has no rule that detects the absence of coverage enforcement in project configuration.
CodeQL has java/android-missing-certificate-pinning for Android TLS pinning, but no general rule for unpinned npm/pip/etc. dependency versions or schema-validation of external API contracts.
CodeQL's logging rules (js/clear-text-logging, js/log-injection) address security concerns only; no rule enforces structured logging or detects use of unstructured console.log-style calls.
No CodeQL rule detects the absence of correlation/trace IDs in logging; this is an observability architecture concern requiring semantic/contextual judgement.

Health check and readiness probe configuration is an infrastructure/deployment concern (Kubernetes probes, Docker HEALTHCHECK, load-balancer endpoints) — it lives in manifests and platform config, not in application source code that static analysis can parse. CodeQL has no rule targeting this class.

Alerting on symptoms vs. causes is a monitoring philosophy / observability design principle. It cannot be mechanically detected in source code; it requires understanding what metrics/alerts are wired up and why. CodeQL has no rule for this.

Postel's Law (be liberal in what you accept, strict in what you send) is an architectural/API design principle. CodeQL has no rule that checks whether input handling is permissive or output encoding is strict in the general sense; individual security rules (e.g. input validation, XSS) address narrow sub-cases but not the principle itself.
